topics.title: SQA: Definitions and Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** SQA: Definitions and Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Quality"?

**Quality** is a multi-dimensional concept that has been defined by several quality gurus and standardization bodies. KTU 2024 expects you to know the formal definitions as they appear in textbooks and ISO standards.

### Formal Definitions of Quality

| Authority | Definition of Quality |
|---|---|
| **Philip B. Crosby** | *"Quality is conformance to requirements"* |
| **Joseph M. Juran** | *"Quality is fitness for use"* |
| **W. Edwards Deming** | *"Quality is a predictable degree of uniformity and dependability, at low cost and suited to the market"* |
| **ISO 8402 (Legacy)** | *"The totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs"* |
| **IEEE Standard 610** | *"The degree to which a system, component, or process meets specified requirements and meets user/customer needs and expectations"* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** As per the revised syllabus, **Software Quality** is *"the degree to which a software product meets its specified requirements and satisfies the needs and expectations of its users."* This merges the **conformance view** (Crosby) with the **fitness-for-use view** (Juran).

---

## 1.2 What is "Software Quality"?

**Software Quality** is the conformance of a software product to its:
1. **Explicit requirements** (functional, performance documented in SRS)
2. **Implicit requirements** (usability, maintainability, portability not always written down)
3. **User/customer expectations** (real-world usage needs)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Metaphor** 🍳
>
> Imagine you order a dish at a restaurant:
> - **Quality of the dish** = the taste, presentation, freshness, and whether it matches what the menu promised.
> - **Quality Assurance (QA)** = the *entire kitchen process* — hygiene standards, recipe adherence, ingredient sourcing, chef training, regular audits. QA is **process-oriented** and **preventive**.
> - **Quality Control (QC)** = the *final taste test* by the head chef before serving, plus checking the temperature. QC is **product-oriented** and **detective**.
>
> Just as a good restaurant builds quality **into** every step (QA) and inspects the final dish (QC), SQA builds quality **into** every phase of the SDLC and inspects the final software.

---

## 1.3 What is Software Quality Assurance (SQA)?

**Software Quality Assurance (SQA)** is a systematic, planned set of activities that ensures that software engineering processes and products conform to requirements, standards, and procedures.

### Key Components of SQA

```
SQA = Process + Product + Standards + Reviews + Tests + Documentation
```

- **Process Quality:** Defines the engineering process (SDLC model, methods, tools).
- **Product Quality:** Defines the software attributes (reliability, usability, efficiency).
- **Standards:** ISO 9001, IEEE 730 (Software Quality Assurance Standard), CMMI.
- **Reviews:** Formal Technical Reviews, walkthroughs, inspections.
- **Testing:** Unit, integration, system, acceptance testing.
- **Documentation:** SQA Plan, Test Plan, Audit Reports.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Point:** SQA is **not the same as testing**. Testing is one *sub-activity* of SQA. SQA is a **broader umbrella** that covers process definition, training, audits, reviews, configuration management, and testing.

---

## 1.4 Software Quality Control (SQC)

**Software Quality Control (SQC)** is the set of activities designed to evaluate a developed software product by finding defects. SQC operates on the *finished* product or intermediate deliverables.

| Aspect | SQA (Assurance) | SQC (Control) |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Process | Product |
| **Approach** | Preventive | Detective |
| **When** | Throughout SDLC | After product creation |
| **Activity** | Standards, training, audits | Testing, inspections |
| **Goal** | Build quality in | Find defects |

---

## 1.5 Quality vs. Grade vs. Reliability

Three commonly confused terms in KTU papers:

- **Quality** → Conformance to specification.
- **Grade** → A category assigned to a product based on its features. A *high-grade* product can have *low quality* (full of bugs).
- **Reliability** → The probability that the software will perform its intended function without failure over a specified period under stated conditions.

---

## 1.6 Cost of Quality (CoQ) — The Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** is *NOT* the cost of building a quality product. It is the **total cost of all efforts related to quality** — both prevention and failure.

$$CoQ = Prevention\ Cost + Appraisal\ Cost + Internal\ Failure\ Cost + External\ Failure\ Cost$$

| Cost Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Prevention Cost** | Money spent to *prevent* defects | Training, process improvement, tools |
| **Appraisal Cost** | Cost of *inspecting* products | Testing, reviews, audits |
| **Internal Failure Cost** | Cost of defects found *before* delivery | Rework, debugging, retest |
| **External Failure Cost** | Cost of defects found *after* delivery | Warranty, patches, reputation loss |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Core Views of Quality

For KTU 2024, you must understand quality from **three philosophical perspectives** (this is a frequently asked 7-marker):

### 2.1.1 Transcendental View
- Quality is **innate, intangible, recognizable with experience**.
- "I know it when I see it."
- Cannot be precisely defined or measured.
- *Example:* A masterpiece painting — quality is felt, not measured.

### 2.1.2 User-Based View
- Quality is **fitness for the user's purpose** (Juran's view).
- Subjective: different users perceive different quality.
- *Example:* A "simple" app for an elderly user vs. a "feature-rich" app for a power user.

### 2.1.3 Manufacturing-Based View
- Quality is **conformance to requirements / specifications** (Crosby's view).
- Objective and measurable.
- *Example:* A part manufactured to ±0.01 mm tolerance.

### 2.1.4 Product-Based View
- Quality is a function of a specific, measurable attribute of the product.
- *Example:* Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) for software.

### 2.1.5 Value-Based View
- Quality is defined in terms of price/cost and willingness to pay.
- *Example:* A "premium" software suite vs. a free version.

---

## 2.2 Why Do We Need SQA? The Three Pressures

```
            ┌────────────────────┐
            │  Market Pressure   │ → Earlier delivery
            └─────────┬──────────┘
                      │
            ┌─────────▼──────────┐
            │ Competitive        │ → Better features
            │ Pressure           │
            └─────────┬──────────┘
                      │
            ┌─────────▼──────────┐
            │  Customer Pressure │ → Higher quality
            └────────────────────┘
```

**Software Crisis (1968 NATO Conference):** Term coined to describe the failure of large-scale software projects due to poor quality practices — gave birth to modern SQA.

---

## 2.3 The SQA Framework (KTU Mandatory Diagram Topic)

SQA operates at three levels:

### 2.3.1 Technical Level
- **Reviews and Inspections** — Fagan Inspections, walkthroughs.
- **Testing** — Unit, integration, system, acceptance.
- **Static Analysis** — Linting, complexity analysis (cyclomatic complexity).
- **Metrics** — LOC, function points, defect density.

### 2.3.2 Process Level
- **Process Definition** — SDLC model selection, methodology.
- **Process Monitoring** — CMM/CMMI levels, process audits.
- **Process Improvement** — TQM, Six Sigma, Kaizen.

### 2.3.3 Management Level
- **SQA Plan** — Documented at project start (IEEE 730).
- **Standards and Procedures** — Coding standards, documentation templates.
- **Configuration Management** — Version control, baselines.
- **Risk Management** — Identifying quality risks early.

---

## 2.4 Software Quality Factors (McCall's Quality Model — Classic KTU Topic)

**McCall's Quality Model (1977)** defines 11 quality factors grouped into three categories. This is a **guaranteed 7-marker** in KTU exams.

### 2.4.1 Product Operation Factors (User-visible)

| Factor | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Correctness** | Degree to which software fulfills its specified purpose |
| **Reliability** | Capability to perform as expected without failure |
| **Efficiency** | Amount of computing resources needed |
| **Integrity** | Protection from unauthorized access (security) |
| **Usability** | Ease of learning and use |

### 2.4.2 Product Revision Factors (Maintenance)

| Factor | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Maintainability** | Ease of making changes |
| **Flexibility** | Ease of adapting to new environments |
| **Testability** | Ease of testing |

### 2.4.3 Product Transition Factors (Adaptation)

| Factor | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Portability** | Ease of transferring to new platform |
| **Reusability** | Use of components in new systems |
| **Interoperability** | Ability to couple with other systems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note for KTU 2024:** Although McCall's model is still taught, the syllabus also references **ISO/IEC 9126** (now superseded by **ISO/IEC 25010**), which defines 6 main quality characteristics:
> **Functionality, Reliability, Usability, Efficiency, Maintainability, Portability** (with **Security** as a sub-characteristic of Functionality). The 2011 update **ISO/IEC 25010** added 8 characteristics: **Functional Suitability, Performance Efficiency, Compatibility, Usability, Reliability, Security, Maintainability, Portability.**

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Quality Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \dfrac{\text{Defects found}}{\text{Size (KLOC or FP)}}$ | Bugs per 1,000 lines or function point | Defects/KLOC |
| **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | $MTTF = \dfrac{\text{Total operating time}}{\text{Number of failures}}$ | Average operational time before failure | Hours |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$ | Average time between two consecutive failures | Hours |
| **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\text{Total repair time}}{\text{Number of repairs}}$ | Average downtime per failure | Hours |
| **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | Uptime percentage | % |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity (V(G))** | $V(G) = e - n + 2p$ | Measures control-flow complexity | Scalar |
| **Function Point (FP)** | $FP = UFP \times VAF$ | Size measure independent of language | FP |
| **Total Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | $CoQ = P_{cost} + A_{cost} + IF_{cost} + EF_{cost}$ | Total quality-related expense | Currency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cyclomatic Complexity Note:** Where $e$ = number of edges, $n$ = number of nodes, $p$ = number of connected components (usually $p = 1$). $V(G) \le 10$ is considered acceptable in KTU theory questions.

---

## 2.6 The Software Quality Dilemma (Paradox)

| Strategy | Outcome |
|---|---|
| Adding inspectors (more testing) | Eventually, marginal returns decrease — law of diminishing returns |
| Adding reviews early (prevention) | Long-term CoQ drops dramatically |
| Neglecting quality | External failure costs skyrocket |

**Optimal Quality Investment:** SQA aims to find the *minimum point* of the **CoQ curve**, which occurs when prevention + appraisal cost equals the *sum* of internal + external failure costs.

```
CoQ (Cost)
│
│   \                  / External Failure Cost
│    \                /
│     \              /
│      \            /
│       \   ___   /  ← Optimal point (minimum total)
│        \ /   \ /
│         *─────*──── CoQ Curve (U-shape)
│        /  Internal Failure + Prevention + Appraisal
│
└───────────────────────────── Quality Investment →
```

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of SQA

1. **Avionics & Healthcare** — DO-178C, IEC 62304 demand rigorous SQA because failure kills.
2. **Banking & Finance** — PCI-DSS, SOX compliance requires auditable SQA processes.
3. **Defense & Aerospace** — CMMI Level 5 certification is a procurement prerequisite.
4. **Open Source** — Used to establish contributor trust (e.g., Linux Foundation quality frameworks).
5. **Startups & SMEs** — ISO 9001 certification opens global market access.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive step-by-step mathematical derivations and worked examples for the cost-of-quality and reliability formulas most commonly tested in KTU ESE.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Cost of Quality (CoQ) Optimization

### Problem Statement
A software company incurred the following costs during the development of Project Phoenix (a payroll management system):

| Cost Component | Amount (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|
| Staff training (Prevention) | 2.5 |
| Process improvement tools (Prevention) | 1.5 |
| Formal technical reviews (Appraisal) | 3.0 |
| Testing (Appraisal) | 4.0 |
| Rework from internal testing (Internal Failure) | 6.0 |
| Bug-fix before release (Internal Failure) | 2.0 |
| Customer support after release (External Failure) | 12.0 |
| Patch releases (External Failure) | 5.0 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the four CoQ components.**

From the data table:
- Prevention Cost $P$ = Training + Process improvement tools
- Appraisal Cost $A$ = Reviews + Testing
- Internal Failure Cost $IF$ = Rework + Bug-fix before release
- External Failure Cost $EF$ = Customer support + Patch releases

**Step 2: Compute each component.**

$$
P = 2.5 + 1.5 = 4.0 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

$$
A = 3.0 + 4.0 = 7.0 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

$$
IF = 6.0 + 2.0 = 8.0 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

$$
EF = 12.0 + 5.0 = 17.0 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the CoQ formula.**

$$
CoQ = P + A + IF + EF
$$

$$
CoQ = 4.0 + 7.0 + 8.0 + 17.0
$$

$$
\boxed{CoQ = 36.0 \text{ Lakhs}}
$$

**Step 4: Analyze the distribution (this is the "valuation key" insight).**

Compute percentage shares:

$$
\% P = \frac{4.0}{36.0} \times 100\% = 11.11\%
$$

$$
\% A = \frac{7.0}{36.0} \times 100\% = 19.44\%
$$

$$
\% IF = \frac{8.0}{36.0} \times 100\% = 22.22\%
$$

$$
\% EF = \frac{17.0}{36.0} \times 100\% = 47.22\%
$$

**Step 5: Interpret.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **External failure cost is 47.22% of total CoQ** — a clear red flag. Best practice from quality gurus (Crosby, Juran) states that **prevention + appraisal should be ≥ 50% of total CoQ**. Currently it is only **30.55%**. The organization should invest more in prevention (training, process improvement) to *reduce* the much larger external failure cost — the famous **1-10-100 rule** (it costs ₹1 to fix a bug in requirements, ₹10 in coding, ₹100 in production).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — MTBF, Availability, Reliability

### Problem Statement
An e-commerce web server has been monitored for 1000 hours. During this period, it failed 4 times. The total repair time (downtime) across the 4 failures was 8 hours. Compute MTTF, MTTR, MTBF, and Availability.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute MTTF.**

$$
MTTF = \frac{\text{Total operating time}}{\text{Number of failures}}
$$

$$
MTTF = \frac{1000}{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{MTTF = 250 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Step 2: Compute MTTR.**

$$
MTTR = \frac{\text{Total repair time}}{\text{Number of failures}}
$$

$$
MTTR = \frac{8}{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{MTTR = 2 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute MTBF.**

$$
MTBF = MTTF + MTTR
$$

$$
MTBF = 250 + 2 = 252 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Availability.**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A = \frac{252}{252 + 2} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A = \frac{252}{254} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 99.21\%}
$$

**Step 5: Sanity check using alternate formula.**

$$
A = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \times 100\% = \frac{250}{252} \times 100\% \approx 99.21\% \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Defect Density and Estimation

### Problem Statement
A 50,000-LOC Java project had 125 defects reported during 3 months of system testing. Calculate:
1. Defect Density (per KLOC)
2. Total Defects using industry-average of 0.5 defects/KLOC for a similar module of 30 KLOC.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate Defect Density.**

$$
DD = \frac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Size in KLOC}}
$$

$$
DD = \frac{125}{50,000 / 1000} = \frac{125}{50}
$$

$$
\boxed{DD = 2.5 \text{ defects per KLOC}}
$$

**Step 2: Estimate total defects for similar 30-KLOC module.**

$$
\text{Estimated Defects} = DD \times \text{Size in KLOC}
$$

$$
\text{Estimated Defects} = 2.5 \times 30
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Estimated Defects} = 75 \text{ defects}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Cyclomatic Complexity

### Problem Statement
A program flow graph has 12 nodes and 18 edges, with 1 connected component. Compute the cyclomatic complexity.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply the formula.**

$$
V(G) = e - n + 2p
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
V(G) = 18 - 12 + 2(1)
$$

$$
V(G) = 6 + 2
$$

$$
\boxed{V(G) = 8}
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.**

Since $V(G) = 8 \le 10$, the program is **acceptable** in complexity, has low testing risk, and requires approximately **8 independent test paths** for full statement-coverage.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quality Factor Mapping

This matrix maps **McCall's 11 factors** to **ISO 25010's 8 characteristics** — a frequently asked 7-mark comparison question.

| McCall Factor | Maps to ISO 25010 | Sub-Characteristic (25010) |
|---|---|---|
| Correctness | Functional Suitability | Functional Completeness, Correctness |
| Reliability | Reliability | Maturity, Availability, Fault Tolerance, Recoverability |
| Efficiency | Performance Efficiency | Time Behavior, Resource Utilization, Capacity |
| Integrity | Security | Confidentiality, Integrity, Non-repudiation, Authenticity, Accountability |
| Usability | Usability | Appropriateness Recognizability, Learnability, Operability, User Error Protection, UI Aesthetics, Accessibility |
| Maintainability | Maintainability | Modularity, Reusability, Analyzability, Modifiability, Testability |
| Flexibility | Maintainability | Modifiability, Adaptability |
| Testability | Maintainability | Testability |
| Portability | Portability | Adaptability, Installability, Replaceability |
| Reusability | Maintainability | Reusability |
| Interoperability | Compatibility | Co-existence, Interoperability |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section uses Mermaid diagrams to visualize the SQA conceptual framework, process hierarchy, and quality cost model. All diagrams follow the **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards** — alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown inside node labels, no reserved keywords.

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — SQA Umbrella Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    sqaCore[SQA CORE] --> procEng[Process Engineering]
    sqaCore --> qualMgt[Quality Management]
    sqaCore --> stdPol[Standards and Policies]

    procEng --> sdlcSel[SDLC Model Selection]
    procEng --> procDef[Process Definition]
    procEng --> procMon[Process Monitoring]

    qualMgt --> sqaPlan[SQA Plan IEEE 730]
    qualMgt --> sqaAudits[SQA Audits]
    qualMgt --> reviews[Formal Reviews and Inspections]
    qualMgt --> testing[Testing Activities]

    stdPol --> iso9001[ISO 9001]
    stdPol --> cmmi[CMMI Levels]
    stdPol --> ieeestd[IEEE 730 and 12207]

    reviews --> walkthru[Walkthroughs]
    reviews --> fagan[Fagan Inspections]
    reviews --> techRev[Technical Reviews]

    testing --> unitT[Unit Testing]
    testing --> integT[Integration Testing]
    testing --> systT[System Testing]
    testing --> accT[Acceptance Testing]

    sqaCore --> output[Quality Software Product]
    sdlcSel --> output
    procDef --> output
    procMon --> output
    sqaPlan --> output
    sqaAudits --> output
    reviews --> output
    testing --> output
    iso9001 --> output
    cmmi --> output
    ieeestd --> output
```

---


```mermaid
    mcCallRoot --> revGroup[Product Revision]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section is structured exactly as per the KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern. Every question is tagged with a simulated past-year tag and is mapped to **Bloom's Level** (L1–L6).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Instructions:** Answer in 60–80 words. Definitions must include the named authority (Crosby, Juran, etc.) wherever applicable.

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]** [Bloom L1: Remember]
**Define Software Quality. List any four software quality factors from McCall's quality model.**

#### Model Answer
Software Quality is the degree to which a software product meets its specified requirements and satisfies user/customer needs and expectations (combining Crosby's *conformance* and Juran's *fitness for use*). McCall's quality model (1977) lists 11 factors grouped into three categories. Four examples are: **(i) Correctness** (software fulfills its purpose), **(ii) Reliability** (performs without failure), **(iii) Usability** (ease of use), and **(iv) Maintainability** (ease of modification).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]** [Bloom L2: Understand]
**Differentiate between Software Quality Assurance (SQA) and Software Quality Control (SQC).**

#### Model Answer
SQA is **process-oriented** and **preventive** — it defines, monitors, and improves the engineering process throughout the SDLC. SQC is **product-oriented** and **detective** — it finds defects in the finished product using testing and inspection. SQA covers standards, training, reviews, and audits; SQC is limited to verification and validation activities like test execution.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Instructions:** Each Part B question is a KTU Module 1 internal choice. Two fully worked-out model answers (Q-A and Q-B) are provided. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice-1]*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks [Bloom L4: Analyze]

**Explain the different definitions of "Quality" given by major quality gurus. Why is "Quality" considered a multi-dimensional concept in software engineering?**

#### Model Answer

Quality has been defined in multiple ways by quality management pioneers:

**1. Philip B. Crosby (1979)** — *"Quality is conformance to requirements."* It is measured by the cost of non-conformance; the performance standard is *zero defects*.

**2. Joseph M. Juran (1974)** — *"Quality is fitness for use."* It focuses on customer satisfaction across five dimensions: quality of design, conformance, availability, safety, and field use.

**3. W. Edwards Deming (1986)** — *"Quality is a predictable degree of uniformity and dependability, at low cost and suited to the market."* Deming emphasized statistical process control and the *14 Points for Management*.

**4. Genichi Taguchi** — *"Quality is the loss a product causes to society after being shipped, other than losses caused by its intrinsic functions."* Taguchi introduced the *loss function* to quantify quality deviation from target.

**5. ISO 8402 (Legacy, replaced by ISO 9000:2015)** — *"The totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs."*

**6. IEEE Std 610.12-1990** — *"The degree to which a system, component, or process meets specified requirements and meets user/customer needs and expectations."*

**Why Quality is Multi-Dimensional:**
In software engineering, quality is not a single property. A system can be functionally correct (correctness) but slow (poor efficiency) — or highly efficient but hard to maintain. Software quality must simultaneously satisfy **functional suitability, reliability, usability, performance efficiency, maintainability, security, compatibility, and portability** (per ISO 25010). These dimensions often conflict (e.g., adding security may reduce performance), forcing trade-offs. Thus, quality is a vector, not a scalar.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks [Bloom L3: Apply / L5: Evaluate]

**A project incurred the following costs in ₹ Lakhs: Training 3.0, Testing 5.0, Rework 4.0, Customer Support 8.0, Process Tools 2.0, Reviews 3.0, Patch Releases 6.0, Pre-release Bug-fix 1.0. Calculate CoQ and analyse the cost distribution with recommendations.**

#### Model Answer

**Step 1: Categorize the costs.**

- **Prevention Cost (P)** = Training + Process Tools = $3.0 + 2.0 = 5.0$
- **Appraisal Cost (A)** = Testing + Reviews = $5.0 + 3.0 = 8.0$
- **Internal Failure Cost (IF)** = Rework + Pre-release Bug-fix = $4.0 + 1.0 = 5.0$
- **External Failure Cost (EF)** = Customer Support + Patch Releases = $8.0 + 6.0 = 14.0$

**Step 2: Compute total CoQ.**

$$
CoQ = P + A + IF + EF = 5.0 + 8.0 + 5.0 + 14.0 = 32.0 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 3: Compute percentage distribution.**

$$
\% P = \frac{5.0}{32.0} \times 100\% = 15.63\%
$$

$$
\% A = \frac{8.0}{32.0} \times 100\% = 25.00\%
$$

$$
\% IF = \frac{5.0}{32.0} \times 100\% = 15.63\%
$$

$$
\% EF = \frac{14.0}{32.0} \times 100\% = 43.75\%
$$

**Step 4: Analysis & Recommendation.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall):** Students often compute the total CoQ correctly but lose 2 marks for **NOT interpreting** the result. KTU awards 1 mark for analysis and 1 mark for the recommendation.

The **external failure cost is 43.75%** of total CoQ — a very unhealthy distribution. The 1-10-100 rule states that fixing a defect post-release is 100× more expensive than fixing it in requirements. **Recommendations:**
1. Invest in **more prevention** (raise P from 15.63% to at least 25%).
2. Introduce **early-life-cycle reviews** (Fagan inspections) to catch defects before testing.
3. Adopt a **defect-tracking tool** to identify root causes and remove them at the source.
4. Aim for an optimal CoQ where $(P + A) \approx (IF + EF)$, with the curve at its minimum.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1 Choice-2]*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks [Bloom L1 + L2: Remember & Understand]

**Explain McCall's Quality Model in detail. Group the 11 quality factors into their three categories with two examples of metrics for each category.**

#### Model Answer

**Introduction:** McCall's Quality Model (1977, McCall, Richards, Walters) is one of the earliest software quality models. It defines quality as a hierarchy of **11 factors** grouped into **3 categories**, each addressing a different stakeholder question.

**Category 1: Product Operation (Product-Run)**

These factors measure how well the software performs during operation.

| Factor | Meaning | Example Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Correctness** | Extent to which software fulfills its specified function | Defect density, MTTF |
| **Reliability** | Ability to perform as expected without failure | MTBF, Availability |
| **Efficiency** | Amount of resources used | Response time, throughput |
| **Integrity** | Security against unauthorized access | Number of security breaches, audit-pass rate |
| **Usability** | Ease of human use | Time to learn, error rate per user |

**Category 2: Product Revision (Product-Revision)**

These factors measure ease of making changes.

| Factor | Meaning | Example Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Maintainability** | Ease of locating and fixing defects | Mean time to repair, cyclomatic complexity |
| **Flexibility** | Ease of supporting new features | Effort to add a feature, % of modular code |
| **Testability** | Ease of testing the program | Test coverage %, % of testable modules |

**Category 3: Product Transition (Product-Transition)**

These factors measure adaptability to new environments.

| Factor | Meaning | Example Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Portability** | Effort to transfer to a new platform | Platform-dependency count, migration time |
| **Reusability** | Use in other applications | % of reusable modules, library-call ratio |
| **Interoperability** | Coupling with other systems | Interface failure count, integration-test pass rate |

**Conclusion:** McCall's model is the **foundation** for later standards like ISO 9126 and ISO 25010, but it has limitations: factors overlap, and weights are subjective. Despite this, it remains essential for KTU-level study.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks [Bloom L4: Analyze]

**A server system is monitored for 500 hours. During this period it failed 5 times, with a total repair time of 10 hours. Compute MTTF, MTTR, MTBF, and Availability. Comment on the system's reliability.**

#### Model Answer

**Given Data:**
- Total operating time $T_{op} = 500$ hours
- Total repair (down) time $T_{repair} = 10$ hours
- Number of failures $N = 5$

**Step 1: Compute MTTF.**

$$
MTTF = \frac{T_{op}}{N} = \frac{500}{5}
$$

$$
\boxed{MTTF = 100 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Step 2: Compute MTTR.**

$$
MTTR = \frac{T_{repair}}{N} = \frac{10}{5}
$$

$$
\boxed{MTTR = 2 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute MTBF.**

$$
MTBF = MTTF + MTTR = 100 + 2
$$

$$
\boxed{MTBF = 102 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Availability.**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\% = \frac{102}{104} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 98.08\%}
$$

**Step 5: Comment on Reliability.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget the **commentary** part and lose 2 marks. The 7-mark question has 5 marks for computation and 2 marks for the qualitative comment.

- The system's **availability of 98.08%** is acceptable for general-purpose business applications but **NOT** acceptable for **mission-critical** systems (avionics, medical, banking) which demand *five nines* (99.999%) availability.
- The system fails roughly **once every 100 hours** — at 24×7 operation, that is a failure every ~4 days, which is too frequent.
- **Recommendations:** (i) introduce redundancy (failover servers), (ii) reduce MTTR through automation of recovery, (iii) conduct root-cause analysis on the 5 failures, (iv) target MTTR $\le$ 0.5 hours to push availability above 99.5%.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Common Pitfalls — Consolidated Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Top 7 Reasons Students Lose Marks in KTU SQA Papers (Module 1)**
>
> 1. **Confusing SQA with testing** — Testing is a *sub-activity* of SQA, not its synonym. (Lose 2 marks easily.)
> 2. **Forgetting the named authority** — "Quality is conformance" without naming Crosby is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **No "comment" or "recommendation"** — Numerical answers in CoQ, MTBF, and DD require **interpretation**; pure computation gets 5/7 only.
> 4. **Mixing up Quality and Grade** — Grade is a *category*; Quality is *conformance*. They are not the same.
> 5. **Wrong unit on Defect Density** — Always express as **defects per KLOC** or **defects per FP**, never just "defects".
> 6. **Confusing Verification and Validation** — V&V carry distinct meanings (build *right* vs. build *right product*). (Lose 2 marks.)
> 7. **Writing only definitions in Part B** — 7-mark questions demand **explanation + example + table**. Pure prose loses marks.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> Use this section as your **last-page glance** before walking into the exam hall.

### 🔑 Definitions You Must Memorize Verbatim
- ✅ **Crosby:** Quality = Conformance to requirements.
- ✅ **Juran:** Quality = Fitness for use.
- ✅ **Deming:** Quality = Predictable degree of uniformity at low cost, suited to market.
- ✅ **IEEE 610:** Degree to which a system meets specified requirements and user needs.
- ✅ **SQA:** Systematic, planned activities ensuring software processes and products conform to requirements, standards, and procedures.
- ✅ **SQC:** Detective, product-oriented activity to find defects in finished software.

### 🔢 Must-Know Formulas
- ✅ $CoQ = P + A + IF + EF$
- ✅ $MTTF = \frac{\text{Operating time}}{\text{Failures}}$
- ✅ $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$
- ✅ $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$
- ✅ $DD = \frac{\text{Defects}}{KLOC}$ (or per FP)
- ✅ $V(G) = e - n + 2p$

### 🧠 Must-Know Models & Lists
- ✅ **McCall's 11 factors** in 3 groups (5 + 3 + 3).
- ✅ **ISO 25010** — 8 quality characteristics: Functional Suitability, Performance Efficiency, Compatibility, Usability, Reliability, Security, Maintainability, Portability.
- ✅ **CoQ components** — Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure.
- ✅ **SDLC phases** and SQA touchpoint in each.
- ✅ **IEEE 730** — SQA Plan standard.
- ✅ **IEEE 1227** — SQA standards reference.
- ✅ **CMMI** — Capability Maturity Model Integration (5 levels).
- ✅ **ISO 9001** — Quality management system standard.

### ⚖️ Must-Know Comparisons
- ✅ SQA vs SQC (process vs product, prevention vs detection).
- ✅ Verification vs Validation (right vs right product, static vs dynamic).
- ✅ Quality vs Grade vs Reliability.
- ✅ Prevention cost vs Failure cost (1-10-100 rule).

### 🎯 Examiner-Favorite Question Types
- ✅ List 5 definitions of quality with authorities (3 marks).
- ✅ McCall's factors with grouping (7 marks).
- ✅ CoQ calculation with percentage analysis (7 marks).
- ✅ MTBF/Availability calculation with reliability commentary (7 marks).
- ✅ Compare SQA vs SQC / V&V / Quality vs Grade (3 or 7 marks).

### 🚫 Never Forget
- ✅ Mention **the authority** when giving a quality definition.
- ✅ Always **interpret** numerical results (e.g., "EF is 47% — too high, suggest prevention").
- ✅ Use correct **units** (KLOC, %, hours, ₹ Lakhs).
- ✅ Show **step-by-step working** for every numerical answer.
- ✅ Add a **diagram** in 7-markers whenever possible (KTU gives 1–2 marks extra for clean structure).

---

> 🏁 **End of Module 1 Notes — SQA: Definitions and Concepts**
> *Prepared in alignment with the KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and OBE guidelines.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: McCall's Classic Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** McCall's Classic Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **McCall's Classic Software Quality Model** is a hierarchical, factor-criteria-metric framework proposed by **Jim McCall, Paul Richards, and Gene Walters in 1977** to define and measure software quality from the user's perspective.

It decomposes the abstract notion of "high-quality software" into **11 quality factors**, which are further refined into **23 quality criteria**, and finally quantified through **measurable software metrics**. The model is also called the **"Quality Triangle"** or **"Triangle of Quality"** because it organizes quality attributes around three user viewpoints.

The hierarchical relationship is formally expressed as:

$$\text{Software Quality} \longrightarrow \{Q_i \mid i = 1, 2, \ldots, 11\} \longrightarrow \{C_j \mid j = 1, 2, \ldots, 23\} \longrightarrow \{M_k \mid k = 1, 2, \ldots\}$$

Where $Q_i$ denotes a Quality Factor, $C_j$ denotes a Quality Criterion, and $M_k$ denotes a Metric.

## 1.2 The Three User Perspectives (Product Viewpoints)

McCall's model defines software quality through three operational lenses:

> [!NOTE]
> **Product Operation** — How well does the software *run* during execution?
>
> **Product Revision** — How easy is it to *change* the software over its lifecycle?
>
> **Product Transition** — How portable and adaptable is the software to *new environments*?

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car Showroom"

Imagine you are buying a car. You judge it using three broad categories:

| What You Want from the Car | McCall's Perspective | Everyday Translation |
|---|---|---|
| Smooth driving, fuel efficiency, comfort | **Product Operation** | "How well does it *drive* today?" |
| Easy servicing, repairable parts | **Product Revision** | "How easily can it be *fixed* later?" |
| Works on any road, resale value | **Product Transition** | "How well does it *adapt* elsewhere?" |

**In the same way**, McCall's model asks three questions of any software system:
1. Does it *work correctly* today? (Operation)
2. Can it be *maintained* tomorrow? (Revision)
3. Can it be *moved* to a new platform next year? (Transition)

## 1.4 Core Definitions of the 11 Quality Factors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Correctness** — The degree to which a program satisfies its specifications and fulfills the user's mission objectives.
>
> **2. Reliability** — The capability of a program to perform its required functions under stated conditions for a specified period of time.
>
> **3. Usability** — The effort required to learn, operate, prepare input, and interpret output of a program.
>
> **4. Integrity** — The degree to which unauthorized access to or modification of the software can be controlled.
>
> **5. Efficiency** — The amount of computing resources (time, memory) required to perform a program's functions.
>
> **6. Maintainability** — The ease with which a program can be corrected, adapted, or enhanced.
>
> **7. Flexibility** — The ease with which a program can be modified to adapt to varying requirements or environments.
>
> **8. Testability** — The ease with which a program can be tested to validate that it meets its requirements.
>
> **9. Portability** — The capability of a program to be transferred from one hardware/software environment to another.
>
> **10. Reusability** — The degree to which a program (or its parts) can be reused in other applications.
>
> **11. Interoperability** — The effort required to couple one system with another system.

These **11 factors** are the central evaluative axes that KTU examiners focus on. Memorize the mapping of each factor to its perspective category.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Decomposition Logic

McCall's model works on a **three-tier pyramid** (top-down abstraction):

**Tier 1 — Quality Factors (QF):** High-level user-visible attributes. These answer the question *"What does the user want?"* They are abstract and often non-measurable directly.

**Tier 2 — Quality Criteria (QC):** Mid-level attributes that act as **bridges** between factors and metrics. A criterion (e.g., "Consistency") contributes to multiple factors (e.g., Maintainability + Reliability).

**Tier 3 — Metrics (M):** Concrete, quantifiable measurements at the source-code or process level. Examples include *lines of code*, *cyclomatic complexity*, *mean time to failure (MTTF)*.

The bridge relationship is formally:

$$Q_i = \sum_{j} w_{ij} \cdot C_j$$

where $w_{ij}$ is the weight of criterion $C_j$ contributing to factor $Q_i$, and the sum is over all criteria that influence $Q_i$.

## 2.2 Why a Three-Tier Model?

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not measure quality directly?**
> Quality is an *abstract concept*. Users say "the software feels buggy" but cannot assign a number. McCall solved this by:
>
> 1. Breaking "quality" into **factors** (what we *want*).
> 2. Breaking factors into **criteria** (what we *can describe*).
> 3. Breaking criteria into **metrics** (what we *can count*).
>
> This creates a **traceable chain of measurement** from subjective experience to objective number.

## 2.3 The 23 Quality Criteria (Mapping Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is **high-yield** for KTU exams. The criteria act as *connective tissue* between factors and metrics.

| Criterion (Mapped To Factors) | Focus Area |
|---|---|
| Auditability (Maintainability, Flexibility) | Traceability of changes and test history |
| Accuracy (Reliability) | Precision of computations and outputs |
| Communication Commonality (Portability, Interoperability) | Use of standard interfaces and protocols |
| Completeness (Correctness, Reliability) | Full implementation of required functions |
| Conciseness (Maintainability) | Minimal, non-redundant code |
| Consistency (Maintainability, Flexibility) | Uniformity of notation, design, documentation |
| Data Commonality (Portability, Reusability) | Use of standard data formats |
| Error Tolerance (Reliability, Usability) | Ability to handle invalid input gracefully |
| Execution Efficiency (Efficiency) | Runtime performance and resource use |
| Expandability (Flexibility, Maintainability) | Ease of architectural growth |
| Generality (Flexibility, Reusability) | Breadth of applicability of components |
| Hardware Independence (Portability) | Decoupling from specific hardware |
| Instrumentation (Testability, Maintainability) | Built-in monitoring and diagnostic hooks |
| Modularity (Maintainability, Flexibility, Reusability) | Independence and cohesion of components |
| Operability (Usability, Efficiency) | Ease of daily operation |
| Security (Integrity) | Protection against unauthorized access |
| Self-Documentation (Maintainability, Usability) | Code clarity and inline documentation |
| Simplicity (Maintainability, Usability) | Lack of complexity |
| Software System Independence (Portability) | Loose coupling to OS / libraries |
| Traceability (Testability, Maintainability) | Forward and reverse links from requirements to code |
| Training (Usability) | Ease of learning the system |
| Understandability (Maintainability, Flexibility, Reusability) | Clarity of code for future developers |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Comprehensive)

> [!TIP]
> McCall's model itself is **not heavily formula-based**, but KTU exams expect you to apply metric-style formulas to compute quality scores. Use the table below as a single revision reference.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Overall Quality Score | $Q = \sum_{i=1}^{11} w_i \cdot Q_i$ | Weighted sum of all quality factors |
| Normalized Factor Score | $Q_i^{norm} = \dfrac{Q_i^{actual}}{Q_i^{ideal}}$ | Ratio of measured to ideal factor value |
| Weighted Criterion Sum | $Q_i = \sum_{j} w_{ij} \cdot C_j$ | Factor as weighted sum of criteria |
| MTTF (Reliability Metric) | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i}{N}$ | Mean time between failures |
| Defect Density (Correctness) | $\text{DD} = \dfrac{\text{Defects found}}{\text{KLOC}}$ | Defects per kilo lines of code |
| Usability Score (Composite) | $U = \alpha \cdot \text{Learn} + \beta \cdot \text{Operate} + \gamma \cdot \text{Interpret}$ | Weighted usability components |
| Portability Index | $P = \dfrac{N_{\text{independent modules}}}{N_{\text{total modules}}}$ | Ratio of platform-agnostic modules |

**Important Note on Notation:** In all formulas above, weights must satisfy the normalization condition:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> McCall's model is the **foundation of nearly every modern quality framework**, including **ISO/IEC 9126** and **ISO/IEC 25010**. Industry uses it to:
>
> - Build **defect prediction dashboards** (Reliability + Correctness factors).
> - Define **release readiness criteria** in DevOps pipelines.
> - Conduct **software product certification** audits.
> - Score vendor proposals in **outsourced development contracts**.

It is still relevant today because the 11 factors are **vendor-agnostic** — they apply equally to mobile apps, embedded firmware, and enterprise SaaS platforms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Factor → Criterion → Metric Relationship

We will now derive the exact mathematical relationship McCall used to translate abstract quality into measurable metrics.

### Step 1 — Quality as a Composite Vector

Let $\mathbf{Q}$ denote the overall quality vector of a software product across the 11 factors:

$$\mathbf{Q} = \begin{bmatrix} Q_1 \\ Q_2 \\ \vdots \\ Q_{11} \end{bmatrix}$$

Each $Q_i$ is a scalar in the range $[0, 1]$, where $0$ indicates *no satisfaction* and $1$ indicates *ideal satisfaction*.

### Step 2 — Decomposition Into Criteria

Each factor $Q_i$ is decomposed into its contributing criteria $C_j$. Using the weighted sum decomposition:

$$Q_i = \sum_{j \in J_i} w_{ij} \cdot C_j$$

Here $J_i$ is the index set of criteria influencing factor $Q_i$, and $w_{ij}$ is the relative weight.

### Step 3 — Decomposition Into Metrics

Each criterion $C_j$ is measured through one or more metrics $M_k$:

$$C_j = \sum_{k \in K_j} w_{jk} \cdot f(M_k)$$

where $f(M_k)$ is a normalization function that converts metric $M_k$ into a $[0,1]$ scale, and $K_j$ is the index set of metrics that feed into criterion $C_j$.

### Step 4 — Chained Substitution

Substituting Step 3 into Step 2:

$$Q_i = \sum_{j \in J_i} w_{ij} \left( \sum_{k \in K_j} w_{jk} \cdot f(M_k) \right)$$

Exchanging the order of summation gives a single expression:

$$Q_i = \sum_{j \in J_i} \sum_{k \in K_j} w_{ij} \cdot w_{jk} \cdot f(M_k)$$

### Step 5 — Final Global Quality Index

Aggregating all factors into a global quality index with weights $w_i$:

$$Q = \sum_{i=1}^{11} w_i \cdot Q_i = \sum_{i=1}^{11} w_i \sum_{j \in J_i} \sum_{k \in K_j} w_{ij} \cdot w_{jk} \cdot f(M_k)$$

This is the **canonical McCall Quality Score** — a single number that a project manager can use to compare candidate products.

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A team measures only the **Reliability** factor ($i = 4$, weight $w_4 = 0.15$). Reliability is decomposed into two criteria: **Completeness** ($C_1$, weight $w_{4,1} = 0.6$) and **Accuracy** ($C_2$, weight $w_{4,2} = 0.4$).
>
> - Completeness is measured by *defect density* $M_1$: $M_1 = 0.5$ defects/KLOC → $f(M_1) = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$
> - Accuracy is measured by *output precision* $M_2$: $M_2 = 0.8$ → $f(M_2) = 0.8$

**Step A — Compute each criterion:**

$$C_1 = 1.0 \cdot f(M_1) = 1.0 \cdot 0.5 = 0.5$$

$$C_2 = 1.0 \cdot f(M_2) = 1.0 \cdot 0.8 = 0.8$$

**Step B — Compute the factor:**

$$Q_4 = w_{4,1} \cdot C_1 + w_{4,2} \cdot C_2$$

$$Q_4 = (0.6)(0.5) + (0.4)(0.8)$$

$$Q_4 = 0.30 + 0.32 = 0.62$$

**Step C — Contribution to global quality:**

$$Q_{\text{rel-contribution}} = w_4 \cdot Q_4 = (0.15)(0.62) = 0.093$$

The reliability factor contributes **0.093** (or 9.3%) to the overall quality index. This kind of calculation is **directly assessable** as a 14-mark KTU problem.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Factor to Criteria Cross-Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix below maps each of the **11 Quality Factors** to the **Quality Criteria** that feed them. This is **examination gold** — KTU regularly asks students to fill or interpret such matrices.

| Quality Factor | Quality Criteria (Selected from the 23) |
|---|---|
| Correctness | Completeness, Traceability, Accuracy |
| Reliability | Error Tolerance, Completeness, Accuracy, Consistency |
| Usability | Operability, Training, Simplicity, Self-Documentation |
| Integrity | Security, Auditability |
| Efficiency | Execution Efficiency, Modularity |
| Maintainability | Modularity, Simplicity, Conciseness, Consistency, Self-Documentation, Expandability, Instrumentation |
| Flexibility | Modularity, Generality, Expandability, Consistency, Understandability |
| Testability | Instrumentation, Modularity, Traceability, Simplicity |
| Portability | Software System Independence, Hardware Independence, Data Commonality, Modularity |
| Reusability | Generality, Modularity, Software System Independence, Data Commonality, Understandability |
| Interoperability | Communication Commonality, Data Commonality, Software System Independence |

### Self-Derivation Exercise (For Exam Practice)

Suppose a project prioritizes **Maintainability (weight = 0.20)** with sub-criteria:
- Modularity ($w = 0.35$)
- Simplicity ($w = 0.25$)
- Instrumentation ($w = 0.40$)

If the normalized metric scores for these criteria are $0.9$, $0.7$, and $0.85$ respectively, derive the maintainability factor contribution.

**Solution:**

$$Q_{\text{maintain}} = 0.20 \cdot \big( (0.35)(0.9) + (0.25)(0.7) + (0.40)(0.85) \big)$$

$$Q_{\text{maintain}} = 0.20 \cdot (0.315 + 0.175 + 0.340)$$

$$Q_{\text{maintain}} = 0.20 \cdot 0.830 = 0.166$$

This is a **17-mark equivalent** KTU calculation pattern.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 McCall's Quality Triangle — Top-Level View

The following diagram depicts McCall's three-perspective organization of the 11 quality factors. This is the diagram most often drawn in KTU board exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SQ[Software Quality]
    SQ --> OP[Product Operation]
    SQ --> RV[Product Revision]
    SQ --> TR[Product Transition]
    OP --> F1[Correctness]
    OP --> F2[Reliability]
    OP --> F3[Usability]
    OP --> F4[Integrity]
    OP --> F5[Efficiency]
    RV --> F6[Maintainability]
    RV --> F7[Flexibility]
    RV --> F8[Testability]
    TR --> F9[Portability]
    TR --> F10[Reusability]
    TR --> F11[Interoperability]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Pyramid — Factors to Metrics

This block diagram illustrates the **three-tier hierarchical decomposition** that is the conceptual backbone of McCall's framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TIER1[Tier 1 - Quality Factors 11 user-visible attributes]
    TIER2[Tier 2 - Quality Criteria 23 mid-level descriptors]
    TIER3[Tier 3 - Software Metrics Quantified measurements]
    TIER1 --> TIER2
    TIER2 --> TIER3
    TIER1 -. User View .-> USER[End User]
    TIER3 -. Raw Data .-> DEV[Developer]
```

## 4.3 Cross-Mapping — Factors to Criteria (Partial Architectural View)

The diagram below shows how a single quality factor (e.g., Maintainability) decomposes into multiple criteria, and how those criteria are themselves measurable.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    QM[Maintainability]
    QM --> C1[Modularity]
    QM --> C2[Simplicity]
    QM --> C3[Conciseness]
    QM --> C4[Consistency]
    QM --> C5[Self-Documentation]
    QM --> C6[Instrumentation]
    C1 --> M1[Module Cohesion Metric]
    C1 --> M2[Coupling Metric]
    C2 --> M3[Cyclomatic Complexity]
    C3 --> M4[Lines of Code LOC]
    C4 --> M5[Naming Convention Index]
    C5 --> M6[Comment Density]
    C6 --> M7[Logging Hook Count]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow — Applying McCall in a Project

This block-level functional flow maps the steps a project team follows when applying McCall's model in real engineering practice.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Step 1 Identify Stakeholder Priorities]
    S2[Step 2 Assign Weights to 11 Factors]
    S3[Step 3 Map Factors to Criteria]
    S4[Step 4 Select Metrics for Criteria]
    S5[Step 5 Collect Metric Data]
    S6[Step 6 Normalize and Score]
    S7[Step 7 Compute Factor Scores]
    S8[Step 8 Compute Global Q Score]
    S9[Step 9 Compare Against Threshold]
    S10[Step 10 Release Decision]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10
    S10 -->|Pass| REL[Release Approved]
    S10 -->|Fail| FB[Feedback Loop to Development]
    FB --> S1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Board examiners often award 2–3 marks for a clean diagram. The above architectures cover both the *theoretical structure* (Sections 4.1, 4.2, 4.3) and the *applied process* (Section 4.4) of McCall's model.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> Define **Software Quality** as per McCall's classic model. List the **11 quality factors** organized under the three product perspectives.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Software quality is the totality of features and characteristics of a software product that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs (McCall, 1977). The 11 factors grouped under the three perspectives are:

| Perspective | Quality Factors |
|---|---|
| **Product Operation** | Correctness, Reliability, Usability, Integrity, Efficiency |
| **Product Revision** | Maintainability, Flexibility, Testability |
| **Product Transition** | Portability, Reusability, Interoperability |

*(2 marks for the list, 1 mark for the definition.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> Differentiate between **Maintainability**, **Flexibility**, and **Testability** — the three factors under the Product Revision perspective.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Maintainability:** Effort required to **locate and fix** a fault in an operational program, or to **adapt** it to a new environment. Focuses on *repair and correction*.
- **Flexibility:** Effort required to **modify** an operational program to meet changing requirements. Focuses on *adaptive change* for new functionality.
- **Testability:** Effort required to **verify** (test) a program to ensure it performs its intended functions. Focuses on *validation and verification*.

*(1 mark each for definition + distinguishing clause.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### OR

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain **McCall's Quality Model** in detail, with a labeled diagram of the quality triangle and the factor–criteria–metric hierarchy. Discuss how the 23 quality criteria act as bridges between the 11 factors and the software metrics. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A software product is evaluated on three quality factors with the following normalized scores and weights: Reliability (score = 0.80, weight = 0.40), Maintainability (score = 0.65, weight = 0.35), and Usability (score = 0.90, weight = 0.25). Compute the **overall McCall Quality Index** and interpret whether the product meets the release threshold of $Q \geq 0.70$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**
McCall's Quality Model, proposed by Jim McCall in 1977, is a hierarchical framework that decomposes software quality into 11 quality factors (user viewpoint), 23 quality criteria (developer viewpoint), and software metrics (measurable quantities).

**Step 2 — Three Perspectives (2 marks):**
The model organizes 11 factors under three user perspectives:

- **Product Operation** — how the software runs (Correctness, Reliability, Usability, Integrity, Efficiency).
- **Product Revision** — how the software is changed (Maintainability, Flexibility, Testability).
- **Product Transition** — how the software is ported to new environments (Portability, Reusability, Interoperability).

**Step 3 — Factor–Criteria–Metric Hierarchy (3 marks):**
Factors are abstract user concerns. Criteria are mid-level attributes that act as *bridges* between factors and metrics. Metrics are concrete, code-level measurements. Example: Factor = *Reliability* is decomposed into criteria such as *Error Tolerance*, *Completeness*, and *Accuracy*, which in turn are measured through metrics like *MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)* and *defect density*.

**Step 4 — Diagram (1 mark):**
Include the quality triangle showing the three perspectives and the hierarchy pyramid showing Factors → Criteria → Metrics (refer to SECTION 4 of these notes).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State the formula (1 mark):**

$$Q = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot Q_i$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values (2 marks):**

$$Q = (0.40 \times 0.80) + (0.35 \times 0.65) + (0.25 \times 0.90)$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term (2 marks):**

$$Q = 0.320 + 0.2275 + 0.225$$

$$Q = 0.7725$$

**Step 4 — Compare with threshold and interpret (2 marks):**

Since $Q = 0.7725 \geq 0.70$, the product **meets the release threshold**. The dominant contributor is Reliability (contribution = 0.320), while Maintainability is the weakest factor (contribution = 0.2275). The team should prioritize maintainability improvements before the next release.

---

### OR

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **Product Revision perspective** of McCall's quality model. List the relevant factors and the criteria that influence them. Describe at least **four metrics** used to measure each criterion. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A software module is evaluated for **Flexibility**. The team uses three criteria with weights and metric-based scores given below. Compute the flexibility factor score.
>
> | Criterion | Weight | Normalized Score |
> |---|---|---|
> | Modularity | 0.45 | 0.85 |
> | Expandability | 0.30 | 0.70 |
> | Generality | 0.25 | 0.90 |
>
> **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define Product Revision (1 mark):**
The Product Revision perspective describes the qualities that determine how *easily* a software product can be **modified** to correct defects, meet new requirements, or adapt to a changed environment.

**Step 2 — List the three factors (1 mark):**
- Maintainability
- Flexibility
- Testability

**Step 3 — Describe the factors and their criteria (3 marks):**

- **Maintainability** — Criteria: Modularity, Simplicity, Conciseness, Consistency, Self-Documentation, Instrumentation, Expandability.
- **Flexibility** — Criteria: Modularity, Generality, Expandability, Consistency, Understandability.
- **Testability** — Criteria: Instrumentation, Modularity, Traceability, Simplicity.

**Step 4 — Discuss at least four metrics (2 marks):**
For example, for Maintainability:
- *Cyclomatic Complexity* — measures code complexity.
- *Lines of Code (LOC)* — measures size and conciseness.
- *Comment Density* — measures self-documentation.
- *Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)* — measures maintainability efficiency.

For Testability:
- *Statement Coverage %, Branch Coverage %* — measure test completeness.
- *Defect Detection Rate* — measures test effectiveness.
- *Module Coupling Coefficient* — measures ease of isolation for testing.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State the relevant formula (1 mark):**

$$Q_{\text{flexibility}} = \sum_{j=1}^{3} w_j \cdot C_j$$

**Step 2 — Identify the data (1 mark):**
Three criteria: Modularity ($w=0.45$, $C=0.85$), Expandability ($w=0.30$, $C=0.70$), Generality ($w=0.25$, $C=0.90$).

**Step 3 — Substitute into the formula (2 marks):**

$$Q_{\text{flexibility}} = (0.45 \times 0.85) + (0.30 \times 0.70) + (0.25 \times 0.90)$$

**Step 4 — Compute each term (2 marks):**

$$Q_{\text{flexibility}} = 0.3825 + 0.2100 + 0.2250$$

$$Q_{\text{flexibility}} = 0.8175$$

**Step 5 — Interpret (1 mark):**
The flexibility factor score is **0.8175** (or 81.75%), indicating *strong* adaptability. Modularity is the strongest contributor (0.3825), and Expandability is the weakest contributor (0.2100).

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in McCall's Model Questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up the three perspectives.** Students often place *Portability* under Product Operation or *Efficiency* under Product Transition. Memorize:
>    - Operation = how it *runs*.
>    - Revision = how it *changes*.
>    - Transition = how it *moves*.
> 2. **Forgetting the 23-criteria bridge layer.** A 7-mark question on McCall's model *always* expects at least the mention of quality criteria as a bridge between factors and metrics. Omitting this costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Not drawing the diagram.** Board examiners explicitly award 1–2 marks for a *labeled* quality triangle or hierarchy pyramid. Even a hand-drawn schematic is acceptable.
> 4. **Confusing correctness and reliability.** Correctness = "does it match specifications?" Reliability = "does it work consistently over time?" These are different.
> 5. **Calculation errors in weighted sums.** Always write the formula *before* substituting numbers, then compute each term on a separate line. Skipping steps costs partial marks.
> 6. **Forgetting normalization constraints.** Weights must sum to 1. If a question gives weights that do not sum to 1, *normalizing* them is often the first sub-step.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — McCall's Classic Model**
>
> - McCall's model was proposed in **1977** by **McCall, Richards, and Walters**.
> - It contains **11 Quality Factors**, **23 Quality Criteria**, and **Metrics** (three-tier hierarchy).
> - The 11 factors are organized under **three perspectives**:
>   - *Product Operation*: Correctness, Reliability, Usability, Integrity, Efficiency.
>   - *Product Revision*: Maintainability, Flexibility, Testability.
>   - *Product Transition*: Portability, Reusability, Interoperability.
> - **Quality Criteria act as bridges** between abstract factors and concrete metrics.
> - The **global quality index** is a weighted sum of all factor scores: $Q = \sum w_i \cdot Q_i$.
> - Each factor is a weighted sum of its contributing criteria: $Q_i = \sum w_{ij} \cdot C_j$.
> - Weights must satisfy $\sum w_i = 1$.
> - The model is the **foundation of ISO/IEC 9126 and ISO/IEC 25010**.
> - **Correctness** is about *specifications*; **Reliability** is about *consistency over time*.
> - **Maintainability** is about *fixing*; **Flexibility** is about *adapting*; **Testability** is about *verifying*.
> - Always *draw a diagram* in 14-mark answers — examiners award 1–2 marks for visual presentation.
> - Remember the worked numerical pattern: **substitute → compute each term → sum → interpret**.
> - The model is *user-centric*, not developer-centric — frame answers from the user's viewpoint.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ISO/IEC 25010 Model and Other Alternative Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** ISO/IEC 25010 Model and Other Alternative Models # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is the ISO/IEC 25010 Model? The **ISO/IEC 25010** is an international standard titled *"Systems and software engineering — Systems and software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE) — System and software quality models."* Published in **2011**, it defines a **quality model** that divides software product quality into **eight top-level characteristics**, each decomposed into several sub-characteristics. It superseded the older ISO/IEC 9126 standard and is currently the most widely cited framework for classifying software quality attributes in academic literature and industry practice. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students are required to (a) list and define all 8 quality characteristics of ISO/IEC 25010, (b) map each sub-characteristic, and (c) compare the model with earlier frameworks such as McCall, Boehm, and ISO/IEC 9126. Expect a direct 7-mark or 14-mark question on this topic in the University Exam. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car Showroom" Imagine you walk into a **car showroom** to evaluate a vehicle. You don't judge the car with a single word like "good." Instead, you mentally tick off multiple dimensions: - Does it **do what a car is supposed to do**? (Functional Suitability) - Does the engine deliver **speed without burning excessive fuel**? (Performance Efficiency) - Can it run on **diesel as well as CNG** without breaking? (Compatibility) - Is the dashboard **easy for a first-time driver**? (Usability) - How often does it **break down, and can the garage fix it fast**? (Reliability) - Will the **anti-theft system and airbags** protect the family? (Security) - Is the engine **modular enough for easy servicing**? (Maintainability) - Can the same model be **shipped and serviced abroad**? (Portability) The ISO/IEC 25010 model is exactly that — a structured showroom checklist applied to **software products**, ensuring no dimension of "quality" is overlooked. ## 1.3 The 8 Quality Characteristics at a Glance > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme wording):** *"A quality characteristic is a category of software quality attributes that bears on a software product's ability to satisfy stated and implied needs when used under specified conditions."* | # | Characteristic | One-line meaning | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Functional Suitability** | Does the software do what it is supposed to do? | | 2 | **Performance Efficiency** | How well does it perform relative to resources used? | | 3 | **Compatibility** | Can it co-exist and interoperate with other systems? | | 4 | **Usability** | How easy is it for users to learn and operate? | | 5 | **Reliability** | Will it perform without failure under stated conditions? | | 6 | **Security** | Can it protect data, privacy, and access rights? | | 7 | **Maintainability** | How easily can it be modified, tested, or fixed? | | 8 | **Portability** | Can it be transferred from one environment to another? | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Hierarchical Decomposition — The "Why" and the "How" The ISO/IEC 25010 model is built on a **three-level hierarchy**: 1. **Level 1 — Quality Characteristic** (the broad category, e.g., Reliability) 2. **Level 2 — Sub-characteristic** (a measurable property, e.g., Maturity) 3. **Level 3 — Quality Measure** (a numerical metric, e.g., Mean Time Between Failures = **MTBF**) ### Why a hierarchy? - A single word like "good" is **too vague** to test or measure. - Sub-characteristics are **observable and testable**, which is what software testers, QA teams, and certification bodies actually measure. - The hierarchy allows **partial compliance** — a system may be highly reliable but poorly portable — which is the reality of real-world engineering trade-offs. ### How does it work in practice? - During **requirements engineering**, a QA analyst maps each user requirement to one or more sub-characteristics. - During **test design**, each sub-characteristic is converted into a measurable quality metric with a target value. - During **evaluation**, the actual value is compared to the target, producing a quality score. ## 2.2 Exhaustive Sub-Characteristic Map (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **The following table is the single most-asked framework in KTU SQA papers. Memorise it verbatim.** | Characteristic | Sub-characteristic | One-line meaning | |---|---|---| | **Functional Suitability** | Functional Completeness | Covers all specified tasks and user objectives | | | Functional Correctness | Provides correct results with required degree of precision | | | Functional Appropriateness | Provides an appropriate set of functions for specified tasks | | **Performance Efficiency** | Time Behaviour | Meets response, processing, and throughput times | | | Resource Utilization | Uses appropriate amounts and types of resources | | | Capacity | Limits parameters (e.g., users, transactions, data) | | **Compatibility** | Co-existence | Performs efficiently while sharing environment with other products | | | Interoperability | Exchanges information with other systems and use that information | | **Usability** | Appropriateness Recognizability | Users recognize the product's suitability for their needs | | | Learnability | Easy to learn how to use | | | Operability | Easy to operate and control | | | User Error Protection | Protects users from making errors | | | User Interface Aesthetics | Provides a pleasant and satisfying user interface | | | Accessibility | Can be used by people with the widest range of capabilities | | **Reliability** | Maturity | Meets reliability needs during normal operation | | | Availability | Is operational and accessible when required | | | Fault Tolerance | Operates as intended despite hardware/software faults | | | Recoverability | Recovers data and re-establishes desired state after interruption | | **Security** | Confidentiality | Ensures data is accessible only to authorized users | | | Integrity | Prevents unauthorized modification of data or software | | | Non-repudiation | Actions or events can be proven to have taken place | | | Accountability | Actions of an entity can be traced uniquely to that entity | | | Authenticity | The identity of a subject or resource can be proved | | | Resistance | Provides protection against unauthorized access and modification | | **Maintainability** | Modularity | Composed of discrete components with low coupling | | | Reusability | Assets can be used in more than one system | | | Analyzability | Easy to diagnose deficiencies or identify parts to modify | | | Modifiability | Can be modified without introducing defects or degrading quality | | | Testability | Easy to test and criteria for testing can be established | | **Portability** | Adaptability | Can be adapted to different environments without new actions | | | Installability | Easy to install and/or uninstall in a specified environment | | | Replaceability | Can replace another software product in the same environment | ## 2.3 The Quality Model Context (Where the 8 Characteristics Fit) The ISO/IEC 25010 standard defines a **two-dimensional quality model**: 1. **Quality in Use Model** (5 characteristics: Effectiveness, Efficiency, Satisfaction, Freedom from Risk, Context Coverage) — evaluated during real usage. 2. **Product Quality Model** (the 8 characteristics above) — evaluated against the requirements specification. $$\text{Total Quality} = f(\text{Product Quality}, \text{Quality in Use})$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU 2024, focus primarily on the **Product Quality Model** (the 8 characteristics). A 1-mark question may ask: *"How many characteristics does ISO/IEC 25010 define?"* — The answer is **8**. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Model - **Test Planning:** Each sub-characteristic becomes a test category in a **test plan document**. - **Defect Classification:** Bugs are tagged by the sub-characteristic they violate. - **Vendor Evaluation:** RFPs (Request for Proposals) mandate ISO/IEC 25010 sub-characteristic scores. - **CMMI / TMMi Audits:** Process improvement frameworks align their goals with quality model characteristics. - **Tool Selection:** Tools like SonarQube, JMeter, Selenium map metrics to specific sub-characteristics. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Evolution from McCall to ISO/IEC 25010 > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus explicitly asks: *"Compare ISO/IEC 25010 with other alternative quality models."* The following derivation walks through the **chronological evolution** of software quality models, which is the analytical backbone of any 14-mark question on this topic. ## 3.1 Step-by-Step Evolution of Software Quality Models ### Step 1: McCall's Quality Model (1977) — The Origin Developed by **James A. McCall** at the US Air Force, this was the **first systematic software quality model**. It proposed **11 quality factors** arranged around three perspectives: **Product Operation, Product Revision, and Product Transition**. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{McCall Quality} &= \{\text{Correctness, Reliability, Efficiency, Integrity, Usability,} \\ &\quad \text{Maintainability, Testability, Flexibility, Portability, Reusability, Interoperability}\} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1a — Operational flow:** - Each **Factor** (high-level) is refined into multiple **Criteria** (mid-level). - Each **Criterion** is then mapped to one or more **Metrics** (measurable quantity). - This 3-tier hierarchy became the *template* that all later models followed. **Step 1b — Limitation:** McCall's factors were user-oriented and lacked fine-grained engineering rigour, especially for non-functional concerns like security. ### Step 2: Boehm's Quality Model (1978) — The Refinement **Barry W. Boehm** extended McCall's idea with a **hierarchical, tree-structured model** that included **quality attributes at the primitive level** (like portability, reliability) and **higher-level utility and maintainability** at the top. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Boehm Hierarchy} = \text{Overall Utility} &\rightarrow \{\text{As-is Utility, Maintainability, Portability}\} \\ &\rightarrow \text{Primitive Attributes} \rightarrow \text{Metrics} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2a — Innovation:** Introduced the *tree-based decomposition* that influenced every subsequent model. **Step 2b — Limitation:** Still lacked security as a first-class concern and did not scale to modern distributed systems. ### Step 3: ISO/IEC 9126 (1991, revised 2001) — The International Standard ISO consolidated McCall and Boehm into an international standard with **6 characteristics**: 1. Functionality 2. Reliability 3. Usability 4. Efficiency 5. Maintainability 6. Portability Each was further split into sub-characteristics (4–5 per characteristic), giving a **rigorous, internationally accepted taxonomy**. **Step 3a — Strength:** Provided a *common vocabulary* for software quality across countries and industries. **Step 3b — Weakness identified by practitioners:** Security was tucked under *Functionality*, Performance was conflated with *Efficiency*, and Compatibility was missing entirely. ### Step 4: ISO/IEC 25010 (2011) — The Modern Framework To address 9126's gaps, ISO introduced the **SQuaRE (Software Quality Requirements and Evaluation)** family of standards, with **25010** at its core. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{New additions} &= \text{Security (separated as its own characteristic)} \\ &\quad + \text{Compatibility (split into co-existence \& interoperability)} \\ &\quad - \text{No characteristics removed; the 6 became 8} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4a — Structural change:** Security is now a *first-class* characteristic, no longer a sub-property of Functionality. **Step 4b — Conceptual change:** A separate **Quality in Use Model** (5 characteristics) was added, modelling the user's experience rather than the product itself. **Step 4c — Mathematical implication:** The 8 characteristics are **orthogonal** (independent), meaning a software product can score high on one and low on another — the model explicitly acknowledges trade-offs. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Quality Score}(S) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} w_i \cdot q_i, \quad \text{where } w_i \text{ is the weight, } q_i \in [0,1] \end{aligned} $$ This weighted-sum formulation is the basis for **quantitative quality evaluation** in industry tools. ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield) | Feature | McCall (1977) | Boehm (1978) | ISO/IEC 9126 (1991) | **ISO/IEC 25010 (2011)** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Number of top-level characteristics | 11 factors | 3 high-level | 6 characteristics | **8 characteristics** | | Security as a separate characteristic | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ (under Functionality) | ✅ | | Compatibility modelled | Partial | Partial | ❌ | ✅ | | Quality-in-use model | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | Hierarchy depth | 3 levels | 3+ levels | 2 levels | **2–3 levels** | | Mathematical rigour | Medium | Medium | High | **Very High** | | Current industry adoption | Low | Low | Moderate | **Very High** | | Suitable for modern web/cloud systems | ❌ | ❌ | Moderate | ✅ | ## 3.3 Other Alternative Quality Models (Often Asked in 3-Mark Questions) ### 3.3.1 FURPS / FURPS+ Model (Hewlett-Packard, late 1980s) $$\text{FURPS} = \{\text{F}, \text{U}, \text{R}, \text{P}, \text{S}\}$$ - **F** — Functional requirements - **U** — Usability - **R** — Reliability - **P** — Performance - **S** — Supportability (plus a "+" for Design constraints, Implementation, Interface, Physical) **Why it matters:** Used heavily by **Rational Unified Process (RUP)** practitioners. Sometimes asked in KTU as a 3-mark direct question. ### 3.3.2 Dromey's Quality Model (1995) **Roger Dromey** proposed linking **product properties to quality attributes** through a **cause–effect chain**: each quality attribute (e.g., Maintainability) is achieved by ensuring certain **product properties** (e.g., modularity, coupling) hold. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Quality Attribute} \xleftarrow{\text{is influenced by}} \text{Product Property} \xleftarrow{\text{is influenced by}} \text{Implementation Choice} \end{aligned} $$ **Why it matters:** Bridges the gap between abstract quality attributes and concrete code-level metrics (e.g., cyclomatic complexity for Maintainability). ### 3.3.3 Per's Quality Model (1995) Similar to McCall, but with emphasis on **object-oriented systems**. Less commonly asked, but worth one line of awareness. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Figure 1: The ISO/IEC 25010 Quality Model — Hierarchical Architecture This Mermaid block-diagram maps the **8 top-level characteristics** to their **sub-characteristics**, mirroring the official ISO 25010 figure. ```mermaid graph TD rootQM["ISO IEC 25010 PRODUCT QUALITY MODEL"] rootQM --> fsChar["FUNCTIONAL SUITABILITY"] rootQM --> peChar["PERFORMANCE EFFICIENCY"] rootQM --> cpChar["COMPATIBILITY"] rootQM --> usChar["USABILITY"] rootQM --> rlChar["RELIABILITY"] rootQM --> scChar["SECURITY"] rootQM --> mtChar["MAINTAINABILITY"] rootQM --> ptChar["PORTABILITY"] fsChar --> fsA["Functional Completeness"] fsChar --> fsB["Functional Correctness"] fsChar --> fsC["Functional Appropriateness"] peChar --> peA["Time Behaviour"] peChar --> peB["Resource Utilization"] peChar --> peC["Capacity"] cpChar --> cpA["Co existence"] cpChar --> cpB["Interoperability"] usChar --> usA["Appropriateness Recognizability"] usChar --> usB["Learnability"] usChar --> usC["Operability"] usChar --> usD["User Error Protection"] usChar --> usE["User Interface Aesthetics"] usChar --> usF["Accessibility"] rlChar --> rlA["Maturity"] rlChar --> rlB["Availability"] rlChar --> rlC["Fault Tolerance"] rlChar --> rlD["Recoverability"] scChar --> scA["Confidentiality"] scChar --> scB["Integrity"] scChar --> scC["Non repudiation"] scChar --> scD["Accountability"] scChar --> scE["Authenticity"] scChar --> scF["Resistance"] mtChar --> mtA["Modularity"] mtChar --> mtB["Reusability"] mtChar --> mtC["Analyzability"] mtChar --> mtD["Modifiability"] mtChar --> mtE["Testability"] ptChar --> ptA["Adaptability"] ptChar --> ptB["Installability"] ptChar --> ptC["Replaceability"] style rootQM fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff style fsChar fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff style peChar fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff style cpChar fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff style usChar fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff style rlChar fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff style scChar fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff style mtChar fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff style ptChar fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Figure 2: Chronological Evolution of Software Quality Models This flow diagram illustrates the **historical derivation chain** that students are expected to draw in KTU exams when answering "Compare quality models" questions. ```mermaid graph LR mccall["MCCALL 1977
11 Quality Factors

Tree Structured

Product Property Based"] furps["FURPS 1980s
5 Letter Acronym
Used in RUP"] mccall --> boehm boehm --> iso9126 iso9126 --> iso25010 mccall --> dromey mccall --> furps furps --> iso25010 style mccall fill:#a9d18e style boehm fill:#a9d18e style iso9126 fill:#ffd966 style iso25010 fill:#c00000,color:#fff style dromey fill:#b4c7e7 style furps fill:#b4c7e7 ``` ## 4.3 Figure 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Quality Evaluation This block diagram shows how the **ISO/IEC 25010 model is used inside a real evaluation pipeline**, mapping each sub-characteristic to a measurable metric and a test tool. ```mermaid graph TD inputReq["REQUIREMENTS SPECIFICATION"] --> mapStep["MAPPING STEP
Requirement to Sub characteristic"] mapStep --> selMetric["METRIC SELECTION
