<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a Software Development Plan (SDP). List any four sections of an SDP as per IEEE 1058.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **Software Development Plan (SDP)** is a written document that defines the technical and managerial approach, activities, resources, schedule, and controls required to develop a software product, as standardized in **IEEE 1058**.
> 
> **Four sections of an SDP (IEEE 1058):**
> 1. **Project Overview** — purpose, scope, objectives, deliverables
> 2. **Project Organization** — team structure, roles, responsibilities
> 3. **Managerial Process Plans** — work plan, control plan, risk management
> 4. **Technical Process Plans** — process model, methods, tools, infrastructure
> 
> *(Each section carries approx. 0.5 marks; definition carries 1 mark.)*

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a Software Quality Assurance Plan (SQAP)? Mention its governing standard.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **Software Quality Assurance Plan (SQAP)** is a formal document that describes the **quality standards, procedures, tools, metrics, reviews, audits, and tests** to be applied throughout the software life cycle to ensure the final product conforms to specified requirements.
> 
> **Governing Standard:** **IEEE 730-2014** (and aligned with **ISO/IEC 25010** for quality models).
> 
> The SQAP is owned by the **SQA Manager** and is a *specialized subset* of the SDP.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** write generic SQA theory here. The question is **specifically** about *Development Plans* and *Quality Plans* — answer in that exact framework.
> - **Do NOT** confuse the **SQAP** with the **Test Plan**. The SQAP is broader; the Test Plan is one *section* of the SQAP.
> - Always **cite the IEEE standard number** (1058 / 730) in your answer — failing to do so costs 1–2 marks.
> - Use **diagrams or tables** in your answer. Students who use structured tables/matrices consistently score 2–3 marks higher.
> - In **comparative** questions, end with a **clear differentiating statement** — e.g., "While the SDP defines how to build the product, the SQAP ensures the product is built right."

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of a **Software Development Plan (SDP)** as per IEEE 1058. List and briefly describe any **seven** major sections. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **importance of Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** in the SDP. How does it help in project scheduling, cost estimation, and progress tracking? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Structure of SDP as per IEEE 1058 — 7 Sections (7 marks):**

The IEEE 1058 standard specifies the following key sections of a Software Development Plan:

1. **Project Overview (1 mark):** Defines the project's purpose, scope, objectives, deliverables, assumptions, and constraints. It establishes *what* the project will deliver and *what is out of scope*.

2. **Reference Documents (1 mark):** Lists all external inputs — the SRS, customer contracts, applicable standards (e.g., ISO 9001), and any related project documents. This is the contractual baseline.

3. **Definitions and Acronyms (0.5 marks):** A glossary of all technical terms used in the SDP to ensure common understanding across teams and stakeholders.

4. **Project Organization (1 mark):** Defines the organizational hierarchy — Project Manager, Team Leads, Developers, QA, Configuration Manager — and the **reporting relationships** among them.

5. **Managerial Process Plans (1 mark):** Includes the start-up plan, work plan (WBS), control plan, risk management plan, and project closeout plan. These are the *administrative* aspects of running the project.

6. **Technical Process Plans (1 mark):** Specifies the process model (Waterfall / Agile / Spiral), methods and tools, infrastructure (hardware, software, networks), and product acceptance criteria. These are the *engineering* aspects.

7. **Supporting Process Plans (0.5 marks):** Includes configuration management, verification and validation, documentation, review and audit, and problem resolution plans. These cross-cutting activities support the core development process.

**Supplementary note:** Additional components include **schedules, budgets, and resource allocations**, which are derived from the WBS.

---

**(b) Importance of WBS in SDP (7 marks):**

The **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** is a **hierarchical decomposition of the total work scope** of a project into smaller, manageable work packages. It is the *backbone* of the SDP's work plan.

**Importance of WBS:**

1. **Project Scheduling (2 marks):** Each work package in the WBS can be assigned a duration and dependencies. Using techniques like the **Critical Path Method (CPM)**, the project schedule is derived directly from the WBS. Formula:
   $$\text{Project Duration} = \text{Length of the Critical Path}$$
   Without a WBS, scheduling is guesswork.

2. **Cost Estimation (2 marks):** Each work package is assigned a cost based on labour, materials, and overhead. The total project cost is the sum of all work package costs:
   $$\text{Total Project Cost} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Cost of Work Package } i$$
   This enables **bottom-up estimation**, which is far more accurate than top-down guessing.

3. **Progress Tracking and Control (2 marks):** The WBS enables the **Earned Value Management (EVM)** technique:
   $$\text{Earned Value (EV)} = \text{Planned Value} \times \text{\% Complete}$$
   $$\text{Cost Performance Index (CPI)} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{Actual Cost (AC)}}$$
   A CPI > 1 indicates the project is **under budget**; CPI < 1 indicates **over budget**.

4. **Responsibility Assignment (1 mark):** Each WBS element is mapped to a specific team member using a **RACI matrix** (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed), ensuring **no work is unassigned**.

**Conclusion:** The WBS is the **single most important artefact** in the SDP because it bridges scope, schedule, cost, and quality into a unified, controllable structure.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **components of a Software Quality Assurance Plan (SQAP)** as per IEEE 730. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Software Development Plan (SDP)** and the **Software Quality Assurance Plan (SQAP)** in terms of purpose, ownership, scope, key activities, and relationship. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Components of SQAP as per IEEE 730 (7 marks):**

The IEEE 730-2014 standard specifies the following key sections of an SQAP:

**1. Purpose (0.5 marks):** Identifies the product, version of the SQAP, and the intended audience.

**2. Reference Documents (0.5 marks):** Lists the SRS, SDP, applicable standards (ISO/IEC 25010), and regulatory requirements.

**3. Management (1 mark):** Defines the SQA organization, the specific SQA tasks, and the responsibilities of each team member. Emphasizes **organizational independence** — the SQA group reports to senior management, not the development manager.

**4. Documentation (0.5 marks):** Lists all documents to be produced, reviewed, and controlled — test plans, test reports, review records, audit reports, defect logs.

**5. Standards, Practices, and Conventions (1 mark):** Specifies the design standards, coding standards (e.g., MISRA C, PEP 8), naming conventions, and documentation templates to be followed.

**6. Reviews and Audits (1.5 marks):** Defines the schedule and procedures for:
   - **Formal Technical Reviews (FTR)**
   - **Walkthroughs**
   - **Inspections**
   - **Process audits** and **product audits**

**7. Test (1 mark):** Specifies test levels (unit, integration, system, acceptance), test types (functional, performance, security), and **test coverage criteria**.

**8. Problem Reporting and Corrective Action (0.5 marks):** Defines the **defect lifecycle** (New → Assigned → In Progress → Fixed → Verified → Closed), bug severity classification, and root cause analysis procedure.

**9. Tools, Techniques, and Methodologies (0.5 marks):** Lists SQA tools — static analyzers (SonarQube), test management tools (JIRA, TestRail), coverage tools (JaCoCo).

**10. Records, Training, and Retention (0.5 marks):** Defines how records are stored, the retention period, and the SQA training requirements for the team.

**Diagram Hint (for full marks):** Draw a hierarchical block diagram of the SQAP with the 11 sections as children of a central SQAP node.

---

**(b) Comparison of SDP and SQAP (7 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **SDP (IEEE 1058)** | **SQAP (IEEE 730)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** (1 mark) | Defines *how* the software will be developed — process, schedule, resources, and controls | Defines *how quality will be assured* — standards, reviews, tests, metrics |
| **Ownership** (1 mark) | **Project Manager** | **SQA Manager** (with organizational independence) |
| **Scope** (1 mark) | Entire project (technical + managerial) | Quality-specific subset of the project |
| **Key Activities** (1.5 marks) | WBS creation, scheduling, risk management, resource allocation, process model selection | Technical reviews, inspections, walkthroughs, audits, testing, defect tracking, metrics collection |
| **Relationship** (1.5 marks) | The SDP is the **parent (umbrella) plan** that governs the entire project | The SQAP is a **specialized child plan** embedded within the SDP; it operationalizes the quality aspects of the SDP |
| **Standard** (0.5 marks) | IEEE 1058 | IEEE 730 |
| **Reporting** (0.5 marks) | Reports to the customer and senior management | Reports to senior management (independent of dev team) |

**Conclusion:** The SDP and SQAP are **complementary, not competing** documents. The SDP is the project's "**flight plan**," and the SQAP is the project's "**co-pilot and quality inspector**." Together, they ensure the software is delivered on time, within budget, and to the required quality standard.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Must Revise Before KTU Exam):**
> 
> - **SDP** stands for **Software Development Plan**, governed by **IEEE 1058**.
> - **SQAP** stands for **Software Quality Assurance Plan**, governed by **IEEE 730**.
> - The **SDP** is owned by the **Project Manager**; the **SQAP** is owned by the **SQA Manager**.
> - The **SQA group** must have **organizational independence** — they report to **senior management**, not the dev team.
> - The **WBS (Work Breakdown Structure)** is the cornerstone of the SDP — it enables scheduling, cost estimation, and progress tracking.
> - **DRE (Defect Removal Efficiency)** is a key metric: DRE > 95% indicates a mature quality process.
> - **Verification** = "Are we building the product right?" | **Validation** = "Are we building the right product?"
> - The SQAP **operates in parallel** with the SDP across all SDLC phases — it is **not** a one-time activity.
> - **Common SQA reviews**: FTR (Formal Technical Review), Walkthrough, Inspection, Audit.
> - **Defect lifecycle** (must memorize): New → Assigned → In Progress → Fixed → Verified → Closed (or Reopened).
> - The SQAP is a **subset/specialization** of the SDP — never treat them as separate, unrelated documents.
> - **Standards in the exam:** Always cite the **IEEE standard number** (1058 for SDP, 730 for SQAP) — this is worth 1–2 marks.
> - **Tools in SQA:** SonarQube (static analysis), JIRA (defect tracking), Selenium (test automation), JaCoCo (code coverage).
> - **CMMI Levels** (Process Maturity): Level 1 (Initial) → Level 2 (Managed) → Level 3 (Defined) → Level 4 (Quantitatively Managed) → Level 5 (Optimizing). Higher levels require stricter SDP and SQAP discipline.
> - For 14-mark answers, always use a **comparison table** and end with a **clear concluding statement** to earn full marks.
> - **Common pitfall:** Confusing the **Test Plan** (a section of the SQAP) with the **SQAP** itself. The Test Plan addresses *testing only*; the SQAP covers the *entire quality framework*.
> - **Real-world application:** Industries like aerospace (DO-178C), medical (FDA 21 CFR 820), and automotive (ISO 26262) **legally mandate** SDP and SQAP compliance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Establishing SQA Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Establishing SQA Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Establishing the SQA Process** is a structured, management-driven engineering activity that defines, plans, documents, measures, implements, and continuously improves the software quality assurance activities across the **entire software development lifecycle (SDLC)**. It transforms abstract quality philosophies into repeatable, measurable, auditable engineering workflows.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 2), establishing the SQA process encompasses the following pillars:

| # | Pillar | Purpose |
|---|--------|---------|
| 1 | **SQA Management and Planning** | Defines organizational commitment, policies, and project SQA plans |
| 2 | **Process and Product Standards** | Codifies engineering expectations (IEEE, ISO, CMMI) |
| 3 | **Process Implementation** | Executes reviews, testing, audits, and verification tasks |
| 4 | **Documentation & Records** | Creates traceable artifacts proving quality was achieved |
| 5 | **Process Metrics** | Quantifies quality using defect density, MTTR, coverage |
| 6 | **Baselining the SQA Process** | Freezes a reference version to enable continuous improvement |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Building a Hospital** 🏥
> 
> Imagine you want to open a hospital. You don't just "treat patients" — you first **establish** the system:
> - **Planning** → Decide what kind of hospital, budget, staff (SQA Plan)
> - **Standards** → Hygiene protocols, dosage checks, surgical procedures (IEEE/ISO standards)
> - **Implementation** → Daily rounds, audits, sterilization (SQA activities)
> - **Documentation** → Patient files, incident logs (SQA records)
> - **Metrics** → Infection rate, recovery time, death rate (defect metrics)
> - **Baseline** → Year 1 performance becomes benchmark for Year 2
> 
> The SQA Process Establishment is exactly this — building the **quality hospital** before admitting the first patient (project).

## 1.3 Why SQA Process Establishment Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without a formally **established** SQA process, software quality becomes **accidental** rather than **engineered**. The IEEE Std 1028 (Reviews) and ISO/IEC 25010 (Quality Model) frameworks exist precisely to convert quality from intuition into a **managed discipline**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phase-Wise Decomposition of SQA Process Establishment

### Phase 1 — SQA Management and Planning

- **Top-Down Commitment**: Senior management defines quality policy, allocates budget, and appoints the **SQA Manager** (independent of development).
- **Project SQA Plan (PSAP)** is produced. It identifies:
  - **Standards** applicable (IEEE 830, IEEE 1028, ISO 9001, CMMI L1–L5)
  - **Reviews** to be conducted (FTR, Walkthrough, Inspection)
  - **Tests** to be executed (Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance)
  - **Tools** to be used (Selenium, JIRA, SonarQube)
  - **Schedule** mapped to SDLC phases
  - **Risk Register** and mitigation

### Phase 2 — Establishing Standards

Two categories:

$$
\text{Standards} = \{\text{Process Standards},\ \text{Product Standards}\}
$$

- **Process Standards** → How work is done (e.g., "All code must undergo FTR before merge").
- **Product Standards** → What is delivered (e.g., "Code coverage $\geq 80\%$").
- Standards are derived from **organizational policy** $\rightarrow$ **industry norms** $\rightarrow$ **project tailoring**.

### Phase 3 — Process Implementation

Implementation follows the **PDCA Cycle** (Deming Cycle):

$$
\text{SQA Implementation} \Rightarrow \text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Check} \rightarrow \text{Act}
$$

- **Plan**: Define what to review/test this iteration
- **Do**: Execute reviews, audits, tests
- **Check**: Compare results against metrics
- **Act**: Feed lessons back into standards

### Phase 4 — Documentation and Records

Five mandatory SQA records (per IEEE Std 730):

1. **SQA Plan**
2. **Review and Audit Reports**
3. **Test Plans and Test Reports**
4. **Problem and Non-Conformance Reports (PNCR)**
5. **Deviation Records and Approvals**

### Phase 5 — SQA Process Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metrics**: Defect Density, Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE), MTTR, Test Coverage, Code Churn, Customer-Reported Defects.

### Phase 6 — Baselining the SQA Process

- A **baseline** is a reviewed and approved reference version of the SQA process.
- Once frozen, deviations require **formal change control**.
- Baselining allows **future projects** to compare against a known-good benchmark.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **exam-critical** for Part B numerical/sub-part (a) questions in KTU ESE.

| Metric | Formula | Unit / Range | Exam Frequency |
|--------|---------|--------------|----------------|
| **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \dfrac{D}{S}$ | defects per KLOC or FP | ★★★★★ |
| **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $DRE = \dfrac{D_{\text{removed}}}{D_{\text{removed}} + D_{\text{shipped}}} \times 100$ | **percentage (%)** | ★★★★★ |
| **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum T_{\text{repair}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | hours | ★★★★ |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $MTBF = \dfrac{\sum T_{\text{operational}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | hours | ★★★★ |
| **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless ratio (0–1) | ★★★★ |
| **Test Coverage (TC)** | $TC = \dfrac{TS_{\text{executed}}}{TS_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | percentage (%) | ★★★ |
| **Requirement Stability Index (RSI)** | $RSI = \dfrac{R_{\text{final}}}{R_{\text{initial}} + R_{\text{added}}} \times 100$ | percentage (%) | ★★★ |
| **Code Coverage (CC)** | $CC = \dfrac{\text{Lines Tested}}{\text{Lines Total}} \times 100$ | percentage (%) | ★★★ |
| **Customer Satisfaction Index** | $CSI = \dfrac{\sum W_i \times S_i}{\sum W_i}$ | weighted score | ★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> In every formula above, **vertical bars** for absolute value (if any) must be written as `\vert x \vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax in your answer sheet. KTU examiners reject ambiguous notation.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Aerospace (DO-178C)**: Establishes SQA via certification levels (A–E). DRE target $\geq 99.99\%$.
- **Medical Devices (FDA/IEC 62304)**: SQA process establishment is legally mandatory.
- **Banking (PCI-DSS, ISO 25010)**: Baselining ensures audit repeatability.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262)**: ASIL-grade SQA process prevents life-critical defects.

## 2.4 Logical Reasoning Behind Each Step

- **Why plan first?** — Without a plan, SQA becomes reactive firefighting instead of proactive prevention.
- **Why separate process and product standards?** — A product can pass all tests but still be built via a broken process (e.g., undocumented code).
- **Why metrics?** — *"You cannot improve what you cannot measure"* — Lord Kelvin.
- **Why baseline?** — Improvement requires a fixed starting point; otherwise comparison is meaningless.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) — Step-by-Step

DRE is the **most frequently asked formula** in KTU Module 2 questions. Let us derive it from first principles so you can reproduce it in the exam hall.

### Step 1 — Define the Universe of Defects

Let:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{removed}} &= \text{Number of defects found and fixed before delivery} \\
D_{\text{shipped}} &= \text{Number of defects that escaped to the customer} \\
D_{\text{total}} &= D_{\text{removed}} + D_{\text{shipped}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic**: Every defect either gets removed internally OR escapes to the customer. There is no third state.

### Step 2 — Express the Removal Ratio

The fraction of defects that were successfully caught by the SQA process is:

$$
\text{Removal Ratio} = \dfrac{D_{\text{removed}}}{D_{\text{total}}}
$$

> **Logic**: Ratio = good outcomes ÷ all outcomes. Here "good" means "caught by us".

### Step 3 — Substitute the Total

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Removal Ratio} &= \dfrac{D_{\text{removed}}}{D_{\text{removed}} + D_{\text{shipped}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Percentage (KTU Convention)

$$
\begin{aligned}
DRE &= \dfrac{D_{\text{removed}}}{D_{\text{removed}} + D_{\text{shipped}}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic**: KTU examiners expect **percentage form**, not a decimal ratio. Always multiply by 100.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem**: During SQA establishment for a 50 KLOC payroll module, the team removed **480 defects** during testing. Post-release, **20 defects** were reported by customers.

**Solution**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{removed}} &= 480 \\
D_{\text{shipped}} &= 20 \\
D_{\text{total}} &= 480 + 20 = 500 \\
DRE &= \dfrac{480}{480 + 20} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{480}{500} \times 100\% \\
&= 0.96 \times 100\% \\
&= 96\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation**: 96% of all defects were removed before shipment. Industry best-practice target is **$\geq 95\%$** for safety-critical systems.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Maturity Levels of an Established SQA Process

| Maturity Level | Characteristics | SQA Process State |
|----------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Level 1 — Initial** | Ad-hoc, hero-based, no metrics | Process is **un-established** |
| **Level 2 — Repeatable** | Basic project management, basic SQA plan | Process is **partially established** |
| **Level 3 — Defined** | Documented standards, organization-wide SQA | Process is **fully established** |
| **Level 4 — Managed** | Quantitative metrics, baselines, statistical control | Process is **measured and baselined** |
| **Level 5 — Optimizing** | Continuous improvement, defect prevention | Process is **self-improving** |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently frame questions as: *"Compare your institute's SQA process with CMMI Level 3 maturity."* Memorize this 5-level matrix.

## 3.3 Boundary Conditions & Edge Cases

- If $D_{\text{shipped}} = 0$, then $DRE = 100\%$ (perfect process). However, this is suspicious — investigate whether defect reporting is actually working.
- If $D_{\text{removed}} = 0$, then $DRE = 0\%$ (catastrophic — no testing occurred).
- **IEEE Std 982.1** defines DRE acceptable range as **$\geq 85\%$** for commercial software.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Establishing the SQA Process (Top-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

> **Reading Guide**: The dashed feedback loop from Step 8 back to Step 4 represents the **PDCA (Deming) cycle** — the heart of continuous SQA improvement.

## 4.2 Architecture Diagram — Roles, Inputs, Outputs in SQA Process Establishment


<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]**
*Define the term "Establishing SQA Process." List any **four** key activities involved in it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

Establishing the SQA Process is the systematic activity of defining, planning, documenting, measuring, and baselining all software quality assurance activities across the SDLC.

Four key activities (1 mark each — partial marking applicable):

1. **SQA Management and Planning** — defining policy, budget, and the Project SQA Plan.
2. **Establishing Standards** — codifying process and product standards (IEEE/ISO/CMMI).
3. **Process Implementation** — executing reviews, audits, and tests per the plan.
4. **Documentation and Metrics** — maintaining records and collecting quality metrics.
5. *(Bonus / Optional)* **Baselining** the process for future reuse.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
*What is a **Project SQA Plan (PSAP)**? State any **four** items it typically contains.*

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

A Project SQA Plan (PSAP) is a document that defines the specific quality activities, standards, responsibilities, tools, and schedules for a given software project.

Four typical contents (½ mark each × 4 = 2 marks, plus 1 mark for the definition):

| # | Item |
|---|------|
| 1 | Scope and applicability of the plan |
| 2 | Referenced standards (IEEE 830, IEEE 1028, ISO 25010) |
| 3 | Planned reviews, audits, and tests |
| 4 | Schedule, tools, responsibilities, and risk register |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 2 Compulsory Question]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **six major components** involved in establishing the SQA process. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 60 KLOC e-commerce system recorded **540 defects** during testing and **30 defects** after release. Calculate:
- (i) Defect Density (DD) *(2 marks)*
- (ii) Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) *(3 marks)*
- (iii) Comment on whether the process satisfies the industry benchmark. *(2 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

The six major components of establishing the SQA process are:

1. **SQA Management and Planning** *(1.5 marks)*
   - Senior management defines quality policy.
   - Appoint independent SQA Manager.
   - Develop Project SQA Plan (PSAP).

2. **Establishing Standards** *(1.5 marks)*
   - Process standards — how work is performed.
   - Product standards — characteristics of deliverables.
   - Tailored from IEEE, ISO, CMMI, organizational policy.

3. **Process Implementation** *(1 mark)*
   - Conduct Formal Technical Reviews (FTR), Walkthroughs, Inspections.
   - Execute unit, integration, system, and acceptance tests.
   - Perform SQA audits at milestones.

4. **Documentation** *(1 mark)*
   - Maintain SQA plan, audit reports, test reports, problem reports, deviation records.

5. **SQA Process Metrics** *(1 mark)*
   - Defect Density, DRE, MTTR, MTBF, test coverage, code coverage.

6. **Baselining the SQA Process** *(1 mark)*
   - Freeze a reference version after validation.
   - Future projects compare deviations against baseline.
   - Drives continuous improvement via PDCA.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Given**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Size } S &= 60 \text{ KLOC} \\
D_{\text{removed}} &= 540 \\
D_{\text{shipped}} &= 30 \\
D_{\text{total}} &= 540 + 30 = 570
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Defect Density** *(2 marks)*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DD &= \dfrac{D_{\text{total}}}{S} \\
&= \dfrac{570}{60} \\
&= 9.5 \text{ defects per KLOC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Defect Removal Efficiency** *(3 marks)*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DRE &= \dfrac{D_{\text{removed}}}{D_{\text{removed}} + D_{\text{shipped}}} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{540}{540 + 30} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{540}{570} \times 100\% \\
&= 0.9474 \times 100\% \\
&\approx 94.74\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Benchmark Evaluation** *(2 marks)*:

- Industry benchmark for commercial software is **DRE $\geq 85\%$** (per IEEE Std 982.1).
- The calculated DRE of **94.74%** exceeds this benchmark — the SQA process is **satisfactory**.
- However, for **safety-critical systems** (aerospace, medical) the target is $\geq 99\%$, so further improvement is recommended.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 2 Choice Question]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **baselining the SQA process**. Why is it considered a prerequisite for **continuous improvement**? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Distinguish between **Process Standards** and **Product Standards** with **two examples each**. Explain how both are integrated during the SQA process establishment. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 marks]

**Role of Baselining** *(5 marks)*:

1. **Definition** *(1 mark)*: A baseline is a reviewed, approved, and frozen reference version of the SQA process that serves as the formal starting point for all future measurement and improvement.
2. **Provides Comparability** *(1 mark)*: Without a baseline, you cannot say whether defect density has improved or worsened — there is nothing to compare against.
3. **Enables Change Control** *(1 mark)*: Any deviation from the baselined process must undergo formal review, preventing uncontrolled process drift.
4. **Supports Reuse** *(1 mark)*: Future projects can adopt the baseline as a proven starting template, reducing time-to-establish SQA.
5. **Audit Anchor** *(1 mark)*: External auditors (ISO, CMMI) require a documented baseline to certify the organization.

**Why It Is a Prerequisite for Continuous Improvement** *(2 marks)*:

Continuous improvement (Kaizen / PDCA Act phase) requires three things: **(i)** a known starting state, **(ii)** measured gaps, and **(iii)** verified corrective changes. Baselining provides **(i)**. Without it, the PDCA cycle degenerates into guesswork — improvement cannot be proven, only asserted.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 marks]

| Aspect | Process Standards | Product Standards |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Focus** | How work is performed | What is delivered |
| **Concern** | Methodology, sequence, roles | Correctness, format, completeness |
| **Examples** | IEEE 1028 (Reviews); IEEE 730 (SQA Plan); CMMI Level 2 Process Areas | Code coverage $\geq 80\%$; response time $\leq 2$s; zero critical defects at release |
| **Enforced By** | SQA audits, process checklists | Reviews, tests, inspections |

**Integration during SQA Establishment** *(3 marks)*:

During SQA process establishment:

1. **Phase 2 — Standards Definition** codifies BOTH process and product standards inside the SQA Plan.
2. **Phase 3 — Implementation** executes process standards (e.g., conduct an FTR) which in turn verify product standards (e.g., does the code meet coverage?).
3. **Phase 4–6 — Records, Metrics, Baselines** capture evidence that BOTH classes of standards were satisfied, producing an auditable quality artifact.

> **Synthesis Statement**: Process standards ensure the *journey* is correct; product standards ensure the *destination* is correct. An established SQA process requires both.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Establishing SQA Process" Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Process vs Product Standards** — Examiners deduct **2 marks** if examples are swapped. Memorize: process = *how*, product = *what*.
> 2. **Forgetting the Multiplier 100 in DRE** — Writing $\frac{540}{570} = 0.947$ instead of $94.74\%$ costs **1 full mark**.
> 3. **Ignoring Units in Defect Density** — Writing "DD = 9.5" without "per KLOC" loses **0.5 to 1 mark** depending on strictness.
> 4. **Listing SQA Activities without Explaining the FLOW** — Merely enumerating 6 items in Part B(a) without showing how they interlink (PDCA, feedback loop) loses **1–2 marks**. Examiners expect structural reasoning, not a bullet dump.
> 5. **Skipping the "Interpretation Step"** — Numerical answers without a concluding comment (e.g., "94.74% > 85% benchmark → process satisfactory") are penalized **1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Not Mentioning IEEE/ISO Anchors** — Referencing "IEEE 730" or "ISO 25010" in your answer earns **bonus credibility marks** even when not explicitly asked.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **SQA Process Establishment = 6 pillars**: Management → Standards → Implementation → Documentation → Metrics → Baseline.
- [x] **PSAP (Project SQA Plan)** is the central artifact — contains scope, standards, reviews, tests, schedule, tools, risks.
- [x] **Process Standards** = how; **Product Standards** = what. Never confuse.
- [x] **IEEE Std 730** governs SQA Plans; **IEEE Std 1028** governs Reviews; **ISO/IEC 25010** defines the quality model.
- [x] **CMMI 5 Levels**: Initial → Repeatable → Defined → Managed → Optimizing. SQA is fully established by Level 3.
- [x] **DRE Formula**: $DRE = \frac{D_{\text{removed}}}{D_{\text{removed}} + D_{\text{shipped}}} \times 100\%$. Always include `%`.
- [x] **DD Formula**: $DD = \frac{D_{\text{total}}}{S}$ where $S$ is in KLOC or Function Points.
- [x] **Industry Benchmark**: DRE $\geq 85\%$ commercial; $\geq 99\%$ safety-critical.
- [x] **Baselining** is the *frozen reference* — deviation requires formal change control.
- [x] **PDCA Cycle** drives continuous SQA improvement: Plan → Do → Check → Act.
- [x] **SQA Manager** must be organizationally **independent** of the development team for objectivity.
- [x] **Five mandatory SQA records** per IEEE 730: PSAP, Audit Reports, Test Reports, PNCR, Deviation Records.
- [x] **MTTR** measures repair speed; **MTBF** measures reliability; **Availability** $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
- [x] **Always end numerical answers** with an interpretation sentence — examiners reward analytical closure.
- [x] **Mermaid diagrams** for KTU answers should be hand-drawn equivalents on paper: flowchart, hierarchy, cycle.

> **Final Exam Mantra**: *"Establishing SQA is not an event — it is a lifecycle. Plan, Standardize, Execute, Document, Measure, Baseline, Improve."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SQA Plan and Project Plan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SQA Plan and Project Plan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — SQA Plan and Project Plan

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Software Quality Assurance Plan (SQA Plan)

The **Software Quality Assurance Plan (SQAP)** is a formal, project-specific document that prescribes the **schedules, processes, procedures, tasks, responsibilities, and resources** required to achieve and maintain product quality throughout the software life cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (IEEE Std 730 aligned):**
> The SQAP is the master document that defines *how* quality will be planned, assured, controlled, and recorded for a specific software project. It is not the test plan — it is the umbrella that governs *all* quality-related activities including reviews, audits, testing, standards compliance, and corrective actions.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "ISO Audit Manual" of Software:**
Imagine a pharmaceutical company manufacturing a new vaccine. Before production begins, the Quality Department writes a thick **Quality Plan** stating: which raw materials will be tested, who signs the batch release, what storage temperatures are acceptable, which regulatory standards apply, and how often the lab equipment is calibrated. The vaccine itself is the *product*, the lab notebooks are *records*, and the Quality Plan is the **SQA Plan**. It does not produce the vaccine — it ensures the vaccine is *produced correctly, consistently, and safely*.

---

### 1.2 The Software Project Plan (SPP / SPMP)

The **Software Project Management Plan (SPMP)** — also called the **Project Plan (PP)** — is a foundational document describing what is to be done, *by whom*, *when*, *how*, and *with what resources*, to deliver a complete software system on time and within budget.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (IEEE Std 1058 aligned):**
> The Project Plan defines the technical and managerial framework in which the software project will be conducted. It establishes baselines for **scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk, communication, and resource allocation**.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Civil Engineer's Master Blueprint & Schedule":**
If building a 20-storey apartment is the *project*, then the SPMP is the roll of paper containing:
- the architectural blueprint (what we are building),
- the Gantt chart (when each floor is poured),
- the workforce list (which electrician does which flat),
- the material order (how much cement, by when),
- the risk register (what if the monsoons delay the foundation?).

The SQA Plan sits *inside* the SPMP (or beside it as a sibling) and acts as the **inspector who walks the site daily checking rebar spacing, concrete mix ratios, and worker safety**.

---

### 1.3 The Relationship — A One-Line Mental Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Project Plan** answers *"HOW do we build the software?"*
> The **SQA Plan** answers *"HOW do we make sure the software is being built correctly?"*
> The SQA Plan is therefore a **mandatory sub-plan of the broader Software Project Plan**.

```
Project Plan (SPMP) ──────┐
   │                       │
   ├── Scope               │
   ├── Schedule            │
   ├── Budget              ├──► SQA Plan (SQAP)
   ├── Risk Plan           │      ├── Reviews
   ├── Configuration Plan  │      ├── Audits
   ├── Test Plan           │      ├── Standards
   └── QA Plan (SQA Plan) ◄┘      └── Records
```
---

### 1.4 KTU Board Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Full Form | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **SQAP** | Software Quality Assurance Plan | **IEEE Std 730** |
| **SPMP** | Software Project Management Plan | **IEEE Std 1058** |
| **SDP** | Software Development Plan | IEEE Std 730 (legacy) |
| **SCMP** | Software Configuration Management Plan | IEEE Std 828 |
| **STP** | Software Test Plan | IEEE Std 829 |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU board examiners expect students to **memorise the IEEE standard numbers** alongside the plan names. Writing *"SQA Plan per IEEE 730"* on the answer script fetches an instant +1 mark.

---

### 1.5 Why These Plans Exist — The Three Pillars

1. **Visibility** — Management, auditors, and clients can *see* how quality will be enforced.
2. **Repeatability** — Successive projects (and new team members) can follow the same recipe.
3. **Accountability** — Each role, responsibility, and metric is *owned* by a named individual or group.

---

### 1.6 Intuitive Summary Table

| Feature | SQA Plan | Project Plan |
|---|---|---|
| Primary audience | QA team, auditors, client | Project manager, sponsor, team leads |
| Focus | **Product + Process quality** | **Delivery on time, scope, budget** |
| IEEE Standard | **IEEE Std 730** | **IEEE Std 1058** |
| Sub-plans it contains | Test plan, review checklists | SQA plan, CM plan, risk plan |
| Frequency of revision | Each phase gate | Each major milestone |
| Length (typical) | 15–40 pages | 40–100+ pages |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Anatomy of the SQA Plan (IEEE Std 730 — Modernised)

IEEE 730 (2014 revision) prescribes **11 mandatory sections**. Each section answers a specific quality question:

| # | Section | Purpose | Sample Content |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Purpose** | *Why does this SQAP exist?* | Project ID, intended audience, scope of SQA effort |
| 2 | **Reference Documents** | *What supporting docs apply?* | SRS, SDD, STD, IEEE 730, organisational quality manual |
| 3 | **Management** | *Who owns quality?* | Org chart of QA team, reporting lines, authority |
| 4 | **Documentation** | *What docs will be produced & reviewed?* | Document control procedure, templates, approval flow |
| 5 | **Standards, Practices & Conventions** | *Which rules are mandatory?* | Coding standards (e.g., MISRA-C, PEP-8), naming conventions |
| 6 | **Reviews & Audits** | *How will work be inspected?* | Type of reviews (walkthroughs, inspections), audit schedule |
| 7 | **Test** | *How will the product be validated?* | Test levels, entry/exit criteria, test environment |
| 8 | **Problem Reporting & Corrective Action** | *How are defects handled?* | Defect tracking tool, severity levels, escalation matrix |
| 9 | **Tools, Techniques & Methodologies** | *What tools support SQA?* | Static analysers (e.g., SonarQube), test management tools |
| 10 | **Records** | *What evidence is retained?* | Review logs, audit reports, test results, retention period |
| 11 | **Training** | *How will staff gain SQA skills?* | Tool training, process training, certification needs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a 14-mark KTU question, examiners award **2 marks per section explained** (5–6 sections = 10–12 marks, plus examples = full marks). Memorising all 11 is *not* necessary; **deep coverage of 5–6 sections** is sufficient.

---

## 2.2 Anatomy of the Project Plan (IEEE Std 1058)

The IEEE 1058 standard prescribes the following structural skeleton:

```
1. Introduction
   1.1 Project Overview
   1.2 Project Deliverables
   1.3 Evolution of the Plan
   1.4 Reference Materials
   1.5 Definitions and Acronyms

2. Project Organisation
   2.1 External Interfaces
   2.2 Internal Structure
   2.3 Project Roles and Responsibilities

3. Managerial Process Plans
   3.1 Start-up Plan
   3.2 Work Plan
   3.3 Control Plan
   3.4 Risk Management Plan
   3.5 Project Closeout Plan

4. Technical Process Plans
   4.1 Process Model
   4.2 Methods, Tools, and Techniques
   4.3 Infrastructure Plan
   4.4 Product Acceptance Plan

5. Supporting Process Plans
   5.1 Configuration Management Plan
   5.2 Verification and Validation Plan   ◄── SQA Plan lives here
   5.3 Documentation Plan
   5.4 Quality Assurance Plan            ◄── OR here as a peer
   5.5 Reviews and Audits
   5.6 Problem Resolution Plan

6. Work Packages, Schedule, and Budget
   6.1 Work Packages
   6.2 Schedule
   6.3 Budget

7. Appendices
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Section 5.2 / 5.4 of the SPMP is exactly where the **SQA Plan is anchored**. Drawing this nesting diagram on the answer sheet guarantees a +2 visual-representation mark.

---

## 2.3 High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Plan Comparison Matrix

| Dimension | SQA Plan (SQAP) | Project Plan (SPMP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Question** | *Are we building it right?* | *Are we building the right thing, on time, on budget?* |
| **Standard** | IEEE 730 | IEEE 1058 |
| **Owner** | SQA Manager | Project Manager |
| **Scope** | Quality-specific | Whole project |
| **Update Frequency** | After each phase gate / audit | After each major milestone |
| **Inputs** | SPMP, organisational SQA policies | SRS, contract, organisational templates |
| **Outputs** | Audit reports, review minutes, defect logs | WBS, Gantt chart, budget, risk register |
| **Audience** | Internal QA team + client | All stakeholders (dev, test, PM, client) |
| **Mandatory Sections (KTU)** | 5–6 of the 11 IEEE sections | 4–5 of the 7 IEEE sections |
| **Failure Consequence** | Defective product, audit failure | Schedule slip, cost overrun, contract breach |

---

## 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet — Quantitative Aspects

Although SQAP and SPMP are predominantly qualitative, several **quantitative controls** appear within them. These are *high-yield* for numerical questions.

### 2.4.1 Effort / Schedule Estimation (within SPMP)

$$
E = a \cdot K^{b}
$$

Where:
- $E$ = effort in person-months
- $K$ = size in KLOC (thousand lines of code) or function points
- $a, b$ = empirically derived constants (e.g., COCOMO: $a = 2.4$, $b = 1.05$ for organic mode)

### 2.4.2 Phase-wise Schedule Allocation (SPMP)

$$
T_{dev} = 0.40 \cdot T_{total}, \quad T_{test} = 0.30 \cdot T_{total}, \quad T_{SQA} = 0.10 \cdot T_{total}, \quad T_{buffer} = 0.20 \cdot T_{total}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **$0.10 \cdot T_{total}$ allocation for SQA activities** is a classic KTU numerical ask. Examiner loves to test whether the student knows that QA effort is *not* free — it must be *budgeted* into the schedule.

### 2.4.3 Review Effectiveness (within SQAP)

$$
E_{review} = \frac{D_{found}}{D_{found} + D_{escaped}}
$$

Where:
- $D_{found}$ = defects detected during review
- $D_{escaped}$ = defects that escaped the review into the next phase

**Typical benchmark:** $E_{review} \geq \mathbf{0.85}$ (i.e., ≥ 85% effectiveness expected from formal inspections).

### 2.4.4 Test Coverage Threshold (within SQAP § 7)

$$
C_{stmt} = \frac{S_{executed}}{S_{total}}, \quad C_{branch} = \frac{B_{executed}}{B_{total}}, \quad C_{req} = \frac{R_{tested}}{R_{total}}
$$

**KTU benchmark values:**
- $C_{stmt} \geq \mathbf{0.90}$
- $C_{branch} \geq \mathbf{0.85}$
- $C_{req} \geq \mathbf{1.00}$ (every requirement must have at least one test)

### 2.4.5 Cost of Quality (CoQ) — A Plan-Level Metric

$$
C_{total} = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\_failure} + C_{external\_failure}
$$

| Component | Example |
|---|---|
| **Prevention** | Training, process improvement, design reviews |
| **Appraisal** | Test execution, inspections, audits |
| **Internal Failure** | Rework before release |
| **External Failure** | Patches, warranty claims, customer returns |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU asks this **4-element decomposition** verbatim. Writing "CoQ = prevention + appraisal + failure" (without separating internal vs. external failure) loses 1 mark.

---

## 2.5 The Why & How — Operational Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Project Initiation:** SPMP is drafted first because the project cannot start without knowing *what to build and when*.

**Step 2 — SQA Sub-Plan Creation:** Within the SPMP, the QA manager drafts the SQAP using the organisational SQA template + IEEE 730 skeleton.

**Step 3 — Standards Selection:** The SQAP references which coding/design standards are mandatory for the team (e.g., ISO/IEC 25010 quality model).

**Step 4 — Reviews & Audits Scheduled:** Review types (walkthrough, inspection, technical review) are tied to milestones in the SPMP's Gantt chart.

**Step 5 — Test Strategy Defined:** SQAP § 7 specifies unit, integration, system, acceptance test levels with entry/exit criteria.

**Step 6 — Defect Workflow:** A formal Defect Life Cycle (New → Assigned → In Progress → Verified → Closed) is embedded in § 8.

**Step 7 — Records & Retention:** SQAP § 10 mandates how long each artefact (audit report, test log, review minutes) is retained — typically 5–7 years for regulated industries.

**Step 8 — Training Plan:** SQAP § 11 ensures every team member is trained in the chosen tools (e.g., JIRA, Selenium, SonarQube).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace (DO-178C)** | Each software level (A–E) requires a PSAC (Plan for Software Aspects of Certification) — structurally identical to SQAP. |
| **Medical Devices (IEC 62304)** | Software Development Plan + SQA Plan are mandatory for FDA / CE submission. |
| **Automotive (ISO 26262)** | Project Plan must include a Safety Plan and Quality Plan; ASIL levels map to SQAP § 6 reviews. |
| **Banking / Finance** | RBI, SEBI, PCI-DSS require documented QA + Change Management plans before production deployment. |
| **Defence (MIL-STD-882E)** | Software System Safety Program Plan embeds SQA + test + audit. |

---

## 2.7 Quick-Recall Numbered Formula List

1. $E = a K^{b}$ — effort from code size.
2. $T_{SQA} = 0.10 \cdot T_{total}$ — SQA schedule allocation.
3. $E_{review} = \dfrac{D_{found}}{D_{found} + D_{escaped}}$ — review effectiveness.
4. $C_{req} \geq 1.00$ — 100% requirement coverage rule.
5. $C_{total} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ — Cost of Quality.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $T_{SQA} \approx 0.10 \cdot T_{total}$?

In a typical V-Model project, total calendar time is distributed across lifecycle activities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= T_{requirements} + T_{design} + T_{coding} + T_{testing} + T_{SQA} + T_{buffer} \\
T_{requirements} &= 0.15 \cdot T_{total} \\
T_{design} &= 0.20 \cdot T_{total} \\
T_{coding} &= 0.25 \cdot T_{total} \\
T_{testing} &= 0.30 \cdot T_{total} \\
T_{SQA} &= 0.10 \cdot T_{total}
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing to verify:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.15 + 0.20 + 0.25 + 0.30 + 0.10 &= 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

> Therefore, the **SQA effort is approximately one-tenth of total project time**, distributed *throughout* all phases (overlapped), not merely an additional block at the end.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Review Effectiveness Equation

Let $N$ be the total number of defects latent in a software artefact before review.

After the review:
- $D_{found}$ defects are detected and removed.
- $D_{escaped} = N - D_{found}$ defects leak into the next phase.

**Definition (Review Effectiveness):**

$$
E_{review} = \frac{D_{found}}{D_{found} + D_{escaped}}
$$

But since $D_{found} + D_{escaped} = N$:

$$
E_{review} = \frac{D_{found}}{N}
$$

**Significance:** If a review finds 17 of 20 latent defects, $E_{review} = 17/20 = 0.85$ (85%). KTU's benchmark is **≥ 85%** for formal inspections; lower for walkthroughs (≈ 60%).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — Test Coverage Calculation

> **Problem (KTU Model):** A module contains 320 statements, 180 branches, and 45 functional requirements. After test execution, the QA team reports that 304 statements, 153 branches, and all 45 requirements have been exercised.
> Compute the three coverage metrics and determine whether the release is *blocker* or *go*.

### Step 1 — Statement Coverage

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{stmt} &= \frac{S_{executed}}{S_{total}} \\
         &= \frac{304}{320} \\
         &= 0.95
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion:** $0.95 \times 100 = \mathbf{95\%}$. Exceeds the 90% benchmark → **PASS**.

### Step 2 — Branch Coverage

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{branch} &= \frac{B_{executed}}{B_{total}} \\
           &= \frac{153}{180} \\
           &= 0.85
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion:** $0.85 \times 100 = \mathbf{85\%}$. Meets the 85% benchmark exactly → **PASS** (marginal).

### Step 3 — Requirement Coverage

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{req} &= \frac{R_{tested}}{R_{total}} \\
        &= \frac{45}{45} \\
        &= 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion:** $1.00 \times 100 = \mathbf{100\%}$. Meets the 100% benchmark → **PASS**.

### Step 4 — Verdict

Since all three benchmarks are satisfied, the module can be released to the next integration stage without a *blocker*. However, the marginal branch coverage (exactly at threshold) should trigger a recommendation for additional **boundary-value tests**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem — Cost of Quality (CoQ) Decomposition

> **Problem:** A project budget is ₹ 50 lakhs. Breakdown:
> - Training & process improvement: ₹ 6 L
> - Test execution & inspections: ₹ 9 L
> - Rework before release: ₹ 4 L
> - Post-release patches & warranty: ₹ 11 L
>
> Compute total CoQ and the CoQ as a percentage of project budget.

### Step 1 — Classify into CoQ categories

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{prevention} &= 6 \text{ L} \quad \text{(Training + process)} \\
C_{appraisal}  &= 9 \text{ L} \quad \text{(Test execution + inspections)} \\
C_{internal\_failure} &= 4 \text{ L} \quad \text{(Rework pre-release)} \\
C_{external\_failure} &= 11 \text{ L} \quad \text{(Post-release)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Total CoQ

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF} \\
          &= 6 + 9 + 4 + 11 \\
          &= 30 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — CoQ as % of Budget

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,CoQ &= \frac{30}{50} \times 100 \\
        &= 60\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation

A CoQ of 60% is **unacceptably high** — the industry benchmark is 15–25%. The dominant contributor is **external failure (₹ 11 L)** which indicates that pre-release reviews and testing are inadequate. **Recommendation:** Increase $C_P$ (prevention) by 50% to drive $C_{EF}$ (external failure) down in future releases.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Escalate from Project Plan Issue to SQA Plan Action

| Signal in Project Plan | Root-Cause Indicator | SQA Plan Counter-Measure |
|---|---|---|
| Schedule slip > 10% | Process weakness | Trigger **process audit** (SQAP § 6) |
| Defect escape rate > 5% | Review weakness | Schedule **additional formal inspection** (SQAP § 6) |
| Tool misuse / inconsistency | Training gap | Conduct **tool refresher workshop** (SQAP § 11) |
| Standards violation in code | Documentation gap | Issue **standard violation log** (SQAP § 5, § 8) |
| Customer complaint spike | External failure spike | Open **CAPA** (Corrective and Preventive Action, SQAP § 8) |

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step: Building an SQA Plan from Scratch

**Step 1:** Identify the **scope** of the project and the **audience** for the SQAP (single client? internal? regulator?).

**Step 2:** Gather the **reference documents** (SRS, SDD, contractual clauses, organisational quality manual).

**Step 3:** Appoint the **SQA Manager** and document the **organisational structure** for QA.

**Step 4:** Identify the **standards, practices, and conventions** that the project must follow (coding, design, documentation).

**Step 5:** Plan the **review and audit schedule** — bind each review to a phase gate in the SPMP's Gantt chart.

**Step 6:** Define the **test strategy** — levels, entry/exit criteria, environment, automation tools.

**Step 7:** Define the **defect reporting and corrective action workflow** — severity levels, response SLAs, escalation.

**Step 8:** List the **tools** that will support SQA — defect trackers, static analysers, test management.

**Step 9:** Specify the **records** that will be retained — type, location, retention period.

**Step 10:** Specify the **training** required — who, what, when, certification.

**Step 11:** Review and **approve** the SQAP — signed off by SQA Manager, Project Manager, and (for regulated projects) the client/regulator.

---

## 3.7 Step-by-Step: Building a Project Plan from Scratch

**Step 1:** Capture the **project overview**, objectives, deliverables, and constraints.

**Step 2:** Define the **organisational structure** — reporting lines, roles, RACI matrix.

**Step 3:** Define the **process model** (Waterfall / Agile / Hybrid) and the life cycle phases.

**Step 4:** Create the **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** — decompose deliverables into work packages.

**Step 5:** Build the **schedule** — Gantt chart, milestones, critical path.

**Step 6:** Build the **budget** — labour, hardware, software licences, training, contingency.

**Step 7:** Build the **risk register** — probability × impact matrix, mitigation plans.

**Step 8:** Build the **supporting plans** — CM, SQA, test, documentation, training (these are often appendices).

**Step 9:** Define the **control plan** — how progress will be measured, reported, and corrected.

**Step 10:** Obtain **sign-off** from the steering committee and update at every major milestone.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Master Architecture — SQA Plan Inside Project Plan

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Software Project Management Plan SPMP] --> B1[Section 1 Introduction]
    A0 --> B2[Section 2 Project Organization]
    A0 --> B3[Section 3 Managerial Process Plans]

    A0 --> B5[Section 5 Supporting Process Plans]
    A0 --> B6[Section 6 Work Packages Schedule Budget]

    B5 --> C1[Configuration Management Plan]

    B5 --> C3[Software Test Plan]
    B5 --> C4[Documentation Plan]
    B5 --> C5[Risk Management Plan]

    C2 --> D1[SQAP Section 1 Purpose]
    C2 --> D2[SQAP Section 2 References]
    C2 --> D3[SQAP Section 3 Management]
    C2 --> D4[SQAP Section 4 Documentation]
    C2 --> D5[SQAP Section 5 Standards]
    C2 --> D6[SQAP Section 6 Reviews and Audits]

    C2 --> D8[SQAP Section 8 Problem Reporting]
    C2 --> D9[SQAP Section 9 Tools]
    C2 --> D10[SQAP Section 10 Records]
    C2 --> D11[SQAP Section 11 Training]
```

---

## 4.2 The Two Plans — Side-by-Side Block Diagram

```mermaid
    end

    P7 -. embeds .-> Q1
    P2 -. gates .-> Q2
    P2 -. gates .-> Q4
    P5 -. feeds .-> Q5
    Q3 -. informs .-> P5
```

---

## 4.3 Defect Reporting and Corrective Action Flow (SQAP § 8)

```mermaid

    node1[Defect Detected] --> node2[Logged in Tracker]
    node2 --> node3{Triage by QA Lead}

    node3 -->|Severity Medium| node6[Assign within 72 hrs]
    node3 -->|Severity Low| node7[Backlog]

    node6 --> node8
    node7 --> node8

    node9 --> node10{QA Verification}

    node10 -->|Fail| node12[Re-open]

    node11 --> node13[Records Archived]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Software Quality Assurance Plan. List any five sections prescribed by IEEE Std 730. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Software Quality Assurance Plan (SQAP)** is a project-specific document, governed by **IEEE Std 730**, that prescribes the schedules, processes, procedures, tasks, responsibilities, and resources required to assure and maintain the quality of a software product throughout its life cycle.

The five sections prescribed by IEEE Std 730 are:

1. **Purpose** — defines scope and audience.
2. **Reference Documents** — lists applicable standards and project documents.
3. **Standards, Practices, and Conventions** — mandatory rules (coding, design, documentation).
4. **Reviews and Audits** — type, frequency, and reporting of inspections.
5. **Test** — test levels, entry/exit criteria, environment, and tools.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Writing only *"it is a quality plan"* without quoting **IEEE Std 730** loses the first mark. Always cite the standard number explicitly.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between the SQA Plan and the Software Project Plan. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| # | SQA Plan (SQAP) | Project Plan (SPMP) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Governed by **IEEE 730** | Governed by **IEEE 1058** |
| 2 | Focuses on **product and process quality** | Focuses on **scope, schedule, budget, and delivery** |
| 3 | Owned by **SQA Manager** | Owned by **Project Manager** |
| 4 | Answers *"Are we building it right?"* | Answers *"Are we building the right thing, on time?"* |
| 5 | Includes reviews, audits, test strategy, defect workflow | Includes WBS, Gantt, budget, risk register, resource plan |
| 6 | Typically a **sub-plan** embedded within the SPMP | A **top-level document** governing the entire project |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Conflating the SQA Plan with the **Test Plan (IEEE 829)** is the most common error. The SQAP governs *all* quality activities; the Test Plan is just one section of it.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### (a) Explain the structure of the Software Quality Assurance Plan as per IEEE Std 730. Describe any six sections in detail. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction.** The Software Quality Assurance Plan (SQAP), standardised by IEEE Std 730, provides a structured framework for ensuring that software engineering processes and products conform to requirements, standards, and procedures.

**Six Sections in Detail:**

**(1) Purpose.** This opening section states the project's unique identity, the scope of the SQA effort, and the intended audience (development team, client, regulator). Example: *"This SQAP governs all SQA activities for the Online Banking Module v2.1 and is binding on every member of Team-C."*

**(2) Reference Documents.** Lists all baseline documents such as the SRS, SDD, organisational quality manual, applicable IEEE/ISO standards, and contractual clauses.

**(3) Management.** Defines the QA organisational structure, reporting relationships, and authority of the SQA Manager. A RACI matrix is typically embedded here.

**(4) Standards, Practices, and Conventions.** Mandates the technical rules — coding standards (e.g., MISRA-C, PEP-8), design conventions (e.g., layered architecture), documentation templates.

**(5) Reviews and Audits.** Specifies the type of reviews (walkthrough, technical review, inspection), the schedule (e.g., design review at end of design phase), the participants, and the records produced.

**(6) Test.** Specifies test levels (unit, integration, system, acceptance), entry and exit criteria, the test environment, automation tools, and the test data strategy.

**Conclusion.** Together, these six sections provide a *complete operational contract* between the project team and the QA function, ensuring quality is engineered into the product rather than inspected in at the end.

---

#### (b) Discuss the structure of the Software Project Plan as per IEEE Std 1058. List its major sections and describe any three in detail. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction.** The Software Project Management Plan (SPMP), per IEEE Std 1058, is the master planning document for any software project. It binds together scope, schedule, cost, quality, and risk into a single coherent plan.

**Major Sections of SPMP (IEEE 1058):**

1. Introduction
2. Project Organisation
3. Managerial Process Plans
4. Technical Process Plans
5. Supporting Process Plans
6. Work Packages, Schedule, and Budget
7. Appendices

**Detailed Description of Three Sections:**

**(1) Project Organisation (Section 2).** Describes the project structure, including external interfaces (client, vendors, regulators), internal team structure (modules, sub-teams), and project roles with their responsibilities (typically represented as a RACI matrix).

**(2) Managerial Process Plans (Section 3).** Encompasses the start-up plan (kick-off, training, infrastructure setup), the work plan (WBS, schedule, milestones), the control plan (Earned Value Analysis, progress reporting), the risk management plan (identification, analysis, mitigation), and the project closeout plan (handover, post-mortem).

**(3) Supporting Process Plans (Section 5).** Houses all discipline-specific sub-plans, including the **Configuration Management Plan**, the **SQA Plan**, the **Test Plan**, the **Documentation Plan**, the **Risk Plan**, and the **Problem Resolution Plan**. These are typically appended to the SPMP for traceability.

**Conclusion.** The SPMP is *dynamic*: it is updated at every major milestone to reflect actual progress, new risks, and scope changes.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### (a) Discuss the relationship between the SQA Plan and the Software Project Plan. Why is the SQA Plan considered a supporting sub-plan of the Project Plan? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction.** The SQA Plan and the Project Plan are not independent documents. They are *complementary*, with the SQA Plan operating as a supporting sub-plan within the broader framework of the Project Plan.

**The Relationship — Four Key Lenses:**

**(1) Hierarchical Nesting.** Per IEEE 1058, Section 5 (*Supporting Process Plans*) explicitly requires a *Quality Assurance Plan* as one of its sub-plans. Therefore, structurally, the SQAP is **contained within** the SPMP.

**(2) Content Overlap with Specialisation.** Both documents contain schedule, resource, and reporting elements. However, the SPMP treats these *project-wide*, whereas the SQAP treats them *quality-specifically*. For example, both have schedules — the SPMP's Gantt chart shows when each module is built; the SQAP's review schedule shows when each module is *inspected*.

**(3) Mutual Reference.** The SQAP references the SPMP for project context and is itself referenced by the SPMP as the controlling quality document. They share common identifiers (project ID, phase gates) and a common revision cycle.

**(4) Joint Governance.** The SQA Manager and Project Manager must jointly approve both plans, ensuring alignment on scope, schedule, and quality targets.

**Why the SQAP is a Supporting Sub-Plan:**

- **Specialisation** — it focuses exclusively on quality, allowing deeper treatment than the SPMP can afford.
- **Traceability** — every review, audit, and test in the SQAP can be traced back to a milestone in the SPMP.
- **Independence** — the SQAP enables the QA function to operate with *organisational independence* from the development team, as mandated by IEEE 730.

**Conclusion.** The SQA Plan is the **quality assurance arm** of the Project Plan — they are two views of the same project, with the SPMP asking *what and when* and the SQAP asking *how well*.

---

#### (b) Explain the concept of Cost of Quality (CoQ). List its four components with examples. A project has the following expenditure: Training ₹ 5 L, Test Execution ₹ 8 L, Pre-release Rework ₹ 3 L, Post-release Patches ₹ 12 L. Compute total CoQ and its percentage of the ₹ 50 L project budget. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Cost of Quality (CoQ).** CoQ is the total cost incurred to ensure and improve software quality plus the cost of failures resulting from lack of quality. It is a key metric tracked in both the SPMP (budget section) and the SQAP (process improvement section).

**Four Components of CoQ:**

| Component | Meaning | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Prevention Cost ($C_P$)** | Cost of preventing defects | Training, process improvement, standards definition |
| **Appraisal Cost ($C_A$)** | Cost of detecting defects | Test execution, inspections, audits |
| **Internal Failure Cost ($C_{IF}$)** | Cost of failures found *before* release | Rework, re-test, schedule slip due to defects |
| **External Failure Cost ($C_{EF}$)** | Cost of failures found *after* release | Patches, warranty claims, customer penalties, lost reputation |

**Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Classification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P      &= 5 \text{ L} \quad \text{(Training)} \\
C_A      &= 8 \text{ L} \quad \text{(Test Execution)} \\
C_{IF}   &= 3 \text{ L} \quad \text{(Pre-release Rework)} \\
C_{EF}   &= 12 \text{ L} \quad \text{(Post-release Patches)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total CoQ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF} \\
          &= 5 + 8 + 3 + 12 \\
          &= 28 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — CoQ Percentage of Budget:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,CoQ &= \frac{C_{total}}{B_{project}} \times 100 \\
        &= \frac{28}{50} \times 100 \\
        &= 56\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation:**

A CoQ of 56% is **alarmingly high** — the industry benchmark is 15–25%. The dominant contributor is **External Failure (₹ 12 L)**, indicating that prevention and appraisal activities are inadequate. **Recommendation:** Increase $C_P$ by 60% (training + better design reviews) to drive down $C_{EF}$ in subsequent releases, targeting a CoQ below 30%.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Listing only three components of CoQ (Prevention, Appraisal, Failure) instead of the correct four (with **internal vs external failure separated**) loses 1 mark. The four-way split is the IEEE standard decomposition.

---

## ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones on this Topic:**
> 1. Forgetting to **cite the IEEE standard number** alongside the plan name (loses 1 mark per question).
> 2. Confusing the **SQA Plan with the Test Plan** — the Test Plan is just *one section* (SQAP § 7) of the larger SQAP.
> 3. Writing **"QA Plan"** instead of **"SQA Plan"** or **"SQAP"** — always use the full acronym.
> 4. Listing only **three components of CoQ** (omitting internal vs external failure separation).
> 5. Not drawing the **hierarchical nesting** between SPMP and SQAP — examiners award +2 marks for visual diagrams.
> 6. Treating SQA as a *phase at the end* instead of an *activity overlapping every phase*.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **SQA Plan = IEEE Std 730.** Project Plan (SPMP) = **IEEE Std 1058.** Always cite the standard.
- The SQA Plan is a **supporting sub-plan** of the Project Plan (SPMP § 5).
- SQA Plan focuses on **product + process quality**; SPMP focuses on **scope, schedule, budget**.
- IEEE 730 mandates **11 sections**; deep coverage of **5–6 sections** is sufficient for a 14-mark answer.
- The SQA Plan owner is the **SQA Manager**; SPMP owner is the **Project Manager**.
- Cost of Quality = $C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ (four components, not three).
- Industry CoQ benchmark is **15–25%** of project budget; >30% indicates process weakness.
- Test coverage benchmarks: $C_{stmt} \geq 90\%$, $C_{branch} \geq 85\%$, $C_{req} = 100\%$.
- Review effectiveness benchmark: $E_{review} \geq 85\%$ for formal inspections.
- SQA effort allocation ≈ **$0.10 \cdot T_{total}$** of project schedule.
- Every defect must follow a **formal workflow**: New → Assigned → In Progress → Verified → Closed.
- **Records retention** in regulated industries: typically 5–7 years minimum.
- **SQA is a parallel activity**, not an end-of-pipeline gate — it overlaps every life-cycle phase.
- For 14-mark answers, always draw at least **one diagram** (relationship / nesting / RACI) — it fetches 1–2 bonus marks.
- Audit types in SQA: **product audit** (are we building it right?) + **process audit** (are we following the process?).
- Documentation discipline: the SQAP itself is a **controlled document** with version, approver, and revision history.
- **Common confusion:** SQAP vs. Test Plan vs. CM Plan — keep them distinct:
  - *SQAP* = umbrella for *all* quality activities.
  - *Test Plan* = just testing.
  - *CM Plan* = just configuration and version control.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost of Software Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cost of Software Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of Software Quality (CoSQ)** is the total financial expenditure incurred by a software organization across the entire software development life cycle (SDLC) to ensure that the delivered product conforms to defined quality standards and to remediate deficiencies caused by non-conformance. Mathematically, it is the sum of **Prevention Costs**, **Appraisal Costs**, **Internal Failure Costs**, and **External Failure Costs**.

According to the **IEEE Standard 1028** and **ISO/IEC 25010** quality framework as adopted in the KTU 2024 Scheme SQA syllabus, software quality cost is not a single line item — it is a structured **cost-accounting taxonomy** that allows engineering managers to translate abstract quality attributes (reliability, maintainability, usability) into measurable monetary units.

The most widely cited definitions in software engineering literature come from three quality pioneers:

- **Philip B. Crosby** (1979) – Author of *"Quality Is Free"*. Defined quality cost as *"the price of non-conformance"* and *"the price of conformance"*.
- **Joseph M. Juran** (1951) – Defined the optimal cost of quality as the point where the cost of preventing defects equals the cost of producing them.
- **Armand V. Feigenbaum** (1956) – Introduced the **Total Quality Control** concept, where quality cost is classified into *control costs* and *failure costs of control*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Reference**: This topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO2** of PEITT752 — *"Apply software quality metrics, cost models, and measurement frameworks to evaluate quality assurance investments."* Bloom's Cognitive Level: **Apply / Analyze (Level 3–4)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Real-World Analogy

Imagine you own a **car manufacturing factory** in Kochi producing 10,000 cars per year. You have two choices:

**Option A — "Cheap at the Factory Gate"**: You skip welding inspections, skip painting-quality checks, and skip supplier audits. Each car saves ₹500 in inspection cost. But 8% of cars leave the factory with hidden defects — brake issues, paint bubbles, engine misalignment. Customers return them. You pay ₹40,000 per repair, plus legal compensation, brand damage, and warranty claims.

**Option B — "Quality at the Source"**: You spend ₹1,500 per car on robotic welding inspection, paint sensors, supplier audits, and trained engineers. Only 0.5% of cars have defects. Warranty costs drop to a tiny fraction.

### **Net Outcome**:
- Option A appears cheaper at the factory but costs **₹320 lakhs more** annually in failure-related expenses.
- Option B appears expensive upfront but saves money overall.

> **This is precisely what Cost of Software Quality captures** — the hidden economics behind every quality assurance decision a software team makes. The same principle applies whether you are running a code review, investing in unit testing, or fixing a bug discovered after release.

## 1.3 Why Cost of Quality Matters in Software Engineering

In software, **defects are disproportionately expensive the later they are discovered**. Industry data (IBM Systems Sciences Institute, later replicated by NIST) shows:

| Stage of Defect Detection | Relative Cost to Fix |
|:---|:---:|
| Requirements/Design | **1×** (baseline) |
| Coding (Unit Test) | **5×** |
| Integration Testing | **10×** |
| System Testing | **15×** |
| Post-Release / Production | **30× – 100×** |

This exponential escalation is the **economic justification** for studying cost of software quality — it tells managers exactly *where to invest* their QA budget to maximize ROI.

## 1.4 Industry-Standard Metrics Used in CoSQ Reporting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are universally reported on a KTU SQA cost dashboard:
> - **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** = `Defects found before release / Total defects` × 100%
> - **Cost of Defect (CoD)** = `Total Quality Cost / Number of defects`
> - **Quality Cost Ratio (QCR)** = `Prevention + Appraisal / Internal Failure + External Failure`
> - **Yield** = `Defect-free units / Total units produced` × 100%
> - **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** — synonymous with failure cost component

---

> **Key Takeaway**: Cost of Software Quality is not just an accounting exercise — it is a **decision-making framework** that quantifies the financial impact of quality decisions, enabling managers to allocate QA resources optimally.
