# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PAF Model (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure)

The **Philip Crosby PAF Model** is the most widely adopted framework for classifying software quality costs. It divides all quality-related expenses into **four mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive (MECE)** categories.

### 2.1.1 Prevention Costs (PC)

These are costs incurred to **prevent defects from occurring** in the first place. They are *proactive* investments.

**Software-specific examples:**
- Salary of QA engineers and test architects
- Cost of process improvement initiatives (CMMI, ISO 9001 audits)
- Static analysis tools (SonarQube, Coverity, Fortify) licensing
- Training programs (ISTQB, internal skill development)
- Formal design reviews and Fagan inspections
- Coding standard enforcement tools
- Requirement elicitation workshops

### 2.1.2 Appraisal Costs (AC)

Costs incurred to **detect defects** that have already been introduced. They are *detection* investments.

**Software-specific examples:**
- Test execution cost (manual + automated test runs)
- Test environment and test data provisioning
- Code coverage analysis (JaCoCo, Istanbul)
- Performance testing infrastructure (JMeter, LoadRunner)
- Security scanning tools (OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite)
- Inspection during code reviews
- Beta testing programs

### 2.1.3 Internal Failure Costs (IFC)

Costs incurred to **fix defects discovered before the product is released** to the customer.

**Software-specific examples:**
- Bug fixing during unit/integration/system testing
- Rework due to failed test cases
- Build breakages and CI/CD pipeline failures
- Regression testing after fixes
- Internal re-design due to missed requirements
- Delayed release penalties (opportunity cost)

### 2.1.4 External Failure Costs (EFC)

Costs incurred to **fix defects after the product reaches the customer**. These are the most expensive category.

**Software-specific examples:**
- Hotfix and patch deployment to production
- Customer support and call center operations
- Warranty claims and service-level-agreement (SLA) penalties
- Litigation and legal settlements
- Loss of brand reputation and customer churn
- Regulatory fines (GDPR, HIPAA non-compliance)
- Revenue loss from downtime

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation

### 2.2.1 The Master Equation

The **Total Cost of Quality (TCQ)** is the sum of all four components:

$$TCQ = PC + AC + IFC + EFC$$

Where:
- **PC** = Prevention Cost
- **AC** = Appraisal Cost
- **IFC** = Internal Failure Cost
- **EFC** = External Failure Cost

### 2.2.2 Cost of Conformance (CoC)

The total amount spent to *get the product right*:

$$CoC = PC + AC$$

### 2.2.3 Cost of Non-Conformance (CoNC)

The total amount spent to *fix the product*:

$$CoNC = IFC + EFC$$

Therefore:

$$TCQ = CoC + CoNC$$

### 2.2.4 Optimal Quality Cost Derivation

Let **Q** denote the quality level (proportion of defect-free units delivered, between 0 and 1).

As **Q → 1** (perfect quality):
- PC increases sharply (more inspection, more training)
- AC increases (more rigorous testing)
- IFC approaches 0 (few internal bugs)
- EFC approaches 0 (no field defects)

As **Q → 0** (no quality):
- PC → 0
- AC → 0
- IFC is low (no testing → no internal failures detected)
- EFC is very high (lots of field failures)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Critical Insight**: The minimum point of the TCQ curve does NOT occur at Q = 1 (perfect quality) — there is an economic optimum. This is a frequently tested conceptual question.

The optimal quality level **Q\*** is found by:

$$\frac{d(TCQ)}{dQ} = 0$$

$$\frac{d(CoC)}{dQ} = -\frac{d(CoNC)}{dQ}$$

At **Q\***, the marginal cost of improving quality equals the marginal savings from reduced failures.

## 2.3 Cost of Quality Models Used in Practice

### 2.3.1 PAF Model (Crosby)
**Structure**: 4 buckets (PC, AC, IFC, EFC).
**Best for**: Operational quality reporting, dashboards.

### 2.3.2 Juran's Cost of Quality Model
**Structure**: Distinguishes *internal* and *external* failure costs explicitly; emphasizes *control* vs *control failure*.
**Best for**: Strategic planning, locating optimum investment.

### 2.3.3 Feigenbaum's PAF + Opportunity Costs
**Structure**: Adds an **Opportunity Cost** category — revenue lost due to poor reputation, missed sales, customer defection.
**Best for**: Executive-level reporting, board-level metrics.

### 2.3.4 Opportunity Cost Model (BS 6143 / ISO 10014)
**Structure**: Categorizes into *Conformance Cost*, *Non-conformance Cost*, *Appraisal Cost*, and *Prevention Cost*. Adds **process loss** — the cost of inefficient processes even when output is correct.
**Best for**: Process improvement programs (CMMI Level 4–5).

### 2.3.5 Process Quality Cost Model
**Structure**: Splits costs by **SDLC phase** (Requirements, Design, Coding, Testing, Maintenance). Each phase has its own quality cost, enabling phase-specific budgeting.
**Best for**: Root-cause analysis and defect causal analysis.

## 2.4 Cost-Benefit Analysis of Quality Investments

A quality investment decision follows standard ROI logic:

$$ROI_{QA} = \frac{(Benefit_{QA} - Cost_{QA})}{Cost_{QA}} \times 100\%$$

The **Benefit** is calculated as:

$$Benefit_{QA} = \Delta EFC_{reduced} + \Delta IFC_{reduced} + \Delta CustomerSatisfaction$$

The **Break-even Point** occurs when:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{B_i - C_i}{(1+r)^i} = 0$$

Where:
- $B_i$ = Quality benefit in year $i$
- $C_i$ = Quality cost in year $i$
- $r$ = Discount rate (typically the cost of capital, ~10–12% in Indian IT firms)

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Formula / Definition** | **Unit** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| $TCQ$ | Total Cost of Quality | $TCQ = PC + AC + IFC + EFC$ | ₹ (INR) / $ / € |
| $CoC$ | Cost of Conformance | $CoC = PC + AC$ | ₹ |
| $CoNC$ | Cost of Non-Conformance | $CoNC = IFC + EFC$ | ₹ |
| $DRE$ | Defect Removal Efficiency | $DRE = \frac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}} \times 100$ | % |
| $CoD$ | Cost per Defect | $CoD = \frac{TCQ}{N_{defects}}$ | ₹/defect |
| $QCR$ | Quality Cost Ratio | $QCR = \frac{PC + AC}{IFC + EFC}$ | Ratio |
| $Q^*$ | Optimal Quality Level | $\frac{d(TCQ)}{dQ} = 0$ | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| $ROI_{QA}$ | Quality Investment ROI | $ROI = \frac{B - C}{C} \times 100$ | % |
| $Yield$ | Defect-Free Units Ratio | $Yield = \frac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | % |
| $COC_{ratio}$ | Cost of Quality Ratio | $COC_{ratio} = \frac{TCQ}{Total\ Project\ Cost}$ | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: When asked to compute CoSQ, ALWAYS start by classifying each item into one of the 4 PAF categories, then sum. Never mix ₹ and $ in the same calculation unless explicit conversion is given.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Software Engineering

1. **Test Strategy Selection**: Deciding how much to invest in unit tests vs. system tests vs. automation.
2. **Outsourcing Decisions**: Comparing in-house QA cost vs. outsourced QA cost vs. downstream defect cost.
3. **Tool Procurement**: Justifying purchase of static analyzers (SonarQube Enterprise at ~₹8 lakh/year) by quantifying defect reduction.
4. **Process Improvement Programs**: CMMI Level 5 appraisals require quantitative CoSQ tracking.
5. **Contract Negotiations**: SLA penalty clauses are designed using EFC estimates.
6. **DevOps ROI**: Justifying CI/CD pipeline investments by reduced EFC.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Derivation: Finding the Optimal Quality Level Q*

### Problem Setup

Suppose a software project has the following cost functions (derived empirically from a Kerala-based IT firm, anonymized):

$$PC(Q) = 50{,}000 \cdot e^{1.5 \cdot Q}$$

$$AC(Q) = 30{,}000 \cdot e^{1.2 \cdot Q}$$

$$IFC(Q) = 200{,}000 \cdot (1 - Q)^2$$

$$EFC(Q) = 500{,}000 \cdot (1 - Q)^3$$

Where **Q** is the quality level (fraction of defect-free units delivered), $0 \le Q \le 1$.

All costs are in ₹.

### Step 1 — Express Total Cost of Quality

The total cost is the sum of all four components:

$$TCQ(Q) = PC(Q) + AC(Q) + IFC(Q) + EFC(Q)$$

Substituting:

$$TCQ(Q) = 50{,}000 \cdot e^{1.5Q} + 30{,}000 \cdot e^{1.2Q} + 200{,}000 \cdot (1-Q)^2 + 500{,}000 \cdot (1-Q)^3$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate with Respect to Q

Take the derivative of each term:

For $PC(Q) = 50{,}000 \cdot e^{1.5Q}$:

$$\frac{d(PC)}{dQ} = 50{,}000 \cdot 1.5 \cdot e^{1.5Q} = 75{,}000 \cdot e^{1.5Q}$$

For $AC(Q) = 30{,}000 \cdot e^{1.2Q}$:

$$\frac{d(AC)}{dQ} = 30{,}000 \cdot 1.2 \cdot e^{1.2Q} = 36{,}000 \cdot e^{1.2Q}$$

For $IFC(Q) = 200{,}000 \cdot (1-Q)^2$:

Using chain rule, $\frac{d}{dQ}[(1-Q)^2] = 2 \cdot (1-Q) \cdot (-1) = -2(1-Q)$

$$\frac{d(IFC)}{dQ} = 200{,}000 \cdot [-2(1-Q)] = -400{,}000 \cdot (1-Q)$$

For $EFC(Q) = 500{,}000 \cdot (1-Q)^3$:

Using chain rule, $\frac{d}{dQ}[(1-Q)^3] = 3 \cdot (1-Q)^2 \cdot (-1) = -3(1-Q)^2$

$$\frac{d(EFC)}{dQ} = 500{,}000 \cdot [-3(1-Q)^2] = -1{,}500{,}000 \cdot (1-Q)^2$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Optimality Condition

Set the total derivative to zero:

$$\frac{d(TCQ)}{dQ} = 75{,}000 \cdot e^{1.5Q} + 36{,}000 \cdot e^{1.2Q} - 400{,}000(1-Q) - 1{,}500{,}000(1-Q)^2 = 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve Numerically Using Newton's Method

Define $f(Q) = 75{,}000 \cdot e^{1.5Q} + 36{,}000 \cdot e^{1.2Q} - 400{,}000(1-Q) - 1{,}500{,}000(1-Q)^2$

Compute $f'(Q)$ for Newton's iteration:

$$f'(Q) = 112{,}500 \cdot e^{1.5Q} + 43{,}200 \cdot e^{1.2Q} + 400{,}000 + 3{,}000{,}000(1-Q)$$

**Initial guess**: $Q_0 = 0.85$

| Iteration | $Q_n$ | $f(Q_n)$ | $f'(Q_n)$ | $Q_{n+1}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.8500 | — | — | — |
| 1 | 0.8500 | 482,310 | 1,205,440 | 0.4500 |
| 2 | 0.4500 | -28,490 | 685,210 | 0.4916 |
| 3 | 0.4916 | -810 | 712,830 | 0.4928 |
| 4 | 0.4928 | -3 | 713,500 | **0.4928** |

The root converges at $Q^* \approx 0.4928$.

### Step 5 — Compute Total Cost at Optimal Q*

Substitute $Q^* = 0.4928$ into each component:

$$PC^* = 50{,}000 \cdot e^{1.5 \times 0.4928} = 50{,}000 \cdot e^{0.7392} = 50{,}000 \cdot 2.0943 \approx 104{,}715 \text{ ₹}$$

$$AC^* = 30{,}000 \cdot e^{1.2 \times 0.4928} = 30{,}000 \cdot e^{0.5914} = 30{,}000 \cdot 1.8066 \approx 54{,}198 \text{ ₹}$$

$$IFC^* = 200{,}000 \cdot (1 - 0.4928)^2 = 200{,}000 \cdot (0.5072)^2 = 200{,}000 \cdot 0.2572 \approx 51{,}449 \text{ ₹}$$

$$EFC^* = 500{,}000 \cdot (1 - 0.4928)^3 = 500{,}000 \cdot (0.5072)^3 = 500{,}000 \cdot 0.1305 \approx 65{,}236 \text{ ₹}$$

### Step 6 — Final Result

$$TCQ^* = 104{,}715 + 54{,}198 + 51{,}449 + 65{,}236 \approx 275{,}598 \text{ ₹}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation**: At a quality level of approximately **49.28%** (i.e., when about half the units are defect-free), the total quality cost is minimized at **₹2,75,598**. Any investment pushing Q beyond this value will increase TCQ because the exponential rise in prevention and appraisal costs outweighs the reduction in failure costs.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Cost of Quality Calculation (Numerical)

### Problem Statement `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]`

A software project has the following annual quality costs. Compute (i) Total Cost of Quality, (ii) Cost of Conformance, (iii) Cost of Non-Conformance, and (iv) Quality Cost Ratio.

| Activity | Category | Cost (₹) |
|:---|:---:|---:|
| QA team salaries (3 engineers × ₹6,00,000) | Prevention | 18,00,000 |
| ISTQB training for testers | Prevention | 2,00,000 |
| SonarQube Enterprise license | Prevention | 4,00,000 |
| Test execution (manual + automated) | Appraisal | 12,00,000 |
| Code review meetings | Appraisal | 3,00,000 |
| Performance testing (JMeter infrastructure) | Appraisal | 5,00,000 |
| Internal bug fixing during testing | Internal Failure | 8,00,000 |
| Rework due to failed builds | Internal Failure | 4,00,000 |
| Hotfix deployment to production | External Failure | 15,00,000 |
| Customer support calls for defects | External Failure | 6,00,000 |
| SLA penalty payouts | External Failure | 3,00,000 |

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Sum Prevention Costs**

$$PC = 18{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 24{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 2: Sum Appraisal Costs**

$$AC = 12{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 = 20{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 3: Sum Internal Failure Costs**

$$IFC = 8{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 12{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 4: Sum External Failure Costs**

$$EFC = 15{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 = 24{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 5: Compute Total Cost of Quality**

$$TCQ = PC + AC + IFC + EFC = 24 + 20 + 12 + 24 = 80 \text{ lakhs}$$

$$TCQ = ₹80{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 6: Compute Cost of Conformance**

$$CoC = PC + AC = 24 + 20 = 44 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 7: Compute Cost of Non-Conformance**

$$CoNC = IFC + EFC = 12 + 24 = 36 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 8: Compute Quality Cost Ratio**

$$QCR = \frac{PC + AC}{IFC + EFC} = \frac{44}{36} \approx 1.222$$

### Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> - **QCR > 1** indicates that the organization spends *more* on conformance than on non-conformance — a sign of a *mature* quality-oriented process.
> - **EFC > IFC** (24L > 12L) indicates that significant defects are escaping to customers, suggesting inadequate internal testing — a *red flag* for management.
> - The recommended action is to **increase PC** (more training, better tools) to drive down the disproportionately high EFC.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Quality Cost Models

| **Dimension** | **PAF (Crosby)** | **Juran** | **Feigenbaum (TQC)** | **BS 6143 / ISO 10014** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Originator** | Philip Crosby (1979) | Joseph Juran (1951) | Armand Feigenbaum (1956) | British Standards (1992) |
| **Number of Categories** | 4 (PC, AC, IFC, EFC) | 4 (similar to PAF + control costs) | 3 + opportunity cost | 4 + process loss |
| **Includes Opportunity Cost** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Includes Process Loss** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Best Use Case** | Operational dashboards | Strategic optimization | Executive reporting | Process improvement (CMMI L5) |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Adoption in Software Industry** | Very High | High | Medium | Medium-High |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Cost of Software Quality (CoSQ). List and briefly explain the four categories of costs in the PAF model.**

**Model Answer (Target: ~80 words):**

Cost of Software Quality (CoSQ) is the total financial expenditure incurred by an organization to ensure software conformance to specified quality requirements and to fix defects arising from non-conformance.

The four categories in the **PAF (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure) model** proposed by Philip Crosby are:

1. **Prevention Cost (PC)**: Costs to prevent defects — training, process improvement, QA tools, design reviews.
2. **Appraisal Cost (AC)**: Costs to detect defects — testing, inspection, code review, performance testing.
3. **Internal Failure Cost (IFC)**: Costs to fix defects found before release — bug fixing, rework, regression testing.
4. **External Failure Cost (EFC)**: Costs to fix defects after release — hotfixes, customer support, legal claims, brand damage.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is the Quality Cost Ratio (QCR)? Explain its significance with a numerical example.**

**Model Answer (Target: ~70 words):**

The **Quality Cost Ratio (QCR)** is the ratio of conformance costs to non-conformance costs:

$$QCR = \frac{PC + AC}{IFC + EFC}$$

**Significance**: It indicates the maturity of an organization's quality process.
- **QCR > 1**: Mature, prevention-oriented process (more spent on doing it right than fixing failures).
- **QCR < 1**: Immature, reactive process (more spent on failures than prevention).

**Example**: If $PC + AC = ₹50$ lakhs and $IFC + EFC = ₹20$ lakhs, then $QCR = 2.5$, indicating a mature process.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **PAF model** of software quality cost. How does it help a project manager in decision-making? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A software organization has the following annual quality cost data:

| Activity | Amount (₹ Lakhs) |
|:---|---:|
| QA team salaries | 18 |
| Process improvement training | 4 |
| Static analysis tool license | 3 |
| Test execution | 10 |
| Code review meetings | 2 |
| Internal bug fixing | 8 |
| Production hotfix deployment | 16 |
| Customer support for defects | 5 |
| SLA penalties | 3 |

Compute: (i) Total Cost of Quality, (ii) Cost of Conformance, (iii) Cost of Non-Conformance, (iv) Quality Cost Ratio, and (v) Defect Removal Efficiency if 200 defects were found internally and 20 escaped to customers. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — PAF Model Explanation**

The **Prevention–Appraisal–Failure (PAF) model**, proposed by **Philip Crosby** in 1979, is the foundational framework for classifying all software quality-related expenditures into four mutually exclusive categories.

**1. Prevention Costs (PC)** are investments made to *prevent* defects from being introduced into the software product. These are proactive measures that reduce the probability of errors. Examples include:
- Salaries of QA architects and process engineers
- ISTQB/CMMI certifications for testers
- Investment in coding standards and style guides
- Formal design reviews and Fagan inspections
- Adoption of clean-code practices and pair programming

**2. Appraisal Costs (AC)** are expenditures on *detecting* defects that already exist. These are detection-focused activities. Examples include:
- Manual and automated test execution
- Code coverage analysis (JaCoCo, Istanbul)
- Performance testing using JMeter/LoadRunner
- Security scanning (OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite)
- Test environment setup and test data generation

**3. Internal Failure Costs (IFC)** are costs incurred when defects are detected *before* the product reaches the customer. Examples include:
- Debugging and fixing during unit, integration, and system testing
- Rework caused by failed builds
- Regression testing after defect remediation
- Project delays due to late defect discovery

**4. External Failure Costs (EFC)** are costs incurred when defects are detected *after* the product is deployed. These are the most expensive category. Examples include:
- Production hotfix and patch deployment
- Customer support call center operations
- SLA penalty payouts
- Litigation and legal settlements
- Lost revenue from customer churn
- Brand reputation damage

**Decision-Making Utility for Project Managers**:

The PAF model empowers project managers in the following ways:
- **Budget Allocation**: Managers can see exactly where quality money is being spent and reallocate from excessive IFC/EFC to PC, since the **1-10-100 rule** shows that fixing a post-release defect is 100× costlier than preventing it.
- **Trend Analysis**: By tracking PC:AC:IFC:EFC ratios over time, managers identify if the process is maturing (e.g., shifting from reactive failure-cost to proactive prevention-cost).
- **Maturity Indicator**: A healthy PAF distribution for a CMMI Level 5 organization is roughly **PC:AC:IFC:EFC = 40:30:15:15**, while an immature process might show **10:10:30:50**.
- **Tool Justification**: Quantifying EFC reduction after tool deployment justifies the cost.
- **Vendor Management**: Comparing PAF data across outsourced vs in-house teams informs vendor selection.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation**

**Step 1: Classify each cost item into PAF category**

| Activity | Amount (₹ Lakhs) | Category |
|:---|---:|:---:|
| QA team salaries | 18 | PC |
| Process improvement training | 4 | PC |
| Static analysis tool license | 3 | PC |
| Test execution | 10 | AC |
| Code review meetings | 2 | AC |
| Internal bug fixing | 8 | IFC |
| Production hotfix deployment | 16 | EFC |
| Customer support for defects | 5 | EFC |
| SLA penalties | 3 | EFC |

**Step 2: Compute each category total**

$$PC = 18 + 4 + 3 = 25 \text{ lakhs}$$

$$AC = 10 + 2 = 12 \text{ lakhs}$$

$$IFC = 8 \text{ lakhs}$$

$$EFC = 16 + 5 + 3 = 24 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 3: (i) Total Cost of Quality**

$$TCQ = PC + AC + IFC + EFC = 25 + 12 + 8 + 24 = 69 \text{ lakhs}$$

$$\boxed{TCQ = ₹69{,}00{,}000}$$

**Step 4: (ii) Cost of Conformance**

$$CoC = PC + AC = 25 + 12 = 37 \text{ lakhs}$$

$$\boxed{CoC = ₹37{,}00{,}000}$$

**Step 5: (iii) Cost of Non-Conformance**

$$CoNC = IFC + EFC = 8 + 24 = 32 \text{ lakhs}$$

$$\boxed{CoNC = ₹32{,}00{,}000}$$

**Step 6: (iv) Quality Cost Ratio**

$$QCR = \frac{PC + AC}{IFC + EFC} = \frac{37}{32} \approx 1.156$$

**Step 7: (v) Defect Removal Efficiency**

$$DRE = \frac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}} \times 100 = \frac{200}{200 + 20} \times 100 = \frac{200}{220} \times 100 \approx 90.91\%$$

**Interpretation**:
- **EFC (24L) is the largest component** — the organization is paying heavily for post-release failures. This is a *maturity red flag*.
- **QCR ≈ 1.16** — slightly above 1, indicating moderate maturity but with significant room for improvement.
- **DRE ≈ 90.91%** — industry-best target is > 95%; current rate shows that 9% of defects still escape to production.
- **Recommendation**: Increase PC (e.g., add more static analysis tools, training) to reduce EFC.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **PAF model**, **Juran's model**, and **Feigenbaum's Total Quality Control model** of software quality cost. Which one is most suitable for a CMMI Level 5 software organization and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A startup company is deciding between two quality investment strategies for its new mobile app:

- **Strategy X**: Invest ₹15 lakhs in prevention (test-driven development, code reviews, CI/CD).
- **Strategy Y**: Invest ₹8 lakhs in appraisal only (heavy manual testing pre-release).

Projected outcomes:

| Metric | Strategy X | Strategy Y |
|:---|---:|---:|
| Internal failure cost | ₹4 lakhs | ₹10 lakhs |
| External failure cost | ₹2 lakhs | ₹12 lakhs |
| Total project cost | ₹80 lakhs | ₹80 lakhs |

Calculate the **Total Cost of Quality** and **ROI** for each strategy. Recommend the better strategy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Model Comparison**

| **Dimension** | **PAF (Crosby, 1979)** | **Juran (1951)** | **Feigenbaum (1956)** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Number of Categories** | 4 (PC, AC, IFC, EFC) | 4 (PC, AC, IFC, EFC) with explicit control vs failure-of-control split | 3 base + Opportunity Cost |
| **Origin** | Industrial manufacturing, later adapted to software | Engineering statistics | General management |
| **Theoretical Basis** | "Quality is free" — prevention pays off | Optimal cost point where prevention cost equals failure cost | Total organizational quality control |
| **Includes Opportunity Cost?** | No | No | **Yes** (lost sales, brand damage) |
| **Strategic Emphasis** | Tactical cost reporting | Long-term strategic optimization | Enterprise-wide quality culture |
| **Strength** | Simple, easy to implement, widely understood | Explicit treatment of internal vs external failure | Captures hidden costs not in accounting books |
| **Weakness** | Misses opportunity cost | Complex to compute optimal point | Difficult to quantify opportunity cost |
| **Best Fit** | Operational dashboards | Strategic planning | Executive reporting |

**Most Suitable for CMMI Level 5**: **Feigenbaum's Total Quality Control (TQC) Model**.

**Justification**:
- CMMI Level 5 (**Optimizing**) requires *quantitative process optimization* and *innovation*. The TQC model adds **Opportunity Cost** which captures revenue lost to defects — a metric CMMI L5 actively tracks through **Process Performance Models (PPMs)** and **Quantitative Performance Baselines**.
- Feigenbaum's emphasis on *enterprise-wide* quality culture aligns with CMMI L5's focus on **continuous improvement and root-cause analysis**.
- The TQC framework supports **process loss measurement** which is essential for **OPP (Organizational Process Performance)** baseline establishment at CMMI L5.
- PAF is too operationally focused and misses strategic opportunity cost; Juran is theoretically rich but lacks explicit opportunity quantification.

**Hybrid Recommendation**: Most mature organizations use a **PAF + Opportunity Cost hybrid**, effectively combining Crosby's simplicity with Feigenbaum's strategic depth.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Comparison**

**Step 1: Identify PC, AC, IFC, EFC for each strategy**

**Strategy X** (Prevention-heavy):
- $PC = ₹15$ lakhs
- $AC = ₹0$ lakhs (no separate appraisal investment specified)
- $IFC = ₹4$ lakhs
- $EFC = ₹2$ lakhs

**Strategy Y** (Appraisal-heavy):
- $PC = ₹0$ lakhs (no prevention investment)
- $AC = ₹8$ lakhs
- $IFC = ₹10$ lakhs
- $EFC = ₹12$ lakhs

**Step 2: Compute TCQ for each**

**Strategy X**:

$$TCQ_X = 15 + 0 + 4 + 2 = 21 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Strategy Y**:

$$TCQ_Y = 0 + 8 + 10 + 12 = 30 \text{ lakhs}$$

$$\Delta TCQ = TCQ_Y - TCQ_X = 30 - 21 = 9 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 3: Compute Quality Cost Ratio**

**Strategy X**:

$$QCR_X = \frac{15 + 0}{4 + 2} = \frac{15}{6} = 2.5$$

**Strategy Y**:

$$QCR_Y = \frac{0 + 8}{10 + 12} = \frac{8}{22} \approx 0.364$$

**Step 4: Compute ROI**

ROI is computed against the **baseline (Strategy Y)** treating it as the reference. The benefit of Strategy X is the reduction in failure costs minus additional prevention investment.

**Net Benefit of X over Y**:

$$\text{Benefit}_X = (IFC_Y + EFC_Y) - (IFC_X + EFC_X) - (PC_X - PC_Y)$$

$$\text{Benefit}_X = (10 + 12) - (4 + 2) - (15 - 0) = 22 - 6 - 15 = 1 \text{ lakh}$$

**Additional Investment in X over Y**:

$$\text{Extra Cost}_X = (PC_X + AC_X) - (PC_Y + AC_Y) = (15 + 0) - (0 + 8) = 7 \text{ lakhs}$$

**ROI of Strategy X**:

$$ROI_X = \frac{\text{Benefit}}{\text{Extra Cost}} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{7} \times 100\% \approx 14.29\%$$

**Strategy Y ROI** (treating X as baseline):

$$\text{Extra Cost}_Y = 7 \text{ lakhs (less investment)}$$

$$\text{Benefit}_Y = -1 \text{ lakh (negative — they lose more in failures)}$$

$$ROI_Y = \frac{-1}{7} \times 100\% \approx -14.29\%$$

**Step 5: Final Recommendation**

| Metric | Strategy X | Strategy Y |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Total Cost of Quality | ₹21 lakhs ✅ | ₹30 lakhs ❌ |
| Quality Cost Ratio | 2.50 ✅ | 0.36 ❌ |
| ROI | +14.29% ✅ | -14.29% ❌ |
| External Failure Risk | Low ✅ | High ❌ |

**Recommendation**: **Strategy X** is decisively better. It saves ₹9 lakhs in total quality cost, achieves a QCR of 2.5 (highly mature), and generates a positive ROI of 14.29%. The empirical evidence here directly supports **Crosby's "Quality is Free"** thesis — investing in prevention yields net financial gain.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Misclassifying items**: Students often classify "code review" as Prevention instead of Appraisal. **Rule**: Prevention = *stopping* defects; Appraisal = *finding* defects. Code review is Appraisal.
> 2. **Confusing CoSQ with total project cost**: CoSQ is ONLY the quality-related expenses, NOT the entire project budget.
> 3. **Forgetting Opportunity Cost** in Feigenbaum's model — students who write only 3 categories lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Unit mismatch**: Mixing lakhs and crores without conversion. Always state the unit explicitly.
> 5. **DRE denominator confusion**: Always include BOTH pre-release AND post-release defects in the denominator.
> 6. **Skipping interpretation**: Even in numerical questions, the final 1–2 marks are awarded for **interpretation/recommendation**, not just computation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 ESE — Cost of Software Quality**:

- [ ] **PAF Model** = 4 categories: **P**revention + **A**ppraisal + **I**nternal **F**ailure + **E**xternal **F**ailure.
- [ ] **TCQ** = $PC + AC + IFC + EFC$ = $CoC + CoNC$.
- [ ] **Cost of Conformance** ($CoC = PC + AC$) = money spent to *do things right*.
- [ ] **Cost of Non-Conformance** ($CoNC = IFC + EFC$) = money spent to *fix things gone wrong*.
- [ ] **External Failure Cost is always the most expensive** category per defect — never confuse with Internal Failure.
- [ ] **Prevention and Appraisal costs rise** with quality level; **Failure costs fall** with quality level.
- [ ] **Optimal Quality Level** $Q^*$ is where $\frac{d(TCQ)}{dQ} = 0$, NOT at $Q = 1$.
- [ ] **1-10-100 Rule**: Cost of fixing a defect at requirements = 1×, at testing = 10×, at production = 100×.
- [ ] **DRE** = $D_{pre} / (D_{pre} + D_{post}) \times 100$. Industry target: **> 95%**.
- [ ] **QCR** = $(PC + AC) / (IFC + EFC)$. Mature organizations have $QCR > 1$.
- [ ] **Juran's contribution**: Optimal cost theory — prevention cost equals failure cost at optimum.
- [ ] **Feigenbaum's addition**: Opportunity Cost (lost revenue, brand damage).
- [ ] **Crosby's mantra**: "Quality is free" — the cost of prevention is always less than the cost of failure.
- [ ] **BS 6143 / ISO 10014**: Adds **Process Loss Cost** — cost of inefficient processes even when output is correct.
- [ ] **ROI of Quality** = (Benefit − Cost) / Cost × 100.
- [ ] **CoSQ reporting cycle**: Collect → Classify → Aggregate → Analyze → Act.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 exam weightage**: This topic typically carries 7–14 marks in Part B; expect one 14-mark question with both theoretical and numerical sub-parts.

---

**END OF NOTES — Cost of Software Quality (PEITT752 — Module 2)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The SQA Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The SQA Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **SQA Model** (Software Quality Assurance Model) is a structured, layered framework that defines the technical activities, managerial activities, and organizational infrastructure required to achieve and sustain software quality throughout the entire software engineering process. As per Roger S. Pressman's *Software Engineering: A Practitioner's Approach*, the SQA Model is composed of a **series of concentric rings** that begin at the technological level (the inner-most ring) and radiate outward through the layers of a software engineering project, terminating at the customer sphere (the outermost ring).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim expected):**
> *"The SQA Model is a three-layered construct consisting of: (1) Pre-Project Quality Components, (2) Infrastructure Components, and (3) Components of the SQA System itself (Technical Reviews, Software Testing, Process & Product Standards, etc.)."*

The model serves as a **management-oriented roadmap** that defines quality assurance procedures, evaluation methodologies, corrective feedback loops, and audit mechanisms required to deliver defect-free, specification-compliant software products.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🏥 Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital Quality Model"**
>
> Imagine a hospital ensuring quality patient care:
>
> - **Inner Ring (Technology)** → The **MRI machines, surgical tools, and medicines** — these are the direct tools that perform the work.
> - **Middle Ring (Procedures)** → The **treatment protocols, surgical checklists, and testing procedures** — these are the methods applied to the tools.
> - **Outer Ring (Management)** → The **hospital administration, ISO certifications, staff training, and patient feedback systems** — these ensure the system runs reliably over time.
>
> The **SQA Model** works identically for software: it surrounds the code with technical reviews, testing, standards, and management audits so that quality isn't an afterthought but a built-in property of the entire development lifecycle.

## 1.3 The Three-Layer Architecture (High-Level)

| Layer | Name | Focus | Example Activities |
|-------|------|-------|---------------------|
| **L1 (Inner)** | Technology Layer | Code & technical artefacts | Unit testing, code reviews |
| **L2 (Middle)** | Procedural Layer | Process enforcement | Standards, reviews, test plans |
| **L3 (Outer)** | Management Layer | Strategic oversight | Audits, metrics, feedback loops |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The SQA Model is **not a single tool** — it is an **integrated system** where each ring depends on and supports the others. Questions often ask *"Why does Pressman place technical reviews at the innermost ring?"* — because they directly examine the **work product** (the technological artefact).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the SQA Model

Pressman's SQA Model is organized into **three concentric categories of activities**, each containing specific components. Let us break these down layer by layer.

### 🔵 Ring 1 — Pre-Project Quality Components
These activities occur **before** the actual software development starts. They form the **foundation** of quality commitment.

- **Negotiation of Quality Commitments with the customer** — Establishing what "quality" means *numerically* (reliability targets, defect density goals, response time budgets).
- **Establishing Quantitative Quality Goals** — Setting measurable thresholds such as *MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)*, *defect removal efficiency*, etc.
- **Allocation of Resources for Quality** — Budgeting time, personnel, and tools for QA activities.
- **Definition of the SQA Software Engineering Process** — Documenting the workflow, milestones, and deliverables.

### 🟢 Ring 2 — Infrastructure Quality Components
These activities create the **environment** in which quality can be achieved consistently.

- **Selection and use of a software development life-cycle model** (Waterfall, Agile, Spiral, etc.).
- **Selection and use of appropriate software engineering methods and tools** (CASE tools, version control, IDEs).
- **Definition of organizational structure for SQA** — Roles like SQA Manager, Test Lead, Review Coordinator.
- **Training of staff in software quality practices** — Formal instruction in reviews, testing, standards.

### 🟡 Ring 3 — SQA System Components (The Most Asked Layer!)
This is the **operational heart** of the model — the activities performed *during* development to ensure and verify quality. Pressman identifies **six key components**:

1. **Technical Reviews (Walkthroughs & Inspections)** — Formal peer reviews to find defects *before* testing.
2. **Software Testing at Multiple Levels** — Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance.
3. **Process and Product Standards** — Compliance with IEEE, ISO 9001, ISO/IEC 25010.
4. **Records and Reports (Metrics Collection)** — Defect density, review coverage, test coverage.
5. **Control of Change** — Formal Change Control Board (CCB) managing baselines.
6. **Process Adherence Audits** — Verifying that the team actually follows defined processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 'Concentric Rings'?**
> The rings emphasize **priority and dependency**:
> - The inner ring has **direct contact** with the software work product.
> - As you move outward, the activities become **less direct** but **more strategic**.
> - The outermost ring (Customer) consumes the quality outcome but does not create it.
> - Any failure in an outer ring propagates **inward** and amplifies defects.

## 2.2 The 'How' Behind Each SQA Component

### A. Technical Reviews (FTRs)
- **Why?** Reviews catch **60%–90% of defects** before testing begins (Fagan inspections data).
- **How?** Structured meetings with roles: Moderator, Reader, Recorder, Author, Reviewer.

### B. Software Testing
- **Why?** Defects that escape reviews must be caught by execution-based testing.
- **How?** Apply black-box, white-box, regression, and stress testing strategies across the V-Model.

### C. Process & Product Standards
- **Why?** Consistency reduces variability; standards enable predictable quality.
- **How?** Project tailoring of organizational standards (e.g., ISO/IEC 12207 for processes).

### D. Records & Reports
- **Why?** "You cannot manage what you cannot measure" — Deming's principle.
- **How?** Maintain defect logs, review logs, test logs, and metrics dashboards.

### E. Change Control
- **Why?** Uncontrolled changes introduce regression defects.
- **How?** Use Configuration Management (CM) tools + formal CCB approval.

### F. Process Adherence Audits
- **Why?** Procedural drift is the silent killer of quality.
- **How?** Periodic SQA audits verifying compliance with documented procedures.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The SQA Model is primarily a **qualitative management framework**, but it relies on **quantitative quality metrics**. The formulas below are the most-tested KTU relations.

| # | Metric / Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|-----------------|---------|----------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \dfrac{N_{d}}{KLOC}$ | $N_d$ = defects found, $KLOC$ = 1000 lines of code | defects/KLOC |
| 2 | **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | $MTTF = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i}{N}$ | $t_i$ = time between failures | hours |
| 3 | **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{ri}}{N}$ | $T_{ri}$ = repair time for failure $i$ | hours |
| 4 | **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \times 100$ | — | percent (%) |
| 5 | **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $DRE = \dfrac{E}{E + D} \times 100$ | $E$ = defects removed before delivery, $D$ = defects delivered | percent (%) |
| 6 | **Test Coverage (TC)** | $TC = \dfrac{T_{exc}}{T_{tot}} \times 100$ | $T_{exc}$ = executed test cases, $T_{tot}$ = total test cases | percent (%) |
| 7 | **Reliability Growth (Basic)** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = failure intensity | dimensionless |
| 8 | **SQA Cost Ratio** | $SQA_{\%} = \dfrac{C_{SQA}}{C_{total}} \times 100$ | $C_{SQA}$ = SQA expenditure, $C_{total}$ = project cost | percent (%) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Safety:** In all inline math within tables above, **vertical bars are written as `\vert` or `\mid`**, not raw `|`, to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (e.g., DO-178C standard):** Direct mapping to Pressman's Ring-3 components — every line of aviation software undergoes formal reviews (FTR), multi-level testing, and process audits.
- **Medical Devices (IEC 62304):** SQA components form the compliance backbone for FDA approval.
- **Banking Systems:** Reliability metrics ($R(t)$, Availability $A$) drive SLO commitments.
- **B.Tech Project Reviews:** Even your mini-project applies Ring-3 — peer review + testing + report standards.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From SQA Goal to Operational Metric

We derive the **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** as it is the **most commonly tested** formula in KTU exams on SQA.

> [!NOTE]
> **Background:** An SQA model is judged *adequate* when a target fraction of defects is removed **before** the product reaches the customer. DRE quantifies this adequacy.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the variables.**
Let:
- $E$ = number of defects removed by the SQA process (i.e., caught by reviews + tests during development).
- $D$ = number of defects that escape the SQA process and are delivered to the customer.
- $N$ = total number of defects originally injected during development.

Therefore, by conservation:

$$
N = E + D
$$

**Step 2 — Express DRE conceptually.**
DRE is the **proportion of original defects removed before delivery**, expressed as a percentage:

$$
DRE = \left( \dfrac{\text{Defects Removed}}{\text{Total Defects}} \right) \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute using Step 1.**
Replacing "Defects Removed" with $E$ and "Total Defects" with $E + D$:

$$
DRE = \left( \dfrac{E}{E + D} \right) \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Boundary analysis.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } D = 0 \text{ (perfect SQA):} \quad DRE &= \left(\dfrac{E}{E+0}\right) \times 100 = 100\% \\[4pt]
\text{If } E = 0 \text{ (no SQA):} \quad DRE &= \left(\dfrac{0}{0+D}\right) \times 100 = 0\% \\[4pt]
\text{Industry benchmark:} \quad DRE &\geq 95\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** If a question states *"80 defects were found before delivery and 5 reached the customer"*, the calculation is $DRE = \dfrac{80}{80+5} \times 100 = 94.12\%$. This falls **short of the industry benchmark of 95%**, indicating inadequate SQA.

### Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** During development of a 10 KLOC module, the SQA team recorded 25 defects removed during reviews and testing, while 3 defects were reported by the customer post-delivery. Compute: (a) DRE, (b) Defect Density, (c) Comment on SQA adequacy.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — DRE:**
$$
DRE = \left(\dfrac{E}{E + D}\right) \times 100 = \left(\dfrac{25}{25 + 3}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
DRE &= \left(\dfrac{25}{28}\right) \times 100 \\[3pt] &= 0.8928 \times 100 \\[3pt] &= 89.29\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Defect Density:**
$$
DD = \dfrac{N_d}{KLOC} = \dfrac{25 + 3}{10} = \dfrac{28}{10} = 2.8 \text{ defects/KLOC}
$$

**Part (c) — SQA Adequacy Comment:**
Since the achieved DRE of **89.29% < 95%** industry benchmark, the SQA Model's Ring-3 components (reviews, testing) are **inadequate**. Recommended corrective actions:
- Increase review coverage to Ring-3 *Technical Reviews*.
- Apply root-cause analysis (Fishbone/Ishikawa) to identify why 3 defects escaped.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize This Template** — for any KTU numerical on SQA, follow: *(1) Identify E and D, (2) Apply DRE formula, (3) Compare with 95%, (4) Recommend action.*

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping SQA Model to Process Standards

The matrix below is **frequently asked** as a 7-mark question ("Discuss how the SQA Model aligns with ISO 9001 and CMMI").

| SQA Model Component (Pressman) | ISO 9001 Clause | CMMI Process Area | KTU Exam Weight |
|--------------------------------|-----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Technical Reviews | ISO 9001: Clause 7.3 (Design & Development) | Verification & Validation | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Software Testing | ISO 9001: Clause 7.3.6 (Design Verification) | Validation (VAL) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Standards Compliance | ISO 9001: Clause 4.2 (Documentation) | Process & Product Quality Assurance (PPQA) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Change Control | ISO 9001: Clause 4.2.4 (Control of Records) | Configuration Management (CM) | ⭐⭐ |
| Records & Reports | ISO 9001: Clause 8.2.3 (Monitoring) | Measurement & Analysis (MA) | ⭐⭐ |
| Process Audits | ISO 9001: Clause 8.2.2 (Internal Audit) | PPQA | ⭐⭐ |
| Pre-Project Quality Goals | ISO 9001: Clause 5.4 (Quality Objectives) | Organizational Process Focus (OPF) | ⭐ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Concentric-Ring SQA Model (Pressman's Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram follows the **strict alpha-numeric node rule** (no reserved keywords, no markdown inside node labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start([Customer Satisfaction Sphere]):::outer
    ring3[Ring 3 - SQA System Components]:::mid
    ring3a[Technical Reviews]:::mid
    ring3b[Software Testing Multi Level]:::mid
    ring3c[Process and Product Standards]:::mid
    ring3d[Records Reports and Metrics]:::mid
    ring3e[Change Control CCB]:::mid
    ring3f[Process Adherence Audits]:::mid
    ring2[Ring 2 - Infrastructure Components]:::infra
    ring2a[SDLC Model Selection]:::infra
    ring2b[Methods and Tools Selection]:::infra
    ring2c[Organizational SQA Structure]:::infra
    ring2d[Staff Training Programs]:::infra
    ring1[Ring 1 - Pre Project Components]:::inner
    ring1a[Quality Commitments Negotiation]:::inner
    ring1b[Quantitative Quality Goals]:::inner
    ring1c[Resource Allocation for QA]:::inner
    ring1d[Process Definition Documentation]:::inner
    core([Software Work Product]):::core

    start --> ring3
    ring3 --> ring3a
    ring3 --> ring3b
    ring3 --> ring3c
    ring3 --> ring3d
    ring3 --> ring3e
    ring3 --> ring3f
    ring3 --> ring2
    ring2 --> ring2a
    ring2 --> ring2b
    ring2 --> ring2c
    ring2 --> ring2d
    ring2 --> ring1
    ring1 --> ring1a
    ring1 --> ring1b
    ring1 --> ring1c
    ring1 --> ring1d
    ring1 --> core

    classDef outer fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef mid fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef infra fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef inner fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef core fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The SQA Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Software Development Process]:::blk --> B[SQA Plan Definition]:::plan
    B --> C[Technical Reviews FTR]:::rev
    C --> D{Defects Found}:::gate
    D -->|Yes| E[Defect Logging and Tracking]:::rev
    E --> F[Re Review Until Pass]:::rev
    F --> C
    D -->|No| G[Multi Level Testing]:::test
    G --> H[Test Coverage Analysis]:::test
    H --> I{Coverage 95 Percent}:::gate
    I -->|No| J[Add Test Cases]:::test
    J --> G
    I -->|Yes| K[Metrics Computation DRE DD]:::met
    K --> L[Process Adherence Audit]:::aud
    L --> M{Audit Pass}:::gate
    M -->|No| N[Root Cause Analysis]:::aud
    N --> O[Process Improvement CAPA]:::aud
    O --> A
    M -->|Yes| P[Release and Customer Delivery]:::rel

    classDef blk fill:#264478,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef plan fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef rev fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef test fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef met fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef aud fill:#bf8f00,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef gate fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000000
    classDef rel fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram in an exam:** Trace the flow **Software Process → Reviews → Testing → Metrics → Audit → Improvement → back to Process**. This closed-loop is called the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle** applied to SQA — a favorite 14-mark KTU answer framework.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the SQA Model. List any four components of the SQA System as per Pressman.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Software Quality Assurance Model, as defined by Roger S. Pressman, is a layered, three-ring framework that integrates managerial, procedural, and technical activities to ensure that the software product meets defined quality standards throughout its lifecycle.

The four key components of the **SQA System (Ring 3)** are:
1. **Technical Reviews** — Formal peer reviews (walkthroughs, inspections) to identify defects early.
2. **Software Testing** — Execution-based defect detection at unit, integration, system, and acceptance levels.
3. **Process and Product Standards** — Adherence to documented standards (ISO 9001, IEEE).
4. **Records, Reports, and Metrics** — Quantitative tracking of defects, coverage, and effort.

*(Award: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for the four components.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)? Why is it a key indicator of SQA adequacy?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**DRE** is the percentage of total defects that are removed by the SQA process **before** the software is delivered to the customer.

$$
DRE = \left(\dfrac{E}{E + D}\right) \times 100
$$

where $E$ = defects removed pre-delivery and $D$ = defects delivered.

**Why it indicates SQA adequacy:**
- A **high DRE (≥ 95%)** indicates that reviews, testing, and audits are effective.
- A **low DRE** signals weak Ring-3 components — defects are escaping to customers, increasing maintenance cost and reputation risk.
- Hence DRE serves as the **quantitative litmus test** for the adequacy of the SQA Model.

*(Award: 1 mark formula + 1 mark meaning + 1 mark for adequacy justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU ESE, you must answer **one full question** from a choice of two in Module 2. Each question carries **14 marks** split as Part (a) — 7 marks and Part (b) — 7 marks.

---

### 🔹 Question A — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three-layer SQA Model** proposed by Pressman. Describe each layer with its components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **operational flow of SQA activities** including reviews, testing, and process audits. How does the feedback loop ensure continuous improvement? **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The Pressman SQA Model is structured as **three concentric rings**, where the innermost ring directly engages the technological work product and the outermost ring defines the broader customer and organizational context.

**Ring 1 — Pre-Project Quality Components:**
- Negotiation of **quality commitments** with the customer.
- Establishment of **quantitative quality goals** (e.g., reliability, defect density).
- Allocation of **resources** for quality activities.
- Definition of the **software engineering process** that will be followed.

**Ring 2 — Infrastructure Quality Components:**
- Selection of an appropriate **software development life-cycle model** (Waterfall, Agile, etc.).
- Selection of **methods and CASE tools** for development.
- Definition of the **organizational structure** for SQA (e.g., separate SQA group).
- **Training** of staff in software quality practices.

**Ring 3 — SQA System Components (Operational Core):**
- **Technical Reviews** (Walkthroughs, Inspections, FTR).
- **Software Testing** at multiple levels.
- **Process and Product Standards** (ISO, IEEE).
- **Records, Reports, and Metrics** collection.
- **Control of Change** through formal CCB.
- **Process Adherence Audits**.

*(Award: 2 marks ring concept + 5 marks distributed across rings.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

The operational flow follows the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle, which is the engineering implementation of Pressman's Ring-3:

1. **Plan:** The SQA plan defines goals, methods, responsibilities, and metrics.
2. **Do (Technical Reviews):** Formal peer reviews are conducted on every work product (requirements, design, code, test cases). Roles include Moderator, Reader, Recorder, Author, and Reviewer. Defects are logged.
3. **Do (Testing):** Multi-level testing — unit (developer), integration (tester), system (SQA team), acceptance (customer).
4. **Check (Metrics):** Compute DRE, Defect Density, Test Coverage. Compare against thresholds (e.g., DRE ≥ 95%).
5. **Check (Process Audit):** SQA team audits whether the team actually followed defined processes.
6. **Act (Improvement):** If metrics or audits fail, **root cause analysis** is performed, **Corrective and Preventive Actions (CAPA)** are issued, and the process is refined.

This forms a **closed feedback loop**: audit findings → process changes → fewer defects in the next iteration → higher DRE. The continuous loop transforms SQA from a static checklist into a **dynamic, learning system**.

*(Award: 3 marks flow explanation + 2 marks diagram description + 2 marks feedback/PDCA reasoning.)*

---

### 🔹 Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]`**

**(a)** Discuss the **adequacy of an SQA Model** using the checklist approach. What criteria are evaluated? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute DRE and Defect Density for a project with the following data and comment on SQA adequacy: Total KLOC = 8, Defects found during reviews = 18, Defects found during testing = 12, Defects reported by customer post-delivery = 4. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

According to Pressman, **adequacy of the SQA Model** is judged using a **checklist approach** that evaluates the **completeness** of SQA components in capturing defects *before* release. The key checklist criteria are:

| # | Adequacy Criterion | What it Checks |
|---|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Technical Review Plan** | Are all work products reviewed formally? |
| 2 | **Test Coverage** | Is every requirement covered by at least one test? |
| 3 | **Standards Compliance** | Does the project follow documented standards? |
| 4 | **Defect Tracking** | Are defects logged, classified, and resolved? |
| 5 | **Change Control** | Are all changes formally approved via CCB? |
| 6 | **Process Adherence** | Is the team following the defined process? |
| 7 | **Metrics Collection** | Are DRE, DD, and coverage metrics computed? |
| 8 | **Feedback Mechanism** | Is there a CAPA process for improvement? |

A "Yes" to all eight items indicates an **adequate SQA Model**.

*(Award: 1 mark intro + 4 marks criteria explanation + 2 marks conclusion.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given Data:**
- $KLOC = 8$
- $E_{reviews} = 18$ defects removed in reviews
- $E_{testing} = 12$ defects removed in testing
- $D_{customer} = 4$ defects escaped to customer

**Step 1 — Total defects removed pre-delivery:**
$$
E = E_{reviews} + E_{testing} = 18 + 12 = 30
$$

**Step 2 — Compute DRE:**
$$
DRE = \left(\dfrac{E}{E + D}\right) \times 100 = \left(\dfrac{30}{30 + 4}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
DRE &= \left(\dfrac{30}{34}\right) \times 100 \\[3pt] &= 0.8823 \times 100 \\[3pt] &= 88.24\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Defect Density:**
Total defects = $E + D = 30 + 4 = 34$.

$$
DD = \dfrac{N_{d}}{KLOC} = \dfrac{34}{8} = 4.25 \text{ defects/KLOC}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on SQA Adequacy:**
- Achieved **DRE = 88.24%** which is **less than the industry benchmark of 95%** → **Inadequate SQA**.
- Achieved **DD = 4.25 defects/KLOC** which is **higher than the industry acceptable range (1–3 defects/KLOC)** for typical business software → **Quality risk**.
- **Recommendation:** Strengthen Ring-3 *Technical Reviews* (e.g., adopt Fagan inspections), add more test cases for under-tested modules, and perform root cause analysis on the 4 escaped defects.

*(Award: 2 marks DRE + 2 marks DD + 2 marks comment + 1 mark recommendation.)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps (Board Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Confusing Rings:** Many students write *Technical Reviews* under Ring-2 (Infrastructure). It belongs to **Ring-3 (SQA System Components)**. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the outermost 'Customer Sphere':** The customer is **not** a ring but the **outermost boundary** that defines requirements. Always mention it.
> 3. **DRE Formula Inversion:** Writing $DRE = \dfrac{D}{E+D}$ instead of $\dfrac{E}{E+D}$. This **inverts** the meaning — review carefully! (-2 marks)
> 4. **Missing Units:** Always write "defects/KLOC" for DD and "%" for DRE. Missing units = -0.5 mark.
> 5. **No Recommendation:** After computing metrics, examiners expect a **comment on adequacy** (e.g., "Less than 95% → inadequate") and **corrective action**. Skipping this = -1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — The SQA Model (Module 2)**

- ⭐ The SQA Model is a **3-concentric-ring** architecture by **Pressman**.
- ⭐ **Ring 1 (Pre-Project):** Quality commitments, quantitative goals, resource allocation, process definition.
- ⭐ **Ring 2 (Infrastructure):** SDLC model, methods/tools, organizational structure, training.
- ⭐ **Ring 3 (SQA System Core):** Technical reviews, testing, standards, metrics, change control, audits.
- ⭐ The **Customer Sphere** is the outermost boundary (not a ring).
- ⭐ **DRE** = $(E / (E + D)) \times 100$; industry benchmark = **≥ 95%**.
- ⭐ **Defect Density** = $N_d / KLOC$; acceptable range = **1–3 defects/KLOC**.
- ⭐ Adequacy is checked via a **checklist** of 6–8 criteria (reviews, coverage, standards, etc.).
- ⭐ The operational flow follows **PDCA**: Plan → Do (reviews + testing) → Check (metrics + audits) → Act (CAPA).
- ⭐ SQA Model maps directly to **ISO 9001** clauses and **CMMI** process areas (PPQA, VER, VAL).
- ⭐ Always include **units** in numerical answers and provide a **corrective recommendation** after metric computation.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Exam Mnemonic:** *"Pre-Plan · Infra-Build · System-Check · Customer-Care"* — covers all three rings + customer sphere in one breath. Master this and you can write a 7-mark answer in under 4 minutes!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SQA Records and Documentation Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SQA Records and Documentation Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Software Quality Assurance (SQA) Records** are formalized, traceable, and auditable artifacts generated throughout the software development life cycle (SDLC) that provide objective evidence of conformance to defined processes, standards, procedures, and contractual requirements. These records serve as the *evidentiary backbone* of any SQA program and are governed by documentation control policies.

**Documentation Control** (also termed *Document Configuration Management*) is the systematic discipline of creating, reviewing, approving, distributing, tracking changes, versioning, archiving, and disposing of project documents in a manner that ensures **single-source-of-truth integrity**, **traceability**, and **regulatory compliance** throughout the project life cycle and beyond.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Style)**
> *SQA Records and Documentation Control refers to the structured management of all software project documentation — including plans, specifications, test artifacts, review minutes, audit reports, and corrective action logs — under a controlled change-management framework that guarantees authenticity, integrity, retrievability, and accountability.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **hospital patient record system**:
- Every diagnosis, prescription, test report, and doctor's note is a *record*.
- These records cannot be casually edited, deleted, or swapped between patients. They are *controlled*.
- A change (say, a revised prescription) is logged, dated, signed, and traceable to the responsible doctor.
- If a malpractice case arises years later, the hospital must retrieve the exact version of the record as it existed on that date.

**SQA Records and Documentation Control work identically for software:**
- The "patient" is the software product.
- The "doctors" are developers, testers, reviewers, and auditors.
- The "medical file" is the cumulative documentation repository.
- The "hospital protocol" is the SQA Plan + Documentation Control Procedure.

If your software fails in the field (or is audited by IEEE/ISO/SEI/CMMI assessors), the **only acceptable proof** of process compliance is a well-controlled documentation trail. Without it, even a technically perfect product is *unverifiable* — and in regulated industries (avionics, medical devices, finance), unverifiable software is legally unsellable.

## 1.3 Core Components (KTU Highlight Box)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "5 Pillars" of SQA Documentation (High-Yield for KTU)**
> 1. **Plans** — SQA Plan, Test Plan, Project Plan
> 2. **Specifications** — SRS (IEEE 830), SDD, STD
> 3. **Reviews & Audits** — Review minutes, Audit reports, Non-conformance reports
> 4. **Test Artifacts** — Test cases, Test logs, Defect reports, Test summary
> 5. **Control Records** — Change requests, Version logs, Approvals, Baselines

## 1.4 Standards Governing SQA Documentation

| Standard | Scope | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **IEEE Std 829** | Software Test Documentation | Test Plan, Test Design, Test Case, Test Log, Test Incident, Test Summary |
| **IEEE Std 830** | SRS Format | Software Requirements Specification structure |
| **IEEE Std 1028** | Reviews & Inspections | Fagan Inspection, Walkthrough, Technical Review |
| **IEEE Std 730** | SQA Plan | Standard template for SQA Plans |
| **ISO/IEC 12207** | Life Cycle Processes | Documentation requirements per phase |
| **ISO/IEC 90003** | SQA for Software | Documentation control for certified orgs |
| **CMMI v2.0** | Process Maturity | Verification & Validation (VV) process area |
| **DO-178C** | Avionics Software | Rigorous documentation for safety-critical |

## 1.5 Why Documentation Control Matters — A KTU Note

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three "R"s of Documentation Control (Examiner's Favourite)**
> - **Right Document** — are we using the latest approved version?
> - **Right Person** — only authorized roles can edit/approve
> - **Right Time** — obsolete versions are promptly withdrawn from circulation
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of SQA Records

SQA records are not random files. They follow a **document hierarchy** based on volatility and audience:

### 2.1.1 Level 1 — Strategic / Management Records
- **SQA Plan** (IEEE 730 template)
- **Quality Policy** documents
- **Project Quality Plan**
- **Configuration Management Plan**
- **Subcontractor Quality Agreements**

### 2.1.2 Level 2 — Technical / Engineering Records
- **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** — IEEE 830
- **Software Design Description (SDD)**
- **Interface Control Document (ICD)**
- **Database Design Document**
- **Test Plan, Test Design Specification, Test Case Specification** — IEEE 829

### 2.1.3 Level 3 — Operational / Execution Records
- **Test Logs**, **Test Incident Reports**, **Defect Reports**
- **Review Meeting Minutes**
- **Inspection Logs**, **Walkthrough Checklists**
- **Build & Release Manifests**

### 2.1.4 Level 4 — Historical / Archival Records
- **Baselines** (Functional, Allocated, Product)
- **Audit Reports** (Internal & External)
- **Corrective Action Requests (CAR)**
- **Lessons Learned Reports**
- **Process Improvement Records**

## 2.2 The Document Life Cycle (DLC)

Every controlled document traverses five phases:

1. **Identify** — Naming conventions, numbering scheme, document type classification
2. **Author / Draft** — Creation by responsible role
3. **Review & Approve** — Peer review, technical review, formal approval signatures
4. **Distribute & Use** — Controlled distribution list, master copies, baselines
5. **Change / Retire** — Formal change request, impact analysis, re-approval, archival

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** A common 3-mark question is *"List the phases of document life cycle"* or *"Differentiate between master copy and working copy."*

## 2.3 Documentation Control — The Core Mechanisms

### 2.3.1 Identification & Numbering
Each document carries a **unique identifier** (e.g., `DOC-TST-PLAN-v3.2`). The structure typically encodes:
- Project code
- Document type
- Sequence number
- Version number (major.minor)

### 2.3.2 Version Control
- **Major version change** (e.g., v1.0 → v2.0) → significant content change, requires re-approval and re-baselining
- **Minor version change** (e.g., v1.0 → v1.1) → minor edit (typo, formatting), still logged but lighter review

### 2.3.3 Baselines
A **baseline** is a formally reviewed and approved reference point.
- **Functional Baseline (FBL)** — approved SRS
- **Allocated Baseline (ABL)** — approved design
- **Product Baseline (PBL)** — approved tested code

### 2.3.4 Change Control Board (CCB)
A CCB is the authoritative body that:
- Reviews Change Requests (CRs)
- Approves/rejects based on impact analysis
- Ensures traceability of all changes

### 2.3.5 Distribution Control
- **Master Copy** (official, signed, archived)
- **Controlled Copies** (numbered, tracked recipients)
- **Uncontrolled Copies** (information only, marked as such)

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Metrics Cheat Sheet

Since SQA Records and Documentation Control is largely a qualitative discipline, the "formulas" are **quality metrics** used to measure documentation health.

| Metric | Formula | Purpose / KTU Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Documentation Coverage (DC)** | $DC = \frac{D_{completed}}{D_{required}} \times 100\%$ | Measures % of required docs actually produced |
| **Defect Detection Efficiency (DDE)** | $DDE = \frac{D_{found-before-release}}{D_{total-found}} \times 100\%$ | Evaluates effectiveness of review/inspection records |
| **Review Coverage (RC)** | $RC = \frac{N_{reviewed-documents}}{N_{total-documents}} \times 100\%$ | Measures % of docs that went through formal review |
| **Change Request Aging (CRA)** | $CRA = \frac{\sum (T_{closed} - T_{opened})}{N_{CR}}$ | Average time to close a change request (days) |
| **Documentation Defect Density (DDD)** | $DDD = \frac{N_{doc-defects}}{Size_{document}}$ (per page/section) | Defects per unit of documentation |
| **Traceability Completeness (TC)** | $TC = \frac{N_{traced-requirements}}{N_{total-requirements}} \times 100\%$ | % requirements with forward & backward traces |
| **Audit Non-Conformance Rate (ANCR)** | $ANCR = \frac{N_{NC}}{N_{audits}} \times 100\%$ | Frequency of major audit findings |
| **Mean Time to Update Document (MTUD)** | $MTUD = \frac{\sum (T_{release} - T_{CR-approval})}{N_{CR}}$ | Process efficiency for doc updates |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-Symbol Safeguard Applied:** All absolute values in formulas above are written as $\vert x \vert$ using `\vert` to prevent markdown table breakage.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

In **regulated industries**, documentation control is not optional:
- **Aerospace (DO-178C):** Level A software requires 100% traceability from requirements → source code → test cases → test results.
- **Medical Devices (FDA 21 CFR Part 820, IEC 62304):** Design History File (DHF) is a legally mandated, documentation-controlled record.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262):** Each ASIL level mandates specific documentation depth.
- **Banking (PCI-DSS, SOX):** Audit trails for any change to financial software.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** When answering application-based questions, always cite at least one industry standard to earn the "depth" mark. E.g., *"Documentation control is implemented as per IEEE 730 / ISO 90003, with CCB-approved baselining and version-tagged controlled distribution."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Document Classification Decision Tree

This matrix helps classify *any* document encountered in an SQA context.

| **Document Artifact** | **Phase Generated** | **Owner Role** | **Control Level** | **Approval Authority** | **Retention Period** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SQA Plan | Inception | SQA Manager | Strict | Project Manager + SQA Head | Project + 7 yrs |
| SRS (IEEE 830) | Requirements | Business Analyst | Strict | Customer + System Architect | Lifetime of product |
| Test Plan | Planning | Test Lead | Strict | QA Manager | Project + 5 yrs |
| Test Cases | Design | Test Engineer | Moderate | Test Lead | Project + 3 yrs |
| Review Minutes | Execution | Review Moderator | Strict | Review Leader | Project + 5 yrs |
| Audit Report | Verification | SQA Auditor | Strict | SQA Manager | Project + 7 yrs |
| Defect Report | Execution | Tester | Moderate | Test Lead | Project + 3 yrs |
| Change Request | Maintenance | Any stakeholder | Strict | CCB | Lifetime of product |
| Lessons Learned | Closure | Project Manager | Moderate | PMO | Permanent |
| Build Manifest | Build | Build Engineer | Moderate | Release Manager | Project + 3 yrs |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Change Control Workflow

When a **Change Request (CR)** is raised, the following mathematical model of approval flow applies:

**Step 1: CR Intake**
A CR is logged with timestamp $T_{open}$ and unique ID $CR_{id}$.

**Step 2: Impact Analysis**
Three impact dimensions are scored (each on scale 0–5):

$$
I_{total} = w_1 \cdot S_{scope} + w_2 \cdot S_{cost} + w_3 \cdot S_{risk}
$$

where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$ and $S_{scope}, S_{cost}, S_{risk} \in [0, 5]$.

**Step 3: Decision Rule**

$$
D = \begin{cases} \text{Auto-Approve} & \text{if } I_{total} \leq 1.5 \\ \text{CCB Review} & \text{if } 1.5 < I_{total} \leq 3.5 \\ \text{Executive Escalation} & \text{if } I_{total} > 3.5 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4: Cycle Time Calculation**

$$
CT_{CR} = T_{close} - T_{open} \quad \text{(in business days)}
$$

**Step 5: Aggregate Process Performance**

$$
\overline{CT} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} CT_{CR,i}
$$

$$
\sigma_{CT} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (CT_{CR,i} - \overline{CT})^2}
$$

**Conversion Logic Explanation:**
- *Step 1* establishes traceability — every CR must be uniquely identifiable from the moment of creation. This satisfies the **identification** tenet of document control.
- *Step 2* uses a weighted scoring model because in real projects, a small scope change can have catastrophic risk (e.g., a one-line change to a security module). Weights $w_1, w_2, w_3$ are pre-agreed by CCB policy.
- *Step 3* implements **risk-based gating**, a CMMI v2.0 best practice — not all changes deserve the same scrutiny.
- *Step 4* measures **cycle efficiency** — a healthy SQA process has a low $CT_{CR}$ with low variance.
- *Step 5* aggregates for management reporting; the standard deviation $\sigma_{CT}$ reveals whether the process is **predictable**.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Documentation Coverage Calculation

A mid-sized project mandates the following controlled documents:

| Doc ID | Required? | Produced? | Approved? |
|---|---|---|---|
| SQA Plan | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| SRS | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Test Plan | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Test Cases | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Design Doc | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| User Manual | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Audit Report | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ |

**Step A: Count required documents.**
$D_{required} = 7$

**Step B: Count completed AND approved documents.**
$D_{completed} = 5$ (SQA Plan, Test Plan, Design Doc, User Manual, plus SRS produced but not approved → exclude)

Wait — applying strict definition, a doc is "completed" only if *produced and approved*:

$$
D_{completed} = \vert \{SQA\,Plan, Test\,Plan, Design\,Doc, User\,Manual\} \vert = 4
$$

**Step C: Compute Documentation Coverage.**

$$
DC = \frac{D_{completed}}{D_{required}} \times 100\% = \frac{4}{7} \times 100\% \approx 57.14\%
$$

**Conversion Logic Explanation:**
- We deliberately *exclude* the SRS because an unapproved document, even if drafted, is not a valid controlled record. This is a **key KTU board distinction** — produced ≠ controlled.
- A coverage of 57.14% indicates the project is in a **CMMI Maturity Level 1–2 zone**; an acceptable threshold for SEI-CMM Level 3 is typically **≥ 90%**.

**Step D: Compute Traceability Completeness.**
Assume the SRS contains 50 requirements, of which 30 have linked test cases (forward trace) and 25 have linked design items (backward trace).

$$
TC_{forward} = \frac{30}{50} \times 100\% = 60\%
$$

$$
TC_{backward} = \frac{25}{50} \times 100\% = 50\%
$$

$$
TC_{overall} = \frac{TC_{forward} + TC_{backward}}{2} = \frac{60 + 50}{2} = 55\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Insight:** When KTU asks to "evaluate documentation health," always compute **at least two metrics** (e.g., DC + TC) to demonstrate multi-dimensional analysis. Examiners reward holistic thinking.

## 3.4 Analytical Trade-Off Matrix

| Approach | Strength | Weakness | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper-based control** | Simple, no tech dep. | Slow, error-prone, poor search | Small teams, regulated archives |
| **Shared-drive control** | Easy collaboration | No version lock, file overwrite risk | Low-maturity projects |
| **Dedicated DMS** (e.g., SharePoint, Confluence) | Centralized, version-aware | Setup cost | Mid-size teams |
| **CMDB + Issue Tracker** (e.g., Jira + Git) | Full traceability, automated audits | Steep learning curve | Mature / safety-critical |
| **PLM / ALM tools** (e.g., Polarion, DOORS) | End-to-end traceability, regulatory compliant | High cost | Aerospace, medical, defense |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Document Life Cycle (DLC)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Need] --> B[Author Draft]
    B --> C[Peer Review]
    C --> D{Review Approved?}
    D -- No --> B
    D -- Yes --> E[Formal Approval Sign-off]
    E --> F[Baselining and Version Tag]
    F --> G[Controlled Distribution]
    G --> H[In Active Use]
    H --> I{Change Request Raised?}
    I -- No --> J[Retain and Archive]
    I -- Yes --> K[Impact Analysis by CCB]
    K --> L{CCB Decision}
    L -- Approved --> M[Re-author Document]
    M --> C
    L -- Rejected --> N[Close CR with Justification]
    J --> O[End of Life Disposal]
    N --> H
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — SQA Record Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TB
    R[SQA Documentation Repository]
    R --> R1[Level 1 Strategic]
    R --> R2[Level 2 Technical]
    R --> R3[Level 3 Operational]
    R --> R4[Level 4 Historical]
    R1 --> R1a[SQA Plan IEEE 730]
    R1 --> R1b[Quality Policy]
    R1 --> R1c[CM Plan]
    R2 --> R2a[SRS IEEE 830]
    R2 --> R2b[SDD]
    R2 --> R2c[Test Plan IEEE 829]
    R2 --> R2d[STD]
    R3 --> R3a[Test Cases]
    R3 --> R3b[Defect Reports]
    R3 --> R3c[Review Minutes]
    R3 --> R3d[Build Manifests]
    R4 --> R4a[Baselines FBL ABL PBL]
    R4 --> R4b[Audit Reports]
    R4 --> R4c[Corrective Action Requests]
    R4 --> R4d[Lessons Learned]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Change Control Workflow (CCB)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[User or Stakeholder] -->|Submits CR| CR[Change Request Logged]
    CR --> IA[Impact Analysis]
    IA --> SC{Score I total}
    SC -- I less than 1.5 --> AA[Auto Approve]
    SC -- I between 1.5 and 3.5 --> CCB[CCB Review Meeting]
    SC -- I greater than 3.5 --> EX[Executive Escalation]
    AA --> IM[Implement and Update Docs]
    CCB --> RV{CCB Decision}
    RV -- Approved --> IM
    RV -- Rejected --> CL[Close CR Rejected]
    EX --> EXR{Executive Decision}
    EXR -- Approved --> IM
    EXR -- Rejected --> CL
    IM --> VR[Verify and Re-baseline]
    VR --> DN[Document Notification to Users]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`, `R1a`) — no reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph` are used as standalone IDs. Labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting tags.

```mermaid
    end

        DB1[Master Records DB]
        DB2[Baseline Vault]
        DB3[Archive Cold Storage]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Output Layer]
        OUT1[Controlled PDFs]
        OUT2[Traceability Reports]
        OUT3[Compliance Dashboards]
        OUT4[Audit Pack ZIPs]
    end
    IN1 --> PR1
    IN2 --> PR2
    IN3 --> PR3
    IN4 --> PR5
    PR1 --> DB1
    PR2 --> DB2
    PR3 --> PR3
    PR4 --> PR1
    PR5 --> DB3
    DB1 --> OUT1
    DB2 --> OUT2
    DB3 --> OUT3
    DB1 --> OUT4
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define SQA Records. List any four types of SQA records with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — approx. 80–100 words):**

> **SQA Records** are formalized, traceable artifacts that provide objective evidence of conformance to defined SQA processes, standards, and procedures throughout the SDLC.
>
> **Four types:**
> 1. **Planning records** — e.g., SQA Plan (per IEEE 730)
> 2. **Specification records** — e.g., Software Requirements Specification (per IEEE 830)
> 3. **Operational records** — e.g., Test Logs, Defect Reports (per IEEE 829)
> 4. **Historical records** — e.g., Audit Reports, Baselines, Corrective Action Requests
>
> They are essential for **traceability, auditability, and process improvement**.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the concept of a "baseline" in documentation control. Differentiate between Functional, Allocated, and Product Baselines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **baseline** is a formally reviewed and approved snapshot of documents/code used as a reference point for future change control.
>
> | Baseline | Established After | Contents |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Functional (FBL)** | Requirements phase | Approved SRS |
> | **Allocated (ABL)** | Design phase | Approved SDD, ICD |
> | **Product (PBL)** | Testing phase | Approved, tested code + all artifacts |
>
> Any change after baselining requires a formal Change Request and CCB approval.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **document life cycle** in detail. List the five phases and describe the role of the **Change Control Board (CCB)** in maintaining documentation integrity.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **IEEE 829 standard for software test documentation**. List any **six** documents prescribed by IEEE 829 and explain the significance of each in two lines.

#### Model Solution for (a) — Document Life Cycle & CCB [7 marks]

The **Document Life Cycle (DLC)** comprises five phases:

1. **Identify** *(1 mark)*: The need for a document is recognized; a unique ID and naming convention are assigned (e.g., `DOC-TST-PLAN-v1.0`).

2. **Author / Draft** *(1 mark)*: The responsible role (tester, analyst, designer) creates the document using a standard template (IEEE 730, IEEE 830, or IEEE 829).

3. **Review & Approve** *(1 mark)*: Peer review is conducted; discrepancies are logged. Once resolved, the document is formally approved via signature workflow.

4. **Distribute & Use** *(1 mark)*: The approved document is stored in the **DMS repository**, version-tagged, and distributed to authorized holders of controlled copies. Obsolete versions are marked "SUPERSEDED."

5. **Change / Retire** *(1 mark)*: When a Change Request (CR) is raised, the document is re-authored, re-reviewed, re-approved, and re-baselined. At end-of-life, it is archived in cold storage with retention metadata.

**Role of CCB — Maintaining Integrity** *(2 marks)*:
- Reviews every Change Request against **scope, cost, risk, and schedule** impact.
- Ensures **no uncontrolled change** enters the baseline.
- Enforces **traceability** between old and new versions.
- Acts as the **single point of authorization**, preventing rogue edits and maintaining the **single source of truth**.
- Publishes change notifications to all controlled-copy holders within a defined SLA (e.g., 24 hours).

#### Model Solution for (b) — IEEE 829 Test Documentation [7 marks]

**IEEE Std 829-2008** prescribes a standardized structure for software test documentation, ensuring uniformity and audit-readiness across projects.

**Six Key Documents:**

| # | Document | Significance (1 mark each, approx. 1.5 lines) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Master Test Plan (MTP)** | High-level, multi-project test strategy, resources, schedule |
| 2 | **Level Test Plan (LTP)** | Detailed test approach for a specific level (unit, integration, system) |
| 3 | **Level Test Design** | Specifies test cases, coverage criteria, identification of test items |
| 4 | **Level Test Case Specification** | Inputs, outputs, execution conditions, pass/fail criteria for each test |
