| 5 | **Level Test Log** | Chronological record of executed tests, dates, tester identity, outcomes |
| 6 | **Level Test Incident Report** | Logs anomalies, defects, and unexpected events during test execution |
| (Bonus) | **Test Summary Report** | Final aggregate report: tests run, passed, failed, residual defects |

Each document enforces **traceability** from requirements → design → test → defects, enabling **regulatory audits** (FDA, DO-178C, ISO 26262).

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 marks]** Define **Documentation Control**. Explain its objectives, key elements, and the role of **versioning, baselining, and controlled distribution** in maintaining document integrity.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **types of SQA records** with a hierarchical classification. Construct a **traceability matrix** linking the following artifacts and explain its importance: *Requirement → Design → Code → Test Case → Defect Report*.

#### Model Solution for (a) — Documentation Control [7 marks]

**Definition** *(1 mark)*: Documentation Control is the systematic management of all project documents through their life cycle, ensuring authenticity, integrity, version control, and controlled access.

**Objectives** *(2 marks)*:
1. Ensure the **right version** of the right document reaches the **right person** at the **right time**.
2. Prevent **unauthorized modifications**.
3. Maintain **traceability** between requirements, design, test, and change artifacts.
4. Enable **auditability** for internal and external assessments.
5. Comply with **standards** (IEEE 730, ISO 90003, CMMI).

**Key Elements** *(2 marks)*:
- **Identification & Numbering** — unique ID per document
- **Versioning** — major.minor scheme
- **Baselining** — frozen reference points (FBL, ABL, PBL)
- **Change Control Board (CCB)** — authorizes all changes
- **Controlled Distribution** — master / controlled / uncontrolled copies
- **Archival & Retention** — long-term storage with retrieval SLAs

**Detailed discussion of three mechanisms** *(2 marks)*:
- **Versioning** prevents "which is latest?" confusion; major versions trigger re-approval, minor versions are light-weight.
- **Baselining** freezes a snapshot, allowing measured changes against a known reference; any deviation requires CR.
- **Controlled Distribution** ensures obsolete documents are physically/electronically withdrawn; master copy remains the canonical source.

#### Model Solution for (b) — Types of SQA Records & Traceability Matrix [7 marks]

**Hierarchical Classification** *(3 marks)*:

| Level | Type | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Strategic | SQA Plan, Quality Policy, CM Plan |
| 2 | Technical | SRS, SDD, STD, Test Plan |
| 3 | Operational | Test Cases, Test Logs, Defect Reports, Review Minutes |
| 4 | Historical | Baselines, Audit Reports, Corrective Action Requests, Lessons Learned |

**Traceability Matrix** *(3 marks)*:

| Req ID | Design Ref | Code Module | Test Case ID | Defect ID (if any) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| REQ-001 | DES-AUTH-01 | AuthService.java | TC-LOGIN-01, TC-LOGIN-02 | DEF-104, DEF-117 | Closed |
| REQ-002 | DES-PAY-02 | PaymentGateway.java | TC-PAY-01 | DEF-121 | Open |
| REQ-003 | DES-RPT-01 | ReportGenerator.java | TC-RPT-01, TC-RPT-02 | — | Pass |

**Importance** *(1 mark)*:
- **Forward Traceability** (Req → Test) ensures every requirement is tested.
- **Backward Traceability** (Code → Req) ensures no orphan code exists.
- Essential for **impact analysis** during change requests and **audit compliance** (CMMI VER, VAL process areas).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "produced" with "controlled"** — A drafted-but-unapproved document is *not* a controlled record. Always mention **approval + version + baselining** to claim full marks.
> 2. **Missing the CCB** — Many students describe change control but forget the role of the **Change Control Board** as the authorizing body. Examiners specifically look for this.
> 3. **No standards cited** — A 7-mark question on documentation must reference at least one standard (IEEE 730, 829, 830, or ISO 90003). Omitting standards = 1–2 marks lost.
> 4. **Weak traceability explanation** — Just drawing a matrix isn't enough; you must state *forward* and *backward* trace and *why* they matter for audits.
> 5. **Mixing up baselines** — FBL = requirements, ABL = design, PBL = product. Reversing any of these is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **No metric/calculation in numerical questions** — When asked to "evaluate," always include at least one metric computation (e.g., DC, TC, CRA).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This)**
>
> ✅ **SQA Records = objective evidence** of process compliance; must be traceable, retrievable, and approved.
>
> ✅ **Four Levels:** Strategic → Technical → Operational → Historical.
>
> ✅ **Five-Phase DLC:** Identify → Author → Review/Approve → Distribute → Change/Retire.
>
> ✅ **Three Baselines:** FBL (requirements), ABL (design), PBL (product).
>
> ✅ **CCB** is the gatekeeper of all changes — risk-based gating using weighted impact score.
>
> ✅ **Three copy types:** Master (canonical), Controlled (tracked), Uncontrolled (info-only).
>
> ✅ **Version scheme:** major.minor — major requires re-approval and re-baselining.
>
> ✅ **Key Standards to Memorize:**
> - IEEE 730 → SQA Plan
> - IEEE 829 → Test Documentation (8 documents)
> - IEEE 830 → SRS
> - IEEE 1028 → Reviews & Inspections
> - ISO/IEC 12207 → Life Cycle Processes
> - ISO/IEC 90003 → SQA for Software
>
> ✅ **Key Metrics:** DC, DDE, RC, CRA, DDD, TC, ANCR, MTUD — know their formulas.
>
> ✅ **Traceability is bidirectional:** Forward (req→test) AND Backward (code→req).
>
> ✅ **Real-world anchors:** DO-178C (avionics), IEC 62304 (medical), ISO 26262 (automotive), FDA 21 CFR Part 820.
>
> ✅ **Examiner's golden phrase:** *"As per IEEE 730 / IEEE 829 / ISO 90003, documentation control ensures…"* — use this opener for instant credibility.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation of Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evaluation of Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Product Evaluation** is the systematic, evidence-based process of measuring, analyzing, and judging the quality attributes of a software product against predefined standards, specifications, and user expectations. It transforms abstract quality characteristics into measurable **quality metrics**, enabling objective comparison, certification, and continuous improvement.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, *Evaluation of Products* refers to the **measurement phase** of the quality assurance lifecycle. It answers three fundamental engineering questions:

1. **What** should we measure? (Quality model + Metrics selection)
2. **How** do we measure it? (Measurement instruments + Procedures)
3. **What** does the data mean? (Interpretation + Decision making)

The foundation rests on the **GQM (Goal–Question–Metric)** paradigm, the **McCall Quality Model**, the **Boehm Model**, the **Dromey Quality Model**, and the modern **ISO/IEC 25010** standard.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Car Showroom Quality Check**
> Imagine you walk into a car showroom. Before buying, you evaluate the car on **fuel efficiency, safety rating, comfort, durability, and resale value**. Each of these is a *quality characteristic*. The liters-per-kilometer, NCAP star rating, and warranty years are *metrics*. The standardized checklist (ISO/bureau standards) is your *quality model*. The inspector who combines these numbers into a verdict is performing **Product Evaluation**.
>
> In software, the "car" is the software product, the "ratings" are quality metrics like defect density or MTBF, and the "showroom standards" are ISO/IEC 25010 characteristics. You don't *guess* the quality — you **measure, model, and judge**.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Quality Models at a Glance

| Year | Model | Core Idea | Strength |
|------|-------|-----------|----------|
| **1977** | **McCall's Model** | 11 quality factors grouped into Product, Product Revision, Product Transition | First structured hierarchy |
| **1978** | **Boehm's Model** | Quality tree with hierarchical characteristics | Adds maintainability deeply |
| **1988** | **Dromey's Model** | Links quality attributes to sub-attributes and software properties | Property-based evaluation |
| **1991** | **ISO/IEC 9126** | 6 characteristics × sub-characteristics | International benchmark |
| **2011** | **ISO/IEC 25010** | 8 characteristics (added Security, Compatibility) | Current global standard |
| **2000s** | **SQuaRE Series** | Replaces 9126; separates Quality Model, Quality Measurement, Quality Evaluation | Modern reference framework |

---

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Direct questions on **McCall's 11 factors**, **ISO/IEC 25010's 8 characteristics**, and the **GQM approach** are extremely common in Module 3 ESE papers. Memorize both the factor names and their grouping.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Quality Evaluation Pipeline

Product evaluation flows through five structured stages. Every KTU ESE question on this topic can usually be mapped to one of these stages.

### Stage 1 — Define Quality Requirements
Identify stakeholder expectations and translate them into measurable *quality requirements*. This is where the **GQM paradigm** operates.

### Stage 2 — Select Quality Model
Choose a structured taxonomy (McCall, Boehm, ISO 25010). The model defines **WHAT** to measure.

### Stage 3 — Select / Design Metrics
Define **HOW** to measure. A *metric* is a numerical scale + a measurement procedure. Formulas live here.

### Stage 4 — Execute Measurement & Data Collection
Run tests, audits, inspections, surveys. Collect raw data with statistical validity.

### Stage 5 — Interpret, Judge & Decide
Compare metrics against thresholds, benchmarks, or baselines. Output is a **quality judgment**: Accept / Reject / Improve.

---

## 2.2 The Goal–Question–Metric (GQM) Approach

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GQM** (Basili & Rombach, 1984) is a top-down measurement paradigm. It starts with **Goals** of the organization, derives **Questions** that operationalize those goals, and finally identifies the **Metrics** needed to answer those questions.

**Structure of a GQM Statement:**

$$
\text{GQM} = \{\text{Object},\ \text{Purpose},\ \text{Quality Focus},\ \text{Viewpoint},\ \text{Environment}\}
$$

**Example GQM Statement:**

> **Analyze** the *E-commerce Checkout Module* for the purpose of *evaluating* its *reliability and performance* from the *viewpoint of the end-user* in the *production environment*.

**Derived Questions:**
- *Q1:* How many transactions fail per hour?
- *Q2:* What is the average response time?
- *Q3:* How often does the system crash under peak load?

**Derived Metrics:** Failure rate, Mean Time To Failure (MTTF), Transaction throughput, Crash frequency.

---

## 2.3 McCall's Quality Model — The 11 Factors

McCall grouped his 11 factors under **3 perspectives**:

### A. Product Operation (Run-time behavior)
1. **Correctness** — Extent to which software satisfies specifications.
2. **Reliability** — Ability to perform under specified conditions.
3. **Efficiency** — Ratio of output to resource consumption.
4. **Integrity** — Prevention of unauthorized access.
5. **Usability** — Ease of learning and operation.

### B. Product Revision (Maintenance)
6. **Maintainability** — Effort to correct/modify.
7. **Flexibility** — Effort to adapt to new environments.
8. **Testability** — Effort to validate performance.

### C. Product Transition (Adaptation)
9. **Portability** — Effort to transfer to new platform.
10. **Reusability** — Extent to which components can be reused.
11. **Interoperability** — Effort to interface with other systems.

> [!NOTE]
> Each factor is rated on a **0–10 scale** through *Metrics* like defect count, MTTF, code complexity, learning time, etc.

---

## 2.4 ISO/IEC 25010 — The 8 Quality Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ISO/IEC 25010 (2011) is the **current international benchmark** referenced by KTU 2024. It defines 8 characteristics, each decomposed into sub-characteristics.

| # | Characteristic | Sub-Characteristics (Selected) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Functional Suitability** | Functional completeness, correctness, appropriateness |
| 2 | **Performance Efficiency** | Time behaviour, resource utilisation, capacity |
| 3 | **Compatibility** | Co-existence, interoperability |
| 4 | **Usability** | Learnability, operability, error protection, aesthetics, accessibility |
| 5 | **Reliability** | Maturity, availability, fault tolerance, recoverability |
| 6 | **Security** | Confidentiality, integrity, non-repudiation, accountability, authenticity |
| 7 | **Maintainability** | Modularity, reusability, analysability, modifiability, testability |
| 8 | **Portability** | Adaptability, installability, replaceability |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Below are the **direct, board-exam-tested formulas**. Avoid using the `|` character in tables — use `\vert` instead.

$$
\boxed{\text{Density of Defects}} \quad DD = \frac{\text{Defects}}{\text{KLOC}} \quad \text{or} \quad DD = \frac{\text{Defects}}{\text{FP}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Code Churn Rate}} \quad CCR = \frac{\text{Lines Added} + \text{Lines Deleted}}{\text{Total Lines}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Availability}} \quad A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Reliability (Mean)}} \quad R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \quad \text{where} \quad \lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTTF}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Mean Time To Failure}} \quad \text{MTTF} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i}{N}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Mean Time To Repair}} \quad \text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i}{N}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe)}} \quad V(G) = E - N + 2P
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Halstead Metrics}} \quad V = N \log_2 \frac{\eta_1}{\eta_2} \quad ; \quad D = \frac{\eta_1}{2} \cdot \frac{N_2}{\eta_2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Customer Satisfaction Index}} \quad CSI = \frac{\sum w_i \cdot s_i}{\sum w_i}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Test Effectiveness}} \quad TE = \frac{\text{Defects Found by Test}}{\text{Total Defects Found}} \times 100\%
$$

| Metric | Formula | Standard Unit | KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|---|
| Defect Density | $DD = \text{Defects} / \text{KLOC}$ | defects / KLOC | Direct 3-mark |
| Availability | $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ | ratio (0–1) | 7-mark derivations |
| Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | probability | ESE Part B |
| Cyclomatic Complexity | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | integer | Theory + Numerical |
| Code Churn | $CCR = (\text{add} + \text{del}) / \text{total}$ | ratio | Internal choice |
| Halstead Volume | $V = N \log_2(\eta_1/\eta_2)$ | bits | Advanced |

> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas are used in **CMMI Level 4/5 audits**, **ISO 9001 software certifications**, **SaaS SLA reporting**, and **DevOps observability dashboards** (Grafana + Prometheus metrics feed straight into these formulas).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate Active.** All algebraic transitions are written explicitly. No truncation.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Quality Factor Rating from McCall's Model

McCall's model assigns each of the 11 factors a rating between **0 and 10**. The overall software quality index $Q_{total}$ is the weighted sum of these ratings.

$$
Q_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{11} w_i \cdot q_i
$$

where $w_i$ is the relative weight of factor $i$ (subjective but normalized) and $q_i$ is the rating (0–10) measured against metric criteria.

**Worked Example (KTU-style Numerical):**

Suppose a team rates the 11 factors with the following values:

| Factor | Weight $w_i$ | Rating $q_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| Correctness | 0.15 | 9 |
| Reliability | 0.12 | 8 |
| Efficiency | 0.10 | 7 |
| Integrity | 0.05 | 6 |
| Usability | 0.10 | 9 |
| Maintainability | 0.10 | 8 |
| Flexibility | 0.06 | 5 |
| Testability | 0.08 | 7 |
| Portability | 0.08 | 6 |
| Reusability | 0.06 | 5 |
| Interoperability | 0.10 | 7 |

**Step 1 — Verify normalization of weights.**

$$
\sum w_i = 0.15 + 0.12 + 0.10 + 0.05 + 0.10 + 0.10 + 0.06 + 0.08 + 0.08 + 0.06 + 0.10
$$

$$
\sum w_i = 1.00
$$

Weights sum to 1, satisfying the normalization constraint.

**Step 2 — Compute each weighted contribution.**

$$
0.15 \times 9 = 1.35
$$

$$
0.12 \times 8 = 0.96
$$

$$
0.10 \times 7 = 0.70
$$

$$
0.05 \times 6 = 0.30
$$

$$
0.10 \times 9 = 0.90
$$

$$
0.10 \times 8 = 0.80
$$

$$
0.06 \times 5 = 0.30
$$

$$
0.08 \times 7 = 0.56
$$

$$
0.08 \times 6 = 0.48
$$

$$
0.06 \times 5 = 0.30
$$

$$
0.10 \times 7 = 0.70
$$

**Step 3 — Sum all weighted contributions to obtain $Q_{total}$.**

$$
Q_{total} = 1.35 + 0.96 + 0.70 + 0.30 + 0.90 + 0.80 + 0.30 + 0.56 + 0.48 + 0.30 + 0.70
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 7.35
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

Since $Q_{total} \in [0, 10]$, a value of **7.35 / 10** indicates *good but improvable* quality. Generally, $\geq 8$ = release-ready; 6–8 = needs improvement; $< 6$ = reject.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Availability and Reliability from Operational Data

A production e-commerce site recorded the following data over 90 days:

- 4 incidents occurred.
- Total operational time = 720 hours.
- Downtimes (in hours): 2.0, 1.5, 3.0, 1.0

**Step 1 — Compute Mean Time To Failure (MTTF).**

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{\text{Total operational time}}{\text{Number of failures}} = \frac{720}{4}
$$

$$
\text{MTTF} = 180\ \text{hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Mean Time To Repair (MTTR).**

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum d_i}{N} = \frac{2.0 + 1.5 + 3.0 + 1.0}{4}
$$

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{7.5}{4} = 1.875\ \text{hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Availability.**

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{180}{180 + 1.875}
$$

$$
A = \frac{180}{181.875}
$$

$$
A \approx 0.9897\ \ (\text{or}\ 98.97\%)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Failure Rate $\lambda$.**

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTTF}} = \frac{1}{180}\ \text{per hour}
$$

$$
\lambda \approx 0.00556\ \text{failures/hour}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Reliability for a 24-hour window.**

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.00556 \times 24}
$$

$$
R(24) = e^{-0.1333}
$$

$$
R(24) \approx 0.8752\ \ (\text{or}\ 87.52\%)
$$

**Interpretation:** The system has a probability of surviving 24 hours without failure of about **87.5%**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Quality Models

| Criterion | McCall | Boehm | Dromey | ISO 9126 | ISO 25010 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Year introduced | 1977 | 1978 | 1988 | 1991 | 2011 |
| # Top-level factors | 11 | 7 | 5 | 6 | 8 |
| Sub-characteristics | Few | Few | Yes (property-based) | Yes | Yes (very detailed) |
| Hierarchical | Yes | Yes (tree) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Standards recognition | Academic | Academic | Academic | International | International |
| Security as factor | No | No | No | Yes (later amendment) | Yes (first-class) |
| KTU exam weight | High | Medium | Medium | Medium | **Very High** |
| Recommended for | Legacy theory | Tree visualization | Property-based | Old projects | Modern projects |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Software Product Evaluation Framework (Top-Down)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stakeholder Goals] --> B[Define Quality Requirements]
    B --> C[Select Quality Model e.g. ISO 25010]
    C --> D[Select Quality Metrics using GQM]
    D --> E[Execute Measurement Procedures]
    E --> F[Collect Raw Data]
    F --> G[Statistical Analysis]
    G --> H[Compare Against Benchmarks]
    H --> I{Acceptable?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Release / Certify Product]
    I -- No  --> K[Feedback to Development]
    K --> C
```

## 4.2 McCall's 11 Quality Factors — Hierarchical Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[McCall Quality Model]
    R --> P[Product Operation]
    R --> V[Product Revision]
    R --> T[Product Transition]
    P --> P1[Correctness]
    P --> P2[Reliability]
    P --> P3[Efficiency]
    P --> P4[Integrity]
    P --> P5[Usability]
    V --> V1[Maintainability]
    V --> V2[Flexibility]
    V --> V3[Testability]
    T --> T1[Portability]
    T --> T2[Reusability]
    T --> T3[Interoperability]
```

## 4.3 The GQM (Goal-Question-Metric) Refinement Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[Organization Goal e.g. Improve Reliability] --> Q1[Q1 How often does system fail]
    G --> Q2[Q2 How quickly does it recover]
    G --> Q3[Q3 What is the impact on user]
    Q1 --> M1[M1 MTBF]
    Q1 --> M2[M2 Failure Rate]
    Q2 --> M3[M3 MTTR]
    Q3 --> M4[M4 Customer Satisfaction Score]
```

## 4.4 ISO/IEC 25010 Quality Model Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ISO[ISO IEC 25010 Quality Model]
    ISO --> C1[Functional Suitability]
    ISO --> C2[Performance Efficiency]
    ISO --> C3[Compatibility]
    ISO --> C4[Usability]
    ISO --> C5[Reliability]
    ISO --> C6[Security]
    ISO --> C7[Maintainability]
    ISO --> C8[Portability]
    C5 --> S1[Maturity]
    C5 --> S2[Availability]
    C5 --> S3[Fault Tolerance]
    C5 --> S4[Recoverability]
    C6 --> SE1[Confidentiality]
    C6 --> SE2[Integrity]
    C6 --> SE3[Non Repudiation]
    C7 --> M1[Modularity]
    C7 --> M2[Analysability]
    C7 --> M3[Modifiability]
    C7 --> M4[Testability]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2023] *Define the GQM approach to software measurement. List its three levels with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- GQM = **Goal–Question–Metric**, a top-down measurement framework proposed by Basili & Rombach.
- **Level 1 — Goal:** Identify *what* the organization wants to achieve (e.g., improve reliability of the payment module).
- **Level 2 — Question:** Operationalize the goal into answerable questions (e.g., how many failures occur per month?).
- **Level 3 — Metric:** Define measurable quantities that answer the questions (e.g., MTTF, defect density).
- (Full 3 marks require all three levels + definition + example.)

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] *List any six quality characteristics defined by the ISO/IEC 25010 standard.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):** Functional Suitability, Performance Efficiency, Compatibility, Usability, Reliability, Security, Maintainability, Portability. *(Any six with brief 1-line description = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### 🔵 Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain McCall's software quality model in detail. Discuss its 11 quality factors grouped under the three perspectives, with one metric for each factor. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A banking software recorded 12 failures in 6 months of operation. The total downtime was 36 hours. Compute MTBF, MTTR, Availability, and the reliability for a 720-hour period. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

- McCall's model was proposed in **1977** by Jim McCall and colleagues to address the gap between technical software properties and user-oriented quality.
- It has **3 perspectives** and **11 quality factors**.
- **Product Operation perspective** (how the software runs):
  - *Correctness* — metric: defect density (defects/KLOC).
  - *Reliability* — metric: MTBF.
  - *Efficiency* — metric: response time / throughput.
  - *Integrity* — metric: number of unauthorized accesses.
  - *Usability* — metric: average training time / task completion time.
- **Product Revision perspective** (ease of maintenance):
  - *Maintainability* — metric: mean time to change (MTTC).
  - *Flexibility* — metric: cost of adaptation.
  - *Testability* — metric: cyclomatic complexity.
- **Product Transition perspective** (adaptation to new environments):
  - *Portability* — metric: number of platforms supported.
  - *Reusability* — metric: % of reused components.
  - *Interoperability* — metric: number of integrated systems.
- Rating scale: each factor scored 0–10; overall quality computed as a weighted sum.
- Advantages: first structured model; widely cited. Limitations: subjective weights; no security factor.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute MTBF.**

Total operational time for 6 months $\approx 6 \times 30 \times 24 = 4320$ hours.

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total operational time}}{\text{Number of failures}} = \frac{4320}{12}
$$

$$
\text{MTBF} = 360\ \text{hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute MTTR.**

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum d_i}{N} = \frac{36}{12}
$$

$$
\text{MTTR} = 3\ \text{hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Availability.**

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{360}{360 + 3}
$$

$$
A = \frac{360}{363} \approx 0.9917 \quad \text{or}\ 99.17\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Failure Rate $\lambda$.**

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{1}{360}
$$

$$
\lambda \approx 0.00278\ \text{failures/hour}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Reliability for $t = 720$ hours.**

$$
R(720) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.00278 \times 720}
$$

$$
R(720) = e^{-2.0} \approx 0.1353\ \text{or}\ 13.53\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Probability of surviving 720 hours (≈30 days) without a single failure is only **13.5%**, which is alarmingly low for a banking application — recommends redundancy.

---

### 🟢 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **(a)** Compare McCall's, Boehm's, and ISO/IEC 25010 quality models with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the Goal–Question–Metric (GQM) approach. Develop one complete GQM statement for evaluating the *security* of an online examination system and derive at least three metrics from it. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Aspect | McCall | Boehm | ISO 25010 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Year | 1977 | 1978 | 2011 |
| Structure | 11 factors in 3 perspectives | Hierarchical quality tree | 8 characteristics with sub-characteristics |
| Focus | User-perceived quality | Hierarchical decomposition | Modern, security-aware |
| Security factor | Absent | Absent | Present (first-class) |
| Limitation | Subjective weights | Hard to operationalize | Complex but rigorous |

- All three models use **hierarchical decomposition** from high-level characteristics to measurable sub-attributes.
- McCall's strength is its **practical usability**; Boehm's strength is its **tree visualization**; ISO 25010's strength is **international standardization** and coverage of security and compatibility.
- Use a comparison table + hierarchical diagram (refer to Section 4 of these notes).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

- **GQM Definition:** Top-down measurement paradigm where *Goals* define purpose, *Questions* operationalize goals, and *Metrics* provide quantifiable answers.
- **GQM Statement for Online Exam Security:**

> *Analyze* the *Online Examination System (OES)* for the purpose of *evaluating* its *security and integrity* from the *viewpoint of the examination authority* in the *production deployment environment*.

- **Derived Questions and Metrics:**

  - *Q1:* How often do unauthorized access attempts occur? → **M1:** Failed login rate per 1000 attempts.
  - *Q2:* How quickly are breaches detected and contained? → **M2:** Mean Time to Detect (MTTD), Mean Time to Contain (MTTC).
  - *Q3:* What is the integrity of answer submissions? → **M3:** Tamper rate (modified submissions / total submissions).
  - *Q4:* What is the cryptographic strength? → **M4:** Number of weak keys / weak cipher usage.
  - *Q5:* What is user trust? → **M5:** Post-exam survey score on confidentiality perception.

- **GQM Refinement Cycle:** Initial measurement → baseline comparison → improvement action → re-measurement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - ❌ Writing "McCall model has 11 characteristics" — wrong; ISO 25010 has 8 *characteristics*. McCall has 11 *factors*.
> - ❌ Confusing **MTBF** with **MTTR** — they are reciprocally distinct: MTBF = uptime between failures; MTTR = repair time.
> - ❌ Forgetting to normalize weights in McCall's quality index formula.
> - ❌ Skipping the unit (e.g., writing $DD = 0.5$ without specifying *defects per KLOC*).
> - ❌ In GQM, students often jump directly from Goal to Metric, bypassing the **Question** layer — KTU explicitly tests for it.
> - ❌ Writing $R(t) = 1 - \lambda t$ — that is the first-order Taylor approximation. The exact exponential form $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is expected.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Product Evaluation = Measuring + Modeling + Judging** the quality of a software product.
- ✅ **McCall's Model** → 11 factors in 3 perspectives (Operation, Revision, Transition). Rating scale 0–10.
- ✅ **Boehm's Model** → quality tree; emphasizes maintainability deeply.
- ✅ **Dromey's Model** → property-based; links quality to software properties.
- ✅ **ISO 9126** → 6 characteristics (international standard, now superseded).
- ✅ **ISO 25010** → **8 characteristics** with rich sub-characteristics — current KTU-mandated benchmark.
- ✅ **GQM Paradigm** → Goal → Question → Metric (top-down, Basili & Rombach).
- ✅ **Defect Density** $DD = \text{Defects}/\text{KLOC}$ — most quoted product metric.
- ✅ **Availability** $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$ — must be reported as ratio or %.
- ✅ **Reliability** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — exponential survival model.
- ✅ **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ — McCabe's metric for testability.
- ✅ **Code Churn** measures development instability — important for maintainability.
- ✅ A good evaluation requires **measurable metrics + validated data + meaningful interpretation**.
- ✅ For KTU 2024 ESE: focus on **ISO 25010 + McCall + GQM + Reliability/Availability formulas**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reviews
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reviews

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Review (IEEE 1028 Standard):** A systematic, disciplined, and documented evaluation of software work products by qualified personnel to identify defects, violations of development standards, and other problems. Reviews are a **static verification technique** — they do **not** require executing the software.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, *Reviews* constitute the principal **static V&V (Verification & Validation)** activity within the Software Quality Assurance (SQA) framework. They are positioned early in the software development lifecycle (SDLC) to detect and remove defects *before* costly dynamic testing begins.

### 1.2 Why Reviews Matter — The "1-10-100" Rule

| Defect Detection Stage | Relative Cost to Fix |
|---|---|
| **Reviews (Static)** | **1× (baseline cost)** |
| Testing (Dynamic) | **10×** |
| Post-Release Maintenance | **100× or more** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Every KTU exam paper tests your ability to differentiate between *static* (reviews, inspections) and *dynamic* (testing, execution) V&V techniques. Reviews are a **white-box, static** activity.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Newspaper Proofreader's Bench"

Imagine a senior newspaper editor who, before going to print, asks **three other journalists** to read an article simultaneously, looking for:
- Spelling & grammar errors
- Inconsistencies in facts
- Tone & style violations
- Missing or unclear sentences

After their individual reading, they convene a **short meeting** with the original writer, log every issue found, and ask the writer to fix them. This is *exactly* what a **Formal Technical Review (FTR)** does in software engineering — only the "article" is a software artifact (SRS, design, code, test plan) and the "errors" are **defects**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A review is *not* a meeting. Most of the productive defect-finding happens **before** the meeting, during *individual preparation*. The meeting is a *consolidation* step.

---

## 1.4 The Five Pillars of a Review (KTU Syllabus Framework)

1. **Pillar 1 — Identification of defects** (not solutions)
2. **Pillar 2 — Verification of standards compliance**
3. **Pillar 3 — Establishing consistency** with prior work products
4. **Pillar 4 — Knowledge transfer** among team members
5. **Pillar 5 — Project management awareness** through metrics

---

## 1.5 KTU Classification of Reviews

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies reviews along a **spectrum of formality**:

```
INFORMAL ───────────────────────────────► FORMAL

Peer Desk-Check  ◄──►  Walkthrough  ◄──►  Technical Review  ◄──►  Inspection
   (lightest)                                  (medium)            (most rigorous)
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always mention the **degree of formality**, the **number of reviewers**, and the **documented outputs** when describing a review type. Examiners award marks for these precise distinctions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IEEE 1028 Review Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The IEEE Standard **1028-2008 (Standard for Software Reviews and Audits)** defines a **6-phase review process**. KTU 2024 explicitly tests each phase in 3-mark short-answer questions.

### Phase 1 — Planning
- **Why:** Define the scope, criteria, participants, schedule, and entrance/exit conditions.
- **How:** The *Moderator* (also called the *Review Leader*) selects the review team, distributes the work product, and sets the meeting agenda.
- **Output:** Review plan document.

### Phase 2 — Individual Preparation (Overlooked but CRITICAL)
- **Why:** This is where **>50% of all defects are detected**.
- **How:** Each reviewer studies the work product *alone* and logs potential defects using a **review checklist**.
- **Output:** Personal notes / defect list per reviewer.

### Phase 3 — Review Meeting (The Actual Meeting)
- **Why:** Consolidate findings, classify severity, and assign rework owners.
- **How:** The *Recorder* (Scribe) writes a **review log**; the *Author* answers questions and clarifies.
- **Output:** Review report / Review log.

### Phase 4 — Rework
- **Why:** Fix the defects found.
- **How:** The **Author** modifies the work product to resolve each logged issue.
- **Output:** Updated work product.

### Phase 5 — Follow-Up
- **Why:** Verify that all rework was completed *and* that no new defects were introduced.
- **How:** The *Moderator* (or a designated reviewer) checks the reworked artifact.
- **Output:** Follow-up report; decision to **close** the review.

### Phase 6 — Closure (Often Implicit in KTU Texts)
- The work product is **accepted**, **rejected** (major rework required), or **accepted with action items** for minor follow-up.

---

## 2.2 Roles & Responsibilities Matrix (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** "List the roles in a formal review and their responsibilities" is a **3-mark** sure-shot question every KTU exam cycle.

| Role | Primary Responsibility | Key Trait |
|---|---|---|
| **Author** | Owns the work product; explains it; performs rework | Subject-matter expert on the artifact |
| **Moderator (Review Leader)** | Manages the review; plans; arbitrates; reports | Neutral — does NOT defend the author |
| **Reviewer (Inspector)** | Identifies defects; checks standards | Technically qualified in the review area |
| **Recorder (Scribe)** | Logs every issue, decision, and action item | Captures verbatim; does NOT filter |
| **Manager** | Decides on scheduling, staffing, resources; NOT in meeting | Ensures the review *happens* on time |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Conflating the **Moderator** with the **Manager**. The Manager does *not* attend the technical review meeting — they authorize and resource it, nothing more.

---

## 2.3 The Four Major Review Types (Deep Dive)

### 2.3.1 Walkthrough
- **Conducted by:** The **author** drives the meeting.
- **Goal:** **Knowledge transfer + defect discovery** through the author's narrative walk-through.
- **Formality:** Low to medium.
- **Meeting Style:** Author presents line-by-line; reviewers ask "what if…" questions.
- **Output:** Minutes of the walkthrough, list of action items.

### 2.3.2 Technical Review (Formal Technical Review — FTR)
- **Conducted by:** A small team (3–5 people) led by a **Moderator**.
- **Goal:** Identify defects, verify technical correctness, ensure conformance to standards.
- **Formality:** Medium to high.
- **Distinctive Features:**
  - Dedicates **meeting time** to peer review.
  - Uses **checklists**.
  - Produces a **written review report**.

### 2.3.3 Inspection (Most Formal — Fagan Inspection)
- **Originator:** Michael Fagan, IBM, 1976.
- **Goal:** **Detect, classify, and eliminate defects** as a formal, statistically measured process.
- **Formality:** **Highest** of all review types.
- **Distinctive Features:**
  - Uses a defined **process** (entry criteria → inspection → exit criteria).
  - **Entry Criteria:** Work product is ready; reviewers prepared.
  - **Exit Criteria:** All defects logged; all action items assigned.
  - **6 Roles:** Author, Moderator, Reader, Recorder, Inspector, Inspection Leader.
  - **Reader Role:** Walks the team through the work product — *paraphrasing* each section, not just reading verbatim.
  - **Root-Cause Analysis:** Inspection data is fed into a process-improvement loop.

### 2.3.4 Management Review
- **Conducted by:** **Management** (not technical staff).
- **Goal:** Evaluate the **state of the project**, the adequacy of the SQA plan, and budget/schedule.
- **Formality:** High, but non-technical.
- **Output:** Status reports, corrective action items.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Review Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical/Analytical Pattern:** Direct 3-mark questions often ask to *define* and *interpret* a metric. 14-mark problems may ask you to compute a metric from a small dataset.

| Metric | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \dfrac{\text{Defects found}}{\text{Work product size}}$ | Defects per unit size (LOC, pages, function points) | defects/LOC |
| **Inspection Rate** | $IR = \dfrac{\text{LOC reviewed}}{\text{Person-hours}}$ | Productivity of inspection | LOC/hr |
| **Defect Detection Rate (DDR)** | $DDR = \dfrac{\text{Defects found per review}}{\text{Total defect opportunities}}$ | Efficiency of the review | ratio (0–1) |
| **Review Yield (RY)** | $RY = \dfrac{\text{Defects found in review}}{\text{Defects found in review + Defects found post-review}}$ | Effectiveness of review catching defects | ratio (0–1) |
| **Review Cost** | $RC = (\text{Person-hours}) \times (\text{Hourly rate})$ | Direct monetary cost of the review | currency |
| **Cost of Finding a Defect** | $C_{defect} = \dfrac{RC}{\text{Defects found}}$ | Per-defect cost | currency |
| **Effort Ratio** | $E_{ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Review effort}}{\text{Development effort}}$ | Overhead due to reviews | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb from KTU Answers:** A healthy **review yield ≥ 0.80** indicates that the review caught most defects *before* testing. A low review yield signals either a *weak checklist* or *under-prepared reviewers*.

---

## 2.5 Review Checklists (The Secret Weapon)

Checklists are **tailored templates** that bias reviewers toward the kinds of defects historically common in that artifact. They are a **direct contributor to review effectiveness**.

**Example — Code Review Checklist (excerpt):**
- Are all variables initialized before use?
- Are array bounds checked?
- Are all loops guaranteed to terminate?
- Are error messages meaningful?
- Are functions doing only one thing?
- Is the code compliant with naming conventions?

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Mark Pattern:** You may be asked to *design a checklist* for a specific artifact (e.g., an SRS, a module test plan). Always include 5–8 high-quality items with brief rationale per item.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | How Reviews Save the Day |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace (DO-178C)** | Mandatory code reviews for every safety-critical line |
| **Medical Devices (FDA)** | Design reviews are *legal evidence* of due diligence |
| **Banking & Finance** | Security & regulatory compliance via review logs |
| **Open-Source (Linux)** | Peer code review before merging to mainline |
| **Automotive (ISO 26262)** | Fagan-style inspections for ASIL-rated modules |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In safety-critical domains, a missed defect can cost lives (Therac-25, Boeing 737 Max). Reviews provide the **paper trail** that regulators, courts, and auditors demand.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing Review Yield (14-Mark Standard Problem)

> A 2,000-LOC Java module is reviewed by a 4-member team. The review finds **38 defects**. After the review, the module enters unit testing, which uncovers **7 additional defects** that escaped the review. Compute:
> (a) Defect Density of the module
> (b) Review Yield
> (c) Cost per Defect Found, given the review consumed 16 person-hours at ₹500/hr.

### Step 1 — Identify Given Quantities

Let us formally define each input variable from the problem statement.

$$
\begin{aligned}
LOC &= 2000 \text{ lines of code} \\
N_{review} &= 38 \text{ defects caught by review} \\
N_{test} &= 7 \text{ defects caught by unit test (post-review)} \\
H_{review} &= 16 \text{ person-hours} \\
R_{hourly} &= \text{₹} \, 500 \text{ per hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Defect Density (Part a)

**Logic:** Defect density is the *total* defects (review + test) normalized by the artifact size. The denominator is LOC since the work product is source code.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= N_{review} + N_{test} \\
&= 38 + 7 \\
&= 45 \text{ defects}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
DD = \frac{N_{total}}{LOC} = \frac{45}{2000} = 0.0225 \text{ defects per LOC}
$$

Expressed in **KLOC (Thousand Lines of Code)** — the more common industry unit:

$$
DD_{KLOC} = 0.0225 \times 1000 = 22.5 \text{ defects/KLOC}
$$

> **Interpretation:** 22.5 defects per 1,000 lines of Java is **moderate** for non-critical software (industry average is 15–50 defects/KLOC at inspection time).

### Step 3 — Compute Review Yield (Part b)

**Logic:** Review yield is the *share* of total defects caught *during* the review (as opposed to those that leaked through).

$$
\begin{aligned}
RY &= \frac{N_{review}}{N_{review} + N_{test}} \\
&= \frac{38}{38 + 7} \\
&= \frac{38}{45} \\
&\approx 0.8444
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** An **RY ≈ 0.84** is **excellent** — well above the 0.80 threshold. The review process is highly effective.

### Step 4 — Compute Cost per Defect (Part c)

**Logic:** Cost per defect is total review cost divided by defects found *in the review* (not total defects, since the question asks "per defect found" in the review context).

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= H_{review} \times R_{hourly} \\
&= 16 \times 500 \\
&= \text{₹} \, 8{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
C_{defect} = \frac{C_{total}}{N_{review}} = \frac{8000}{38} \approx \text{₹} \, 210.53
$$

> **Interpretation:** For every ₹210 spent in this review, one defect was caught — vastly cheaper than finding it in production, where rework cost would be 10×–100× higher.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Inspection Effort Estimation (Analytical)

> A 1,500-LOC module is to undergo a **Fagan inspection**. Industry data suggests:
> - Preparation rate: **90 LOC/hour**
> - Meeting rate: **150 LOC/hour**
> - Rework rate: **20 LOC/hour**
> - Team size: **4 people** in the meeting, **4 people** in preparation
> Estimate total person-hours.

### Step 1 — Define Variables

$$
\begin{aligned}
LOC &= 1500 \\
R_{prep} &= 90 \text{ LOC/hr} \\
R_{meet} &= 150 \text{ LOC/hr} \\
R_{rework} &= 20 \text{ LOC/hr} \\
N_{team} &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Individual Phase Durations (Calendar Hours)

**Preparation Phase:**

$$
T_{prep} = \frac{LOC}{R_{prep}} = \frac{1500}{90} \approx 16.67 \text{ hours per reviewer}
$$

**Meeting Phase** (all reviewers in same room, parallel):

$$
T_{meet} = \frac{LOC}{R_{meet}} = \frac{1500}{150} = 10 \text{ hours of meeting time}
$$

**Rework Phase** (author only, applies to roughly 15% of code touched):

$$
\text{Assumed rework LOC} = 0.15 \times 1500 = 225 \text{ LOC}
$$

$$
T_{rework} = \frac{225}{R_{rework}} = \frac{225}{20} = 11.25 \text{ hours}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Total Person-Hours

**Logic:** Person-hours = (per-person effort) × (number of people in each phase). Preparation and rework are *serial* per person; the meeting is *parallel* across all reviewers.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{prep}^{total} &= T_{prep} \times N_{team} = 16.67 \times 4 = 66.67 \text{ PH} \\
H_{meet}^{total} &= T_{meet} \times N_{team} = 10 \times 4 = 40.00 \text{ PH} \\
H_{rework}^{total} &= T_{rework} \times 1 = 11.25 \text{ PH}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{H_{total} = 66.67 + 40.00 + 11.25 = 117.92 \text{ person-hours}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Almost **57%** of the effort is in **preparation**, confirming the KTU high-yield insight that *most defect detection happens before the meeting*.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Review Types (Exam-Ready Summary)

| Attribute | Walkthrough | Technical Review | **Inspection** | Management Review |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Formality** | Low | Medium | **High** | High |
| **Conducted by** | Author | Team + Moderator | **Inspection team** | Management |
| **Primary Goal** | Knowledge transfer + defects | Defects + standards | **Defect removal** | Project status |
| **Roles Defined** | No (ad-hoc) | Yes (5 roles) | **Yes (6 roles)** | N/A |
| **Uses Checklists** | Optional | **Yes** | **Yes (mandatory)** | N/A |
| **Entry/Exit Criteria** | No | Optional | **Yes (mandatory)** | N/A |
| **Metrics Collected** | Minimal | Moderate | **Extensive (statistical)** | Schedule/cost metrics |
| **Best For** | Early artifacts | Design, code | **Safety-critical code** | Project tracking |
| **Output Document** | Minutes | Review report | **Inspection report + log** | Status report |
| **Originated By** | — | Various | **Michael Fagan (1976)** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick (For Exams):** Think **"WAIT-MI"** — **W**alkthrough → **A**d-hoc, **I**nspection → **M**ost formal, **T**echnical → middle. **MI** for "Most Important" → **Inspection**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The IEEE 1028 Review Process — Sequential Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Author Completes Work Product] --> B[Phase 1: Planning - Moderator assembles team and distributes material]
    B --> C[Phase 2: Individual Preparation - Each reviewer studies the artifact and logs defects]
    C --> D[Phase 3: Review Meeting - Team consolidates findings with the Recorder]
    D --> E{Decision: Accept / Reject / Accept with Action Items}
    E -->|Accept| F[Phase 5: Closure - Work product is baselined]
    E -->|Reject| G[Phase 4: Rework - Author fixes logged defects]
    E -->|Accept with Items| G
    G --> H[Phase 5: Follow-up - Moderator verifies all fixes]
    H -->|All clear| F
    H -->|Issues remain| G
    F --> I[Process Improvement: Update checklists and metrics database]
```

> **Figure 4.1 — IEEE 1028 Six-Phase Review Lifecycle.** The loop between *Phase 4* and *Phase 5* captures the iterative nature of rework and verification.

---

## 4.2 Roles and Information Flow in a Formal Review

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Manager] -->|Authorizes and resources| B[Moderator]
    B -->|Distributes material| C1[Reviewer 1]
    B -->|Distributes material| C2[Reviewer 2]
    B -->|Distributes material| C3[Reviewer 3]
    C1 -->|Defect notes| D[Review Meeting]
    C2 -->|Defect notes| D
    C3 -->|Defect notes| D
    E[Author] -->|Explains work product| D
    F[Recorder] -->|Captures findings| D
    D -->|Review log| B
    B -->|Review report| A
    D -->|Action items| E
    E -->|Reworked product| G[Follow-up Verification]
    G -->|Accept or Reject| B
```

> **Figure 4.2 — Information Flow and Authority Hierarchy.** Notice the **Manager** is *outside* the technical loop — a critical KTU distinction.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Static vs. Dynamic Verification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Software Quality Assurance - SQA] --> B[Verification - Are we building the product right?]
    A --> C[Validation - Are we building the right product?]
    B --> D[Static Techniques - No code execution]
    B --> E[Dynamic Techniques - Code is executed]
    C --> E
    D --> D1[Walkthroughs]
    D --> D2[Technical Reviews]
    D --> D3[Inspections]
    D --> D4[Peer Desk-Checks]
    E --> E1[Unit Testing]
    E --> E2[Integration Testing]
    E --> E3[System Testing]
    E --> E4[Acceptance Testing]
```

> **Figure 4.3 — Position of Reviews in the SQA V&V Framework.** Reviews fall under **static verification**; this is the *first* line of defense before any testing begins.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Fagan Inspection Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Work product ready for inspection] --> A1[Entry Check: Are all preconditions met?]
    A1 -->|No| A2[Reject: Send back to author]
    A1 -->|Yes| B1[Planning: Inspection Leader assigns roles and materials]
    B1 --> B2[Overview: Author briefly presents the artifact]
    B2 --> B3[Individual Preparation: Each inspector studies and logs issues]
    B3 --> B4[Inspection Meeting: Reader paraphrases, team logs defects, Recorder captures]
    B4 --> B5[Reanalysis: Inspection Leader compiles and classifies the log]
    B5 --> C1[Rework: Author fixes defects]
    C1 --> C2[Follow-up: Inspection Leader verifies all fixes]
    C2 -->|All clear| C3[Exit Check: Are exit criteria met?]
    C3 -->|Yes| D1[Inspection Report: Logged for process improvement]
    C3 -->|No| B5
    A2 -->|Author fixes| A1
```

> **Figure 4.4 — Fagan Inspection Lifecycle.** The **Reader** role (paraphrasing) is unique to inspections and is a frequent KTU question topic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define a formal technical review. List any three roles in a formal review with their responsibilities.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Formal Technical Review (FTR)** is a structured, planned, and documented evaluation of a software work product by a team of qualified personnel, conducted to identify defects, verify adherence to standards, and promote consensus. It is led by a *Moderator* and follows a defined process including planning, individual preparation, a review meeting, rework, and follow-up.

**Three roles (1 mark for any 3 with correct responsibilities):**

1. **Author** — Owns the work product, explains it during the meeting, and performs the rework to address defects.
2. **Moderator (Review Leader)** — Plans the review, mediates discussions, ensures the meeting stays on agenda, and produces the final review report.
3. **Reviewer** — Identifies potential defects in the work product, both during individual preparation and the review meeting.
4. **Recorder** — Documents all defects, decisions, and action items raised during the meeting in the review log.
5. **Manager** — Provides organizational support, schedules the review, and authorizes resources — but does **not** attend the review meeting.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write "the manager leads the review." The **Moderator** leads the review; the **Manager** only *enables* it. This single error can cost 1 full mark.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between a walkthrough and an inspection.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Walkthrough | Inspection |
|---|---|---|
| **Led by** | The author | A trained inspection leader (Moderator) |
| **Formality** | Low / informal | Highly formal, follows a defined process |
| **Goal** | Knowledge transfer + defect identification | Systematic defect detection, classification, and statistical measurement |
| **Process Steps** | No strict entry/exit criteria | Strict entry and exit criteria mandatory |
| **Checklist Use** | Optional | Mandatory |
| **Output** | Minutes of meeting | Formal inspection report + defect log |
| **Roles** | No defined roles | Six defined roles including a *Reader* |

The **walkthrough** is best suited for *early* and *educational* reviews, while the **inspection** is reserved for *safety-critical* and *mature* work products where statistical defect data drives process improvement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write that *inspections* take less time than walkthroughs. The opposite is true: **inspections are the most time-consuming** but also the most effective. Don't get this backwards.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each part (a) and (b) carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a) Explain the IEEE 1028 standard review process in detail. List all six phases with their objectives. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **IEEE 1028-2008 Standard for Software Reviews and Audits** defines a structured, six-phase process applicable to all types of formal software reviews.

1. **Phase 1 — Planning** *(1 mark)*
   - **Objective:** Establish the scope, schedule, participants, and entry/exit criteria of the review.
   - The **Moderator** is appointed, the work product is identified, the team is assembled, and the review package (artifact, checklists, prior logs) is distributed.

2. **Phase 2 — Individual Preparation** *(1 mark)*
   - **Objective:** Allow each reviewer to thoroughly study the artifact and log potential defects using the supplied checklist.
   - This is the **most defect-rich** phase; reviewers work independently, often at their own pace, and document issues, questions, and inconsistencies.

3. **Phase 3 — Review Meeting (Review/Inspection Meeting)** *(1 mark)*
   - **Objective:** Consolidate individual findings, classify defects, and assign action items.
   - The **Recorder** logs every issue verbatim. The **Author** answers questions and clarifies intent. No rework is performed *during* the meeting.

4. **Phase 4 — Rework** *(1 mark)*
   - **Objective:** Resolve all defects and issues raised in the review.
   - The **Author** updates the work product. Only items logged in the review log are addressed — no scope creep.

5. **Phase 5 — Follow-Up** *(1 mark)*
   - **Objective:** Verify that every rework item has been correctly addressed and no new defects have been introduced.
   - The **Moderator** (or a designated verifier) re-inspects the reworked artifact against the review log.

6. **Phase 6 — Closure (often implicit)** *(1 mark)*
   - **Objective:** Officially close the review and archive records.
   - A **review report** is finalized, metrics are recorded, and the work product is **baselined**, **rejected** for major rework, or **conditionally accepted** with action items.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The IEEE 1028 process is **scale-invariant** — it applies to walkthroughs, technical reviews, and inspections with appropriate rigor. The discipline of *individual preparation* and *documented closure* is the heart of its effectiveness.

---

**(b) Describe the Fagan Inspection method in detail. Highlight its six roles and the entry/exit criteria. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Fagan Inspection**, introduced by **Michael E. Fagan of IBM in 1976**, is the most rigorous and structured form of software review. It is a *statistical* process — every defect is classified, counted, and fed into process improvement.

**Process Steps (3 marks):**

1. **Planning** — Inspection Leader selects the team and distributes materials.
2. **Overview** — Author gives a brief walkthrough (only for context, not for defect finding).
3. **Individual Preparation** — Each inspector studies the artifact and notes defects.
4. **Inspection Meeting** — The **Reader** paraphrases the artifact; the team discusses and classifies defects; the **Recorder** logs them.
5. **Rework** — Author fixes the defects.
6. **Follow-Up** — Inspection Leader verifies all fixes; the inspection is closed or restarted.

**Six Roles (3 marks):**

| Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Author** | Owns the artifact; performs rework; **not** a reviewer in the meeting |
| **Inspection Leader (Moderator)** | Manages the entire inspection; arbitrates; reports |
| **Reader** | Walks the team through the artifact **paraphrasing** each section |
| **Recorder** | Logs every defect verb

topics.title: Software Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Software Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is Software Testing?

**Software Testing** is the systematic, planned activity of executing a program or system with the intent of finding defects. It is the process of evaluating and verifying that a software product or application does what it is supposed to do, thereby ensuring the delivery of a **defect-free**, **reliable**, and **high-quality** product to the end-user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> Software testing is an *investigative process* conducted to provide stakeholders with information about the quality of the product or service under test, with respect to the context in which it is intended to operate. It is the cornerstone activity of the **Software Quality Assurance (SQA)** process.

### Formal IEEE Definition
According to **IEEE Standard 829**, software testing is *"the process of operating a system or component under specified conditions, observing or recording the results, and making an evaluation of some aspect of the system or component."*

### Core Objectives of Software Testing (The 5 Pillars)

1. **Defect Detection** — Identify bugs, errors, and gaps against the original requirements.
2. **Verification & Validation (V&V)** — Ensure the product is built *right* (verification) and the *right* product is built (validation).
3. **Risk Reduction** — Minimize the probability of failure in the operational environment.
4. **Confidence Building** — Provide measurable confidence to stakeholders and end-users.
5. **Compliance & Audit** — Meet contractual, regulatory, and legal requirements (e.g., ISO 25010, CMMI).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Testing a New Car Before Delivery":**
> Imagine an automobile company is about to deliver a brand-new car to a customer. Before handover, the car goes through a sequence of tests:
>
> - **Unit Testing** → Each individual part (brake pad, headlight, engine piston) is tested in isolation by the manufacturing unit.
> - **Integration Testing** → Parts are assembled and tested together — does the brake work when the brake pedal is pressed? Does the headlight turn on when the switch is flipped?
> - **System Testing** → The entire car is driven on a test track — does it accelerate, brake, steer, and turn on all signals as a complete unit?
> - **Acceptance Testing** → The customer takes a test drive to confirm the car meets *their* personal requirements.
>
> **Software Testing is exactly this process**, but for a software system. Each "part" of the software is checked, the parts are tested together, the whole system is validated, and finally the user accepts it.

---

## 1.3 The Testing Mindset: Why "Breaking" Software is a Good Thing

A common misconception is that testers *destroy* software. In reality, testers are the **last line of defense** before the user encounters a defect. The famous quote by **Edsger Dijkstra** captures this perfectly:

> *"Program testing can be used to show the presence of bugs, but never to show their absence!"*

This means **exhaustive testing is impossible** — we can only prove that defects *exist*, not that the software is *100% defect-free*. Hence, we use **test strategies** and **test case design techniques** to maximize defect detection with optimal effort.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology — Bug, Defect, Error, Failure, Fault

| Term | Formal Definition | Example |
|------|-------------------|---------|
| **Error** | A human mistake in the code (made by the developer). | Typo: writing `==` instead of `=` in a condition. |
| **Defect / Bug / Fault** | An imperfection in the software artifact that *may* cause a failure. | The login button does not respond to a click. |
| **Failure** | The observable, incorrect behavior of the system when executed. | The application crashes when invalid email is entered. |
| **Root Cause** | The fundamental reason that triggered the defect chain. | A missing null-pointer check in the input handler. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Many students interchange *bug*, *defect*, *fault*, and *error*. Use **error** for the human action, **defect/fault** for the artifact, and **failure** for the observed runtime behavior. This distinction often fetches a 1-mark bonus in 2-mark definitions.

---

## 1.5 The Testing Quadrant (Agile Testing)

Agile testing classifies tests into four quadrants, each supporting a different purpose:

- **Q1 (Unit/Component Tests)** — Technology-facing, supports the team (automated).
- **Q2 (System/Integration Tests)** — Technology-facing, supports the team.
- **Q3 (System/User Acceptance Tests)** — Business-facing, supports the team in critiquing the product.
- **Q4 (Exploratory/Usability Tests)** — Business-facing, supports the product (manual).

This framework helps teams plan **what to automate**, **what to test manually**, and **what the business must own**.

---

## 1.6 Why Software Testing is Indispensable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of a Bug in Production (Industry Statistics):**
> - A bug found during the **requirements phase** costs roughly **$100** to fix.
> - A bug found during **system testing** costs roughly **$1,500** to fix.
> - A bug found **after release in production** costs roughly **$10,000+** to fix.
>
> *(Source: IBM Systems Sciences Institute — well-cited industry data)*
>
> This is the **1:10:100 Rule** in software engineering — testing early is exponentially cheaper than testing late.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Seven Principles of Software Testing (ISTQB Foundation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These seven principles are **HIGH-YIELD** for KTU 2-mark and 6-mark questions. Memorize them by name and example.

1. **Testing shows the presence of defects, not their absence.**
   - Testing reduces the *probability* of undiscovered defects, but cannot prove that none exist.

2. **Exhaustive testing is impossible.**
   - Instead, we use techniques like **equivalence partitioning** and **risk-based testing**.

3. **Early testing saves time and money.**
   - Apply the **V-Model** — test planning starts during requirements gathering, not after coding.

4. **Defects cluster together.**
   - The **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)** — 80% of defects are usually found in 20% of the modules.

5. **Beware of the pesticide paradox.**
   - If the same test cases are run repeatedly, they stop finding new defects. Tests must be **regularly reviewed and revised**.

6. **Testing is context-dependent.**
   - The approach for testing a safety-critical medical device is vastly different from testing a mobile game.

7. **Absence-of-errors is a fallacy.**
   - A software that is 99% bug-free but does not meet the *user's needs* is useless.

---

## 2.2 Verification vs. Validation (V&V) — The KTU Favourite

> [!NOTE]
> **This is one of the most frequently asked 3-mark and 7-mark questions in KTU SQA papers.**

| Aspect | **Verification** | **Validation** |
|--------|------------------|----------------|
| **Definition** | *Are we building the product right?* | *Are we building the right product?* |
| **Focus** | Internal consistency, design, code, specs. | Real-world usage and user needs. |
| **Timing** | Performed **during development** (static). | Performed **after development** (dynamic). |
| **Methods** | Reviews, walkthroughs, inspections, desk-checking. | Black-box, white-box, integration, system, UAT. |
| **Activity Type** | **Static Testing** (no code execution). | **Dynamic Testing** (code is executed). |
| **Question Answered** | Does the design match the specification? | Does the system meet the user's actual needs? |
| **Example** | Inspecting an SRS document for clarity. | Executing the login module to confirm it works. |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** **V**erification = **V**iewing the documents (static). **V**alidation = **V**alidating by running (dynamic).

---

## 2.3 Test Case Design Techniques — Black-Box & White-Box

### 2.3.1 Black-Box (Specification-Based) Techniques
The tester views the software as a **black box** — only inputs and outputs matter. Internal code structure is **not** examined.

**A. Equivalence Partitioning (EP)**
- Divide the input domain into **classes (partitions)** of equivalent data, such that testing one representative value from each class is sufficient.
- *Example:* For an input field that accepts ages `1 to 100`, valid partitions = `{1, 50, 100}`, invalid = `{0, 101, -5, "abc"}`.

**B. Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)**
- Tests the values at the **edges** of equivalence partitions, where defects are most likely.
- *Example:* For the range `1 to 100`, test `0, 1, 2, 99, 100, 101`.
- Formula: For range `[a, b]`, test `a-1, a, a+1, b-1, b, b+1` (**6 boundary values**).

**C. Decision Table Testing**
- Tabulates inputs (conditions) and their corresponding outputs (actions) to capture complex business rules.
- Useful when combinations of inputs trigger different outputs.

**D. State Transition Testing**
- Models the system as a **finite state machine (FSM)**. Tests valid/invalid state transitions.
- *Example:* An ATM transitions from `Idle → CardInserted → PINEntered → CashDispensed → Idle`.

**E. Use Case Testing**
- Each **use case** (a sequence of user-system interactions) becomes a test case.

### 2.3.2 White-Box (Structure-Based) Techniques
The tester examines the **internal logic**, branches, and paths in the source code.

**A. Statement Coverage**
- Execute every statement at least once.
- Formula: $\text{Statement Coverage} = \frac{\text{Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100\%$

**B. Branch Coverage (Decision Coverage)**
- Execute every branch (true/false outcome) at least once.
- Formula: $\text{Branch Coverage} = \frac{\text{Branches Executed}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100\%$

**C. Path Coverage**
- Execute every independent path from entry to exit. (Most exhaustive — often impractical.)

**D. Condition Coverage (MC/DC)**
- Each boolean sub-expression must evaluate to both `true` and `false`.

**E. Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe's Metric)**
- Determines the minimum number of independent paths to test.
- Formula: $V(G) = E - N + 2P$
  - $E$ = number of edges in the control flow graph.
  - $N$ = number of nodes.
  - $P$ = number of connected components (usually 1).
- Alternate formula: $V(G) = \text{Number of decision points} + 1$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Testing Metrics & Coverage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Table — Memorize for KTU Numerical & Short-Answer Questions**

| Metric / Formula | Definition | Units / Range |
|------------------|------------|----------------|
| $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | Cyclomatic complexity (McCabe) | Integer ≥ 1 |
| $V(G) = \pi + 1$ | Cyclomatic complexity using decision points ($\pi$ = predicate nodes) | Integer ≥ 1 |
| $\text{Statement Coverage} = \frac{S_{\text{ex}}}{S_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % of statements executed | 0% – 100% |
| $\text{Branch Coverage} = \frac{B_{\text{ex}}}{B_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % of branches executed | 0% – 100% |
| $\text{Path Coverage} = \frac{P_{\text{ex}}}{P_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % of independent paths executed | 0% – 100% |
| $\text{Defect Density} = \frac{D}{K}$ (or per KLOC) | Defects per unit size (e.g., per 1000 LOC) | Defects/KLOC |
| $\text{Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)} = \frac{D_{\text{removed pre-release}}}{D_{\text{removed pre-release}} + D_{\text{released}}} \times 100$ | Effectiveness of pre-release testing | 0% – 100% |
| $\text{Reliability} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | System reliability metric | 0 – 1 |
| $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$ | Mean Time Between Failures | Hours / cycles |
| $\text{Test Effectiveness} = \frac{\text{Defects found in testing}}{\text{Total defects (testing + post-release)}} \times 100$ | % of defects caught before release | 0% – 100% |
| $E = \frac{F_{\text{ex}}}{F_{\text{total}}}$ (Equivalence Partitioning efficiency) | Fractions of partitions tested | 0 – 1 |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values (e.g., $\vert V(G) \vert$) in any future LaTeX expressions. Never use `|` to avoid breaking markdown tables.

---

## 2.5 Software Testing Life Cycle (STLC)

The STLC defines the phases of testing, parallel to the **Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)**:

1. **Requirement Analysis** — Identify testable requirements; clarify ambiguities.
2. **Test Planning** — Define the test strategy, resources, schedule, and exit criteria. Output: **Test Plan document**.
3. **Test Case Development** — Write detailed test cases, scripts, and test data. Output: **Test Case document**.
4. **Test Environment Setup** — Deploy software on hardware/software configurations that mirror production.
5. **Test Execution** — Run test cases; log defects; track actual vs. expected results.
6. **Test Closure** — Archive test artifacts; analyze lessons learned; release the **Test Closure Report**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Often Asks:** *"List the phases of STLC with deliverables."* Memorize the deliverable of each phase (Test Plan, Test Case, Defect Report, Test Summary Report, etc.).

---

## 2.6 Levels of Testing (The Testing Pyramid)

| Level | Scope | Who Tests | When |
|-------|-------|-----------|------|
| **Unit Testing** | Individual function/method/class. | Developer | During coding (TDD). |
| **Integration Testing** | Interaction between modules/components. | Developer / Tester | After unit testing. |
| **System Testing** | Complete integrated system. | Independent test team. | Before UAT. |
| **Acceptance Testing (UAT)** | Business & user requirements. | End-user / client. | Pre-release. |

### Types of Integration Testing

- **Top-Down Integration** — Test from the top (main module) down to stubs. Pros: early skeleton. Cons: heavy stubs.
- **Bottom-Up Integration** — Test from the bottom (leaf modules) up using drivers. Pros: no stubs. Cons: late system test.
- **Sandwich (Hybrid) Integration** — Combines both. Best for large systems.
- **Big-Bang Integration** — All modules integrated at once. Cons: hard to isolate defects.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of Testing Metrics

- **Cyclomatic Complexity** guides code review and refactoring (high $V(G)$ → high risk).
- **Defect Density** compares the quality of different software modules or releases.
- **DRE** quantifies the effectiveness of the test team and helps justify QA investment.
- **Reliability ($MTBF$)** is critical for **safety-critical systems** (avionics, medical devices, nuclear plants).
- **Branch Coverage ≥ 85%** is a common contractual requirement in government/defense software projects.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Cyclomatic Complexity from Source Code (McCabe's Metric)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most frequently asked numerical problem in KTU SQA Module 3. Practice this format.**

### Problem Statement
Compute the **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G)$ for the following C function using McCabe's three methods, and identify the minimum number of test cases required for path coverage.

```c
int calculateDiscount(int price, int age, int isMember) {
    int discount = 0;                              // statement 1
    if (price > 1000 && age < 60) {                // decision 1
        discount = 10;                             // statement 2
        if (isMember == 1) {                       // decision 2
            discount = discount + 5;               // statement 3
        } else {
            discount = discount + 2;               // statement 4
        }
    } else if (age >= 60) {                        // decision 3
        discount = 20;                             // statement 5
    } else {
        discount = 0;                              // statement 6
    }
    return discount;                               // statement 7
}
```

---

### Method 1: Using Predicate Nodes (Decision Points)

**Step 1: Identify the predicate (decision) nodes in the code.**

A predicate node is any statement that contains a conditional branch — `if`, `else if`, `&&`, `||`, `for`, `while`, `do-while`, `case`.

Scanning the code:

| Line | Decision Construct | Predicate Node? |
|------|--------------------|-----------------|
| `if (price > 1000 && age < 60)` | Compound condition with `&&` (treated as ONE predicate node in McCabe's original method, but expanded into two for modified condition/decision coverage). | **Yes (1)** |
| `if (isMember == 1)` | Simple if condition. | **Yes (2)** |
| `else if (age >= 60)` | Else-if condition. | **Yes (3)** |

**Step 2: Count the predicate nodes.**

$$
\pi = 3 \text{ (number of decision points)}
$$

**Step 3: Apply McCabe's formula $V(G) = \pi + 1$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= \pi + 1 \\
V(G) &= 3 + 1 \\
V(G) &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** There are **4 independent paths** in this function. The minimum number of test cases needed to cover all paths is **4**.

---

### Method 2: Using the Control Flow Graph (Edges & Nodes)

**Step 1: Draw the control flow graph (CFG).**

We construct the CFG with the following nodes (representing statements/conditions) and edges (representing control flow).

For clarity, we use the following node labels:

- Node `A1` — entry.
- Node `B1` — `if (price > 1000 && age < 60)` (decision 1).
- Node `C2` — `discount = 10;` (statement 2).
- Node `D2` — `if (isMember == 1)` (decision 2).
- Node `E3` — `discount = discount + 5;` (statement 3).
- Node `F3` — `discount = discount + 2;` (statement 4).
- Node `G2` — `else if (age >= 60)` (decision 3).
- Node `H5` — `discount = 20;` (statement 5).
- Node `I5` — `discount = 0;` (statement 6).
- Node `J7` — `return discount;` (statement 7).
- Node `K7` — exit.

**Step 2: Count nodes and edges.**

$$
N = 11 \text{ (nodes A1, B1, C2, D2, E3, F3, G2, H5, I5, J7, K7)}
$$

$$
E = 14 \text{ (edges connecting the nodes)}
$$

(Detailed edge list: A1→B1, B1→C2, B1→G2, C2→D2, D2→E3, D2→F3, E3→J7, F3→J7, G2→H5, G2→I5, H5→J7, I5→J7, J7→K7, and an extra edge from the `&&` short-circuit logic to count the compound predicate as two — totaling 14 edges. The exact count is **E = 14**.)

**Step 3: Apply $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ with $P = 1$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= E - N + 2P \\
V(G) &= 14 - 11 + 2(1) \\
V(G) &= 14 - 11 + 2 \\
V(G) &= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** A slight discrepancy (4 vs. 5) arises based on whether compound boolean conditions (`&&`) are counted as one or two predicate nodes. In KTU exams, use **Method 1 (predicate nodes)** and clearly state your assumption about compound conditions to avoid losing marks.

---

### Method 3: Using Regions in the CFG

**Step 1: Count the bounded regions in the CFG.**

Visualize the CFG: there is one outer (unbounded) region and bounded regions for each branch.

- Region 1: `isMember == 1` true branch.
- Region 2: `isMember == 1` false branch.
- Region 3: `else if (age >= 60)` true branch.
- Region 4: `else if (age >= 60)` false branch (discount = 0).
- Region 5: outer region (the "world outside" the function).

$$
\text{Regions} = 5
$$

**Step 2: Apply $V(G) = \text{Regions}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= 5 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Final Answer and Test Case Derivation

$$
V(G) = 4 \text{ (using Method 1 — standard KTU approach)}
$$

**The 4 independent test paths are:**

| Test ID | Path | Inputs (price, age, isMember) | Expected Output |
|---------|------|-------------------------------|-----------------|
| **T1** | Entry → C2 → E3 → J7 (Both conditions true, member) | (1500, 30, 1) | 15 |
| **T2** | Entry → C2 → F3 → J7 (First true, not member) | (1500, 30, 0) | 12 |
| **T3** | Entry → H5 → J7 (Senior citizen path) | (800, 65, 0) | 20 |
| **T4** | Entry → I5 → J7 (Default no-discount) | (500, 30, 0) | 0 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mark Distribution (Typical 7-mark question):**
> - Predicate identification: 1 mark.
> - Formula application: 1 mark.
> - Correct $V(G)$: 1 mark.
> - Listing independent paths: 2 marks.
> - Test case derivation: 2 marks.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Equivalence Partitioning & Boundary Value Analysis

### Problem Statement
A login form accepts a **password** that must be between **8 and 16 characters** long, contain at least one uppercase letter, and one digit. Apply **Equivalence Partitioning (EP)** and **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)** to derive test cases.

---

### Step 1: Identify Input Conditions

- **Length constraint:** $8 \leq L \leq 16$
- **Uppercase constraint:** At least one `[A-Z]`.
- **Digit constraint:** At least one `[0-9]`.

### Step 2: Equivalence Partitions

| Partition | Class Description | Example Value | Validity |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------|----------|
| **E1** | Valid length, valid format | `"Hello123"` | Valid |
| **E2** | Length < 8 | `"Ab1"` (3 chars) | Invalid |
| **E3** | Length > 16 | `"Abcdefghij1234567"` (17 chars) | Invalid |
| **E4** | No uppercase | `"abcdef1"` | Invalid |
| **E5** | No digit | `"Abcdefgh"` | Invalid |
| **E6** | Empty / null | `""` | Invalid |

### Step 3: Boundary Value Analysis (On Length)

For the range `[8, 16]`, BVA requires testing:

- `7` (just below minimum)
- `8` (minimum boundary)
- `9` (just above minimum)
- `15` (just below maximum)
- `16` (maximum boundary)
- `17` (just above maximum)

### Step 4: Final Test Case Table

| TC ID | Input | Length | Format | Expected Result |
|-------|-------|--------|--------|-----------------|
| TC01 | `"Hello123"` | 8 | Valid | **Accept** |
| TC02 | `"HelloWorld1234ABC"` | 17 | Valid | **Reject** (too long) |
| TC03 | `"Ab1"` | 3 | Valid | **Reject** (too short) |
| TC04 | `"hello123"` | 8 | No uppercase | **Reject** |
| TC05 | `"HELLOabc"` | 8 | No digit | **Reject** |
| TC06 | `""` (empty) | 0 | N/A | **Reject** |
| TC07 | 7-char valid format | 7 | Valid | **Reject** |
| TC08 | 8-char valid format | 8 | Valid | **Accept** |
| TC09 | 9-char valid format | 9 | Valid | **Accept** |
| TC10 | 15-char valid format | 15 | Valid | **Accept** |
| TC11 | 16-char valid format | 16 | Valid | **Accept** |
