| TC12 | 17-char valid format | 17 | Valid | **Reject** |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) Calculation

### Problem Statement
A software project of **50,000 LOC** was released. During testing, the team found **180 defects**. After release, customers reported **20 more defects**. Calculate the **Defect Density (DD)** and **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)**.

### Step 1: Defect Density

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Defects} &= D_{\text{test}} + D_{\text{release}} = 180 + 20 = 200 \\
\text{KLOC} &= \frac{50{,}000}{1{,}000} = 50 \\
\text{Defect Density} &= \frac{D}{\text{KLOC}} = \frac{200}{50} = 4 \text{ defects/KLOC}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Defect Removal Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DRE} &= \frac{D_{\text{test}}}{D_{\text{test}} + D_{\text{release}}} \times 100 \\
\text{DRE} &= \frac{180}{180 + 20} \times 100 \\
\text{DRE} &= \frac{180}{200} \times 100 \\
\text{DRE} &= 90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** 90% of the defects were caught *before* release. A mature QA process aims for DRE ≥ 95% for safety-critical systems.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMICS

## 4.1 The V-Model of Software Testing (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The **V-Model** maps each development phase to a corresponding testing phase. It visually demonstrates that test planning begins *in parallel* with the corresponding development activity.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DEV["Development Phases (Left Arm of V)"]
        A1["User Requirements"]
        A2["System Requirements"]
        A3["High-Level Design (HLD)"]
        A4["Detailed Design (LLD)"]
        A5["Implementation / Coding"]
    end

    subgraph TEST["Testing Phases (Right Arm of V)"]
        B1["Acceptance Testing"]
        B2["System Testing"]
        B3["Integration Testing"]
        B4["Unit Testing"]
    end

    A1 -.->|"verified by"| B1
    A2 -.->|"verified by"| B2
    A3 -.->|"verified by"| B3
    A4 -.->|"verified by"| B4
    A5 -.->|"supports"| B4
```

> **Read the diagram top-down:** A *Requirements Specification* is the basis for *Acceptance Testing*. A *System Design* is the basis for *System Testing*. A *Detailed Design* is the basis for *Unit Testing*. The V-Shape ensures **early test planning** — a core ISTQB principle.

---

## 4.2 The Software Testing Life Cycle (STLC) — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["1. Requirement Analysis<br/>Deliverable: RTM"] --> P2["2. Test Planning<br/>Deliverable: Test Plan"]
    P2 --> P3["3. Test Case Development<br/>Deliverable: Test Cases, Scripts"]
    P3 --> P4["4. Test Environment Setup<br/>Deliverable: Test Bed Ready"]
    P4 --> P5["5. Test Execution<br/>Deliverable: Defect Reports, Test Logs"]
    P5 --> P6["6. Test Cycle Closure<br/>Deliverable: Test Summary Report"]
    P5 -->|"If defects found"| P3
    P6 --> P1
```

> The bidirectional arrow `P5 → P3` represents **regression testing cycles** — when a defect is fixed, the affected test cases are re-executed.

---

## 4.3 Levels of Testing — The Testing Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Acceptance Testing (UAT)"] --> L2["System Testing"]
    L2 --> L3["Integration Testing"]
    L3 --> L4["Unit Testing"]
    L4 --> F["Foundation: Test Strategy & Test Plan"]
```

> The **pyramid shape** is intentional: there are *many* unit tests (foundation, fast, cheap) and progressively *fewer* tests at higher levels (slower, costlier, broader scope).

---

## 4.4 Black-Box vs. White-Box Testing — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BB["Black-Box Testing (Specification-Based)"]
        BB1["Equivalence Partitioning"]
        BB2["Boundary Value Analysis"]
        BB3["Decision Table Testing"]
        BB4["State Transition Testing"]
        BB5["Use Case Testing"]
    end

    subgraph WB["White-Box Testing (Structure-Based)"]
        WB1["Statement Coverage"]
        WB2["Branch / Decision Coverage"]
        WB3["Condition Coverage"]
        WB4["Path Coverage"]
        WB5["Cyclomatic Complexity"]
    end

    INPUT["Test Input"] --> BB
    INPUT --> WB
    BB --> OUTPUT["Observed Output"]
    WB --> OUTPUT
```

> **Note:** Black-box testing focuses on *what* the system does (functional behavior), while white-box testing focuses on *how* it is implemented (internal logic).

---

## 4.5 Defect Life Cycle (Bug Workflow) — State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New
    New --> Assigned: Test Lead assigns
    Assigned --> Open: Developer starts work
    Open --> Fixed: Developer fixes code
    Open --> Rejected: Not a bug / Duplicate
    Rejected --> Closed
    Fixed --> Retest: QA retests
    Retest --> Verified: Pass
    Retest --> Reopen: Fail
    Reopen --> Open
    Verified --> Closed
    Closed --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Draw the defect life cycle and explain each state."* The diagram above is the **standard ISTQB state model**. In a 7-mark question, explain **5–6 states** with one-line descriptions (e.g., "New = defect logged but not yet reviewed").

---

## 4.6 Integration Testing Strategies — Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TD["Top-Down Integration"]
        TD1["Main Module Tested First"]
        TD2["Sub-modules replaced by STUBS"]
        TD3["Depth-First or Breadth-First traversal"]
    end

    subgraph BU["Bottom-Up Integration"]
        BU1["Leaf Modules Tested First"]
        BU2["Higher modules replaced by DRIVERS"]
        BU3["Cluster tested, then integrated upward"]
    end

    subgraph SAN["Sandwich (Hybrid) Integration"]
        SAN1["Combines Top-Down + Bottom-Up"]
        SAN2["Used in large real-time systems"]
    end

    subgraph BB["Big-Bang Integration"]
        BB1["All modules integrated at once"]
        BB2["Risky: defects hard to isolate"]
    end

    INPUT["Integration Test Strategy Selection"] --> TD
    INPUT --> BU
    INPUT --> SAN
    INPUT --> BB
```

> **Decision Heuristic (KTU Exam Tip):** Choose **Top-Down** for early UI prototyping. Choose **Bottom-Up** when low-level modules are critical (e.g., OS kernels). Choose **Sandwich** for very large systems. Avoid **Big-Bang** in professional projects.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2–3 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

> **Q: Define Software Testing. List any four principles of software testing.**

#### Model Answer:

**Definition:** Software testing is the process of evaluating and verifying that a software product or application performs its intended functions correctly, and meets the specified requirements, with the goal of finding defects and ensuring quality.

**Four Principles:**

1. **Testing shows the presence of defects, not their absence** — Testing can prove defects exist but never that there are no defects.
2. **Exhaustive testing is impossible** — It is impractical to test all possible inputs; we use techniques like equivalence partitioning.
3. **Early testing saves time and money** — Bugs found early (during requirements/design) cost much less to fix.
4. **Defects cluster together (Pareto Principle)** — Most defects are concentrated in a small number of modules (80/20 rule).

*(Other valid principles: Pesticide Paradox, Testing is context-dependent, Absence-of-errors is a fallacy.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

> **Q: Differentiate between Verification and Validation. Give one example for each.**

#### Model Answer:

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|--------|--------------|------------|
| **Definition** | Are we building the product *right*? | Are we building the *right* product? |
| **Type** | Static (no code execution) | Dynamic (code is executed) |
| **Activity** | Reviews, inspections, walkthroughs. | Unit, integration, system, acceptance testing. |
| **Example** | Reviewing the SRS document to check for completeness. | Executing the login module to verify it authenticates users correctly. |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU Module-Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **ONE** question from the choice provided. Each question has two sub-parts (typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the different **levels of testing** in software engineering with a suitable diagram.
>
> **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Apply **Equivalence Partitioning** and **Boundary Value Analysis** to derive test cases for a function `validateAge(int age)` which accepts ages between **18 and 60** (inclusive). Show all valid and invalid partitions and boundary values.

---

#### Model Answer for (a):

The four levels of testing in software engineering are:

1. **Unit Testing**
   - Tests individual units (functions, methods, classes) in isolation.
   - Performed by developers using tools like JUnit (Java), pytest (Python), NUnit (.NET).
   - Catches defects at the **lowest granularity**.

2. **Integration Testing**
   - Tests the **interaction** between integrated modules.
   - Strategies: Top-Down, Bottom-Up, Sandwich, Big-Bang.
   - Detects interface defects (mismatched data formats, call-sequence errors).

3. **System Testing**
   - Tests the **complete integrated system** against the SRS.
   - Includes functional and non-functional testing (performance, security, load, usability).
   - Performed by an **independent QA team**.

4. **Acceptance Testing (UAT)**
   - Validates the system against **business requirements** and user needs.
   - Performed by the end-user or client.
   - Types: Alpha (in-house), Beta (limited users), Gamma (full release).

*(Attach the V-Model or Testing Pyramid diagram — see SECTION 4.)*

---

#### Model Answer for (b):

**Step 1: Identify the valid range.** Valid ages: $18 \leq age \leq 60$.

**Step 2: Equivalence Partitions.**

| Partition | Class | Example | Validity |
|-----------|-------|---------|----------|
| **E1** | $18 \leq age \leq 60$ | 30 | **Valid** |
| **E2** | $age < 18$ | 10 | **Invalid** |
| **E3** | $age > 60$ | 70 | **Invalid** |
| **E4** | $age \leq 0$ (negative / zero) | -5 | **Invalid** |

**Step 3: Boundary Values.**

- `17` (just below minimum)
- `18` (minimum)
- `19` (just above minimum)
- `59` (just below maximum)
- `60` (maximum)
- `61` (just above maximum)

**Step 4: Final Test Case Table.**

| TC ID | Input (age) | Class | Expected Result |
|-------|-------------|-------|-----------------|
| TC01 | 30 | Valid (E1) | Accept |
| TC02 | 10 | Invalid (E2) | Reject |
| TC03 | 70 | Invalid (E3) | Reject |
| TC04 | -5 | Invalid (E4) | Reject |
| TC05 | 17 | Boundary below min | Reject |
| TC06 | 18 | Boundary min | Accept |
| TC07 | 19 | Boundary above min | Accept |
| TC08 | 59 | Boundary below max | Accept |
| TC09 | 60 | Boundary max | Accept |
| TC10 | 61 | Boundary above max | Reject |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain **Cyclomatic Complexity** with McCabe's method. Compute the cyclomatic complexity for the given code snippet and derive the minimum number of test cases.
>
> ```c
> int classify(int marks) {
>     if (marks >= 90) return 'A';
>     else if (marks >= 75) return 'B';
>     else if (marks >= 50) return 'C';
>     else return 'F';
> }
> ```
>
> **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **defect life cycle** with a neat diagram. List at least **five states** with their meanings.

---

#### Model Answer for (a):

**Cyclomatic Complexity** is a software metric introduced by Thomas J. McCabe in 1976. It quantifies the number of **independent linear paths** through a program's source code, providing a measure of code complexity, testability, and maintainability.

**McCabe's Three Methods:**

1. **Method 1 — Predicate Nodes:** $V(G) = \pi + 1$, where $\pi$ is the number of decision points (`if`, `else if`, `for`, `while`, `case`).
2. **Method 2 — Control Flow Graph:** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$.
3. **Method 3 — Regions:** $V(G) = $ number of enclosed regions in the CFG + 1 (the outer region).

**Computation for the Given Code:**

Predicate nodes: `if (marks >= 90)`, `else if (marks >= 75)`, `else if (marks >= 50)`. So $\pi = 3$.

$$
V(G) = \pi + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4
$$

**Minimum Test Cases Required:** 4 (one for each independent path: marks ≥ 90, 75 ≤ marks < 90, 50 ≤ marks < 75, marks < 50).

**Test Cases:**

| TC | Input (marks) | Path Taken | Output |
|----|---------------|-----------|--------|
| T1 | 95 | `marks >= 90` | 'A' |
| T2 | 80 | `marks >= 75` (and < 90) | 'B' |
| T3 | 60 | `marks >= 50` (and < 75) | 'C' |
| T4 | 30 | default `else` | 'F' |

---

#### Model Answer for (b):

The **Defect Life Cycle** (also called the **Bug Life Cycle**) describes the various states a defect transitions through from the moment it is identified until it is closed. *(See the state diagram in SECTION 4.5.)*

**Five States with Meanings:**

1. **New** — A defect is freshly logged by a tester; not yet reviewed by the test lead.
2. **Assigned** — The defect has been reviewed and assigned to a developer for resolution.
3. **Open** — The developer has begun analyzing and fixing the defect.
4. **Fixed** — The developer has made the code change; the defect is awaiting retesting by the QA team.
5. **Retest** — The QA team re-executes the test case to verify the fix; if it passes, the defect moves to **Verified** → **Closed**. If it fails, the defect is **Reopened** and goes back to the **Open** state.
6. *(Bonus)* **Closed** — The defect is confirmed fixed and the issue is formally closed.
7. *(Bonus)* **Rejected** — The developer determines the issue is not a valid defect (e.g., duplicate, not reproducible, or not a bug per requirements).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing "Defect" with "Failure":** An *error* is a human mistake. A *defect* is the resulting flaw in the code. A *failure* is the observable runtime symptom. Mixing up these three terms costs **1–2 marks** in 3-mark definition questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the outer region in $V(G)$:** When using the regions method, students often forget to count the **unbounded (outer) region**. A common mistake: counting only bounded regions. The correct count always includes the outer region.
> 3. **Incomplete test case tables:** For BVA, students sometimes list only 3 boundary values instead of the standard **6** (a-1, a, a+1, b-1, b, b+1). Examiners deduct marks for "missing boundary points."
> 4. **Confusing Verification with Validation:** These are **not** the same. Verification is **static** (document review), Validation is **dynamic** (code execution). Writing them as synonyms is a 7-mark question killer.
> 5. **Cyclomatic Complexity formula errors:** Using $V(G) = \pi - 1$ (instead of $+1$) is a recurring mistake. Memorize: **$V(G) = \pi + 1$**.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Software Testing**

### Core Definitions
- **Software Testing:** Systematic execution of software with the intent to find defects (IEEE 829).
- **Error:** Human mistake made by the developer (the *cause*).
- **Defect / Bug / Fault:** Imperfection in the software artifact (the *flaw*).
- **Failure:** Observable, incorrect runtime behavior (the *symptom*).
- **Verification:** *Are we building the product right?* (Static, document review).
- **Validation:** *Are we building the right product?* (Dynamic, execution-based).
- **Quality:** Conformance to requirements + fitness for use (Crosby's + Juran's definition).

### The 7 ISTQB Testing Principles (High-Yield)
1. Testing shows the presence of defects, not their absence.
2. Exhaustive testing is impossible.
3. Early testing saves time and money.
4. Defects cluster together (Pareto 80/20).
5. Beware of the pesticide paradox.
6. Testing is context-dependent.
7. Absence-of-errors is a fallacy.

### The 4 Levels of Testing (Pyramid Order)
1. **Unit Testing** — developer, isolated function/method.
2. **Integration Testing** — interaction of modules (Top-Down / Bottom-Up / Sandwich / Big-Bang).
3. **System Testing** — full system, independent QA team.
4. **Acceptance Testing (UAT)** — end-user / client, business validation.

### The 6 Phases of STLC
1. Requirement Analysis → RTM (Requirements Traceability Matrix).
2. Test Planning → Test Plan.
3. Test Case Development → Test Cases & Scripts.
4. Test Environment Setup → Test Bed.
5. Test Execution → Defect Reports, Test Logs.
6. Test Closure → Test Summary Report.

### Black-Box Test Design Techniques
- **Equivalence Partitioning (EP)** — input domain divided into valid/invalid classes.
- **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)** — test the *edges* (a-1, a, a+1, b-1, b, b+1).
- **Decision Table Testing** — for complex business rules with multiple conditions.
- **State Transition Testing** — for finite state machines (ATM, traffic light, login flow).
- **Use Case Testing** — derived from use case diagrams.

### White-Box Test Design Techniques
- **Statement Coverage** — every line executed.
- **Branch Coverage** — every `if/else` outcome executed.
- **Path Coverage** — every independent path.
- **Condition Coverage** — every boolean sub-expression evaluated both ways.
- **Cyclomatic Complexity ($V(G)$)** — number of independent paths.

### Key Formulas (Memorize Exactly)
- $V(G) = \pi + 1$ — Cyclomatic complexity (predicate nodes method).
- $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ — Cyclomatic complexity (graph method).
- $V(G) = \text{Regions (including outer)}$ — Cyclomatic complexity (regions method).
- $\text{Statement Coverage} = \frac{S_{\text{ex}}}{S_{\text{total}}} \times 100$.
- $\text{Branch Coverage} = \frac{B_{\text{ex}}}{B_{\text{total}}} \times 100$.
- $\text{Defect Density} = \frac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$.
- $\text{DRE} = \frac{D_{\text{test}}}{D_{\text{test}} + D_{\text{release}}} \times 100$.
- $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$.
- $\text{Reliability} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.

### Defect Life Cycle — 7 Key States
**New → Assigned → Open → Fixed → Retest → (Verified → Closed) OR (Reopen → Open)**.
*Branch:* **Rejected → Closed** (if not a valid defect).

### Integration Testing Strategy Selection Heuristic
- **Top-Down** → when UI / high-level logic is critical early.
- **Bottom-Up** → when low-level utility modules are critical.
- **Sandwich (Hybrid)** → for large real-time / enterprise systems.
- **Big-Bang** → **avoid in professional projects**; risky and hard to isolate defects.

### V-Model Mapping (KTU Favorite)
- **Requirements** ↔ **Acceptance Testing**.
- **System Design** ↔ **System Testing**.
- **Detailed Design** ↔ **Integration Testing**.
- **Code** ↔ **Unit Testing**.

### "Don't Forget" Pitfalls (Frequently Tested)
- BVA requires **6 boundary values**, not 3.
- Cyclomatic complexity formula is **$V(G) = \pi + 1$** (NOT $\pi - 1$).
- Verification = static, Validation = dynamic. NEVER confuse them.
- Defect = artifact flaw. Failure = observed runtime behavior. Error = human action.
- DRE aims for **≥ 95%** in mature / safety-critical systems.
- Branch coverage **always implies** statement coverage; the reverse is not true.

### KTU 2024 Expected Question Patterns
- **3-mark questions:** Define testing / verification / validation. List 4 principles. Differentiate V&V.
- **7-mark questions:** Explain levels of testing. Explain EP & BVA with example. Draw defect life cycle.
- **14-mark questions:** Compute cyclomatic complexity with derivation. Apply EP, BVA, decision tables. Compare black-box vs. white-box. Full STLC walkthrough.

> **Final Examiner's Note:** When answering KTU SQA questions, **always pair definitions with one real-world example** and **draw a labeled diagram** wherever possible. The combination of (1) crisp definition, (2) numerical or graphical illustration, and (3) real-world analogy is the highest-scoring template.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Quality Metrics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Product Quality Metrics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Product Quality Metrics** are quantitative measures of the degree to which a software product possesses desired attributes or characteristics. They provide an objective, repeatable, and verifiable means of assessing the *fitness-for-use* of a software product at every stage of its lifecycle — from requirements specification, through source code, to the deployed system in operation.

According to the **ISO/IEC 25010:2011** standard (the contemporary reference cited in the KTU 2024 SQA syllabus), product quality is decomposed into **eight software product quality characteristics**, each further refined into sub-characteristics. The eight primary characteristics are:

1. **Functional Suitability**
2. **Performance Efficiency**
3. **Compatibility**
4. **Usability**
5. **Reliability**
6. **Security**
7. **Maintainability**
8. **Portability**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (paraphrased for 2-mark direct questions):**
> *Product quality metrics are the measurable attributes of a software product used to evaluate its quality characteristics quantitatively, enabling objective comparison, prediction, and improvement of the product over its lifecycle.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Car Dashboard" View

Imagine you are driving a car. The dashboard is **NOT** the car — it is a set of **gauges** that *quantify* the health of the car:

- **Speedometer** → tells you instantaneous performance (analogous to *Performance Efficiency*).
- **Fuel Gauge** → tells you remaining operational capacity (analogous to *Reliability/Availability*).
- **Engine Temperature Meter** → tells you internal stress and stability (analogous to *Maintainability/Complexity*).
- **Odometer** → tells you cumulative wear (analogous to *Mean Time To Failure*).

In the **same way**, product quality metrics are the *dashboard instruments* of a software product. They do not change the product itself, but they **quantify** the otherwise abstract notion of "good software." Without them, software quality is mere opinion; with them, it becomes **engineering**.

### 1.3 Three Sub-Classes of Product Metrics

| Sub-Class | What it Measures | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Metrics** | Product attributes derivable from intermediate work products (specs, designs, code) | Cyclomatic Complexity, LOC, CK metrics |
| **External Metrics** | Product attributes measurable by observing the running system | MTTF, Defect Density, Throughput |
| **Quality-in-Use Metrics** | Effect of the product on users in a specific context of use | User Task Success Rate, Satisfaction Score |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three classes?** Because the KTU examiner expects you to recognize that *quality is multi-dimensional* — you cannot judge a software product by looking only at its code, nor only by running it; you must combine **static** (internal) and **dynamic** (external) measures, all evaluated **in-use**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 McCall's Quality Model — The Foundation

**James A. McCall** (1977, General Electric / US Air Force) proposed the first systematic quality model. It organizes 11 quality factors around a triangular perspective:

- **Product Operation** (5 factors) — concerns the *operational* phase.
- **Product Revision** (3 factors) — concerns the *maintenance* phase.
- **Product Transition** (3 factors) — concerns the *adaptation/porting* phase.

#### 2.1.1 The 11 McCall Quality Factors

| # | Factor | Category | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Correctness** | Operation | Degree to which program satisfies its specification. |
| 2 | **Reliability** | Operation | Capability of program to maintain its level of performance. |
| 3 | **Efficiency** | Operation | Amount of computing resources and code required. |
| 4 | **Integrity** | Operation | Access control / security of software and data. |
| 5 | **Usability** | Operation | Ease of human operation. |
| 6 | **Maintainability** | Revision | Effort required to locate and fix defects. |
| 7 | **Flexibility** | Revision | Effort required to modify an operational program. |
| 8 | **Testability** | Revision | Effort required to test a program. |
| 9 | **Portability** | Transition | Effort required to transfer program between environments. |
| 10 | **Reusability** | Transition | Extent to which program can be reused in new applications. |
| 11 | **Interoperability** | Transition | Effort required to couple one system with another. |

> [!NOTE]
> **McCall's Quality Index Formula** (often asked in KTU Part-A):
> $$Q = a_1 \cdot C_1 + a_2 \cdot C_2 + \cdots + a_{11} \cdot C_{11}$$
> where $C_i$ is the measured value of the $i^{th}$ quality factor (0 to 1) and $a_i$ is the relative weight (with $\sum a_i = 1$).

#### 2.1.2 McCall's Triangle of Quality

```
            USABILITY
                |
                |
    MAINTAINABILITY — FLEXIBILITY
                |
                |
              ...  (organized in a triangle)
```

A useful KTU mnemonic: **C-R-E-I-U** (Operation), **M-F-T** (Revision), **P-R-I** (Transition).

### 2.2 FURPS Quality Model (Hewlett-Packard / Grady)

| Letter | Stands For | Category |
|---|---|---|
| **F** | Functional | Functional features & capabilities |
| **U** | Usability | Human factors, aesthetics, consistency, documentation |
| **R** | Reliability | Failure frequency, recoverability, predictability |
| **P** | Performance | Speed, throughput, accuracy, efficiency |
| **S** | Supportability (incl. **+S**: Design constraints, Implementation, Interface, Physical) | Maintainability, configurability, installability, testability |

### 2.3 Metrics for the Analysis Model — Function Points (FP)

**Albrecht's Function Point Analysis (FPA)** measures the *functionality* delivered by an application from the **user's perspective**, independent of language/platform.

The **Unadjusted Function Point (UFP)** is computed from five information-domain values, each weighted:

| Information Domain Value | Symbol | Weight (Low / Avg / High) |
|---|---|---|
| External Inputs | EI | 3 / 4 / 6 |
| External Outputs | EO | 4 / 5 / 7 |
| External Inquiries | EQ | 3 / 4 / 6 |
| External Interface Files (Logical Files / Master Files) | EIF | 5 / 7 / 10 |
| Internal Logical Files | ILF | 7 / 10 / 15 |

The complexity rating (Low/Avg/High) is determined by counting the number of **Data Element Types (DETs)** and **Record Element Types (RETs)** for each function.

### 2.4 Metrics for the Source Code

#### 2.4.1 LOC (Lines of Code) and KLOC

$$\text{LOC} = \text{physical or logical lines in the source code}$$

$$D = \frac{\text{Defects}}{\text{KLOC}} \quad \text{(Defect Density per KLOC)}$$

$$P = \frac{\text{Productivity}}{\text{KLOC per person-month}}$$

#### 2.4.2 Halstead's Software Science

Halstead defined four primary counts: $\eta_1$ (number of distinct operators), $\eta_2$ (number of distinct operands), $N_1$ (total occurrences of operators), $N_2$ (total occurrences of operands). The vocabulary and length:

$$\eta = \eta_1 + \eta_2 \quad \text{(Program Vocabulary)}$$

$$N = N_1 + N_2 \quad \text{(Program Length)}$$

$$V = N \cdot \log_2 \eta \quad \text{(Volume)}$$

$$D = \frac{\eta_1}{2} \cdot \frac{N_2}{\eta_2} \quad \text{(Difficulty)}$$

$$E = D \cdot V \quad \text{(Effort)}$$

$$B = \frac{E^{2/3}}{3000} \quad \text{(Estimated Number of Bugs)}$$

$$T = \frac{E}{18} \text{ seconds} \quad \text{(Estimated Time to Implement)}$$

#### 2.4.3 Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe)

For a control flow graph $G$:

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

where $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components. For a single connected component $(P=1)$:

$$V(G) = E - N + 2$$

Equivalently, $V(G)$ = number of *predicate nodes* (branches) + 1.

#### 2.4.4 Chidamber-Kemerer (CK) Object-Oriented Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Weighted Methods per Class | WMC | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i$ | Sum of cyclomatic complexities of methods |
| Depth of Inheritance Tree | DIT | Maximum path length to root | Deeper trees → higher complexity |
| Number of Children | NOC | Count of immediate subclasses | Reuse indicator |
| Coupling Between Objects | CBO | Count of coupled classes | Lower is better |
| Response For a Class | RFC | $\vert RS \vert$ where RS = set of methods invoked in response to a message | Lower indicates more testable class |
| Lack of Cohesion in Methods | LCOM | $\vert P \vert - \vert Q \vert$ if positive, else 0 (P = pairs that do not share, Q = pairs that share instance variables) | 0 = highly cohesive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory trick for CK metrics:** **W, D, N, C, R, L** → **"W**e **D**on't **N**eed **C**omplex **R**ules **L**earned."

### 2.5 Metrics for Testing

| Metric | Formula | Plain Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Defect Density | $D = \dfrac{\text{Defects found}}{\text{KLOC or FP}}$ | Bugs per unit size |
| Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) | $MTTF = \dfrac{\text{Total observed time}}{\text{Number of failures}}$ | Average uptime between failures |
| Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) | $MTTR = \dfrac{\text{Total repair time}}{\text{Number of repairs}}$ | Average downtime per failure |
| Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) | $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$ | Average total cycle time |
| Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTBF}$ | Fraction of time the system is up |
| Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) | $DRE = \dfrac{\text{Defects removed during development}}{\text{Defects latent at start} - \text{Defects remaining at release}}$ | Effectiveness of QA |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of Product Quality Metrics

- **Project managers** use defect density, MTTF, and effort metrics for *planning, budgeting, and release-go decisions*.
- **Developers** use cyclomatic complexity and CK metrics to predict *testability* and *maintainability*.
- **Testers** use reliability metrics (MTTF, Availability) to certify *SLA compliance*.
- **Architects** use Function Points and Bang metrics for *size estimation* and *architectural risk evaluation*.
- **Process improvement** (CMMI Level 4–5) requires quantitative management, which is impossible without product metrics.

> [!NOTE]
> **McCabe's Rule of Thumb (KTU favourite):** $V(G) \le 10$ is *good*; $V(G) > 30$ is *unstable / untestable*. This single line has appeared in multiple KTU Module-3 questions.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Domain | Metric | Formula / Symbol | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Size | LOC, KLOC | $\text{count}$ | lines |
| Size | Function Point (FP) | $FP = UFP \times (0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum F_i)$ | dimensionless, $F_i = 0..5$ |
| Complexity | Cyclomatic | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | integer $\ge 1$ |
| Vocabulary | Halstead Volume | $V = N \log_2 \eta$ | bits |
| OO Design | WMC, DIT, NOC, CBO, RFC, LCOM | see above | integer |
| Reliability | MTTF, MTBF, MTTR | time-based | hours / days |
| Reliability | Availability | $A = MTTF / MTBF$ | $0 \le A \le 1$ |
| Quality | Defect Density | $D = \text{Defects} / \text{KLOC}$ | defects/KLOC |
| Quality | DRE | ratio, often expressed as % | $0\% \le DRE \le 100\%$ |
| Quality | McCall's Index | $Q = \sum a_i C_i$ | $0 \le Q \le 1$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Function Point Calculation (Full Derivation)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style, 7 marks):**
> A software project has the following information-domain values determined during analysis:
>
> - 12 External Inputs of *average* complexity
> - 8 External Outputs of *low* complexity
> - 14 External Inquiries of *average* complexity
> - 5 External Interface Files of *high* complexity
> - 3 Internal Logical Files of *average* complexity
>
> The system has 14 system parameters, all rated average (value = 3) except for the online data entry, which is rated as high (value = 5). Compute the Function Point (FP).

**Step 1 — Identify weight table values from §2.3:**

For each of the five information domains, the question states the *complexity rating*; the weight $w$ is taken from the central column ("Avg") of the standard Albrecht table.

| Domain | Count | Complexity | Weight $w$ | Subtotal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| EI | 12 | Avg | 4 | $12 \times 4 = 48$ |
| EO | 8 | Low | 4 | $8 \times 4 = 32$ |
| EQ | 14 | Avg | 4 | $14 \times 4 = 56$ |
| EIF | 5 | High | 10 | $5 \times 10 = 50$ |
| ILF | 3 | Avg | 10 | $3 \times 10 = 30$ |

**Step 2 — Compute Unadjusted Function Point (UFP):**

$$UFP = 48 + 32 + 56 + 50 + 30 = 216$$

**Step 3 — Sum the 14 General System Characteristics (GSC) $F_i$ values:**

The standard FPA has 14 GSCs each rated 0–5. We are told: 13 GSCs are average (= 3), and 1 GSC (online data entry) is high (= 5).

$$\sum_{i=1}^{14} F_i = (13 \times 3) + 1 \times 5 = 39 + 5 = 44$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Complexity Adjustment Factor (CAF):**

$$CAF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum F_i = 0.65 + 0.01 \times 44 = 0.65 + 0.44 = 1.09$$

**Step 5 — Compute Final Function Point:**

$$FP = UFP \times CAF = 216 \times 1.09 = 235.44$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $FP \approx 235.44$ function points. (In industry, FP is reported to 1 decimal place.)

**Step 6 — Optional translation to LOC (if a language is given):**

If the language is, e.g., **Java at 53 LOC/FP** (industry average):

$$\text{Estimated LOC} = 235.44 \times 53 \approx 12{,}478 \text{ LOC}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cyclomatic Complexity from a Control Flow Graph

**Problem Statement (KTU Module-3, 7 marks):**
> Given the pseudocode below, draw the control flow graph and compute the McCabe cyclomatic complexity using **three independent methods**.
>
> ```
> 1: read x
> 2: IF x > 0 THEN
> 3:     y = x * 2
> 4:     IF y > 10 THEN
> 5:         z = y - 10
> 6:     ELSE
> 7:         z = y + 10
> 8:     ENDIF
> 9: ELSE
> 10:    y = -x
> 11: ENDIF
> 12: print y, z
> ```

**Step 1 — Enumerate the nodes (each statement = 1 node):**

$N_1=$read x, $N_2=$IF x>0, $N_3=$y=x\*2, $N_4=$IF y>10, $N_5=$z=y-10, $N_6=$z=y+10, $N_7=$y=-x, $N_8=$print y,z.

Total nodes: $N = 8$.

**Step 2 — Enumerate the directed edges:**

| From | To | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | sequential |
| 2 | 3 | TRUE of x>0 |
| 2 | 7 | FALSE of x>0 |
| 3 | 4 | sequential |
| 4 | 5 | TRUE of y>10 |
| 4 | 6 | FALSE of y>10 |
| 5 | 8 | join after inner IF |
| 6 | 8 | join after inner IF |
| 7 | 8 | sequential after outer ELSE |
| 8 | exit | terminal |

Total edges: $E = 10$.

Number of connected components: $P = 1$ (single connected program graph).

**Step 3 — Method A: McCabe's original formula $V(G) = E - N + 2P$:**

$$V(G) = 10 - 8 + 2 \cdot 1 = 4$$

**Step 4 — Method B: Predicate-node + 1 method:**

Count the predicate (decision) nodes: $N_2$ (x>0) and $N_4$ (y>10). Total = 2.

$$V(G) = \text{Predicate nodes} + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$

> [!NOTE]
> This appears to disagree with Method A. The reason: McCabe's $E - N + 2P$ formula **adds the implicit edge from the final node to the start of the program** when it is omitted. The "+1" in the predicate method absorbs that extra edge for *single-exit* programs. For academic KTU problems, when the graph is drawn *with* the return-to-exit edge included, $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ is the *definitive* answer.

**Step 5 — Method C: Number of independent paths (basis set):**

A basis set of paths through the graph is:

1. $1 \to 2 \to 7 \to 8$ (outer FALSE branch)
2. $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5 \to 8$ (outer TRUE, inner TRUE)
3. $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 6 \to 8$ (outer TRUE, inner FALSE)

So the basis set has **3** linearly independent paths, confirming $V(G) = 3$ when no extra exit edge is added. **For the KTU board, draw the graph carefully; then use whichever method the question demands.**

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Halstead Metrics Derivation

**Problem Statement (KTU 14-mark style):**
> For the Java fragment below, compute the four primary Halstead measures, the program vocabulary, length, volume, difficulty, effort, and the estimated number of bugs $B$.
>
> ```java
> a = a + b;
> c = a * b;
> ```

**Step 1 — Identify operators and operands:**

- Operators ($\eta_1 = 5$ distinct): `=`, `+`, `*`, `;`, newline (treated as an operator by some conventions — we'll use the *strict* 4-operator form here for clarity: `=`, `+`, `*`, `;`).
  - Distinct operators: $\eta_1 = 4$ (`=`, `+`, `*`, `;`).
  - Total operator occurrences: $N_1 = 5$ (line 1: `=`, `+`, `;` = 3; line 2: `=`, `*`, `;` = 3 ⇒ actually 6; we count `;` at end of each line. Let us recount strictly below.)
- Operands ($\eta_2$ distinct): `a`, `b`, `c`. So $\eta_2 = 3$.
- Total operand occurrences: each variable appears as many times as written.
  - Line 1: `a`, `b` (2 operands)
  - Line 2: `c`, `a`, `b` (3 operands)
  - Total operand occurrences: $N_2 = 5$.

**Step 2 — Recount operators including `;`:**

Each statement ends with `;`. So $N_1$: line 1 has `=`, `+`, `;` (3); line 2 has `=`, `*`, `;` (3). Total $N_1 = 6$. Distinct $\eta_1 = 4$.

**Step 3 — Program Vocabulary and Length:**

$$\eta = \eta_1 + \eta_2 = 4 + 3 = 7$$

$$N = N_1 + N_2 = 6 + 5 = 11$$

**Step 4 — Program Volume:**

$$V = N \cdot \log_2 \eta = 11 \cdot \log_2 7 = 11 \times 2.807 = 30.88 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5 — Difficulty:**

$$D = \frac{\eta_1}{2} \cdot \frac{N_2}{\eta_2} = \frac{4}{2} \cdot \frac{5}{3} = 2 \times 1.6667 = 3.33$$

**Step 6 — Effort:**

$$E = D \cdot V = 3.33 \times 30.88 \approx 102.9 \text{ (Halstead units, often interpreted as elementary mental discriminations)}$$

**Step 7 — Estimated Bugs (B):**

$$B = \frac{E^{2/3}}{3000} = \frac{102.9^{2/3}}{3000} = \frac{(102.9)^{0.6667}}{3000} = \frac{21.96}{3000} \approx 0.0073$$

Interpretation: roughly **0.0073 expected bugs** in this 2-line fragment, i.e., effectively one minor bug per ~135 such fragments — a sanity check for the metric.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's edge case:** If the question uses Halstead's *estimated length* $\hat{N} = \eta_1 \log_2 \eta_1 + \eta_2 \log_2 \eta_2$ instead of $N$, then the metric suite changes. **Always read the question's stated formula first.**

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Reliability / Availability Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
> During a 1000-hour soak test, a server failed 5 times. The cumulative repair time for those 5 failures was 25 hours. Compute MTTF, MTTR, MTBF, and Availability $A$.

**Step 1 — Compute MTTR:**

$$MTTR = \frac{\text{Total repair time}}{\text{Number of repairs}} = \frac{25 \text{ h}}{5} = 5 \text{ h}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total observed uptime:**

The problem gives 1000 hours as the **total observed time** (soak window). Total repair time = 25 h. Therefore total **uptime** = $1000 - 25 = 975$ h.

**Step 3 — Compute MTTF:**

$$MTTF = \frac{\text{Total uptime}}{\text{Number of failures}} = \frac{975}{5} = 195 \text{ h}$$

**Step 4 — Compute MTBF:**

$$MTBF = MTTF + MTTR = 195 + 5 = 200 \text{ h}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Availability:**

$$A = \frac{MTTF}{MTBF} = \frac{195}{200} = 0.975 = 97.5\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The system is available 97.5% of the time — comfortably above a typical "three-nines" (99.9%) SLA? **No, 97.5% is roughly 2½-nines;** it would *fail* a 99.9% SLA. KTU questions often ask this exact business interpretation.

### 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
> 200 defects were found before release; 100 of them were found before testing began (i.e., during development). 50 defects escaped to the field. Compute DRE.

**Step 1 — Identify variables (DRE in its "pre-release removal" form):**

- Defects found **before testing** (during development): 100
- Defects found **during testing** (pre-release): 200
- Defects **remaining at release** (= field-escaped): 50

**Step 2 — DRE formula (the version most used in KTU/Pressman):**

$$DRE = \frac{\text{Defects removed during development}}{\text{Defects removed during development} + \text{Defects found after release}}$$

Sometimes it is presented as:

$$DRE = \frac{\text{Defects found before release}}{\text{Defects found before release} + \text{Defects found after release}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply (using the second form, since "before release" is the more inclusive numerator):**

$$DRE = \frac{100 + 200}{(100 + 200) + 50} = \frac{300}{350} = 0.857 = 85.7\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** The KTU board is strict about which DRE variant you use. If the question explicitly says "defects removed during development," use the first form. If it says "defects found before release," use the second. **State the formula before substituting.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — McCall's 11 Quality Factors Grouped by Triangle Category

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[McCall Product Quality Model]
    A --> B[Product Operation]
    A --> C[Product Revision]
    A --> D[Product Transition]

    B --> B1[Correctness]
    B --> B2[Reliability]
    B --> B3[Efficiency]
    B --> B4[Integrity]
    B --> B5[Usability]

    C --> C1[Maintainability]
    C --> C2[Flexibility]
    C --> C3[Testability]

    D --> D1[Portability]
    D --> D2[Reusability]
    D --> D3[Interoperability]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style D fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Product Quality Metrics Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    M[Product Quality Metrics]
    M --> S[Size Metrics]
    M --> C[Complexity Metrics]
    M --> O[Object Oriented Metrics]
    M --> R[Reliability Metrics]
    M --> Q[Quality Effectiveness Metrics]

    S --> S1[LOC and KLOC]
    S --> S2[Function Point FP]
    S --> S3[Bang Metric]

    C --> C1[McCabe Cyclomatic V G]
    C --> C2[Halstead Volume V]
    C --> C3[Halstead Difficulty D]
    C --> C4[Halstead Effort E]

    O --> O1[WMC]
    O --> O2[DIT]
    O --> O3[NOC]
    O --> O4[CBO]
    O --> O5[RFC]
    O --> O6[LCOM]

    R --> R1[MTTF]
    R --> R2[MTTR]
    R --> R3[MTBF]
    R --> R4[Availability A]

    Q --> Q1[Defect Density]
    Q --> Q2[Defect Removal Efficiency DRE]
    Q --> Q3[Customer Reported Defects]
    Q --> Q4[Mean Time to Defect Fix]

    style M fill:#264478,color:#ffffff
    style S fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style O fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style R fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style Q fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Architecture: How Product Quality Metrics Flow in a Software Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ[Requirements Specification]
    DSGN[Design Documents]
    CODE[Source Code]
    TEST[Test Reports]
    REL[Released Product]
    FBK[Field Usage Data]

    REQ -->|FP, Bang| MET1[Analysis Metrics]
    DSGN -->|Cohesion, Coupling| MET2[Design Metrics]
    CODE -->|Cyclomatic, Halstead, CK| MET3[Code Metrics]
    TEST -->|Defect Density, MTTF| MET4[Test Metrics]
    REL -->|MTBF, Availability| MET5[Operational Metrics]
    FBK -->|Field defects, MTTF| MET5

    MET1 --> DASH[Quality Dashboard]
    MET2 --> DASH
    MET3 --> DASH
    MET4 --> DASH
    MET5 --> DASH

    DASH --> DEC[Release Go-NoGo Decision]
    DASH --> IMPR[Process Improvement CMMI L4 L5]
    DASH --> PLAN[Project Planning Estimates]

    style DASH fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style DEC fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
    style IMPR fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
    style PLAN fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — GQM (Goal-Question-Metric) Paradigm as the Metric Engineering Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G[Goal e.g. Improve reliability of billing module]
    G --> Q1[Question 1 What is current MTTF?]
    G --> Q2[Question 2 How many field defects per KLOC?]
    G --> Q3[Question 3 What is customer satisfaction?]

    Q1 --> M1[Metric MTTF in hours]
    Q2 --> M2[Metric Defect Density]
    Q3 --> M3[Metric CSAT Score 1 to 5]

    M1 --> A[Analysis and Interpretation]
    M2 --> A
    M3 --> A

    A --> ACT[Action Plan e.g. add fault tolerance, increase code review]

    style G fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
    style ACT fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — FPA Workflow Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    STEP1[Step 1 Identify information domain counts]
    STEP2[Step 2 Determine DET and RET for each function]
    STEP3[Step 3 Assign complexity Low Avg High]
    STEP4[Step 4 Look up weight from standard table]
    STEP5[Step 5 Compute UFP sum of weights]
    STEP6[Step 6 Rate 14 GSC on scale 0 to 5]
    STEP7[Step 7 Compute CAF = 0.65 + 0.01 sum F]
    STEP8[Step 8 Compute FP = UFP times CAF]

    STEP1 --> STEP2
    STEP2 --> STEP3
    STEP3 --> STEP4
    STEP4 --> STEP5
    STEP5 --> STEP6
    STEP6 --> STEP7
    STEP7 --> STEP8

    style STEP8 fill:#00b050,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below are *modeled* after KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) style, with full part-(a) and part-(b) choices and full step-by-step model answers.

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. Define *Product Quality Metrics*. Mention any **two** internal and **two** external product quality metrics with one-line justification. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for examples with justification):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Product quality metrics are quantitative, measurable attributes of a software product that are collected at intermediate or final work products to indicate the extent to which the product satisfies its specified quality characteristics. They enable objective evaluation, prediction, and improvement of the software product.

**Two Internal Metrics (1 mark):**
- **Cyclomatic Complexity $V(G)$** — derivable directly from the source code; predicts the testing effort and the number of independent execution paths.
- **Lines of Code (LOC/KLOC)** — counts the size of the source; combined with defects, gives *defect density*.

**Two External Metrics (0.5 mark):**
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** — observed by running the system and recording the time between failures.
- **Availability $A = MTTF / MTBF$** — directly observed in operational environments as the fraction of time the system is functional.

---

#### Q2. List the **11 quality factors** of **McCall's Quality Model** and state which **phase** of software lifecycle each factor primarily addresses. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — full 11-factor table is required; awarded 0.25 per correctly placed factor + phase pair):**

| # | Factor | Phase |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Correctness | Product Operation |
| 2 | Reliability | Product Operation |
| 3 | Efficiency | Product Operation |
| 4 | Integrity | Product Operation |
| 5 | Usability | Product Operation |
| 6 | Maintainability | Product Revision |
| 7 | Flexibility | Product Revision |
| 8 | Testability | Product Revision |
| 9 | Portability | Product Transition |
| 10 | Reusability | Product Transition |
| 11 | Interoperability | Product Transition |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice between A and B)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme ESE — Module 3, Set A]**

> **(a)** Compute the **Function Point (FP)** for a project whose five information-domain values are tabulated below. The complexity rating of every function is the same: *Low*. The 14 GSCs are rated as 3 each, except for the GSC *"Performance"* which is rated as 5.
>
> | Domain | Count |
> |---|---|
> | External Inputs (EI) | 20 |
> | External Outputs (EO) | 12 |
> | External Inquiries (EQ) | 10 |
> | External Interface Files (EIF) | 4 |
> | Internal Logical Files (ILF) | 6 |
>
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is **McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity**? For the following program, draw the **control flow graph** and compute $V(G)$ using the formula $V(G) = E - N + 2P$. State McCabe's rule-of-thumb bands.
>
> ```
> START
> read n
> i = 0
> sum = 0
> WHILE i < n DO
>     IF i mod 2 == 0 THEN
>         sum = sum + i
>     ELSE
>         sum = sum - i
>     ENDIF
>     i = i + 1
> ENDWHILE
> print sum
> END
> ```
>
> **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part A(a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Pick the "Low" weight from the standard FPA table (§2.3):**

Low weights: EI=3, EO=4, EQ=3, EIF=5, ILF=7.

**Step 2 — Compute each subtotal and UFP:**

| Domain | Count | Low Weight | Subtotal |
|---|---|---|---|
| EI | 20 | 3 | 60 |
| EO | 12 | 4 | 48 |
| EQ | 10 | 3 | 30 |
| EIF | 4 | 5 | 20 |
| ILF | 6 | 7 | 42 |

$$UFP = 60 + 48 + 30 + 20 + 42 = 200$$

**Step 3 — Sum the 14 GSC ratings:**

13 GSCs rated 3, and 1 GSC rated 5:

$$\sum F_i = 13 \times 3 + 1 \times 5 = 39 + 5 = 44$$

**Step 4 — Compute CAF and FP:**

$$CAF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times 44 = 0.65 + 0.44 = 1.09$$

$$FP = UFP \times CAF = 200 \times 1.09 = 218$$

**Result (1 mark):** $FP = 218$ function points.

##### Model Solution — Part A(b) (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** McCabe's cyclomatic complexity $V(G)$ is a structural metric that measures the **number of linearly independent paths** through a program's source code, computed from its control flow graph. It predicts testing effort and identifies high-risk modules.

**Control Flow Graph (drawn as nodes 1–9; 2 marks for graph + 1 for the node/edge counts):**

```
  1:START
   |
  2:read n
   |
  3:i=0, sum=0        (combined into one node for brevity)
   |
  4:WHILE i<n  [DECISION]
   /       \
 5:IF i%2==0 (FALSE)   8: i=i+1 (after loop)
  /         \
6:sum=sum+i  7:sum=sum-i
  \         /
   ----> 8 : i=i+1
         |
         \  (loops back to 4)
          --> 4
 8 --> 9:print sum
 9 --> 10:END
```

**Step 1 — Count nodes $N$ and edges $E$:**

| Element | Value |
|---|---|
| Nodes $N$ | 10 (START, read, init, WHILE, IF, sum=sum+i, sum=sum-i, i=i+1, print, END) |
| Edges $E$ | 12 (sequential, both branch arms of the IF, loop-back, terminal) |
| Connected components $P$ | 1 |

**Step 2 — Apply McCabe's formula:**

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P = 12 - 10 + 2(1) = 4$$

**Step 3 — McCabe's Rule-of-Thumb Bands (1 mark):**

| $V(G)$ | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| 1–10 | Simple, low risk |
| 11–20 | Moderate risk, testable |
| 21–50 | High risk, untestable in unit form |
| $> 50$ | Untestable; refactor immediately |

**Result (1 mark):** $V(G) = 4$ — module is in the *simple / low risk* band.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme ESE — Module 3, Set B]**

> **(a)** What are **Halstead's Software Science metrics**? Derive (with explanation) the **Volume, Difficulty, Effort, and Estimated Bugs** for the following C-like fragment:
>
> ```
> a = b + c;
> d = a - c;
> ```
>
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A server is observed for **2000 hours** during which it failed **8 times**. The **total repair time** was **40 hours**.
> Compute **MTTF, MTTR, MTBF**, and the **Availability $A$**. If the SLA demands 99% availability, does the system meet the SLA? Justify.
>
> **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part B(a) (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Halstead's Software Science is a set of metrics that measure the *size* and *complexity* of a program based on the **counting of operators and operands** in the source code. It is language-independent.

**Step 1 — Identify operators and operands (2 marks for the counts):**

Operators (line 1: `=`, `+`, `;`; line 2: `=`, `-`, `;`):
- Distinct operators $\eta_1 = 4$ (`=`, `+`, `-`, `;`)
- Total operator occurrences $N_1 = 6$

Operands (line 1: `a`, `b`, `c`; line 2: `d`, `a`, `c`):
- Distinct operands $\eta_2 = 4$ (`a`, `b`, `c`, `d`)
- Total operand occurrences $N_2 = 6$

**Step 2 — Vocabulary and Length (1 mark):**

$$\eta = \eta_1 + \eta_2 = 4 + 4 = 8$$

$$N = N_1 + N_2 = 6 + 6 = 12$$

**Step 3 — Volume (1 mark):**

$$V = N \cdot \log_2 \eta = 12 \cdot \log_2 8 = 12 \times 3 = 36 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Difficulty (0.5 mark):**

$$D = \frac{\eta_1}{2} \cdot \frac{N_2}{\eta_2} = \frac{4}{2} \cdot \frac{6}{4} = 2 \times 1.5 = 3.0$$

**Step 5 — Effort (0.5 mark):**

$$E = D \cdot V = 3.0 \times 36 = 108$$

**Step 6 — Estimated Bugs (1 mark):**

$$B = \frac{E^{2/3}}{3000} = \frac{108^{2/3}}{3000} = \frac{22.52}{3000} \approx 0.0075$$

**Interpretation (0.5 mark):** The fragment is expected to contain approximately **0.0075 latent bugs** — in other words, roughly one bug per ~133 such fragments.

##### Model Solution — Part B(a) — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Halstead):**
> - Students often forget to include the **semicolon** as an operator (some references do; some don't). If the question says "as per Halstead's strict definition," include it.
> - Do **NOT** compute $V$ using $\log_{10}$ — it must be $\log_2$ (the base of *binary discrimination*).
> - The Estimated Bug formula $B = E^{2/3}/3000$ is empirical; do **not** invent variants.

---

##### Model Solution — Part B(b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — MTTR (1 mark):**

$$MTTR = \frac{\text{Total repair time}}{\text{Number of failures}} = \frac{40 \text{ h}}{8} = 5 \text{ h}$$

**Step 2 — Total uptime (1 mark):**

$$\text{Uptime} = \text{Total observed time} - \text{Total repair time} = 2000 - 40 = 1960 \text{ h}$$

**Step 3 — MTTF (1 mark):**

$$MTTF = \frac{\text{Total uptime}}{\text{Number of failures}} = \frac{1960}{8} = 245 \text{ h}$$

**Step 4 — MTBF (1 mark):**

$$MTBF = MTTF + MTTR = 245 + 5 = 250 \text{ h}$$

**Step 5 — Availability $A$ (1 mark):**

$$A = \frac{MTTF}{MTBF} = \frac{245}{250} = 0.98 = 98\%$$

**Step 6 — SLA comparison (2 marks):**

The required SLA is 99% availability. The computed $A = 98\% < 99\%$. Therefore, the system **does NOT meet the SLA**. To meet 99%, the system would need either:
- Reduce MTTR from 5 h to ≤ 2.5 h, or
- Increase MTTF (fewer or less severe failures).

**Result (1 mark):** $MTTF = 245$ h, $MTTR = 5$ h, $MTBF = 250$ h, $A = 98\%$. **SLA NOT met.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where KTU students lose marks on these questions:**
> 1. **Confusing MTBF and MTTF.** MTBF is the *full cycle* time (uptime + repair); MTTF is *uptime only*. Many students subtract instead of add.
> 2. **Using total observed time as uptime** — forgetting to subtract the repair time. A common 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Expressing Availability as a percentage** without first stating the decimal form. Always write $A = 0.98$ *and then* $= 98\%$.
> 4. **Forgetting to label units** (hours, days) in MTTF/MTTR/MTBF.
> 5. **In FPA**, mixing up the *complexity rating* (Low/Avg/High) with the *GSC rating* (0–5). These are **two different rating systems** in the same problem.
> 6. **In Cyclomatic Complexity**, drawing the graph with $P > 1$ by mistake — usually the program is a single component, so $P = 1$. If the graph has multiple disconnected sub-graphs, the +2P form is essential.

---

### 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section for the night-before-the-exam revision pass.**

- **Product Quality Metrics** = quantitative measures of a software product's attributes; they make quality **observable** and **engineerable**.
- **Three classes:** Internal (static), External (dynamic), Quality-in-Use (user-context).
- **McCall's 11 factors** are grouped into 3 categories:
  - *Operation* → **Correctness, Reliability, Efficiency, Integrity, Usability**
  - *Revision* → **Maintainability, Flexibility, Testability**
  - *Transition* → **Portability, Reusability, Interoperability**
- **FURPS(+)** — Functional, Usability, Reliability, Performance, Supportability (+Design constraints, +Implementation, +Interface, +Physical).
- **Function Point (FP)** = $UFP \times (0.65 + 0.01 \sum F_i)$, with $UFP = \sum (\text{count} \times \text{weight})$ over EI, EO, EQ, EIF, ILF.
- **McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ = predicate nodes + 1. Band guidance: $\le 10$ good, 11–20 moderate, 21–50 risky, $> 50$ untestable.
- **Halstead metrics** use $\eta_1, \eta_2, N_1, N_2$: $V = N \log_2 \eta$, $D = \frac{\eta_1}{2} \cdot \frac{N_2}{\eta_2}$, $E = D \cdot V$, $B = E^{2/3} / 3000$.
- **CK (OO) metrics** — **W, D, N, C, R, L** = WMC, DIT, NOC, CBO, RFC, LCOM. Lower CBO, RFC, LCOM is generally better; DIT and NOC have *context-dependent* ideals.
- **Reliability metric trio:** $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$; $A = MTTF / MTBF$.
- **DRE** — the "percentage of defects removed before release." High DRE is good; industry target is 95%+.
- **Defect Density** $D = \text{Defects} / \text{KLOC}$ — used to compare products and to flag problem modules.
- **McCall's Quality Index** $Q = \sum a_i C_i$ with $\sum a_i = 1$.
- **GQM Paradigm** — *Goal → Question → Metric* — the recommended engineering method for *selecting* the right metric for a project goal.
- **Always** (i) state the formula before substituting, (ii) preserve units, (iii) interpret the numerical answer in plain English. KTU examiners reward interpretation, not just arithmetic.
- **Common pitfall to avoid:** mixing up the FPA complexity weight (Low/Avg/High) with the GSC rating (0–5) — they live in different columns of the same problem.
- **Common pitfall to avoid:** in $V(G) = E - N + 2P$, forgetting the implicit *return edge* from the terminal node — draws the answer off by 1.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Assurance Activities: Improvement Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process Assurance Activities: Improvement Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Assurance** is a systematic set of activities that objectively evaluates the software development and maintenance processes used within an organization against defined standards, policies, procedures, and regulations. The **Improvement Process** sub-component specifically focuses on iteratively analyzing, measuring, refining, and optimizing these processes to enhance product quality, reduce defects, increase predictability, and improve engineering efficiency.

In the KTU 2024 OBE framework, Process Improvement is treated as a **closed-loop feedback activity** that ensures the *process used to build the product* is itself continually maturing, independent of product-level testing.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen**
> Imagine a restaurant. *Product Quality Assurance* is checking whether the food served to the customer is tasty and hygienic. *Process Assurance* is walking into the kitchen and asking: "Is the chef washing his hands? Is the recipe standardized? Is the cooking time measured? Is the kitchen getting cleaner every month?"
> - **Process Definition** = Writing down the recipe (SOPs).
> - **Process Measurement** = Timing each dish and counting customer complaints.
> - **Process Control** = Discarding dishes that take too long.
> - **Process Improvement** = Realizing that marinating 30 minutes early makes the dish better, and updating the recipe.

If the *recipe itself* is bad, no amount of tasting the final dish will help. This is exactly why software engineering focuses on **process maturity** — the belief (from the SEI/CMMI school) that *a good process produces good products*.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Process Assurance

The following metrics are the **industry-standard KPIs** used to benchmark any improvement program:

- **CMM/CMMI Maturity Level** — 1 (Initial) → 5 (Optimizing).
- **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** — ratio of defects found pre-release vs. total.
- **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** — prevention + appraisal + failure costs.
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** — reliability of the process output.
- **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** — statistical measure of process stability.
- **Cycle Time / Lead Time** — duration of one end-to-end process execution.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Stage Process Improvement Lifecycle

Process improvement is **never a one-time event**; it is a recurring life-cycle. The KTU 2024 syllabus aligns with the classical five-stage model:

#### Stage 1 — Process Analysis
- Identify existing processes (often called *"as-is" processes*).
- Map workflows, roles, artifacts, and entry/exit criteria.
- Tools: SIPOC diagrams, value-stream mapping, process archeology (reconstructing processes from Git history, tickets, logs).

#### Stage 2 — Process Definition (Standardization)
- Document the *"to-be" process* in a **Software Process Description** (per IEEE Std 1074 / ISO 12207).
- Produce Process Assets: standards, templates, checklists, guidelines.
- Output is the **Process Baseline**.

#### Stage 3 — Process Measurement
- Collect quantitative data: size (LOC/Function Points), effort (person-hours), defects, schedule.
- Apply **Goal-Question-Metric (GQM)** paradigm to link business goals to measurable indicators.

#### Stage 4 — Process Control
- Apply statistical control (SPC — Statistical Process Control).
- Compare actual metrics against baselines; trigger corrective action when out of control limits.

#### Stage 5 — Process Change & Improvement
- Introduce controlled changes (CMMI CAR — Causal Analysis and Resolution).
- Pilot the change in one project, measure, then roll out organization-wide.
- Feed learnings back into Stage 2's baseline → **closed loop**.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

| Stage | Why It Is Needed | How It Adds Value |
|---|---|---|
| Analysis | You cannot fix what you do not understand. | Reveals bottlenecks and waste. |
| Definition | Tribal knowledge does not scale. | Enables repeatability & training. |
| Measurement | Opinions are not data. | Provides objective evidence. |
| Control | Variation is the enemy of quality. | Stabilises output. |
| Change | Standing still = falling behind. | Drives continuous improvement. |

### 2.3 The IDEAL Model (SEI)

The **IDEAL** model is a phased improvement framework prescribed by the Software Engineering Institute (SEI). It is a frequent KTU exam question.

- **I — Initiating**: Build the business case, secure sponsorship, define scope.
- **D — Diagnosing**: Analyse current state, identify strengths/weaknesses.
- **E — Establishing**: Plan the improvement (priorities, resources, schedules).
- **A — Acting**: Implement the improvement (pilots, rollouts).
- **L — Learning**: Analyse results, refine, and generalise lessons learned.

### 2.4 PDCA (Deming / Shewhart Cycle)

The **Plan-Do-Check-Act** cycle is the atomic unit of continuous improvement:

- **Plan**: Identify the goal & method.
- **Do**: Execute on a small scale.
- **Check**: Measure results against expectation.
- **Act**: Standardise the change or restart the cycle.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> For process improvement, the "formulas" are mostly **statistical/process quality equations**. Memorise the table below — it is high-yield for KTU 14-mark derivations.

| # | Formula / Metric | Mathematical Form | Units / Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $\text{DRE} = \dfrac{D_{\text{pre}}}{D_{\text{pre}} + D_{\text{post}}}$ | 0 to 1 (or %) | Effectiveness of pre-release defect removal. |
| 2 | **Defect Density (DD)** | $\text{DD} = \dfrac{\text{Defects found}}{\text{Size (KLOC or FP)}}$ | per KLOC / per FP | Normalised product quality. |
| 3 | **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | $\text{CoQ} = C_{\text{prev}} + C_{\text{app}} + C_{\text{int}} + C_{\text{ext}}$ | Currency | Total quality-related spend. |
| 4 | **Process Yield (PY)** | $\text{PY} = \dfrac{N_{\text{passed}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | 0 to 1 | Ratio of process outputs without rework. |
| 5 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} PY_{i}$ | 0 to 1 | Yield across *n* sequential process steps. |
| 6 | **Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | ≥ 1.33 is capable | Statistical control of process. |
| 7 | **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)** | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours / cycles | Process reliability. |
| 8 | **Effort Variance** | $\sigma^{2} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(E_{i} - \bar{E})^{2}$ | (person-hr)$^{2}$ | Predictability of process. |
| 9 | **Stabilised Yield After Improvement** | $\text{New RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - d_{i,\text{new}})$ | 0 to 1 | Quantifies improvement impact. |

> **Boundary conditions**: $0 \le D_{pre}, D_{post}, N_{passed}, N_{total}$ ; $\sigma > 0$ ; USL, LSL are user-defined specification limits.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

Process improvement frameworks are mandatory in:
- **Aerospace / Defence** — CMMI Level 5 is contractually required (e.g., Indian Defence PSUs).
- **Banking & FinTech** — ISO 9001 / CMMI for audit-readiness.
- **Medical Devices** — IEC 62304 mandates process evidence.
- **IT Service Management** — ISO 20000 and ITIL piggyback on process improvement principles.

A high **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** correlates with high first-time-right deliveries — the holy grail of any IT services firm.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section derives the most examination-relevant quantitative relationships in process improvement. Every algebraic step is expanded so you can reproduce the solution in the answer sheet.

### 3.1 Derivation: Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)

**Statement.** Given the number of defects caught before release $D_{pre}$ and the number reported by customers after release $D_{post}$, derive the fraction of defects removed by the in-house process.

**Step 1 — Define total defects in the product universe.**

The total number of defects that ever existed in the released product is the sum of those removed internally plus those that escaped to the field.

$$
D_{\text{total}} = D_{pre} + D_{post}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the removal fraction.**

By definition, the *Defect Removal Efficiency* is the share of total defects captured by our in-house process.

$$
\text{DRE} = \frac{D_{\text{pre}}}{D_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression for $D_{\text{total}}$.**

$$
\text{DRE} = \frac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}}
$$

**Step 4 — Bound the metric.**

- If $D_{post} = 0$ (zero customer-reported defects) → $\text{DRE} = 1$ (100 %, ideal).
- If $D_{pre} = 0$ (no internal testing) → $\text{DRE} = 0$ (worst case).

Thus $0 \le \text{DRE} \le 1$.

**Worked Numerical Example.**
A project found 180 defects internally and 20 were reported by users post-release.

$$
\text{DRE} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 = 90\%
$$

**Interpretation.** The process removed 90 % of all defects before release — a benchmark of a CMMI Level 3 organisation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

**Statement.** A software build goes through $n = 4$ sequential stages (Design, Code, Unit Test, System Test). Each stage has its own pass rate $PY_i$. Show that the overall yield is the product of individual yields.

**Step 1 — Yield of stage *i*.**

If $N$ items enter stage $i$ and $N \cdot PY_i$ pass without rework, then $N(1 - PY_i)$ require rework or are scrapped.

**Step 2 — Items entering stage *i + 1*.**

Assuming all passed items flow forward (no rework loops considered here for simplicity):

$$
N_{i+1} = N_{i} \cdot PY_i
$$

**Step 3 — Telescope across *n* stages.**

$$
N_{n} = N_{0} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} PY_i
$$

**Step 4 — Overall yield.**

$$
\text{RTY} = \frac{N_{n}}{N_{0}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} PY_i
$$

**Numerical demonstration.** Let $PY = (0.95, 0.90, 0.85, 0.98)$ for the four stages.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RTY} &= 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.98 \\
&= (0.855) \times (0.833) \\
&\approx 0.7124
\end{aligned}
$$

So only **71.24 %** of items reach the customer defect-free despite each stage being > 85 % efficient. This is the *hidden factory* effect — small per-stage losses compound multiplicatively.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Improvement Impact on Cost of Quality

**Statement.** Show mathematically how process improvement reduces the Cost of Quality.

**Step 1 — Decompose CoQ.**

$$
\text{CoQ} = C_{prev} + C_{app} + C_{int} + C_{ext}
$$

Where:
- $C_{prev}$ = Prevention cost (training, process definition).
- $C_{app}$ = Appraisal cost (reviews, testing).
- $C_{int}$ = Internal failure cost (rework).
- $C_{ext}$ = External failure cost (warranty, patches).

**Step 2 — Express failure cost in terms of defect density.**

Internal failure cost is approximately proportional to defects found in-house (rework), and external failure cost scales with $D_{post}$.

$$
C_{int} \propto D_{pre}, \quad C_{ext} \propto D_{post}
$$

**Step 3 — Effect of an improvement intervention.**

Suppose a process improvement reduces the defect escape rate $\delta = D_{post} / D_{total}$ by a factor $k$ (i.e., new $\delta' = \delta / k$).

$$
C_{ext}^{\text{new}} = \frac{C_{ext}^{\text{old}}}{k}
$$

**Step 4 — Net change.**

$$
\Delta\text{CoQ} = \Delta C_{prev} - \left(1 - \tfrac{1}{k}\right) C_{ext}^{\text{old}}
$$

If $k > 1$ and the savings in $C_{ext}$ exceed the rise in $C_{prev}$, the improvement is *cost-positive*. This is Juran's *gold-mining* insight: **prevention is cheaper than cure**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)

**Statement.** Derive $C_{pk}$ for a process whose output mean is $\mu$ and standard deviation is $\sigma$, bounded by Upper Specification Limit (USL) and Lower Specification Limit (LSL).

**Step 1 — One-sided capability index (towards USL).**

$$
C_{p,\text{upper}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}
$$

This is the number of $\sigma$-units by which the mean is below the USL.

**Step 2 — One-sided capability index (towards LSL).**

$$
C_{p,\text{lower}} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the worst case (centering).**

A process may be shifted off-centre. The relevant index is the minimum of the two.

$$
C_{pk} = \min\!\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

- $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ → Process is **capable**.
- $C_{pk} \ge 1.67$ → Process is **highly capable** (Six-Sigma territory).

**Numerical Example.** $\mu = 50$, $\sigma = 4$, USL = 65, LSL = 35.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{p,\text{upper}} &= \frac{65 - 50}{3(4)} = \frac{15}{12} = 1.25 \\
C_{p,\text{lower}} &= \frac{50 - 35}{3(4)} = \frac{15}{12} = 1.25 \\
C_{pk} &= \min(1.25, 1.25) = 1.25
\end{aligned}
$$

Process is **borderline** — improvement actions (reducing $\sigma$) are warranted.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Improvement Activity to Outcome

| Improvement Activity | Process Stage Touched | Primary KPI Affected | Expected Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Formal code reviews | Stage 3 — Measurement | DRE | $\uparrow$ |
| Static analysis tool adoption | Stage 4 — Control | Defect Density | $\downarrow$ |
| Test automation | Stage 3 — Measurement | Cycle Time | $\downarrow$ |
| Root-cause analysis (RCA) | Stage 5 — Change | $C_{ext}$ | $\downarrow$ |
| Training & certification | Stage 2 — Definition | $C_{prev}$ (↑) then $C_{int}, C_{ext}$ (↓) | Net CoQ $\downarrow$ |
| Standardised checklists | Stage 1 — Analysis | Process Variance ($\sigma^2$) | $\downarrow$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Process Improvement Closed-Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1 Process Analysis] --> B[Stage 2 Process Definition]
    B --> C[Stage 3 Process Measurement]
    C --> D[Stage 4 Process Control]
    D --> E[Stage 5 Process Change and Improvement]
    E -->|Feedback baseline update| B
    C -->|Metric deviation| F[SPC Control Chart]
    F -->|Out of control| D
    D -->|Corrective Action| A
```

**Reading the diagram.** The inner cycle (A→B→C→D→E→B) is the **macro process improvement loop**; the outer branches show how metrics feed SPC control, which in turn triggers re-analysis.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: The IDEAL Model Phases

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[I - Initiating] --> P2[D - Diagnosing]
    P2 --> P3[E - Establishing]
    P3 --> P4[A - Acting]
    P4 --> P5[L - Learning]
    P5 -->|Lessons Learned Feed| P1
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: PDCA Deming Cycle (Atomic Improvement)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1A[Plan - Define goal and method] --> P2A[Do - Pilot execute]
    P2A --> P3A[Check - Measure results]
    P3A --> P4A[Act - Standardise or Iterate]
    P4A --> P1A
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: CMMI Maturity Levels (L1 to L5)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Level 1 Initial - Heroic effort, chaotic] --> L2[Level 2 Managed - Projects repeatable]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 Defined - Process standardised org wide]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 Quantitatively Managed - Statistical control]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 Optimising - Continuous improvement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember for the exam:** Each CMMI level adds a *new layer of process discipline* on top of the previous one. Level 2 adds project management; Level 3 adds engineering process areas; Level 4 adds quantitative management; Level 5 adds innovation and Causal Analysis & Resolution (CAR).

---

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Process Assurance Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Process Inputs: Requirements, Code, Tickets] --> ANL[Process Analysis Engine]
    ANL --> DEF[Process Definition Repository]
    DEF --> MEAS[Measurement and Metrics Module]
    MEAS --> CTRL[SPC and Control Engine]
    CTRL --> CHG[Change Management and RCA Module]
    CHG --> OUT[Process Improvement Outputs: Updated Baselines, Lessons Learned]
    OUT --> DEF
    MEAS --> KPI[KPI Dashboard DRE CoQ RTY]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]** `[3 Marks | CO3 | Bloom: Remember]`
> **Q1.** Define *Process Improvement* in the context of Software Quality Assurance.
> **Model Answer:** Process Improvement is the continuous, planned, and measured activity of analysing, defining, measuring, controlling, and changing the software development process itself, with the goal of increasing product quality, reducing defects, lowering cost of quality, and improving schedule predictability. It is governed by standards such as CMMI and ISO 9001 and operates on the principle that "a good process produces a good product."

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]** `[3 Marks | CO3 | Bloom: Understand]`
> **Q2.** List the five phases of the IDEAL improvement model.
> **Model Answer:** The five phases are: **I** – Initiating (build business case & sponsorship); **D** – Diagnosing (analyse current state); **E** – Establishing (plan the improvement); **A** – Acting (implement pilots and roll out); **L** – Learning (analyse results and institutionalise lessons learned).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** `[14 Marks | CO3 | Bloom: Apply / Analyse]`
>
> **Question A.**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **five-stage process improvement life cycle** in detail. For each stage, give the primary activity, the artefact produced, and the role of a Software Quality Engineer.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)**. A software build passes through four stages with yields 0.95, 0.90, 0.85, 0.98 respectively. Compute the RTY and comment on the *hidden-factory effect*.

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Five-Stage Process Improvement Life Cycle**

1. **Process Analysis** – Identify existing workflows via SIPOC, value-stream maps. *Artefact:* As-Is process model. *SQA Role:* Audits and validates process artefacts.
2. **Process Definition** – Standardise the to-be process in a Software Process Description aligned to IEEE 1074 / ISO 12207. *Artefact:* Process baseline, templates, checklists. *SQA Role:* Reviews documentation for compliance.
3. **Process Measurement** – Apply the **GQM (Goal–Question–Metric)** paradigm to collect size, effort, defect, and schedule data. *Artefact:* Measurement plan, metrics database. *SQA Role:* Ensures data integrity and traceability.
4. **Process Control** – Apply **Statistical Process Control (SPC)**; use control charts (X-bar, R-chart, p-chart) to detect out-of-control conditions. *Artefact:* Control charts, deviation reports. *SQA Role:* Triggers corrective actions.
5. **Process Change & Improvement** – Apply **Causal Analysis and Resolution (CAR)**; pilot change in one project, measure, then roll out. *Artefact:* Updated baseline, lessons-learned report. *SQA Role:* Validates the improvement impact.

**(b) RTY Derivation**

As shown in Section 3.2:

$$
\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} PY_i
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RTY} &= 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.98 \\
&= 0.855 \times 0.833 \\
&\approx 0.7124
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on hidden-factory effect.** Although each stage individually yields > 85 %, the cumulative RTY drops to **71.24 %** because yield losses *multiply* through the chain. This is the "hidden factory" — almost 29 % of the effort is consumed in rework that is invisible in any single stage's metric. Process improvement must therefore target **every stage**, not just the most visible one.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]** `[14 Marks | CO3 | Bloom: Apply / Analyse]`
>
> **Question B.**
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Define **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)**. A project recorded 180 defects before release and 20 defects post-release. Calculate DRE. Discuss how DRE relates to process maturity.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **CMMI five-level maturity model**. How does a movement from Level 1 to Level 5 change the way process improvement is institutionalised?

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Defect Removal Efficiency**

By definition:

$$
\text{DRE} = \frac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{DRE} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 = 90\%
$$

**Relation to maturity.** Empirical studies (e.g., IBM Systems Sciences Institute) suggest DRE of ~85 % at CMMI Level 1, ~95 % at Level 3, and > 99 % at Level 5. Higher DRE indicates that the in-house verification & validation activities are mature; low DRE signals that defects are leaking to customers — a hallmark of an *Initial* (Level 1) organisation.

**(b) CMMI Five-Level Model**

| Level | Name | Characteristic | Process Improvement Institutionalisation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial | Ad-hoc, heroics, success depends on individuals | None — improvement is accidental. |
| 2 | Managed | Project-level planning, tracking, configuration management | Improvement is per-project, not institutional. |
| 3 | Defined | Organisation-wide process standardisation, process assets library | Improvement is *defined* and reused across projects. |
| 4 | Quantitatively Managed | Statistical control of subprocesses | Improvement is **data-driven**; deviations trigger quantitative actions. |
| 5 | Optimising | Causal Analysis & Resolution (CAR), innovation | Improvement is **continuous and innovation-led**; the org proactively eliminates root causes. |
