As the organisation climbs levels, process improvement shifts from **reactive** (Level 1) → **repeatable** (Level 2) → **defined** (Level 3) → **measured** (Level 4) → **continuously optimising** (Level 5).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing pitfalls in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Process Assurance with Product Testing.** Process assurance audits *how* the work is done; product testing validates *what* is built. Examiners deduct marks if you mix the two.
> 2. **Skipping the formula derivation.** In 14-mark questions, you must *derive*, not just state, formulas like DRE, RTY, $C_{pk}$. Marks are split between definition, formula, substitution, and interpretation.
> 3. **Forgetting the "feedback loop".** Improvement processes are *cyclical*; describing them as linear loses you the "closed-loop" mark.
> 4. **Mixing IDEAL and PDCA.** IDEAL is a *program-level* improvement model; PDCA is an *operational-level* cycle. Using them interchangeably costs marks.
> 5. **CMMI levels — order matters.** Listing the five levels out of order is a guaranteed partial-mark loss.
> 6. **No numerical example in Part B.** Always illustrate formulas with a numerical example; the KTU pattern awards 1–2 marks for substitution and computation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Process Assurance** ≠ Product Testing; it audits and improves the *process itself*.
- [x] The **5-stage life cycle**: Analysis → Definition → Measurement → Control → Change (closed loop).
- [x] **IDEAL Model** = Initiating, Diagnosing, Establishing, Acting, Learning (program-level).
- [x] **PDCA Cycle** = Plan, Do, Check, Act (operational-level atomic cycle).
- [x] **CMMI Levels** = Initial (1) → Managed (2) → Defined (3) → Quantitatively Managed (4) → Optimising (5).
- [x] **DRE** = $D_{pre} / (D_{pre} + D_{post})$; target ≥ 95 % for mature organisations.
- [x] **Defect Density** = Defects / KLOC or per FP; lower is better.
- [x] **RTY** = product of stage yields; demonstrates the *hidden-factory effect*.
- [x] **$C_{pk}$** = $\min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$; ≥ 1.33 is capable.
- [x] **CoQ** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure; prevention is the cheapest lever.
- [x] **GQM (Goal–Question–Metric)** is the canonical way to link business goals to measurable metrics.
- [x] **CAR (Causal Analysis & Resolution)** is the CMMI Level 5 specific practice driving continuous improvement.
- [x] Standards: **ISO 9001**, **CMMI**, **ISO 12207**, **IEEE 1058** are exam-favourites.
- [x] Always illustrate a 14-mark answer with a **numerical example** and a **diagram** (PDCA / IDEAL / Maturity pyramid).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Quality Metrics
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process Quality Metrics

# Process Quality Metrics: The Heartbeat of Software Engineering Excellence

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Quality Metrics** are quantitative measurement instruments applied at the procedural layer of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) to evaluate, control, predict, and continuously improve the effectiveness, efficiency, and maturity of the methodologies, workflows, and engineering practices used to construct software artifacts.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Process Metrics are categorized under the **Goal-Question-Metric (GQM)** paradigm, where measurable indicators are derived from organizational quality goals and mapped to specific engineering questions. They operate at the **macro-process level** rather than the **product level**, meaning they measure *how well* the team builds software, not *how good* the software itself is.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hospital Analogy**: Imagine a hospital. The doctors (developers) treat patients (the software) using established procedures (the process). Now, the hospital administration does not only measure patient recovery rates (product metrics) — they also track the *process* of treatment: average surgery duration, anesthesia administration accuracy, sterilization cycle compliance, post-operative infection rates per ward, and nurse-to-patient ratios. **Process Quality Metrics are exactly this — they measure the healthcare delivery system, not just patient outcomes.** A hospital can have great survival rates by luck, but only a well-measured process guarantees sustained excellence.

### Why Process Metrics Matter in KTU Context

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. Just as OBE measures student learning outcomes, Process Quality Metrics measure **engineering process outcomes**. They enable:

1. **Predictive Capability** — Forecast defect arrival rates before release.
2. **Root Cause Analysis** — Identify whether poor quality stems from coding, testing, or design.
3. **Benchmarking** — Compare team performance across projects using industry baselines (e.g., ISBSG, CMMI).
4. **ROI Justification** — Quantify the return on investment for process improvement initiatives like Six Sigma or CMMI adoption.
5. **Continuous Improvement** — Feed data into PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycles and Kaizen events.

### Classification of Process Quality Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of Process Measurement (IEEE Std 1061 / KTU Module 3)**

| Pillar | Focus | Example Indicators |
|--------|-------|--------------------|
| **Productivity Metrics** | Output per unit input | Lines of Code per person-month, Function Points delivered |
| **Quality Metrics** | Defectiveness and reliability | Defect Density, DRE, MTBF |
| **Schedule/Performance Metrics** | Time adherence | Effort Variance, Schedule Slippage Index |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of Process Measurement

A robust process quality metric must satisfy five cardinal properties:

1. **Validity** — It must genuinely measure what it claims to measure.
2. **Reliability** — Repeated measurements under identical conditions yield identical values.
3. **Quantifiability** — The metric must produce a numeric value, not a qualitative judgment.
4. **Sensitivity** — It must respond to meaningful process variations.
5. **Cost-Effectiveness** — The cost of data collection must not exceed the benefit of the information.

### 2.2 Process Maturity Models — The Context for Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CMMI (Capability Maturity Model Integration)** defines five maturity levels. Each level demands progressively more rigorous process metrics:
> - **Level 1 (Initial):** Ad hoc, no metrics baseline.
> - **Level 2 (Managed):** Project-level metrics — earned value, effort, schedule.
> - **Level 3 (Defined):** Organization-wide standard process metrics.
> - **Level 4 (Quantitatively Managed):** Statistical Process Control (SPC) — control charts, Cpk.
> - **Level 5 (Optimizing):** Causal analysis, defect prevention metrics, ROI of innovations.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU University Examinations**

| # | Metric Name | Formula | Boundary Conditions | Typical Units |
|---|-------------|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \dfrac{D}{S}$ | $D \geq 0,\ S > 0$ | defects per KLOC or per FP |
| 2 | **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $DRE = \left(\dfrac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}}\right) \times 100$ | $0\% \leq DRE \leq 100\%$ | percentage |
| 3 | **Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)** | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $\sigma > 0$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda > 0$ (constant failure rate) | hours |
| 5 | **Cyclomatic Complexity $V(G)$** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Test/Code Coverage** | $Coverage = \left(\dfrac{I_{executed}}{I_{total}}\right) \times 100$ | $0\% \leq Coverage \leq 100\%$ | percentage |
| 7 | **Effort Variance** | $Variance_{effort} = \dfrac{E_{actual} - E_{estimated}}{E_{estimated}} \times 100$ | can be negative or positive | percentage |
| 8 | **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | $CoQ = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\ failure} + C_{external\ failure}$ | all $C \geq 0$ | currency units |
| 9 | **Schedule Slippage Index** | $SSI = \dfrac{T_{actual} - T_{planned}}{T_{planned}}$ | can be negative (early delivery) | dimensionless ratio |
| 10 | **Yield (First Pass Yield)** | $Yield = \dfrac{N_{passed}}{N_{total}}$ | $0 \leq Yield \leq 1$ | ratio |

Where:
- $D$ = Number of defects
- $S$ = Software size (KLOC or Function Points)
- $D_{pre}$ = Defects removed before release
- $D_{post}$ = Defects found after release (in field)
- $USL, LSL$ = Upper and Lower Specification Limits
- $\mu$ = Process mean
- $\sigma$ = Process standard deviation
- $\lambda$ = Failure rate
- $E_{actual}, E_{estimated}$ = Actual vs. estimated effort (person-hours)
- $C_{prevention}$ = Cost of prevention activities (training, tools)
- $C_{appraisal}$ = Cost of inspection, testing, reviews
- $C_{internal\ failure}$ = Cost of rework before delivery
- $C_{external\ failure}$ = Cost of warranty, patch releases, reputational damage

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Six Sigma Implementation:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.5$ is the industry benchmark for a "Six Sigma capable" process, corresponding to **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**.
- **CMMI Level 4 Audits:** Organizations must demonstrate statistical control of their development process via Shewhart control charts.
- **Agile/Scrum Velocity Tracking:** Modern Agile frameworks repurpose traditional process metrics into **velocity, burndown rate, and lead time** — all process-level quality indicators.
- **DevOps DORA Metrics:** Deployment Frequency, Lead Time for Changes, Change Failure Rate, and Mean Time to Recovery are direct descendants of classical process metrics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cyclomatic Complexity from Graph Theory Foundations

The cyclomatic complexity $V(G)$ of a software module is mathematically derived from **Euler's theorem for planar graphs**. This derivation is a high-yield topic for KTU University Exams.

**Step 1 — Define the Control Flow Graph (CFG)**

Let $G = (N, E)$ represent the control flow graph of a program module, where:
- $N$ = the set of nodes (program statements, decision points)
- $E$ = the set of directed edges (control flow paths)

**Step 2 — State Euler's Formula for Connected Planar Graphs**

For any connected planar graph:

$$
V_{graph} - E_{graph} + F_{graph} = 2
$$

Where $F_{graph}$ is the number of faces (regions) of the planar embedding.

**Step 3 — Adapt for Program Flow**

For a structured program with a single entry and exit node, the cyclomatic complexity $V(G)$ equals the number of linearly independent paths through the program, which by Euler's adaptation becomes:

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P
$$

Where $P$ = number of connected components (for a single program, $P = 1$).

**Step 4 — Alternative Forms (often used in KTU exam answers)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= E - N + 2P \\
&= \pi + 1 \quad \text{(where } \pi = \text{ number of predicate nodes)} \\
&= \text{Number of regions in the CFG}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Cyclomatic Complexity Calculation

**Problem:** Given a program module whose CFG has $E = 14$ edges, $N = 10$ nodes, and $P = 1$ connected component. Compute $V(G)$ and interpret the result.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= E - N + 2P \\
&= 14 - 10 + 2(1) \\
&= 14 - 10 + 2 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- The module has **6 linearly independent execution paths**.
- The minimum number of test cases required for **branch coverage** is 6.
- **McCabe's Threshold Rule:** $V(G) \leq 10$ indicates acceptable complexity; $V(G) > 10$ flags the module for refactoring.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) Calculation

**Problem:** A software product was released with 45 defects found during pre-release testing. After release, customers reported 5 additional defects. Compute the DRE.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{pre} &= 45 \\
D_{post} &= 5 \\
DRE &= \left(\frac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \left(\frac{45}{45 + 5}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \left(\frac{45}{50}\right) \times 100 \\
&= 0.9 \times 100 \\
&= 90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- 90% of defects were removed before release — **industry benchmark for "good" is $\geq 95\%$**.
- The team needs to enhance pre-release testing techniques (e.g., add fuzz testing, static analysis).

### 3.4 Worked Example: Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)

**Problem:** A software testing process has specification limits $LSL = 4.0$ defects/KLOC and $USL = 6.0$ defects/KLOC. The process mean is $\mu = 5.0$ defects/KLOC with $\sigma = 0.5$. Compute $C_{pk}$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right) \\
C_{upper} &= \frac{6.0 - 5.0}{3 \times 0.5} = \frac{1.0}{1.5} = 0.667 \\
C_{lower} &= \frac{5.0 - 4.0}{3 \times 0.5} = \frac{1.0}{1.5} = 0.667 \\
C_{pk} &= \min(0.667,\ 0.667) = 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $C_{pk} = 0.667 < 1.0$ → The process is **not capable** (defects are not within statistical control).
- To reach **Six Sigma** ($C_{pk} \geq 1.5$), $\sigma$ must be reduced to $\sigma \leq 0.222$ defects/KLOC.

### 3.5 Worked Example: Cost of Quality (CoQ) Breakdown

**Problem:** A project reports the following annual costs (in INR Lakhs): Prevention = ₹8, Appraisal = ₹12, Internal Failure = ₹15, External Failure = ₹25. Compute CoQ and the **Prevention-Appraisal Ratio (PAR)**.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CoQ &= C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\ failure} + C_{external\ failure} \\
&= 8 + 12 + 15 + 25 \\
&= 60 \text{ Lakhs} \\
PAR &= \frac{C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal}}{C_{internal\ failure} + C_{external\ failure}} \\
&= \frac{8 + 12}{15 + 25} = \frac{20}{40} = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- CoQ = ₹60 Lakhs/year.
- $PAR = 0.5 < 1.0$ indicates the organization is **spending more on failure than prevention** — a classic anti-pattern. Best practice targets $PAR \geq 1.0$.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Process Metric

| Engineering Goal | Recommended Metric | KTU Bloom Level |
|------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| Reduce late-stage defects | Defect Removal Efficiency | Apply (L3) |
| Justify process investment | Cost of Quality | Analyze (L4) |
| Quantify code testability | Cyclomatic Complexity | Apply (L3) |
| Measure team throughput | Function Points per person-month | Apply (L3) |
| Achieve statistical control | Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$ | Analyze (L4) |
| Predict field reliability | MTTF / MTBF | Evaluate (L5) |
| Improve scheduling accuracy | Schedule Slippage Index | Apply (L3) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Quality Metrics — Architectural Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[SDLC Process Layer] --> nodeB[Requirement Phase]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Design Phase]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Implementation Phase]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Testing Phase]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Maintenance Phase]
    nodeB --> nodeM1[Requirement Volatility Index]
    nodeB --> nodeM2[Requirements Traceability Coverage]
    nodeC --> nodeM3[Design Review Effectiveness]
    nodeC --> nodeM4[Cyclomatic Complexity]
    nodeD --> nodeM5[Defect Density]
    nodeD --> nodeM6[Code Coverage Percentage]
    nodeD --> nodeM7[Static Analysis Score]
    nodeE --> nodeM8[Defect Removal Efficiency]
    nodeE --> nodeM9[Test Case Effectiveness]
    nodeE --> nodeM10[Mean Time to Detect]
    nodeF --> nodeM11[Mean Time Between Failures]
    nodeF --> nodeM12[Cost of Quality]
    nodeM1 --> nodeAgg[Process Quality Dashboard]
    nodeM2 --> nodeAgg
    nodeM3 --> nodeAgg
    nodeM4 --> nodeAgg
    nodeM5 --> nodeAgg
    nodeM6 --> nodeAgg
    nodeM7 --> nodeAgg
    nodeM8 --> nodeAgg
    nodeM9 --> nodeAgg
    nodeM10 --> nodeAgg
    nodeM11 --> nodeAgg
    nodeM12 --> nodeAgg
    nodeAgg --> nodeOut1[Continuous Improvement PDCA]
    nodeAgg --> nodeOut2[CMMI Maturity Progression]
    nodeAgg --> nodeOut3[Six Sigma Capability Assessment]
```

### 4.2 GQM (Goal-Question-Metric) Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeG1[GOAL: Achieve High Product Quality] --> nodeQ1[QUESTION: How effective is our testing process?]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeMetric1[METRIC: Defect Removal Efficiency]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeMetric2[METRIC: Test Case Effectiveness]
    nodeG1 --> nodeQ2[QUESTION: How predictable is our delivery schedule?]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeMetric3[METRIC: Schedule Slippage Index]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeMetric4[METRIC: Effort Variance Percentage]
    nodeG1 --> nodeQ3[QUESTION: How mature is our development process?]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeMetric5[METRIC: Process Capability Index Cpk]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeMetric6[METRIC: Cyclomatic Complexity]
```

### 4.3 Cost of Quality Categorization Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeCoQ[Cost of Quality CoQ] --> nodeP[Prevention Costs]
    nodeCoQ --> nodeA[Appraisal Costs]
    nodeCoQ --> nodeIF[Internal Failure Costs]
    nodeCoQ --> nodeEF[External Failure Costs]
    nodeP --> nodeP1[Training and Certification]
    nodeP --> nodeP2[Process Improvement Initiatives]
    nodeP --> nodeP3[Tool Acquisition]
    nodeA --> nodeA1[Code Inspections]
    nodeA --> nodeA2[Test Execution]
    nodeA --> nodeA3[Static Analysis Tooling]
    nodeIF --> nodeIF1[Rework Effort]
    nodeIF --> nodeIF2[Defect Triage Meetings]
    nodeEF --> nodeEF1[Warranty Claims]
    nodeEF --> nodeEF2[Hotfix Releases]
    nodeEF --> nodeEF3[Reputation Damage]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define Process Quality Metrics. List any four commonly used process metrics in software engineering.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Process Quality Metrics are quantitative measurements applied at the procedural layer of the SDLC to evaluate, control, and improve the efficiency, effectiveness, and maturity of software development workflows. They measure *how well* the team builds software, rather than *how good* the resulting product is.

**Four commonly used process metrics:**

1. **Defect Density** — Number of defects per unit size (KLOC or Function Points).
2. **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** — Percentage of defects removed before release.
3. **Cyclomatic Complexity** — Number of linearly independent paths in a module.
4. **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** — Total expenditure on prevention, appraisal, and failure activities.

*Examiner's Note:* A common mistake is confusing *process metrics* with *product metrics*. Product metrics (e.g., lines of code, function points measured on the artifact itself) measure the software. Process metrics measure the development workflow. Award full marks only if the student clearly distinguishes the two.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Explain the significance of Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) as a process quality metric.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** measures the percentage of defects eliminated before the software is released to end-users. The formula is:
>
> $$
> DRE = \left(\frac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}}\right) \times 100
> $$
>
> where $D_{pre}$ = defects found before release and $D_{post}$ = defects reported after release by customers.

**Significance:**

- **Predictive Power:** A high DRE (industry standard $\geq 95\%$) indicates that the testing and review processes are robust, reducing post-release maintenance costs.
- **Process Indicator:** DRE is a leading indicator of process maturity. CMMI Level 3 organizations typically achieve DRE $> 95\%$.
- **Cost Reduction:** Every defect caught pre-release costs approximately **10× less** than one caught post-release, due to avoided warranty claims and reputational damage.
- **Continuous Improvement:** Tracking DRE trends over multiple projects reveals whether testing process enhancements are effective.

---

### Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model | Internal Choice Option A]**

### Question A:

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the Goal-Question-Metric (GQM) approach for defining process quality metrics. Discuss its advantages over ad-hoc metric selection. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A software testing process has specification limits $LSL = 2.0$ defects/KLOC and $USL = 4.0$ defects/KLOC. The current process mean is $\mu = 2.8$ defects/KLOC with standard deviation $\sigma = 0.4$. Calculate:
   - (i) The Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$.
   - (ii) Comment on whether the process is Six Sigma capable.
   - (iii) What value of $\sigma$ would be required to achieve $C_{pk} = 1.5$? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

The **Goal-Question-Metric (GQM)** approach, formalized by **Victor Basili** at the University of Maryland, is a top-down measurement framework comprising three levels:

1. **Goal Level:** Define a measurable quality goal (e.g., "Reduce field defects by 40% within 6 months").
2. **Question Level:** Pose questions that operationalize the goal (e.g., "What is our current DRE?", "What is the average defect density per module?").
3. **Metric Level:** Define the quantitative data needed to answer each question (e.g., DRE formula, defect count, module size).

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeG[Goal: Achieve High Software Quality] --> nodeQ1[Q1: How effective is testing?]
    nodeG --> nodeQ2[Q2: How stable is the codebase?]
    nodeG --> nodeQ3[Q3: How predictable is delivery?]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeM1[M1: Defect Removal Efficiency]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeM2[M2: Test Coverage Percentage]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeM3[M3: Defect Density]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeM4[M4: Cyclomatic Complexity]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeM5[M5: Effort Variance]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeM6[M6: Schedule Slippage Index]
```

**Advantages over ad-hoc metric selection:**

- **Alignment with Business Goals:** Every metric traces back to a strategic objective, eliminating vanity metrics.
- **Traceability:** Auditors and management can trace any metric to its originating question and goal.
- **Customizability:** Each project tailors its own GQM hierarchy while maintaining methodological rigor.
- **Avoids Metric Overload:** Limits metrics to only those answering meaningful questions.
- **Supports OBE Philosophy:** Aligns naturally with KTU's Outcome-Based Education framework by focusing on *measurable outcomes*.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**(i) Calculation of $C_{pk}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right) \\
C_{upper} &= \frac{4.0 - 2.8}{3 \times 0.4} = \frac{1.2}{1.2} = 1.0 \\
C_{lower} &= \frac{2.8 - 2.0}{3 \times 0.4} = \frac{0.8}{1.2} = 0.667 \\
C_{pk} &= \min(1.0,\ 0.667) = 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Comment on Six Sigma Capability:**

Since $C_{pk} = 0.667 < 1.0$, the process is **NOT Six Sigma capable**. Six Sigma requires $C_{pk} \geq 1.5$. The process is producing defects beyond statistical control limits.

**(iii) Required $\sigma$ for $C_{pk} = 1.5$:**

Since the mean is closer to LSL (off-center), $C_{lower}$ governs. Setting $C_{lower} = 1.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} &= 1.5 \\
\frac{2.8 - 2.0}{3\sigma} &= 1.5 \\
\frac{0.8}{3\sigma} &= 1.5 \\
3\sigma &= \frac{0.8}{1.5} = 0.5333 \\
\sigma &= \frac{0.5333}{3} = 0.1778 \text{ defects/KLOC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Required $\sigma \leq 0.178$ defects/KLOC** for the process to be Six Sigma capable.

---

### Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model | Internal Choice Option B]**

### Question B:

**(a)** Explain the Cost of Quality (CoQ) model with its four components. How does the Prevention-Appraisal-Failure (PAF) model help in process improvement? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A software project has the following metrics over three consecutive releases:
   - Release 1: 120 defects found pre-release, 30 defects reported post-release.
   - Release 2: 150 defects found pre-release, 12 defects reported post-release.
   - Release 3: 180 defects found pre-release, 6 defects reported post-release.

   Calculate the **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** for each release and analyze the trend. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

The **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** model, popularized by **Joseph Juran** and **Philip Crosby**, classifies all quality-related expenditures into four categories:

$$
CoQ = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\ failure} + C_{external\ failure}
$$

**The Four Components:**

1. **Prevention Costs ($C_{prevention}$):** Investment in activities that *prevent* defects from occurring.
   - Examples: Training programs, process improvement initiatives, tool acquisition, quality planning.

2. **Appraisal Costs ($C_{appraisal}$):** Cost of activities that *detect* defects before delivery.
   - Examples: Inspections, code reviews, test execution, static analysis tool licensing.

3. **Internal Failure Costs ($C_{internal\ failure}$):** Cost of defects discovered *before* release.
   - Examples: Rework effort, retesting, defect triage meetings, schedule slippage due to bug fixes.

4. **External Failure Costs ($C_{external\ failure}$):** Cost of defects discovered *after* release by customers.
   - Examples: Warranty claims, hotfix engineering, customer support escalation, reputation damage, litigation.

**PAF Model Logic:**

The PAF model posits an inverse relationship: **increasing prevention/appraisal spending reduces failure costs exponentially**. Optimal organizations target a ratio:

$$
PAR = \frac{C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal}}{C_{internal\ failure} + C_{external\ failure}} \geq 1.0
$$

When $PAR \geq 1.0$, the organization spends more on building quality *in* than on repairing defects *out* — a hallmark of mature processes (CMMI Level 4+).

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**DRE for Release 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DRE_1 &= \left(\frac{120}{120 + 30}\right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{120}{150}\right) \times 100 = 80.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**DRE for Release 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DRE_2 &= \left(\frac{150}{150 + 12}\right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{150}{162}\right) \times 100 = 92.59\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**DRE for Release 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DRE_3 &= \left(\frac{180}{180 + 6}\right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{180}{186}\right) \times 100 = 96.77\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trend Analysis:**

| Release | $D_{pre}$ | $D_{post}$ | DRE | Total Defects |
|---------|-----------|------------|-----|---------------|
| 1 | 120 | 30 | 80.00% | 150 |
| 2 | 150 | 12 | 92.59% | 162 |
| 3 | 180 | 6 | 96.77% | 186 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:**
> - The DRE has improved from **80% → 92.59% → 96.77%** across three releases, indicating **significant process maturation**.
> - Pre-release defect detection has increased (likely due to improved testing rigor).
> - Post-release defects have dropped dramatically (30 → 12 → 6), demonstrating **reduced customer-facing failures**.
> - By Release 3, the process approaches the industry benchmark of $\geq 95\%$ — a CMMI Level 3 characteristic.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing Product and Process Metrics:** Students often quote "Lines of Code" as a process metric. LOC of an existing artifact is a *product* metric; LOC *produced per person-month* is a process metric. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **DRE Formula Inversion:** A frequent mistake is computing $\frac{D_{post}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}}$. Always verify the numerator is *pre-release* defects.
> 3. **Missing Process Capability Context:** When $C_{pk}$ is asked, students compute it correctly but forget to interpret whether it satisfies the Six Sigma threshold ($C_{pk} \geq 1.5$). Always include the interpretation step.
> 4. **Skipping the GQM Diagram:** For 7-mark questions on GQM, a diagram is mandatory. Without it, expect a 2-mark deduction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Process Quality Metrics**

- ✅ Process metrics measure the *engineering workflow*; product metrics measure the *artifact*.
- ✅ **DRE Formula:** $\frac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}} \times 100$ — industry benchmark $\geq 95\%$.
- ✅ **Cyclomatic Complexity:** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ — McCabe threshold $V(G) \leq 10$.
- ✅ **$C_{pk}$ Interpretation:** $< 1.0$ = not capable; $1.0$–$1.33$ = marginally capable; $\geq 1.5$ = Six Sigma capable.
- ✅ **CoQ Formula:** Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure.
- ✅ **PAR Target:** $\geq 1.0$ indicates mature quality investment philosophy.
- ✅ **GQM Approach:** Basili's top-down framework linking goals → questions → metrics.
- ✅ **CMMI Levels:** Level 4 demands SPC and $C_{pk}$; Level 5 demands causal analysis.
- ✅ **Six Sigma Benchmark:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.5$ corresponds to **3.4 DPMO**.
- ✅ **MTBF / MTTF:** $MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ for constant failure rate processes.
- ✅ **Effort Variance:** Negative values indicate under-estimation; positive values indicate over-estimation.
- ✅ **Schedule Slippage:** Always compute as $(T_{actual} - T_{planned}) / T_{planned}$, can be negative for early delivery.
- ✅ **Test Coverage Goal:** Industry standard $80\%$ branch coverage for critical systems; $100\%$ for safety-critical (avionics, medical).
- ✅ **Process vs. Project Metrics:** Process metrics apply across projects; project metrics are project-specific instances.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Change Control Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Change Control Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Change Control Process** is the formal, systematic, and auditable procedure within **Software Configuration Management (SCM)** that governs how proposed modifications to software configuration items (CIs) are **requested, evaluated, approved, scheduled, implemented, verified, and released** throughout the software lifecycle. It is the operational backbone of the broader **Configuration Control** discipline, ensuring that no change to a baselined software artifact is introduced arbitrarily.

As per the **IEEE 828-2012 Standard for Configuration Management in Systems and Software Engineering**, change control is a multi-stakeholder, gate-based workflow that balances three competing forces: **functional improvement**, **risk containment**, and **schedule/cost discipline**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Change Control Process is a structured, multi-stage workflow comprising Change Request (CR) submission, Impact Analysis, Change Control Board (CCB) Review, Decision, Implementation, Verification, and Closure, designed to ensure that every modification to a baselined Software Configuration Item is authorized, traceable, and does not degrade the integrity of the system."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Surgery Approval System"

Imagine a hospital where a patient (the **software system**) has been declared stable after a major operation (the **baseline release**). Now a doctor wants to change the patient's medication (a **change request**).

Would the hospital let the doctor just walk in and alter the prescription? **Absolutely not.** Instead, the hospital follows a strict protocol:

| Hospital Analogy | Software Change Control Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient in stable condition | Baselined Software (frozen, audited) |
| Doctor prescribes new medicine | Developer submits a **Change Request (CR)** |
| Senior medical board reviews risk | **Change Control Board (CCB)** evaluates impact |
| Board approves/rejects | **Decision Gate** (Approve / Reject / Defer) |
| Nurse administers medicine | Developer **implements** the change |
| Post-surgery check-up & vitals | **Verification & Regression Testing** |
| Updated medical records | **Configuration Audit & updated baselines** |

This analogy makes one truth immediately clear: **the goal of change control is NOT to prevent change (that would be stagnation) — it is to prevent *uncontrolled* change**, which is the single largest cause of software defects in large-scale engineering projects.

---

## 1.3 Why Change Control Exists — The Three Pillars

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of Change Control (High-Yield KTU Concept)**
> 1. **Traceability** — Every change is linked to a requester, a reason, a decision, and an outcome.
> 2. **Integrity** — The system never deviates silently from its approved baseline.
> 3. **Accountability** — Clear ownership at every gate (who approved, who implemented, who verified).

Without these, projects suffer from **"configuration drift"** — where the production software slowly diverges from what was tested, documented, and signed off.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology You Must Know (KTU Glossary)

- **Configuration Item (CI):** Any artifact (code, document, model, test script) placed under configuration control.
- **Baseline:** A formally approved reference point of CIs (e.g., *v1.0, v2.0*).
- **Change Request (CR):** A formal proposal to modify one or more CIs.
- **Change Control Board (CCB):** The multi-disciplinary body that reviews, prioritizes, and authorizes changes.
- **Impact Analysis:** The technical study of what a proposed change will affect (files, modules, tests, costs, risks).
- **Configuration Audit:** Independent verification that the implemented change matches the approved change.

---

## 1.5 Physical / Operational Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **standard SQA metrics** are evaluated during the change control process and frequently appear in KTU numerical/descriptive questions. They are stated in **bold** for emphasis:

- **Mean Time to Change (MTTC):** Average time from CR submission to deployment.
- **Change Failure Rate (CFR):** $\text{CFR} = \dfrac{\text{Number of failed changes}}{\text{Total number of changes deployed}} \times 100\%$
- **Change Lead Time:** Time from CR approval to production release.
- **Defect Rejection Ratio (DRR):** $\text{DRR} = \dfrac{\text{Defects introduced by change}}{\text{Defects closed by change}}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Phases of the Change Control Process (Decomposed)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to articulate the **seven canonical phases** of the change control workflow. Each phase has a *gate* (an entry/exit condition) that must be satisfied before the next phase begins.

### Phase 1 — Change Request (CR) Initiation
- Triggered by a stakeholder (customer, tester, developer, support engineer).
- Captured in a standardized **CR Form** containing:
  - Unique **CR ID** (e.g., `CR-2024-0387`).
  - **Requester**, date, priority (`Low / Medium / High / Critical`).
  - Affected CIs and modules.
  - **Business Justification** (why is this change needed?).
- The CR is logged in the **Configuration Management Database (CMDB)**.

### Phase 2 — Initial Screening & Categorization
- A **Configuration Manager (CM)** performs a first-level triage.
- Categorizes the change as:
  - **Standard Change:** Low risk, pre-approved, routine (e.g., typo fix in help text).
  - **Normal Change:** Requires full CCB review.
  - **Emergency Change:** Expedited path for production hotfixes (requires post-hoc CCB ratification).

### Phase 3 — Impact Analysis (Technical + Business)
- Conducted by the **Lead Developer / Architect** with QA support.
- Evaluates the **blast radius** of the change:

  | Dimension | What is Analyzed |
  |---|---|
  | **Code Impact** | Modules, classes, APIs touched |
  | **Test Impact** | Number of test cases requiring re-execution |
  | **Schedule Impact** | Effort in person-hours/days |
  | **Risk Impact** | Probability of regression, security exposure |
  | **Cost Impact** | Resource utilization, downstream licensing |

### Phase 4 — CCB Review & Decision
- The **Change Control Board** convenes (weekly/bi-weekly or ad hoc for emergencies).
- Members typically include: Project Manager, Lead Architect, QA Lead, Customer Representative, Configuration Manager.
- The CCB records a formal **Decision**: `Approve`, `Reject`, `Defer`, or `Request More Information`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The CCB is a decision-making body, not a technical body.** It weighs business priorities, cost-benefit, and risk acceptance. The technical feasibility has already been established in Phase 3.

### Phase 5 — Implementation
- Only **approved** CRs proceed.
- The change is developed in an **isolated branch** (in Git: `feature/CR-2024-0387`).
- Strict adherence to coding standards, peer review, and unit testing.
- All changes are committed with the CR ID in the commit message for traceability.

### Phase 6 — Verification & Configuration Audit
- **Verification:** QA performs targeted testing (new functionality + regression).
- **Configuration Audit:** An independent auditor verifies that:
  - The implemented change matches the approved CR scope (no scope creep).
  - All related documentation (design, user manual, test scripts) is updated.
  - Version numbers, release notes, and baselined artifacts are consistent.

### Phase 7 — Closure & Release
- The CR is **closed** in the CMDB.
- The modified CIs are merged into the **mainline** and a new baseline is formed (e.g., `v2.1`).
- A **Change Advisory Record** is archived for future audits.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Phase — Engineering Rationale

| Phase | Why it Exists (The Real Reason) |
|---|---|
| CR Initiation | Without a paper trail, there is no accountability and no way to measure trends. |
| Screening | Prevents the CCB from being overwhelmed by trivial requests. |
| Impact Analysis | Prevents the classic "small change, massive regression" disaster. |
| CCB Review | Brings cross-functional perspective — no single engineer makes a unilateral call. |
| Implementation | Isolates risk; allows rollback if the change fails. |
| Verification & Audit | Proves the change is *complete*, *correct*, and *consistent* with intent. |
| Closure | Feeds the **process improvement loop** (e.g., CMMI, ISO 9001). |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Metric Table

> [!NOTE]
> **The table below is the exam-day cheat sheet for quantitative questions on Change Control.**

| Metric / Symbol | Formula | Unit | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Change Failure Rate (CFR)** | $\text{CFR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{failed}}}{N_{\text{deployed}}} \times 100$ | **%** | Lower is better. >15% indicates poor change hygiene. |
| **Mean Time to Change (MTTC)** | $\text{MTTC} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(T_{\text{deploy},i} - T_{\text{submit},i})}{n}$ | **Hours / Days** | Measures process throughput efficiency. |
| **Change Lead Time (CLT)** | $\text{CLT} = T_{\text{deploy}} - T_{\text{approve}}$ | **Hours / Days** | Measures implementation velocity post-approval. |
| **Defect Rejection Ratio (DRR)** | $\text{DRR} = \dfrac{D_{\text{introduced}}}{D_{\text{resolved}}}$ | **Dimensionless** | If DRR > 1, the change is creating more bugs than it fixes — **reject the change.** |
| **Change Request Backlog** | $\text{Backlog} = N_{\text{open CRs}} - N_{\text{closed CRs per sprint}}$ | **Count** | Indicator of process saturation. |
| **Approved Change Implementation Ratio (ACIR)** | $\text{ACIR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{implemented}}}{N_{\text{approved}}} \times 100$ | **%** | Measures CCB decision-to-execution efficiency. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR STUDENTS:**
> In exam scripts, always wrap absolute-value expressions in $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ rather than using the vertical pipe `|`, which can confuse evaluators reading handwritten work.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The change control process is the **operational spine** of every regulated industry:

- **Aerospace (DO-178C compliance):** Every line of code in an aircraft autopilot is change-controlled.
- **Medical Devices (FDA 21 CFR Part 11):** A change without documented approval is a regulatory violation.
- **Banking & FinTech (PCI-DSS, SOX):** Uncontrolled changes to payment systems are an audit failure.
- **DevOps / SRE:** Modern **CI/CD pipelines** are essentially automated change control workflows (e.g., GitHub Actions + Pull Request approval gates).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — A Change Request Lifecycle

Below is an **exhaustive, gate-by-gate derivation** of a single change request as it moves through the process. Every transition is shown explicitly so that no logical step is left implicit.

Let us trace **CR-2024-0387**: *"Fix the date-formatting bug in the invoice generation module (uses MM/DD/YYYY but should use DD/MM/YYYY for EU customers)."*

### Step 1: Submission
- The CR is logged at **Day 0 (T₀)** in the CMDB.
- The CR ID is assigned: `CR-2024-0387`.
- The requester (a customer support engineer) provides the business justification: *"3 customer complaints in the EU region; non-compliance with regional formatting standard."*
- Initial priority is set to `High`.

### Step 2: Screening
- The Configuration Manager evaluates and classifies it as a **Normal Change** (not standard, not an emergency).
- Triage completed at **Day 1 (T₁ = 1 day)**.

### Step 3: Impact Analysis
- The Lead Architect investigates:
  - **Code Impact:** 1 module (`InvoiceGenerator.java`), 1 utility class (`DateUtil.java`), 1 property file.
  - **Test Impact:** 14 test cases in `InvoiceGenTestSuite` need re-execution.
  - **Schedule Impact:** Estimated 8 person-hours for development + 4 hours for testing.
  - **Risk Impact:** Low — isolated to formatting, no business logic touched.
- Impact analysis completed at **Day 3 (T₃ = 3 days)**.

### Step 4: CCB Decision
- The CCB meets on **Day 4**.
- After deliberation, the decision is: **APPROVE**, with a condition: *"Must include a unit test that covers both US and EU formatting."*
- Approval timestamp: **Day 4 (T₄ = 4 days)**.

### Step 5: Implementation
- Developer creates a branch: `git checkout -b fix/CR-2024-0387-date-format`.
- Code change is implemented; peer review conducted.
- Implementation complete at **Day 6 (T₆ = 6 days)**.

### Step 6: Verification & Audit
- QA executes the 14 test cases + the new conditional test.
- All 15 tests pass.
- Configuration audit confirms documentation (user manual section 4.7) has been updated.
- Verification timestamp: **Day 7 (T₇ = 7 days)**.

### Step 7: Closure
- The CR is closed; release notes are updated; new baseline **`v2.1.3`** is published.
- Closure timestamp: **Day 7 (T₇ = 7 days)**.

### Step 8: Metric Calculation
Now we compute the key change control metrics for this CR.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Change Lead Time (CLT)} &= T_{\text{deploy}} - T_{\text{approve}} \\
&= 7 - 4 \\
&= 3 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mean Time to Change (MTTC)} &= T_{\text{deploy}} - T_{\text{submit}} \\
&= 7 - 0 \\
&= 7 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Defect Rejection Ratio (DRR)} &= \frac{D_{\text{introduced}}}{D_{\text{resolved}}} \\
&= \frac{0 \text{ new defects}}{1 \text{ original defect fixed}} \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A DRR of 0 indicates a *clean change* — the developer did not introduce any regressions. This is the gold standard for any SQA-driven organization.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Classifying Change Types

The KTU examiner frequently asks students to distinguish between categories of changes. The matrix below is the authoritative reference:

| Change Type | Risk Level | Approval Path | Example | Documentation Depth |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Emergency** | High (already in prod) | Verbal CCB Chair → Post-hoc ratification | Production server down, security patch | Full, retrospective |
| **Major** | High | Full CCB review | Architecture redesign, database migration | Full (CR + Design Doc + Test Plan) |
| **Minor** | Medium | Lead Dev + QA Lead | Bug fix, refactoring | Standard CR form |
| **Standard** | Low | Pre-approved template | Library version bump, comment fix | Minimal (log entry) |
| **Cosmetic** | Negligible | None (auto-approved) | Typo in UI label | None |

---

## 3.3 The Decision Tree — When to Reject a Change

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The CCB must REJECT a change if ANY of the following conditions hold. This is a high-yield KTU concept.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reject} \iff &\left( \text{DRR}_{\text{projected}} > 1 \right) \;\lor\; \left( \text{Schedule Impact} > \text{Release Window} \right) \\
&\lor\; \left( \text{Risk Score} > \text{Acceptable Threshold} \right) \;\lor\; \left( \text{Business Justification Missing} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Plain English:** A change is rejected if it is likely to introduce *more* defects than it resolves, or if it threatens the release schedule, or if its risk is too high, or if no one can articulate why it is needed.

---

## 3.4 The Traceability Chain — The "Golden Thread"

One of the most frequently tested KTU concepts is the **end-to-end traceability** that the change control process enforces. The chain is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Business Need} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Requirement} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Design} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Code} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Test} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Release} \;\rightarrow\; \text{CR} \;\rightarrow\; \text{CCB Decision} \;\rightarrow\; \text{New Code} \;\rightarrow\; \text{New Test} \;\rightarrow\; \text{New Baseline}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Why this matters:** In regulated industries (aerospace, medical, nuclear), the auditor can ask: *"Show me how this line of production code traces back to an approved customer requirement."* If the golden thread is broken, the product is non-compliant and cannot be shipped.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Change Control Process — End-to-End Mermaid Flow

The diagram below visualizes the **seven-phase workflow** with explicit decision gates and the human roles (actors) responsible at each stage. Every node ID is alphanumeric to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Phase 1: Change Request Submission] --> A2[Configuration Manager Logs in CMDB]
    A2 --> A3[Phase 2: Initial Screening]
    A3 --> A4{Is this an Emergency}
    A4 -- Yes --> A5[Fast Track: CCB Chair Verbal Approval]
    A4 -- No --> A6[Phase 3: Impact Analysis by Lead Architect]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Phase 4: CCB Review Meeting]
    A7 --> A8{CCB Decision}
    A8 -- Reject --> A9[Archive CR with Rejection Reason]
    A8 -- Defer --> A10[Move to Future Backlog]
    A8 -- Approve --> A11[Phase 5: Implementation in Isolated Branch]
    A11 --> A12[Phase 6: QA Verification and Regression]
    A12 --> A13{All Tests Pass}
    A13 -- No --> A14[Rollback and Reopen CR]
    A14 --> A11
    A13 -- Yes --> A15[Configuration Audit]
    A15 --> A16[Phase 7: Closure and New Baseline]
    A16 --> A17[Archive Complete Audit Trail]
    A17 --> A18[Process Improvement Feedback Loop]
    A18 --> A1
    A9 --> A18
    A10 --> A18
```

---

## 4.2 Roles & Responsibilities — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ[Requester: Customer, Tester, Dev] -->|Submits CR| CM[Configuration Manager]
    CM -->|Triage and Categorize| CCB[Change Control Board]
    CCB -->|Approve or Reject| LEAD[Lead Developer and Architect]
    LEAD -->|Implement in Branch| QA[QA Team]
    QA -->|Verify and Audit| RELEASE[Release Manager]
    RELEASE -->|Promote to New Baseline| AUDIT[Independent Auditor]
    AUDIT -->|Final Sign Off| CM
```

---

## 4.3 Change Classification Matrix — Visual Reference

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Any Incoming Change Request] --> B2{Risk and Scope Assessment}
    B2 -->|Trivial and Pre Approved| B3[STANDARD: Auto Approved]
    B2 -->|Minor and Low Risk| B4[MINOR: Lead Approval Needed]
    B2 -->|Significant and Cross Module| B5[MAJOR: Full CCB Review]
    B2 -->|Production Down or Security| B6[EMERGENCY: Expedited Path]
    B3 --> B7[Log and Deploy]
    B4 --> B8[Single Reviewer Sign Off]
    B5 --> B9[Multi Stakeholder Meeting]
    B6 --> B10[Post Hoc CCB Ratification]
```

---

## 4.4 The Golden Thread of Traceability

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Business Need] --> C2[Requirement Spec]
    C2 --> C3[Design Document]
    C3 --> C4[Source Code]
    C4 --> C5[Test Case]
    C5 --> C6[Released Baseline]
    C6 --> C7[Change Request]
    C7 --> C8[CCB Decision Record]
    C8 --> C9[Updated Code]
    C9 --> C10[Updated Test]
    C10 --> C11[New Baseline]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Change Control Process. List its main objectives.*

**Model Answer (Memorize the Structure):**

The **Change Control Process** is a formal, auditable workflow within Software Configuration Management that governs how modifications to baselined software configuration items (CIs) are requested, evaluated, approved, implemented, and verified.

Its three main objectives are:
1. **To maintain baseline integrity** — ensure no unauthorized change enters the production system.
2. **To ensure traceability** — every change is linked to a requester, reason, and outcome.
3. **To balance risk and benefit** — the CCB evaluates whether the value of a change outweighs its cost, schedule, and regression risk.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a Change Control Board (CCB)? Mention any four of its members.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Change Control Board (CCB)** is a multi-disciplinary decision-making body that reviews, prioritizes, and authorizes or rejects all change requests for a software project.

Four typical members are:
1. **Project Manager** — owns schedule and cost trade-offs.
2. **Lead Software Architect** — assesses technical feasibility and impact.
3. **Quality Assurance Lead** — evaluates testing and risk implications.
4. **Customer / Product Owner Representative** — ensures business alignment.
   *(Other acceptable members: Configuration Manager, Security Lead, Operations Lead.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> Students frequently lose 3–5 marks in 14-mark questions by (1) writing the phases as a mere list without explaining the *gate conditions* between them, and (2) failing to mention the **CCB's role** explicitly. Always tie each phase to *who does what* and *what is the exit criterion*.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the seven phases of the Change Control Process with a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Distinguish between **Standard, Minor, Major, and Emergency** changes with one real-world example for each. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) The Seven Phases — Detailed Explanation**

**Phase 1 — Change Request Initiation:**
Any stakeholder (customer, QA, developer, support) raises a CR. The CR is documented in a standardized form containing a unique ID, the requester, business justification, and affected CIs. It is logged in the CMDB.

*Gate Condition:* The CR must have a clear business justification before it can proceed.

**Phase 2 — Initial Screening & Categorization:**
The Configuration Manager triages the CR into one of three categories: *Standard, Normal, or Emergency*. Standard changes bypass the CCB; emergencies are fast-tracked; normal changes proceed to impact analysis.

*Gate Condition:* The CR must be classified and risk-scored.

**Phase 3 — Impact Analysis:**
The Lead Architect and QA Lead jointly analyze the technical, schedule, cost, and risk impact. The output is a structured Impact Analysis Report.

*Gate Condition:* The impact report must be attached to the CR before CCB review.

**Phase 4 — CCB Review and Decision:**
The CCB convenes and decides one of four outcomes: **Approve, Reject, Defer, or Request More Info**. The decision is recorded with signatures and timestamps.

*Gate Condition:* A formally recorded decision must exist in the CMDB.

**Phase 5 — Implementation:**
Approved changes are developed in an isolated branch with peer review, unit testing, and CR-ID-tagged commits.

*Gate Condition:* Code must pass peer review and unit tests before merging.

**Phase 6 — Verification and Configuration Audit:**
QA executes targeted + regression tests. An independent auditor verifies that the implementation matches the approved scope and that documentation is updated.

*Gate Condition:* All tests must pass and the audit must be clean.

**Phase 7 — Closure and Release:**
The CR is closed, a new baseline is published, and the audit trail is archived for compliance.

*Gate Condition:* Closure sign-off from QA, Dev, and CM.

*(Award full 7 marks for: each phase explained with its gate condition, plus the neat diagram from Section 4.1.)*

---

**(b) Distinction of Change Types**

| Type | Risk | Approval Path | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | Negligible | Pre-approved template | Updating a third-party logging library to the latest patch version |
| **Minor** | Low | Lead Developer + QA Lead | Fixing an off-by-one error in the shopping cart total calculation |
| **Major** | High | Full CCB review | Migrating the user authentication system from session-based to OAuth 2.0 |
| **Emergency** | Critical | CCB Chair verbal → post-hoc ratification | Applying a security patch to a production server that is under active cyber-attack |

*(Award 7 marks: 1.5 marks per type × 4 types = 6 marks, plus 1 mark for overall conclusion about why classification matters.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of the **Change Control Board (CCB)** in software projects. Why is it essential in regulated industries like aerospace or medical devices? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The project received **120 change requests** in a quarter. Out of these, **85 were approved**, **20 were rejected**, and **15 were deferred**. Of the 85 approved, **10 caused production failures** after deployment. Compute the **Change Failure Rate (CFR)** and the **Approved Change Implementation Ratio (ACIR)**. Comment on the result. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Role of the CCB**

The Change Control Board is the **central governance body** of the change control process. Its core responsibilities are:

1. **Review and Decision** — examines every Normal-priority CR and renders a binding decision.
2. **Cross-Functional Perspective** — ensures that technical, business, QA, and operations viewpoints are all considered.
3. **Prioritization** — when multiple CRs compete for limited resources, the CCB ranks them by business value.
4. **Risk Acceptance** — formally accepts or transfers the risk introduced by an approved change.
5. **Audit Trail Custodian** — owns the official record of all decisions for compliance audits.

**Why essential in regulated industries:**

In **aerospace (DO-178C)** and **medical devices (FDA 21 CFR Part 11)**, every change to verified software must be:
- **Authorized** by a named individual or board.
- **Traced** to a documented requirement.
- **Re-verified** with the same rigor as the original baseline.
- **Retained** in records for 15+ years (aerospace) or the life of the device (medical).

The CCB is the **legal mechanism** by which this accountability is enforced. A software change without CCB approval is, in these domains, a **regulatory non-conformance** that can ground a fleet of aircraft or trigger a product recall.

*(Award full 7 marks: 4 marks for CCB roles, 3 marks for regulated-industry rationale with at least one named standard.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Computation**

**Given Data:**
- Total CRs received, $N_{\text{total}} = 120$
- Approved CRs, $N_{\text{approved}} = 85$
- Rejected CRs, $N_{\text{rejected}} = 20$
- Deferred CRs, $N_{\text{deferred}} = 15$
- Approved CRs that caused production failures, $N_{\text{failed}} = 10$

**Step 1: Verify the totals.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{total}} &= N_{\text{approved}} + N_{\text{rejected}} + N_{\text{deferred}} \\
120 &= 85 + 20 + 15 \\
120 &= 120 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Change Failure Rate (CFR).**

The CFR is calculated against **deployed changes**, not approved changes. Assuming all 85 approved CRs were deployed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CFR} &= \frac{N_{\text{failed}}}{N_{\text{deployed}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{10}{85} \times 100 \\
&= 11.76\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Approved Change Implementation Ratio (ACIR).**

The ACIR measures how many approved CRs were actually implemented (assumed to be all 85 in this problem):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ACIR} &= \frac{N_{\text{implemented}}}{N_{\text{approved}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{85}{85} \times 100 \\
&= 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation and Comment.**

The **CFR of 11.76%** is *moderately high*. Industry benchmarks (per DORA / Accelerate State of DevOps reports) target a CFR below 5% for elite performers and below 10% for high performers. A value of 11.76% indicates that the impact analysis or pre-deployment verification process is **insufficient**.

The **ACIR of 100%** is excellent — every approved change was implemented, indicating strong execution discipline.

**Recommendation:** The project should:
1. Strengthen **regression test coverage** before deployment.
2. Introduce **canary releases** to detect failures before full rollout.
3. Conduct a **root cause analysis** on the 10 failed changes to identify common patterns (e.g., specific module, specific developer, specific time of week).

*(Award full 7 marks: 2 marks for CFR derivation, 2 marks for ACIR derivation, 2 marks for interpretation, 1 mark for recommendation.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Print This Section Before Walking Into The Exam Hall**

- ✅ **Change Control Process = 7 phases:** Submit → Screen → Impact Analyze → CCB Decide → Implement → Verify & Audit → Close.
- ✅ **Every phase has a gate** — never skip the gate; it is the examiner's favorite 2-marker.
- ✅ **CCB is a decision body, NOT a technical body** — impact analysis is done *before* the CCB meets.
- ✅ **Three pillars:** Traceability, Integrity, Accountability. Memorize this phrase.
- ✅ **IEEE 828-2012** is the authoritative standard for SCM and is the first citation you should make in any long-answer.
- ✅ **Change types:** Standard (auto), Minor (lead), Major (CCB), Emergency (fast-track + post-hoc).
- ✅ **The Golden Thread:** Business Need → Requirement → Design → Code → Test → Release → CR → Decision → New Code → New Test → New Baseline.
- ✅ **Key formulas to remember cold:**
  - $\text{CFR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{failed}}}{N_{\text{deployed}}} \times 100$
  - $\text{MTTC} = T_{\text{deploy}} - T_{\text{submit}}$
  - $\text{CLT} = T_{\text{deploy}} - T_{\text{approve}}$
  - $\text{DRR} = \dfrac{D_{\text{introduced}}}{D_{\text{resolved}}}$
  - $\text{ACIR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{implemented}}}{N_{\text{approved}}} \times 100$
- ✅ **DRR > 1 means REJECT the change** — this is a one-liner that earns easy marks.
- ✅ **Regulated industries (aerospace, medical, banking) require formal CCB approval** — name the standard (DO-178C, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, PCI-DSS) for full credit.
- ✅ **Configuration Audit is independent of QA testing** — it verifies *what* was changed matches *what* was approved, not whether the change works.
- ✅ **Scope Creep** = a change that goes beyond the original CR's approved boundary. The audit phase catches this.
- ✅ **Baseline integrity** is the *non-negotiable* outcome of change control — state this as the closing sentence of any 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Templates and Checklists
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Templates and Checklists

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Templates in Software Quality Assurance?

A **Template** in SQA is a *predesigned, standardized, reusable document skeleton* that defines the structure, sections, headings, fields, and expected content of an artifact produced during the software development and quality assurance lifecycle. Templates enforce consistency across teams, projects, and organizations by ensuring that every deliverable — be it a test plan, test case, review report, or defect log — captures the same critical information in the same format.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Glossary):** A *template* is a structured, parameterized document framework that acts as a blueprint for producing SQA artifacts. It provides a controlled layout, mandatory fields, optional fields, and often embedded guidance text, so that different authors produce uniform deliverables of predictable quality.

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy — The "Medical Prescription Pad"

Imagine visiting five different doctors. Without a standardized prescription pad:

- Doctor A scribbles medicine names on a torn paper slip.
- Doctor B uses a letterhead but forgets patient age or allergy info.
- Doctor C writes dosage, but not duration.
- The chemist (pharmacist) struggles to interpret each one.

Now introduce a **uniform prescription template** with fixed fields for *Patient Name, Age, Weight, Date, Diagnosis, Drug Name, Dosage, Frequency, Duration, Doctor Signature*. Suddenly every prescription is legible, complete, legally compliant, and easy to audit. **That is exactly what an SQA template does** — it transforms free-form, inconsistent engineering artifacts into standardized, auditable, and reusable records.

## 1.2 What Are Checklists in Software Quality Assurance?

A **Checklist** in SQA is a *curated, itemized list of verification points, criteria, or tasks* that an engineer, reviewer, or tester works through to confirm that an artifact, process, or product meets a defined standard of completeness, correctness, or compliance. Unlike templates (which define *what to write*), checklists define *what to check* or *what to do*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** A *checklist* is a verification aid consisting of a finite set of yes/no, present/absent, or pass/fail items, each representing a known quality criterion. It is used during reviews, inspections, testing, release readiness, and process audits to ensure that no critical quality attribute is overlooked.

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy — The "Pilot's Pre-Flight Checklist"

Before takeoff, a commercial pilot reads out loud an itemized list: *flaps, fuel, altimeter, transponder, weather radar, landing gear*… Even an experienced captain does not "wing it." Every item is binary — checked or unchecked — and the plane does not move until *every* box is satisfied. An SQA checklist works identically: it converts the abstract goal of "quality" into **concrete, verifiable, sequential questions** that humans (or tools) can mechanically answer.

## 1.3 Why Both? Templates vs. Checklists — The Quality Twin Pillars

| Aspect | Template | Checklist |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Purpose | **Standardize creation** of artifacts | **Verify completeness/correctness** of artifacts |
| Output Type | A *filled-in* document | A *ticked-off* list of items |
| When Used | At the **start** of an SQA activity | At the **end** (or during) an SQA activity |
| Cognitive Load | Reduces effort to *author* | Reduces effort to *verify* |
| Typical Examples | Test Plan Template, Defect Report Template | Code Review Checklist, Release Checklist |
| Failure Mode Avoids | Incomplete, inconsistent, non-auditable artifacts | Missed steps, overlooked defects, forgotten criteria |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Takeaway:** Templates ensure **uniformity of input**. Checklists ensure **completeness of output**. Together they form a closed-loop quality gate — the template guarantees we *write everything we should*, and the checklist guarantees we *check everything we wrote*.

## 1.4 Categories of SQA Templates (KTU 2024 Module 4 Coverage)

1. **Test Documentation Templates**
   - Master Test Plan Template (IEEE 829 aligned)
   - Test Case Specification Template
   - Test Procedure Specification Template
   - Test Log Template
   - Test Incident Report (Defect Report) Template
   - Test Summary Report Template

2. **Review and Inspection Templates**
   - Fagan Inspection Template
   - Walkthrough Agenda Template
   - Technical Review Minutes Template
   - Peer Review Log Template

3. **Process and Project Templates**
   - Software Quality Assurance Plan (SQAP) Template
   - Project Plan Template
   - Configuration Management Plan Template
   - Risk Management Plan Template

4. **Defect and Change Management Templates**
   - Defect/Incident Report Template
   - Change Request Template
   - Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Template

5. **Compliance and Audit Templates**
   - CMMI Process Audit Template
   - ISO 9001 Audit Template
   - Process Compliance Report Template

## 1.5 Categories of SQA Checklists (KTU 2024 Module 4 Coverage)

1. **Product/Artifact Checklists**
   - Requirements Review Checklist
   - Design Review Checklist
   - Code Review Checklist
   - Test Case Review Checklist
   - User Interface (UI) Quality Checklist
   - Documentation Checklist

2. **Process Checklists**
   - Test Process Checklist
   - Release Readiness Checklist
   - Deployment Checklist
   - Process Audit Checklist

3. **Testing-Type Specific Checklists**
   - Unit Testing Checklist
   - Integration Testing Checklist
   - System Testing Checklist
   - Acceptance Testing Checklist (UAT Checklist)
   - Performance Testing Checklist
   - Security Testing Checklist

4. **Maintenance and Support Checklists**
   - Pre-Release Checklist
   - Post-Release Validation Checklist
   - Backup Recovery Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In Module 4 questions, examiners often ask students to *"design a checklist for X"* or *"list the sections of a Test Plan template"*. Memorize the structure of at least **two templates** (Test Plan, Defect Report) and **two checklists** (Code Review, UAT) — these are the most frequently examined.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Well-Designed SQA Template

A high-quality SQA template is not just a blank Word document. It must contain **six structural elements**:

1. **Header Block** — Project name, module, document version, author, date, approver, status (Draft/Review/Approved).
2. **Purpose & Scope** — One-paragraph statement of why this artifact exists and what it covers.
3. **Mandatory Fields** — Sections that *must* be filled (e.g., in a Test Case: Test ID, Pre-conditions, Steps, Expected Result).
4. **Optional/Conditional Fields** — Sections used only when applicable (e.g., Performance threshold in a generic test case).
5. **Embedded Guidance Text** — Italicized instructions inside each section telling the author *what* to write and *why* it matters.
6. **Footer / Approval Block** — Signature lines, change history, references to standards (IEEE 829, ISO/IEC/IEEE 29119).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters (KTU 2024 NEP perspective):** A template without embedded guidance is a *form*. A template with guidance text is a *teaching tool*. The latter is what the KTU syllabus expects when it asks about "templates as process enablers."

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Well-Designed SQA Checklist

A robust checklist follows the **CRAFT** principles used by Atul Gawande (*The Checklist Manifesto*) and adapted for software engineering:

| Letter | Principle | Meaning in SQA Context |
|---|---|---|
| **C** | Concise | Each item is a single, atomic yes/no question. Avoid compound items like "Are all inputs validated and error messages user-friendly?" (splits into two items). |
| **R** | Relevant | Every item maps to a real defect class observed historically or a regulatory requirement. No "fluff" items. |
| **A** | Actionable | Failing an item triggers a clear next step (e.g., "Raise a defect"). |
| **F** | Focused | Items grouped by phase, severity, or category for efficient review. |
| **T** | Traceable | Each item references a requirement ID, standard clause, or risk reference. |

## 2.3 The "Template → Document → Checklist" Closed-Loop Process

In a mature SQA process, the three artifacts interact as follows:

```
Template (blank)  ──►  Author fills it  ──►  Document (content)  ──►  Checklist applied  ──►  Verified Quality
       ▲                                                                                            │
       └──────────── Continuous improvement (lessons learned feed back into template revisions) ◄────┘
```

This loop is the practical realization of the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle as applied to SQA documentation:

- **Plan** — Define the template.
- **Do** — Use it to produce the artifact.
- **Check** — Apply the checklist to verify the artifact.
- **Act** — Update the template based on gaps discovered.

## 2.4 Standards & Frameworks Referenced by KTU 2024

The KTU Module 4 syllabus aligns templates and checklists with these industry standards:

- **IEEE Std 829** — Standard for Software Test Documentation (defines 8 test document templates).
- **IEEE Std 1028** — Standard for Software Reviews and Inspections (checklist-driven).
- **IEEE Std 830** — SRS template backbone.
- **ISO/IEC/IEEE 29119** — Software Testing Standard (test case & test plan templates).
- **CMMI v2.0** — Process area templates and checklists for verification & validation.
- **ISTQB Foundation Syllabus** — Universal checklist for test design techniques.
- **ISO 25010** — Quality model whose characteristics become checklist items.

> [!TIP]
> **For 14-mark questions**, examiners reward students who *cite the standard*. Writing *"Test Plan Template as per IEEE 829-2008"* scores higher than *"a Test Plan template"*.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Templates & Checklists

| # | Construct | Key Field / Item | KTU-Mandated Standard | Common Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Test Plan Template** | Test ID, scope, approach, schedule, deliverables, exit criteria | IEEE 829 §5 | Forgetting "Exit Criteria" |
| 2 | **Test Case Template** | Test ID, preconditions, inputs, steps, expected result, actual result, status | IEEE 829 §6 / ISO 29119-3 | Conflating steps with expected results |
| 3 | **Defect Report Template** | Defect ID, severity, priority, reproducer steps, screenshots, build version | IEEE 829 §9 | Missing "Severity" and "Priority" distinction |
| 4 | **Code Review Checklist** | Naming conventions, indentation, cyclomatic complexity ≤ 10, error handling, logging | IEEE 1028 | Compound items like "code is clean" |
| 5 | **UAT Checklist** | Business rule coverage, edge cases, accessibility, performance, data migration | ISO 29119-2 | Skipping non-functional criteria |
| 6 | **Release Readiness Checklist** | All critical defects closed, regression passed, sign-offs obtained, rollback plan | CMMI VER/VAL | Forgetting rollback plan |
| 7 | **Requirements Review Checklist** | Unambiguous, testable, traceable, complete, consistent, prioritized | IEEE 830 | Ignoring traceability |
| 8 | **Security Testing Checklist** | OWASP Top 10 coverage, SQL injection, XSS, auth, encryption | OWASP / NIST | Skipping authorization tests |

> **Notation note:** The vertical bar symbol \vert used in any cell above represents "divides" or "such that" (LaTeX convention) and does not break the markdown table layout.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation & Medical Industries** — Used checklists for decades before software adopted them; the 1935 B-17 crash (pilot forgot to release a lock) is the canonical origin story.
- **Banking Software** — RBI/SEBI-mandated audit checklists for core banking deployments.
- **Medical Device Software (FDA)** — IEC 62304 mandates verification checklists for safety-critical code.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262)** — ASIL-rated checklists for functional safety software.
- **Agile/DevOps Teams** — Definition of Done (DoD) is essentially a *living checklist*; Release Notes follow a *living template*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Real-World Connection:** When a question asks *"Give two real-world examples where checklists prevent failures,"* the B-17 bomber and the WHO Surgical Safety Checklist are the two safest answers.
