# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Process Standards?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Process Standard** is a formally documented set of guidelines, criteria, and prescribed practices that define *how* a specific engineering or managerial activity must be planned, executed, monitored, and improved within an organization. In Software Quality Assurance (SQA), process standards ensure that the *manner* in which software is developed is consistent, repeatable, measurable, and continuously improvable, irrespective of the individual developer or project.

In simpler words: a process standard does **not** tell you *what the product should look like* (that's a **product standard**). Instead, it tells you **how the work should be performed** to reach that product.

## 1.2 What are Quality Assurance (QA) Standards?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Assurance Standards** are internationally recognized frameworks that codify the best practices for establishing, sustaining, and auditing an organization's quality management system (QMS). They are published by international standards bodies (ISO, IEEE, SEI) and serve as the **reference benchmark** against which a software organization's process maturity is certified or appraised.

The two dominant families of QA standards relevant to KTU Module 4 are:

| Standard Family | Issuing Body | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 9000 / ISO 9001** | International Organization for Standardization (ISO) | Generic Quality Management System (QMS) for any industry, including software |
| **CMMI (Capability Maturity Model Integration)** | Software Engineering Institute (SEI), Carnegie Mellon | Software-specific process maturity framework |
| **ISO/IEC 25010** | ISO / IEC JTC 1 | Software product quality model |
| **ISO/IEC 12207** | ISO / IEC JTC 1 | Software life cycle processes |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Kitchen" Analogy

Imagine a five-star hospital kitchen that must serve 1,000 meals a day:

- **Product Standard** = the *recipe card* stating the exact dish (ingredients, taste, presentation).
- **Process Standard** = the *Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)* that says: wash hands for 30 seconds, pre-heat oven to 180 °C, sanitize cutting boards between vegetables and meat, log temperatures every 15 minutes, label allergens.

Even if two chefs follow the *same recipe* (product), if one skips the hand-wash step and the other does not, the **quality of the output is unpredictable**. **QA Standards (ISO 9001, CMMI)** are those SOPs. They guarantee that **every dish (software release) leaving the kitchen (organization)** has been prepared under controlled, verified, and audited conditions.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *Process = HOW*, *Product = WHAT*, *QA = PROVE it was done right*, *QC = CATCH defects after the fact*.

## 1.4 Why Are QA Standards Critical in Software Engineering?

Software is **intangible**, **logic-dense**, and **defect-prone**. Without enforced process standards:

- A junior developer may skip code reviews.
- Test cases may be inconsistent across teams.
- A bug fix in one module may silently break another.
- Senior management has **no visibility** into project health.

QA standards convert this **chaotic, person-dependent** process into a **disciplined, organization-dependent, measurable** process. They enable:

1. **Repeatability** — same process yields predictable results.
2. **Auditability** — external auditors can certify the organization.
3. **Continuous Improvement** — defects become *data* for process refinement.
4. **Customer Confidence** — ISO 9001 certification is a global procurement prerequisite.
5. **Risk Reduction** — mature processes (CMMI Level 4–5) statistically predict defects before testing.

## 1.5 Quality Assurance vs. Quality Control (Board Favorite Distinction)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a guaranteed **2-mark short question** in KTU ESE for SQA. Memorize this contrast.

| Aspect | **Quality Assurance (QA)** | **Quality Control (QC)** |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | **Process** | **Product** |
| Timing | **Preventive** — during development | **Detective** — after development |
| Activity | Process definition, audits, training | Testing, reviews, defect detection |
| Goal | *Prevent* defects | *Find* defects |
| Approach | Process-oriented | Product-oriented |
| Example | Conducting a process audit on coding standards | Running unit tests to find bugs |
| Standards | ISO 9001, CMMI | ISO/IEC 25010, IEEE 829 |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Quality Assurance Standard

Every credible QA standard (ISO 9001, CMMI) is built upon a **PDCA Cycle** (Plan–Do–Check–Act) backbone introduced by **Walter Shewhart** and popularized by **W. Edwards Deming**.

### 2.1.1 The PDCA (Deming) Cycle

The cycle is recursive and applies to **every process** in the QMS.

- **PLAN**: Establish objectives, processes, and KPIs (e.g., *"Reduce defect leakage to < 5%"*).
- **DO**: Execute the planned processes and capture data (defect logs, effort logs).
- **CHECK**: Compare actual performance against the plan using metrics (Defect Density, MTTR, Yield).
- **ACT**: Take corrective action; update the standard for the next iteration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Both ISO 9001:2015 (Clauses 4–10) and CMMI v2.0 model view 1.0 are essentially **elaborations of the PDCA cycle**. Whenever a KTU question asks *"Which quality philosophy do modern QA standards follow?"* — the answer is **PDCA (Deming Cycle)**.

## 2.2 ISO 9000 Family — Deep Dive

The ISO 9000 family is the **most widely adopted QMS standard in the world** (over **1 million certified organizations** in 170+ countries as of 2024).

### 2.2.1 Three Core Documents

1. **ISO 9000:2015** — *Fundamentals and vocabulary*. Defines terms (defect, process, product, customer). Does **not** require certification.
2. **ISO 9001:2015** — *Requirements*. The **only certifiable** document in the family. Specifies QMS requirements.
3. **ISO 9004:2018** — *Guidelines for performance improvements*. Goes *beyond* ISO 9001 to address sustained success. Not certifiable.

### 2.2.2 The Seven Quality Management Principles (ISO 9001:2015)

These principles are the **philosophical foundation** of the standard.

1. **Customer Focus** — understand current & future needs; meet requirements.
2. **Leadership** — top management establishes unity of purpose and direction.
3. **Engagement of People** — competent, empowered, engaged people at all levels.
4. **Process Approach** — activities are managed as interconnected processes.
5. **Improvement** — reactive, incremental, breakthrough, innovation.
6. **Evidence-based Decision Making** — facts, data, analysis drive decisions.
7. **Relationship Management** — manage relationships with interested parties (suppliers, partners).

### 2.2.3 ISO 9001:2015 — High-Level Structure (HLS)

The standard is built on **10 clauses**:

| Clause | Title | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| 1–3 | Scope, Normative Refs, Terms | Context |
| **4** | **Context of the Organization** | Internal/external issues, scope of QMS |
| **5** | **Leadership** | Top management commitment, roles |
| **6** | **Planning** | Risks, opportunities, quality objectives |
| **7** | **Support** | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented info |
| **8** | **Operation** | Operational planning, design, production, release |
| **9** | **Performance Evaluation** | Monitoring, measurement, internal audit, management review |
| **10** | **Improvement** | Nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key shift in ISO 9001:2015:** Replaced the old term *"Preventive Action"* (which existed in 2008 version) with **"Risk-Based Thinking"** integrated into Clause 6. This aligns ISO 9001 with **Annex SL** — the common framework for all ISO management standards.

## 2.3 CMMI (Capability Maturity Model Integration) — Deep Dive

CMMI is the **software-industry-specific** counterpart to ISO 9001. It was developed by the **Software Engineering Institute (SEI)** at **Carnegie Mellon University** (USA) in the early 1990s, integrating three earlier models: CMM for Software, CMM for Systems Engineering, and EIA 731.

### 2.3.1 The Five Maturity Levels

CMMI defines **five maturity levels** (in v1.x) and a slightly different staging in v2.0, but the original five-level staging is the KTU-syllabus expectation.

| Level | Name | Key Characteristic | Process Posture |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Initial** | Chaotic, heroic individual effort | Unpredictable, reactive |
| **2** | **Managed** | Projects are planned, tracked, controlled | Repeatable at project level |
| **3** | **Defined** | Organization-wide standard processes exist | Proactive, defined |
| **4** | **Quantitatively Managed** | Subprocesses measured with statistical techniques | Measured & controlled |
| **5** | **Optimizing** | Continuous, incremental, innovation-driven | Focus on optimization |

### 2.3.2 Process Areas (PAs) by Level — KTU High-Yield Table

| Maturity Level | Key Process Areas (PAs) |
|---|---|
| **Level 2: Managed** | Requirements Management (REQM), Project Planning (PP), Project Monitoring & Control (PMC), Supplier Agreement Management (SAM), Measurement & Analysis (MA), Process & Product Quality Assurance (PPQA), Configuration Management (CM) |
| **Level 3: Defined** | Organizational Process Focus (OPF), OPD, OPM, Integrated Product Management (IPM), Risk Management (RSKM), Decision Analysis & Resolution (DAR), **Technical Solution (TS)**, **Product Integration (PI)**, **Verification (VER)**, **Validation (VAL)**, Organizational Training (OT) |
| **Level 4: Quantitatively Managed** | Organizational Process Performance (OPP), Quantitative Project Management (QPM) |
| **Level 5: Optimizing** | Organizational Innovation & Deployment (OID), Causal Analysis & Resolution (CAR) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Trigger:** If a KTU question asks *"What process area in CMMI Level 2 is directly responsible for SQA audits?"* — the answer is **PPQA (Process and Product Quality Assurance)**.

### 2.3.3 CMMI Maturity Level vs. Capability Level

- **Maturity Level** applies to the **entire organization** (staged representation).
- **Capability Level** (0–3) applies to an **individual process area** (continuous representation).

| Capability Level | Equivalent Description |
|---|---|
| 0 — Incomplete | Process does not exist or fails |
| 1 — Performed | Process achieves its purpose |
| 2 — Managed | Planned, monitored, controlled |
| 3 — Defined | Organizationally defined and tailored |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Defect Density (DD)** | $\text{DD} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Size (KLOC or FP)}}$ | per KLOC or per FP |
| **Process Yield (PY)** | $\text{PY} = \dfrac{\text{Defects found in current phase that originated earlier}}{\text{Total defects escaping the phase}}$ | dimensionless, $0 \le \text{PY} \le 1$ |
| **Mean Time to Failure (MTTF)** | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i}{N}$ | hours, $t_i$ = time of $i$-th failure |
| **Mean Time to Repair (MTTR)** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} r_i}{N}$ | hours, $r_i$ = repair time of $i$-th failure |
| **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | dimensionless, $0 \le A \le 1$ |
| **Reliability Growth** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ where $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTTF}}$ | failure rate $\lambda$ per hour |
| **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | $\text{CoQ} = \text{Prevention} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure}$ | currency units |
| **CMMI Process Yield** | $Y = \dfrac{\text{Defects removed before test}}{\text{Defects removed before test} + \text{Defects found in test}}$ | per phase |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Industry

- **Software outsourcing contracts** with the US DoD, EU governments, and Fortune 500 IT vendors mandate **CMMI Level 3 or higher** (or **ISO 9001 certification**).
- **Tata Consultancy Services (TCS)**, **Infosys**, and **Wipro** are all **CMMI Level 5** appraised organizations.
- **ISO 9001 certification** is mandatory for vendors supplying to ISO-compliant automotive, aerospace (AS9100), and medical (ISO 13485) supply chains.
- CMMI-based process improvements have shown empirical **30–80% defect reduction** and **20–40% schedule slippage reduction** (per SEI transition data).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Defect Density Calculation (Board Pattern)

> **Problem:** During testing of a 25 KLOC e-commerce module, the QA team recorded 87 unique defects. Compute the **Defect Density** and comment on industry benchmark compliance.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- Number of defects, $D = 87$
- Size, $S = 25 \text{ KLOC}$

**Formula:**

$$
\text{Defect Density} = \frac{D}{S}
$$

**Substitute values:**

$$
\text{DD} = \frac{87 \text{ defects}}{25 \text{ KLOC}}
$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

$$
\text{DD} = 3.48 \text{ defects per KLOC}
$$

**Industry benchmarks (per CMMI/SEI published data):**

| Application Domain | Typical DD (defects/KLOC) |
|---|---|
| Avionics / Safety-critical | < 0.5 |
| Medical software | < 1.0 |
| Enterprise business software | 0.5 – 2.0 |
| Web/e-commerce | 2.0 – 5.0 |
| Student projects | 10 – 50 |

**Interpretation:** With **3.48 defects/KLOC**, the e-commerce module is **within the typical range for web applications** but on the higher end. CMMI Level 4–5 organizations target **< 1.0** even for non-critical software, suggesting that the project is operating at **CMMI Maturity Level 1–2 equivalent** in process maturity.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cost of Quality (CoQ) Analysis

> **Problem:** A software company incurs the following annual quality costs. Compute the **Total Cost of Quality** and identify the dominant category.
>
> - Prevention costs (training, process design): ₹ 4,50,000
> - Appraisal costs (reviews, testing): ₹ 3,20,000
> - Internal failure costs (rework, re-test): ₹ 6,80,000
> - External failure costs (warranty, patches): ₹ 9,50,000

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the CoQ formula:**

$$
\text{CoQ} = C_{\text{prev}} + C_{\text{appr}} + C_{\text{int}} + C_{\text{ext}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\text{CoQ} = 4{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}20{,}000 + 6{,}80{,}000 + 9{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Add in pairs:**

$$
4{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}20{,}000 = 7{,}70{,}000
$$

$$
6{,}80{,}000 + 9{,}50{,}000 = 16{,}30{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Sum:**

$$
\text{CoQ} = 7{,}70{,}000 + 16{,}30{,}000 = 24{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Compute failure cost percentage:**

$$
\text{Failure Cost \%} = \frac{C_{\text{int}} + C_{\text{ext}}}{\text{CoQ}} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{16{,}30{,}000}{24{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \approx 67.92\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Nearly **68%** of the quality budget is spent on **failure costs** (rework + warranty). This is a textbook signature of a **CMMI Level 1–2 organization**. Mature organizations (CMMI 4–5) flip this ratio — **prevention + appraisal > 70%**, and failure costs drop to **< 30%**. Investing ₹ 1 in prevention saves **₹ 10 in failure costs** (Deming's 1:10:100 rule, with 1:10:100 representing the cost ratio of fixing a defect at the requirements stage, design stage, and post-release stage respectively).

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Process Yield Across a Software Lifecycle

> **Problem:** A software project tracks defects across four phases: **Requirements, Design, Coding, Testing**. The data are:
>
> | Phase | Defects Injected | Defects Removed in this Phase |
> |---|---|---|
> | Requirements | 60 | 30 |
> | Design | 40 | 25 |
> | Coding | 80 | 60 |
> | Testing | 30 | 25 |
>
> Compute the **Process Yield (PY)** for each phase, the **Overall Roll-up Yield**, and the **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** of the testing phase.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Phase-by-Phase Process Yield:**

The PY for a phase = (Defects removed in phase that originated *earlier*) ÷ (Total defects escaping the phase). For simplicity, using **defects removed within the phase as the numerator** and **(defects in phase − removed + injected later)** as the denominator is the textbook KTU simplification:

$$
\text{PY}_{\text{Req}} = \frac{30}{60} = 0.50
$$

$$
\text{PY}_{\text{Des}} = \frac{25}{40} = 0.625
$$

$$
\text{PY}_{\text{Cod}} = \frac{60}{80} = 0.75
$$

$$
\text{PY}_{\text{Test}} = \frac{25}{30} \approx 0.833
$$

**Step 2 — Overall Roll-up Yield (multiplicative):**

$$
Y_{\text{overall}} = \text{PY}_{\text{Req}} \times \text{PY}_{\text{Des}} \times \text{PY}_{\text{Cod}} \times \text{PY}_{\text{Test}}
$$

$$
Y_{\text{overall}} = 0.50 \times 0.625 \times 0.75 \times 0.833
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply stepwise:**

$$
0.50 \times 0.625 = 0.3125
$$

$$
0.3125 \times 0.75 = 0.2344
$$

$$
0.2344 \times 0.833 \approx 0.1952
$$

**Final overall yield:** $\approx 19.5\%$

**Step 4 — Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) of Testing:**

$$
\text{DRE}_{\text{Test}} = \frac{\text{Defects removed in test}}{\text{Defects in test}} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{25}{30} \times 100 \approx 83.3\%
$$

**Step 5 — Defect Escape Rate (DER) of Testing:**

$$
\text{DER}_{\text{Test}} = 100\% - \text{DRE}_{\text{Test}} = 16.7\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Only **19.5%** of injected defects survive *through* the entire pipeline and reach the customer — wait, this needs a clearer reading. The correct interpretation: the **roll-up yield** of 19.5% means only ~20% of the value-adding capability is realized. CMMI Level 4 targets **> 95%** overall roll-up yield. The Requirements phase is the **biggest leak** (PY = 0.50), confirming the **1:10:100 cost rule** — fix requirements defects, not coding defects.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Between ISO 9001 and CMMI

| Selection Criterion | ISO 9001:2015 | CMMI v2.0 |
|---|---|---|
| Industry scope | Generic — any organization | Software, systems, services |
| Certifiable? | Yes (third-party) | Appraised (SEI-authorized) |
| Cost of implementation | Low – Medium | Medium – High |
| Prescriptive? | Less prescriptive; principle-based | More prescriptive; process-area-based |
| Maturity measurement | Binary (certified / not) | Granular (5 levels) |
| Best for | Small/medium orgs, multi-industry | Software-intensive, large orgs |
| Audit cycle | 3 years (surveillance) | 3 years typical |
| Alignment | Compatible with CMMI via mapping studies | Maps to ISO 9001 partially |

## 3.5 Derivation of the PDCA-to-ISO Mapping

The 10-clause structure of ISO 9001:2015 maps explicitly to the PDCA cycle:

| PDCA Stage | ISO 9001:2015 Clauses |
|---|---|
| **PLAN** | 4 (Context), 5 (Leadership), 6 (Planning) |
| **DO** | 7 (Support), 8 (Operation) |
| **CHECK** | 9 (Performance Evaluation) |
| **ACT** | 10 (Improvement) |

This mapping is **explicitly stated in the ISO 9001:2015 standard**, Annex A.4, and is a guaranteed KTU 2-mark question.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The ISO 9001:2015 QMS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ctx["Clause 4: Context of Organization"]
    ldr["Clause 5: Leadership"]
    plan["Clause 6: Planning and Risk"]
    sup["Clause 7: Support and Documentation"]
    opr["Clause 8: Operation"]
    mon["Clause 9: Performance Evaluation"]
    imp["Clause 10: Improvement"]
    cust["External Customer Needs"]
    pdca["PDCA Cycle Backbone"]
    ctxt["Internal and External Issues"]
    sw["Software Project Life Cycle"]

    cust --> ctx
    ctxt --> ctx
    ctx --> ldr
    ldr --> plan
    plan --> sup
    sup --> opr
    opr --> mon
    mon --> imp
    imp -->|Feedback Loop| ctx
    opr -.-> sw
    pdca -.-> ctx
    pdca -.-> ldr
    pdca -.-> plan
    pdca -.-> sup
    pdca -.-> opr
    pdca -.-> mon
    pdca -.-> imp
```

## 4.2 The Five-Level CMMI Maturity Staircase

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["Level 1: INITIAL\nHeroic effort, ad hoc"]
    L2["Level 2: MANAGED\nProject-level discipline"]
    L3["Level 3: DEFINED\nOrg-wide standard process"]
    L4["Level 4: QUANTITATIVELY MANAGED\nStatistical control"]
    L5["Level 5: OPTIMIZING\nContinuous innovation"]

    L1 ==> L2
    L2 ==> L3
    L3 ==> L4
    L4 ==> L5
    L5 -.->|Feedback| L1

    p2["REQM, PP, PMC, SAM, MA, PPQA, CM"]
    p3["OPF, OPD, OPM, IPM, RSKM, TS, PI, VER, VAL, OT"]
    p4["OPP, QPM"]
    p5["OID, CAR"]

    L2 -.-> p2
    L3 -.-> p3
    L4 -.-> p4
    L5 -.-> p5
```

## 4.3 PDCA Cycle Flow (Deming Wheel)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P["PLAN\nDefine objectives\nIdentify processes\nSet KPIs"]
    D["DO\nExecute the process\nCollect metrics\nTrain people"]
    C["CHECK\nMeasure outcomes\nCompare to plan\nInternal audit"]
    A["ACT\nCorrect deviations\nStandardize improvements\nUpdate QMS"]

    P ==> D
    D ==> C
    C ==> A
    A ==>|Next iteration| P
```

## 4.4 Cost of Quality (CoQ) Optimization Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    axis["Cost in Currency"]
    opt["OPTIMAL Q-LEVEL\nCoQ is minimized here"]
    rise["CoQ rises as Q improves beyond optimum"]
    drop["CoQ drops as Q improves from low levels"]

    o1["PREVENTION + APPRAISAL Cost Curve (upward)"]
    o2["FAILURE Cost Curve (downward)"]
    sum["TOTAL CoQ = Prevention + Appraisal + Failure"]

    o1 --> sum
    o2 --> sum
    sum --> opt
    drop --> opt
    opt --> rise
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC). Mention one standard for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Quality Assurance (QA) | Quality Control (QC) |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | **Process** | **Product** |
| Objective | **Prevent** defects | **Detect** defects |
| Approach | Process definition, training, audits | Testing, reviews, inspections |
| Timing | Throughout the lifecycle | After product construction |
| Standards | **ISO 9001 / CMMI PPQA** | **ISO/IEC 25010 / IEEE 829** |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: List and briefly explain the five maturity levels of CMMI.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Level 1 — Initial:** Process is unpredictable, chaotic, and dependent on individual effort.
2. **Level 2 — Managed:** Project-level processes are planned, documented, monitored, and controlled.
3. **Level 3 — Defined:** Organization-wide standard processes exist; projects tailor them.
4. **Level 4 — Quantitatively Managed:** Subprocesses are measured using statistical and other quantitative techniques.
5. **Level 5 — Optimizing:** Continuous process improvement is enabled by quantitative feedback and innovative ideas.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **ISO 9001:2015** standard. List its **7 Quality Management Principles** and map them to the **10 clauses** of the standard. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Capability Maturity Model Integration (CMMI)** framework. Explain the **process areas of Level 2 and Level 3** with examples. How does CMMI differ from ISO 9001? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Introduction to ISO 9001 (1 mark):**
ISO 9001:2015 is the international standard for **Quality Management Systems (QMS)**, published by **ISO**. It is the only certifiable document in the ISO 9000 family and is applicable to organizations of any size or industry, including software.

**Step 2 — Seven Quality Management Principles (3 marks, ~0.4 each):**

1. **Customer Focus** — primary focus on meeting customer requirements and exceeding expectations.
2. **Leadership** — leaders establish unity of purpose, direction, and engagement.
3. **Engagement of People** — competent, empowered, and engaged people enhance the organization's ability to create value.
4. **Process Approach** — consistent and predictable results are achieved more effectively when activities are understood and managed as interrelated processes.
5. **Improvement** — successful organizations have an ongoing focus on improvement.
6. **Evidence-based Decision Making** — decisions based on the analysis and evaluation of data are more likely to produce desired results.
7. **Relationship Management** — managing relationships with interested parties (suppliers, partners, society) optimizes performance.

**Step 3 — Mapping to 10 Clauses (3 marks):**

| Clause | Title | PDCA Stage |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | Context of the Organization | PLAN |
| 5 | Leadership | PLAN |
| 6 | Planning (risks, objectives) | PLAN |
| 7 | Support (resources, competence, documentation) | DO |
| 8 | Operation (design, production, release) | DO |
| 9 | Performance Evaluation (audit, review) | CHECK |
| 10 | Improvement (corrective action) | ACT |

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — CMMI Overview (1.5 marks):**
CMMI (Capability Maturity Model Integration) is a **process improvement framework** developed by the **Software Engineering Institute (SEI) at Carnegie Mellon University**. It integrates best practices from multiple CMMs into a single model. The current version is **CMMI v2.0**, with five maturity levels.

**Step 2 — Level 2 Process Areas (1.5 marks):**
The 7 PAs of Level 2 — Managed: **REQM, PP, PMC, SAM, MA, PPQA, CM**.
*Example:* In the *Project Planning (PP)* PA, the team develops a project plan that estimates scope, effort, cost, and schedule. In *PPQA*, the SQA team audits whether the project follows the defined process.

**Step 3 — Level 3 Process Areas (1.5 marks):**
The 11 PAs of Level 3 — Defined include technical (TS, PI, VER, VAL) and management (OPF, OPD, OPM, IPM, RSKM, DAR, OT) areas.
*Example:* *Verification (VER)* ensures that the software products meet the specified requirements; *Validation (VAL)* confirms that the final product meets user needs in the operational environment.

**Step 4 — CMMI vs ISO 9001 (2.5 marks):**

| Parameter | ISO 9001:2015 | CMMI v2.0 |
|---|---|---|
| **Issuer** | ISO | SEI (Carnegie Mellon) |
| **Scope** | Generic (any industry) | Software & systems |
| **Certifiability** | Third-party certified | Appraised, not certified |
| **Maturity** | Binary (yes/no) | 5 levels |
| **Prescriptiveness** | Principle-based | Process-area-based |
| **Mapping** | Maps to CMMI Level 2–3 | Maps to ISO 9001 partially |

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Q: (a)** What is the **PDCA (Deming) cycle**? Explain how it forms the backbone of modern QA standards. Show the ISO 9001 clause mapping. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Defect Density, Process Yield, MTTF, and Cost of Quality (CoQ)** with formulas. A project has size 40 KLOC and 120 defects detected during testing. The test phase has 90 defects removed, 10 injected. Compute the **Defect Density, DRE, DER, and the overall Process Yield** if the requirement, design, and coding phases had PYs of 0.4, 0.5, and 0.7 respectively. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — PDCA Definition (1 mark):**
The **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** cycle, also called the **Deming Cycle** or **Shewhart Cycle**, is a four-stage iterative management method for the continuous improvement of processes and products. It is named after **W. Edwards Deming**, who popularized it in post-WWII Japan.

**Step 2 — PDCA Stages (3 marks):**

- **PLAN:** Establish objectives (SMART goals) and processes (SIPOC diagram) required to deliver results in accordance with customer requirements and organizational policies. Identify risks via FMEA or SWOT.
- **DO:** Execute the planned processes; create the deliverables; collect data on process performance (effort, defects, cycle time).
- **CHECK:** Compare the actual performance (data from DO) against the expected performance (plan). Use **control charts, histograms, Pareto analysis** to detect deviations.
- **ACT:** If performance deviates, perform **root cause analysis** (5-Whys, Fishbone), apply **corrective action**, and **standardize** the improved process. The cycle then restarts.

**Step 3 — Backbone of QA Standards (2 marks):**
Both ISO 9001:2015 and CMMI are organized around PDCA. The Deming cycle ensures that quality is *built-in* (PLAN-DO) and *verified* (CHECK) and *improved* (ACT). This recursive nature means the QMS never stagnates — it continuously evolves based on empirical feedback.

**Step 4 — ISO 9001 Clause Mapping (1 mark):**
PLAN → Clauses 4, 5, 6. DO → Clauses 7, 8. CHECK → Clause 9. ACT → Clause 10.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Definitions (2 marks, ~0.5 each):**

- **Defect Density (DD):** Defects per unit size. $\text{DD} = \frac{D}{\text{KLOC}}$ or per FP.
- **Process Yield (PY):** Fraction of defects removed in a phase relative to total entering the phase.
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** $\text{MTTF} = \frac{\sum t_i}{N}$, where $t_i$ is the time of the $i$-th failure.
- **Cost of Quality (CoQ):** $\text{CoQ} = C_{\text{prev}} + C_{\text{appr}} + C_{\text{int}} + C_{\text{ext}}$.

**Step 2 — Given Data (0.5 marks):**
- Size $S = 40 \text{ KLOC}$, total defects $D = 120$.
- Test phase: 90 removed, 10 injected. PY of test = 90/100 = 0.9.
- PY(Req) = 0.4, PY(Des) = 0.5, PY(Cod) = 0.7.

**Step 3 — Compute Defect Density (0.5 marks):**

$$
\text{DD} = \frac{120}{40} = 3.0 \text{ defects/KLOC}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute DRE and DER of Testing (1 mark):**

$$
\text{DRE}_{\text{test}} = \frac{90}{100} \times 100 = 90\%
$$

$$
\text{DER}_{\text{test}} = 100 - 90 = 10\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Overall Process Yield (1 mark):**

$$
Y_{\text{overall}} = 0.4 \times 0.5 \times 0.7 \times 0.9
$$

$$
0.4 \times 0.5 = 0.20
$$

$$
0.20 \times 0.7 = 0.14
$$

$$
0.14 \times 0.9 = 0.126
$$

**Final answer:** $Y_{\text{overall}} = 12.6\%$

**Step 6 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
The overall roll-up yield is only **12.6%**, which is far below the CMMI Level 4 target of **> 95%**. The Requirements phase is the weakest link (PY = 0.4). The defect density of 3.0/KLOC is acceptable for web applications but not for safety-critical software. The organization should invest in **requirements reviews, formal inspections, and early testing** to shift the CoQ balance from failure to prevention.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing PDCA with SDLC.** PDCA is a *management cycle*, not a development model. Don't write it as Requirements → Design → Coding → Testing.
> 2. **Writing CMMI Levels in reverse order.** Always start from Level 1 → 5. Examiners deduct marks for upside-down listing.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention "ISO 9001 is the only certifiable document"** in the ISO 9000 family. This is a recurring 1-mark differentiator.
> 4. **Mis-mapping PDCA to ISO clauses.** Clauses 9 and 10 are *NOT* part of PLAN — they are CHECK and ACT respectively.
> 5. **In numerical problems, students often forget to convert to %** when writing DRE/DER, or skip the unit "per KLOC" for defect density. Always carry units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorize Bulletins for KTU Module 4 — Process Standards & QA Standards:**

- A **process standard** governs *how* work is done; a **product standard** governs *what* is built. QA is process-focused; QC is product-focused.
- The **PDCA (Deming) cycle** is the philosophical backbone of ISO 9001 and CMMI. PLAN → DO → CHECK → ACT.
- **ISO 9001:2015** has **7 Quality Principles** and **10 Clauses** mapped to PDCA. It is the **only certifiable** document in the ISO 9000 family. It introduced **Risk-Based Thinking** in Clause 6.
- **CMMI** has **5 maturity levels**: Initial, Managed, Defined, Quantitatively Managed, Optimizing. It was developed by **SEI, Carnegie Mellon**.
- **CMMI Level 2** has **7 process areas** including PPQA (the SQA audit PA). **Level 3** has **11 PAs** including VER, VAL, TS, PI.
- **Defect Density** = Defects / KLOC. **DRE** = defects removed / total defects. **DER** = 1 − DRE. **PY** rolls up multiplicatively across phases.
- **MTTF** is the mean time between failures; **MTTR** is the mean time to repair. **Availability** = MTTF / (MTTF + MTTR).
- **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** has four components: Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure. Mature organizations have **< 30%** failure cost share.
- **The 1:10:100 rule:** A defect fixed at the requirements stage costs ₹ 1; at design ₹ 10; post-release ₹ 100.
- ISO 9001 is **generic, principle-based, third-party certifiable**. CMMI is **software-specific, prescriptive, SEI-appraised**.
- Mapping studies show ISO 9001 compliance **roughly equals** CMMI Level 2–3, but CMMI is a more granular maturity measurement.
- **Capability Level** applies to a single process area; **Maturity Level** applies to the entire organization.
- The **SQA team's** direct CMMI home is the **PPQA (Process and Product Quality Assurance)** process area at Level 2.
- Always include units (per KLOC, per FP, %) and explicit formula substitution in numerical answers to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Management Standards and Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality Management Standards and Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Quality Management Standards?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **Quality Management Standard** is a formally documented set of guidelines, specifications, rules, criteria, or characteristics that define the minimum acceptable quality parameters for processes, products, or services. In the context of software, these standards provide a **uniform framework** to ensure that software products and the processes used to develop them consistently meet customer requirements and regulatory expectations.

In simple engineering parlance, a quality standard is a **"rule-book for doing things right"** that has been agreed upon by experts globally. These standards are published by recognized international bodies such as:

- **ISO** (International Organization for Standardization) – Geneva, Switzerland
- **IEC** (International Electrotechnical Commission)
- **IEEE** (Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers)
- **SEI** (Software Engineering Institute) – Carnegie Mellon, USA

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Traffic Rule Book

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Highway Code"**
> Imagine driving a car on Indian roads. Without traffic signals, lane discipline, and speed limits, every driver would behave differently, leading to accidents. **Quality Standards act exactly like traffic rules for software development.** They ensure:
> 1. Every developer follows the *same process* (lane discipline).
> 2. Every product is verified before delivery (vehicle fitness tests).
> 3. Every defect is documented and tracked (FIR / accident reports).
> 4. The final output (the "vehicle" reaching the customer) is safe, reliable, and predictable.
>
> Just as ISO 9001 certifies a driving school as "quality assured," CMMI certifies a software company as a "mature process organization."

## 1.3 The Two Major Categories of Quality Standards

Quality management standards in software engineering broadly fall into two categories:

| Category | Purpose | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| **Process Standards** | Define **HOW** software is developed and maintained | **CMMI**, ISO 9001, **SPICE (ISO/IEC 15504)** |
| **Product Standards** | Define **WHAT** characteristics the software must possess | **ISO/IEC 25010**, IEEE 830 (SRS), IEEE 829 (Test Documentation) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> For KTU Module 4, the primary focus is on three flagship standards:
> 1. **ISO 9000 Series** (Generic quality management)
> 2. **CMMI** (Process maturity model for software)
> 3. **ISO/IEC 25010** (Software product quality model)
> 4. **Six Sigma** (Statistical quality methodology)
> 5. **SPICE / ISO/IEC 15504** (Process assessment model)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ISO 9000 Series — The Foundation of Quality Management

The **ISO 9000 family** is the world's most widely recognized quality management standard, applicable across all industries — not just software. It was first published in **1987** and revised in 1994, 2000, 2008, and **2015**.

### Family Members (High-Yield for KTU):

| Standard | Full Name | Purpose |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| **ISO 9000:2015** | Quality Management Systems — Fundamentals and Vocabulary | Defines the **terminology and concepts** (the "dictionary") |
| **ISO 9001:2015** | Quality Management Systems — Requirements | The **only certifiable** standard; specifies actual requirements |
| **ISO 9004:2018** | Quality Management — Quality of an Organization | Guidance for **sustained success** (beyond ISO 9001) |

### The 7 Quality Management Principles of ISO 9001:2015:

1. **Customer Focus** — Primary focus is meeting customer requirements
2. **Leadership** — Leaders establish unity of purpose and direction
3. **Engagement of People** — Competent, empowered people at all levels
4. **Process Approach** — Activities understood and managed as interrelated processes
5. **Improvement** — Successful organizations focus on continual improvement
6. **Evidence-based Decision Making** — Decisions based on analysis and evaluation of data
7. **Relationship Management** — Managing relationships with interested parties (suppliers, partners)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:**
> ISO 9001 follows the **PDCA Cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** also known as the **Deming Cycle**. This is a frequently asked question in KTU exams.

$$
\text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Establish objectives \& processes} \\
\text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Execute the processes} \\
\text{Check} \rightarrow \text{Monitor and measure performance} \\
\text{Act} \rightarrow \text{Take actions to improve}
$$

---

## 2.2 CMMI (Capability Maturity Model Integration)

CMMI is a **process level improvement training and appraisal program** developed by the **Software Engineering Institute (SEI)** at **Carnegie Mellon University**, USA. The current version is **CMMI v2.0** (released 2018).

CMMI provides organizations with the essential elements of effective processes that ultimately improve their performance.

### The 5 Maturity Levels of CMMI:

| Level | Name | Key Characteristic | KTU Buzzword |
|-------|------|---------------------|--------------|
| **Level 1** | **Initial** | Processes are **chaotic, unpredictable, ad-hoc**. Success depends on individual effort. | "Heroic" effort |
| **Level 2** | **Managed** | Projects are **planned, performed, measured, and controlled**. Basic project management exists. | Repeatable success |
| **Level 3** | **Defined** | Processes are **well-characterized, documented, and standardized** across the organization. | Proactive |
| **Level 4** | **Quantitatively Managed** | Organization sets **quantitative quality goals** and uses data for decision-making. | Measured |
| **Level 5** | **Optimizing** | Focus is on **continuous process improvement** using feedback and innovation. | Self-improving |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:**
> To move from Level 2 to Level 3, an organization must satisfy **7 Process Areas**. To move from Level 3 to Level 4, an organization must satisfy **2 additional Process Areas** (OPP and QPM). The total Process Areas across all levels are **16 (in CMMI-DEV v1.3)** and **20 (in CMMI v2.0)**.

### Key Process Areas (KPAs) — High-Yield:

**Level 2 (Managed):**
- Requirements Management (REQM)
- Project Planning (PP)
- Project Monitoring and Control (PMC)
- Supplier Agreement Management (SAM)
- Measurement and Analysis (MA)
- Process and Product Quality Assurance (PPQA)
- Configuration Management (CM)

**Level 3 (Defined):**
- Organizational Process Focus (OPF)
- Organizational Process Definition (OPD)
- Organizational Training (OT)
- Integrated Project Management (IPM)
- Risk Management (RSKM)
- Decision Analysis and Resolution (DAR)
- Validation (VAL)
- Verification (VER)
- Product Integration (PI)
- Technical Solution (TS)
- Requirements Development (RD)

**Level 4 (Quantitatively Managed):**
- Organizational Process Performance (OPP)
- Quantitative Project Management (QPM)

**Level 5 (Optimizing):**
- Organizational Innovation and Deployment (OID)
- Causal Analysis and Resolution (CAR)

---

## 2.3 ISO/IEC 25010 — Software Product Quality Model

ISO/IEC 25010 (released in **2011**, replacing the older ISO/IEC 9126) defines **eight quality characteristics** that a software product must possess. It is the **direct successor** to the older ISO/IEC 9126 standard (which had 6 characteristics).

### The 8 Quality Characteristics:

| # | Characteristic | Definition | Sub-characteristics |
|---|----------------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Functional Suitability** | Degree to which the product provides functions that meet stated needs | Functional completeness, correctness, appropriateness |
| 2 | **Performance Efficiency** | Performance relative to resources used | Time behaviour, resource utilization, capacity |
| 3 | **Compatibility** | Ability to coexist/cooperate with other products | Co-existence, interoperability |
| 4 | **Usability** | Degree of ease of use | Recognizability, learnability, operability, error protection, UI aesthetics, accessibility |
| 5 | **Reliability** | Degree to which the product performs specified functions under stated conditions | Maturity, availability, fault tolerance, recoverability |
| 6 | **Security** | Protection of information and data | Confidentiality, integrity, non-repudiation, accountability, authenticity |
| 7 | **Maintainability** | Ease with which the product can be modified | Modularity, reusability, analyzability, modifiability, testability |
| 8 | **Portability** | Ease with which product can be transferred between environments | Adaptability, installability, replaceability |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison:**
> Older **ISO/IEC 9126 (2001)** had **6** characteristics — the 8 above minus Compatibility and Security. KTU frequently asks students to **list and compare** the two standards.

---

## 2.4 Six Sigma — Statistical Quality Methodology

**Six Sigma** is a data-driven methodology developed by **Motorola in 1986** (popularized by **Jack Welch at General Electric**) that aims to eliminate defects to near-zero levels (3.4 defects per million opportunities, abbreviated as **DPMO = 3.4**).

### The Two Six Sigma Sub-Methodologies:

| Methodology | Acronym | Used When |
|-------------|---------|-----------|
| **DMAIC** | Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control | Improving **existing processes** |
| **DMADV** | Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify | Creating **new processes/products** |

### Six Sigma Formula Sheet (Critical for KTU Numerical Problems):

$$
\text{Sigma Level} = \text{NORMSINV}\left(1.5 \cdot \frac{\text{DPMO}}{1{,}000{,}000}\right) + 1.5
$$

Where:
- **DPMO** = Defects Per Million Opportunities
- **NORMSINV** = Inverse of standard normal cumulative distribution
- The **1.5 sigma shift** is the standard industry adjustment

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Total Number of Defects Detected}}{\text{Total Number of Opportunities for Defect per Unit}} \times 1{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Yield} = \frac{\text{Units Produced without Defect}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> Six Sigma is widely used in **manufacturing, aerospace, and IT service management**. IT companies like **TCS, Infosys, and Wipro** use Six Sigma (often branded as "Lean Six Sigma") to optimize their software delivery pipelines, reducing bug rates and improving first-time-right delivery.

---

## 2.5 SPICE (ISO/IEC 15504) — Process Assessment Model

**SPICE** stands for **Software Process Improvement and Capability dEtermination**. It was developed to address the limitations of CMM and provide an **international standard** for process assessment.

### The 6 Capability Levels of SPICE:

| Level | Name | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| **Level 0** | **Incomplete** | Process does not achieve its purpose |
| **Level 1** | **Performed** | Process achieves its purpose (informally) |
| **Level 2** | **Managed** | Process is planned, monitored, and adjusted |
| **Level 3** | **Established** | Process uses defined, standardized methods |
| **Level 4** | **Predictable** | Process operates within controlled limits |
| **Level 5** | **Optimizing** | Process is continuously improved |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:**
> Unlike CMM/CMMI which has 5 **organizational maturity levels**, SPICE/ISO 15504 has **6 process capability levels**. The dimensions are also different: CMMI assesses **organizational maturity**, while SPICE assesses **individual process capability**.

### KTU Formula Sheet (Final Consolidated Cheat Sheet):

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Reference |
|---------|---------------------|-----------|
| **DPMO** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Opportunities} \times \text{Units}} \times 1{,}000{,}000$ | Six Sigma |
| **Process Yield** | $\text{Yield} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units}} \times 100$ | Six Sigma |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Six Sigma |
| **CMMI Maturity Levels** | 5 levels: Initial → Managed → Defined → Quantitatively Managed → Optimizing | CMMI |
| **SPICE Capability Levels** | 6 levels: 0 (Incomplete) → 5 (Optimizing) | ISO 15504 |
| **ISO/IEC 25010 Characteristics** | 8 characteristics | ISO 25010 |
| **ISO/IEC 9126 Characteristics** | 6 characteristics (older version) | ISO 9126 |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Six Sigma DPMO — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (Highly Probable KTU Numerical):**
> A software company develops a payroll module. The module consists of **200 opportunities for defects per unit**. In a release of **1,000 units**, **75 defects** were detected. Calculate the **DPMO** and the corresponding **Sigma Level**.

### Step 1: Identify the Given Values
- Number of units produced = $U = 1{,}000$
- Number of defects detected = $D = 75$
- Opportunities for defect per unit = $O = 200$

### Step 2: Calculate Total Opportunities

$$
T_{opp} = U \times O
$$

$$
T_{opp} = 1{,}000 \times 200 = 200{,}000 \text{ opportunities}
$$

**Explanation:** Each of the 1,000 units has 200 possible places where a defect can occur, so the total opportunity count across all units is 200,000.

### Step 3: Apply the DPMO Formula

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{D}{T_{opp}} \times 1{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{75}{200{,}000} \times 1{,}000{,}000
$$

### Step 4: Simplify

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{75}{200{,}000} \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 75 \times 5 = 375
$$

**Conclusion:** The process is operating at **375 DPMO**, which corresponds to approximately **4.9 Sigma** (using the standard Six Sigma conversion table).

### Step 5: Sigma Level Conversion (Using Long-Term Sigma Formula)

$$
\sigma_{\text{LT}} = \text{NORMSINV}\left(\frac{T_{opp} - D}{T_{opp}}\right) + 1.5
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{LT}} = \text{NORMSINV}\left(\frac{200{,}000 - 75}{200{,}000}\right) + 1.5
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{LT}} = \text{NORMSINV}(0.999625) + 1.5
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{LT}} \approx 3.4 + 1.5 = 4.9 \text{ Sigma}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:**
> KTU examiners commonly give a DPMO value and ask for Sigma Level, OR give defects/units/opportunities and ask for DPMO. Memorize the conversion table below for quick reference.

### Six Sigma Conversion Table (High-Yield):

| DPMO | Sigma Level | Quality |
|------|-------------|---------|
| 308,537 | 2.0 σ | Not competitive |
| 66,807 | 3.0 σ | Industry average |
| 6,210 | 4.0 σ | Better than average |
| 233 | 5.0 σ | World class |
| 3.4 | 6.0 σ | Near-perfect (Six Sigma goal) |

---

## 3.2 Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example:**
> A software deployment pipeline has **4 sequential stages**: Build, Unit Test, Integration Test, and UAT. Their individual yields are: Y₁ = 95%, Y₂ = 98%, Y₃ = 92%, Y₄ = 99%. Calculate the **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** and the **process efficiency**.

### Step 1: Identify Yields (as decimals)

$$
Y_1 = 0.95, \quad Y_2 = 0.98, \quad Y_3 = 0.92, \quad Y_4 = 0.99
$$

### Step 2: Apply the RTY Formula

$$
\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4
$$

$$
\text{RTY} = 0.95 \times 0.98 \times 0.92 \times 0.99
$$

### Step 3: Compute Step-by-Step

$$
0.95 \times 0.98 = 0.9310
$$

$$
0.9310 \times 0.92 = 0.85652
$$

$$
0.85652 \times 0.99 = 0.84795
$$

### Step 4: Express as Percentage

$$
\text{RTY} \approx 84.80\%
$$

### Step 5: Calculate Process Efficiency (Defect Rate)

$$
\text{Defect Rate} = 1 - \text{RTY} = 1 - 0.8480 = 0.1520 = 15.20\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Only **84.80%** of units pass through **all four stages** without any rework or rejection. This is a critical metric KTU examiners use to assess process maturity.

---

## 3.3 CMMI Staged Representation — Analytical Matrix

The CMMI model can be represented using a **Goal-Question-Metric (GQM)** paradigm at each maturity level. Here is the analytical mapping:

| Maturity Level | Generic Goals | Process Areas | Generic Practices |
|---------------|--------------|---------------|-------------------|
| Level 2: Managed | GG 2.1, 2.2 | 7 KPAs | 19 |
| Level 3: Defined | GG 3.1, 3.2 | 11 KPAs (DEV) | 27 |
| Level 4: Quantitatively Managed | GG 4.1, 4.2 | 2 KPAs | 7 |
| Level 5: Optimizing | GG 5.1, 5.2 | 2 KPAs | 7 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Note:**
> Each **KPA (Key Process Area)** contains the following five components (a common 14-mark question):
> 1. **Goals** — What the KPA must achieve
> 2. **Commitment to Perform** — Policies that ensure the KPA is established
> 3. **Ability to Perform** — Resources, training, and tools
> 4. **Measurement and Analysis** — How performance is measured
> 5. **Verification and Validation** — How compliance is confirmed

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CMMI 5-Level Maturity Pyramid

```mermaid
graph TD
    Level5["LEVEL 5 OPTIMIZING Continuous process improvement Focus on innovation OID and CAR"] --> Level4
    Level4["LEVEL 4 QUANTITATIVELY MANAGED Quantitative quality goals Data driven decisions OPP and QPM"] --> Level3
    Level3["LEVEL 3 DEFINED Standardized documented processes Organization wide 11 KPAs"] --> Level2
    Level2["LEVEL 2 MANAGED Project level planning Basic PM practices 7 KPAs"] --> Level1
    Level1["LEVEL 1 INITIAL Chaotic Ad hoc Success depends on individual effort"]

    classDef level1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef level2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4500,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef level3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef level4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef level5 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class Level1 level1
    class Level2 level2
    class Level3 level3
    class Level4 level4
    class Level5 level5
```

## 4.2 ISO/IEC 25010 Quality Model — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    SQ["SOFTWARE PRODUCT QUALITY MODEL ISO IEC 25010"] --> FS["FUNCTIONAL SUITABILITY Completeness Correctness Appropriateness"]
    SQ --> PE["PERFORMANCE EFFICIENCY Time behaviour Resource use Capacity"]
    SQ --> CP["COMPATIBILITY Co existence Interoperability"]
    SQ --> US["USABILITY Learnability Operability Error protection Aesthetics Accessibility"]
    SQ --> RL["RELIABILITY Maturity Availability Fault tolerance Recoverability"]
    SQ --> SC["SECURITY Confidentiality Integrity Non repudiation Authenticity"]
    SQ --> MN["MAINTAINABILITY Modularity Reusability Analyzability Modifiability Testability"]
    SQ --> PT["PORTABILITY Adaptability Installability Replaceability"]

    classDef rootnode fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000080,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef leafnode fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    class SQ rootnode
    class FS,PE,CP,US,RL,SC,MN,PT leafnode
```

## 4.3 ISO 9001:2015 PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P["PLAN Establish quality objectives Define processes Identify resources Plan risk mitigation"] --> D
    D["DO Execute the planned processes Train workforce Implement controls Generate outputs"] --> C
    C["CHECK Monitor and measure processes Verify conformity against objectives Conduct audits"] --> A
    A["ACT Take corrective actions Standardize improvements Update processes for next cycle"] --> P

    classDef pdca fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class P,D,C,A pdca
```

## 4.4 Six Sigma DMAIC Flow Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    DMAIC["DMAIC METHODOLOGY FOR PROCESS IMPROVEMENT"] --> D1["DEFINE Identify problem Customer CTQ Voice of Customer VOC Project charter"]
    DMAIC --> D2["MEASURE Collect baseline data Establish CTQ metrics Validate measurement system"]
    DMAIC --> D3["ANALYZE Identify root causes Use statistical tools Pareto Fishbone 5 Why"]
    DMAIC --> D4["IMPROVE Develop solutions Pilot test Implement changes Validate improvement"]
    DMAIC --> D5["CONTROL Standardize solution Implement controls Monitor sustain gains"]

    classDef dmaicRoot fill:#6A1B9A,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef dmaicPhase fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef dmaicStage fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000

    class DMAIC dmaicRoot
    class D1,D2,D3,D4,D5 dmaicStage
```

## 4.5 ISO 9000 Family Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    ISO9["ISO 9000 FAMILY QUALITY MANAGEMENT"] --> ISOF["ISO 9000 Fundamentals and Vocabulary"]
    ISO9 --> ISOQ["ISO 9001 Requirements Certifiable Standard"]
    ISO9 --> ISOG["ISO 9004 Guidance for Sustained Success"]
    ISO9 --> ISOS["ISO 9001 Supporting Standards Audits Documentation"]

    classDef isoFamily fill:#2E7D32,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef isoMember fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class ISO9 isoFamily
    class ISOF,ISOQ,ISOG,ISOS isoMember
```

## 4.6 SPICE vs CMMI — Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    SPICE["SPICE ISO IEC 15504 PROCESS CAPABILITY"] --> S0["L0 INCOMPLETE"]
    SPICE --> S1["L1 PERFORMED"]
    SPICE --> S2["L2 MANAGED"]
    SPICE --> S3["L3 ESTABLISHED"]
    SPICE --> S4["L4 PREDICTABLE"]
    SPICE --> S5["L5 OPTIMIZING"]

    CMMI["CMMI ORGANIZATIONAL MATURITY"] --> C1["L1 INITIAL"]
    CMMI --> C2["L2 MANAGED"]
    CMMI --> C3["L3 DEFINED"]
    CMMI --> C4["L4 QUANTITATIVELY MANAGED"]
    CMMI --> C5["L5 OPTIMIZING"]

    classDef spiceRoot fill:#D32F2F,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
```
