# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define ISO 9000. List its family members. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **ISO 9000** is a series of international standards published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) that provide a framework for **quality management systems (QMS)** applicable to any organization regardless of size or industry.

**Family Members:**

| Standard | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| **ISO 9000** | Fundamentals and vocabulary |
| **ISO 9001** | Requirements (the certifiable standard) |
| **ISO 9004** | Guidance for sustained organizational success |
| **ISO 19011** | Guidelines for auditing management systems |

**Mark Allocation:** Definition (1) + Family listing (1.5) + Application note (0.5)

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Process Standards and Product Standards. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Process Standards | Product Standards |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Focus** | Define HOW software is built | Define WHAT the software should be |
| **Examples** | CMMI, ISO 9001, SPICE | ISO/IEC 25010, IEEE 830, IEEE 829 |
| **Objective** | Improve development methodology | Define quality characteristics of final product |
| **Scope** | Organization-level process maturity | Specific software product attributes |
| **Assessment** | Through appraisals (SCAMPI) | Through testing and measurement |

**Mark Allocation:** Definition (1) + Comparison table (1.5) + Examples (0.5)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### PART B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, Module 4]**

### **Question A (a)** — 7 Marks
**Explain the Capability Maturity Model Integration (CMMI). Describe its five maturity levels in detail with examples.**

### **Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
CMMI (Capability Maturity Model Integration) is a process improvement framework developed by the **Software Engineering Institute (SEI)** at Carnegie Mellon University. It provides organizations with a structured way to **assess and improve** their software development and maintenance processes. The model integrates multiple disciplines (systems engineering, software engineering, integrated product and process development) into a single framework.

**Five Maturity Levels (5 Marks):**

**Level 1 — Initial:**
- The process is **chaotic, unpredictable, and ad-hoc**.
- Success depends entirely on the **competence and effort of individuals** (often called "heroes").
- Schedules, budgets, and quality are **unpredictable**.
- *Example:* A startup where deadlines are met only because one skilled developer works overtime.

**Level 2 — Managed:**
- Basic **project management practices** are established.
- Processes are **planned, performed, measured, and controlled** at the project level.
- 7 KPAs: Requirements Management, Project Planning, Project Monitoring and Control, Supplier Agreement Management, Measurement and Analysis, PPQA, Configuration Management.
- *Example:* A company that successfully delivers projects using documented plans and tracks defects.

**Level 3 — Defined:**
- Processes are **well-characterized, documented, and standardized** at the organization level.
- A **standard process** is defined and adapted for each project.
- 11 KPAs including Organizational Process Focus, OPD, OT, IPM, RSKM, etc.
- *Example:* An organization that has a standard Software Development Life Cycle used across all teams.

**Level 4 — Quantitatively Managed:**
- Organization establishes **quantitative quality goals** for processes and software.
- Uses **statistical and other quantitative techniques** to manage processes.
- 2 KPAs: Organizational Process Performance (OPP), Quantitative Project Management (QPM).
- *Example:* A company using Six Sigma metrics to predict defect density and cycle time.

**Level 5 — Optimizing:**
- Focus on **continuous process improvement** using feedback from the process itself.
- 2 KPAs: Organizational Innovation and Deployment (OID), Causal Analysis and Resolution (CAR).
- *Example:* An organization that conducts root-cause analysis of every defect and feeds learnings back into the process.

---

### **Question A (b)** — 7 Marks
**Explain the ISO/IEC 25010 software product quality model. List its eight quality characteristics with at least two sub-characteristics each.**

### **Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
ISO/IEC 25010, published in 2011, is the international standard that defines a **quality model for software products**. It replaced the older ISO/IEC 9126 standard and provides a comprehensive framework for evaluating software quality.

**Eight Quality Characteristics (6 Marks — 0.75 each):**

| # | Characteristic | Sub-characteristics | Definition |
|---|----------------|---------------------|------------|
| 1 | **Functional Suitability** | Completeness, Correctness, Appropriateness | Capability to provide functions meeting stated and implied needs |
| 2 | **Performance Efficiency** | Time behaviour, Resource utilization, Capacity | Performance relative to amount of resources used under stated conditions |
| 3 | **Compatibility** | Co-existence, Interoperability | Ability to coexist and operate with other products/systems |
| 4 | **Usability** | Learnability, Operability, Error protection, UI aesthetics, Accessibility | Degree to which the product can be used by specified users effectively |
| 5 | **Reliability** | Maturity, Availability, Fault tolerance, Recoverability | Capability to maintain specified performance under conditions for a specified time |
| 6 | **Security** | Confidentiality, Integrity, Non-repudiation, Accountability, Authenticity | Protection of information and data from unauthorized access |
| 7 | **Maintainability** | Modularity, Reusability, Analyzability, Modifiability, Testability | Ease with which the product can be modified to correct defects or adapt to changes |
| 8 | **Portability** | Adaptability, Installability, Replaceability | Ease with which the product can be transferred from one environment to another |

---

### PART B — Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

### **Question B (a)** — 7 Marks
**Explain Six Sigma methodology in detail. What is DPMO? A software module has 850 defects in 50,000 units, with each unit having 250 opportunities for defects. Calculate the DPMO and identify the corresponding Sigma Level.**

### **Model Solution:**

**Six Sigma Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Six Sigma is a **data-driven quality methodology** that seeks to improve process quality by identifying and removing the causes of defects and minimizing variability. It was developed by **Motorola in 1986** and later popularized by **Jack Welch at General Electric**. The goal is to reach a quality level of **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**.

**Two Methodologies (1 Mark):**
- **DMAIC**: Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control — for improving **existing** processes
- **DMADV**: Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify — for creating **new** processes

**DPMO Definition (0.5 Mark):**
DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities) is the standard metric used to quantify process quality at the Six Sigma level.

**Calculation (4 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Identify given values
$$
D = 850 \text{ defects}, \quad U = 50{,}000 \text{ units}, \quad O = 250 \text{ opportunities/unit}
$$

**Step 2:** Total opportunities
$$
T_{opp} = U \times O = 50{,}000 \times 250 = 12{,}500{,}000
$$

**Step 3:** Apply DPMO formula
$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{D}{T_{opp}} \times 1{,}000{,}000 = \frac{850}{12{,}500{,}000} \times 1{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{850}{12.5} = 68
$$

**Step 4:** Identify Sigma Level using conversion table
From the standard Six Sigma conversion table, **68 DPMO** corresponds to approximately **5.2 Sigma Level** (between 5.0 σ = 233 DPMO and 6.0 σ = 3.4 DPMO).

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** The process is operating at approximately **5.2 sigma**, indicating a very high-quality process but slightly below the world-class Six Sigma benchmark.

---

### **Question B (b)** — 7 Marks
**Compare CMM/CMMI and SPICE (ISO/IEC 15504). What are the key differences between the two process assessment frameworks?**

### **Model Solution:**

**CMMI Overview (1.5 Marks):**
CMMI (Capability Maturity Model Integration) is a process improvement framework developed by SEI. It provides organizations with a model for **organizational maturity assessment** across 5 levels. It uses the SCAMPI (Standard CMMI Appraisal Method for Process Improvement) method for formal appraisals.

**SPICE Overview (1.5 Marks):**
SPICE (Software Process Improvement and Capability dEtermination), standardized as **ISO/IEC 15504**, is an international standard for **process capability assessment**. It was developed to address the regional limitations of CMM and provide a globally consistent framework.

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Aspect | CMM / CMMI | SPICE (ISO/IEC 15504) |
|--------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Origin** | SEI, Carnegie Mellon (USA, 1987) | ISO/IEC (International, 1995) |
| **Scope** | Organizational maturity assessment | Individual process capability assessment |
| **Levels** | **5 Maturity Levels** | **6 Capability Levels (0–5)** |
| **Structure** | Staged representation (5 levels) OR Continuous representation | Continuous representation only |
| **Reference Model** | CMMI-DEV, CMMI-ACQ, CMMI-SVC | ISO/IEC 15504 Part 2 (Reference Model) |
| **Process Dimensions** | Process Areas (16 in v1.3, 20 in v2.0) | Processes grouped by category |
| **Assessment** | SCAMPI A, B, C | ISO/IEC 15504 Part 3 (Assessment) |
| **Geographic Adoption** | Predominantly USA, India, Japan | Europe, Australia, global ISO adoption |
| **Industry Focus** | Originally defense/software; now multi-domain | Software and systems engineering |

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** While both frameworks serve similar purposes (process improvement and assessment), CMMI focuses on organizational maturity while SPICE focuses on individual process capability. Modern organizations may use both in a complementary manner.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **CMM vs CMMI Confusion (Loss: 2 Marks):** Writing "CMM has 5 levels" without clarifying that CMMI v2.0 has reorganized process areas. Always mention the SEI origin.
>
> 2. **Forgetting ISO 9001 is the ONLY certifiable standard** in the ISO 9000 family. Students often write that ISO 9000 is certifiable — **wrong**, it is just a vocabulary document.
>
> 3. **ISO/IEC 9126 vs ISO/IEC 25010 Confusion:** Many KTU answers confuse the two. Remember: **9126 has 6 characteristics** (no Security, no Compatibility), **25010 has 8 characteristics**.
>
> 4. **DPMO Formula Errors:** Students often multiply by 1,000 instead of 1,000,000 or divide in the wrong order. Memorize: $\text{DPMO} = (\text{Defects} \div \text{Total Opportunities}) \times 10^6$.
>
> 5. **Confusing Six Sigma DMAIC and DMADV:** DMAIC = Improve existing; DMADV = Design new. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 6. **SPICE vs CMMI Levels:** SPICE has **6** capability levels (0 to 5), CMMI has **5** maturity levels (1 to 5). Mixing these up is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
>
> 7. **PDCA Cycle Order:** Plan → Do → Check → Act. Memorize this in correct order.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4:**

- ✅ **ISO 9000 Family:** ISO 9000 (vocabulary), ISO 9001 (certifiable requirements), ISO 9004 (guidance for sustained success)
- ✅ **ISO 9001:2015 has 7 Quality Management Principles** and uses the **PDCA (Deming) Cycle**
- ✅ **CMMI** has **5 Maturity Levels**: Initial → Managed → Defined → Quantitatively Managed → Optimizing
- ✅ **CMMI Level 2 has 7 KPAs**, Level 3 has 11 KPAs (DEV v1.3), Levels 4 & 5 have 2 KPAs each
- ✅ **CMM was created by SEI** at Carnegie Mellon; current version is CMMI v2.0
- ✅ **ISO/IEC 25010 has 8 Quality Characteristics** (replaced ISO/IEC 9126 which had 6)
- ✅ **The 8 characteristics:** Functional Suitability, Performance Efficiency, Compatibility, Usability, Reliability, Security, Maintainability, Portability
- ✅ **Six Sigma** was developed by **Motorola (1986)**, popularized by **Jack Welch (GE)**
- ✅ **Six Sigma goal:** 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)
- ✅ **DMAIC:** Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control (improve existing process)
- ✅ **DMADV:** Define → Measure → Analyze → Design → Verify (create new process)
- ✅ **DPMO Formula:** $\text{DPMO} = (\text{Defects} \div \text{Opportunities}) \times 1{,}000{,}000$
- ✅ **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** = product of all individual stage yields
- ✅ **SPICE / ISO/IEC 15504** has **6 Capability Levels** (0 = Incomplete to 5 = Optimizing)
- ✅ **SPICE assesses individual processes**; **CMMI assesses organizational maturity**
- ✅ **Process Standards focus on HOW** (CMMI, ISO 9001); **Product Standards focus on WHAT** (ISO/IEC 25010)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Scheme Tip:**
> For 14-mark questions, always structure your answer with: **Definition → Diagram/Tabulation → Explanation with Examples → Conclusion**. This matches the KTU mark distribution pattern (typically 2 + 7 + 3 + 2 marks). Use the diagrams from Section 4 as visual aids — KTU examiners reward well-structured, diagram-supported answers with higher marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Progress Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Progress Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Project Progress Control** is the systematic, continuous process of monitoring, measuring, evaluating, and regulating a software project's execution against its established baseline plan (scope, schedule, cost, and quality), employing formal techniques such as **Earned Value Management (EVM)**, variance analysis, and corrective action workflows to ensure alignment with strategic objectives.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme SQA syllabus (Module 4)**, Project Progress Control is positioned as a managerial discipline that integrates with Quality Assurance to guarantee that the software being built conforms not only to specifications but also to contractual time and cost constraints. It is the operational execution of the **Plan → Do → Check → Act (PDCA)** cycle at the project management layer.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Road Trip GPS 🚗**
>
> Imagine you are driving from Kerala to Delhi (a 2,500 km journey planned over 5 days). Your GPS is essentially a **Project Progress Control System**:
> - The **original route** = Project Baseline Plan
> - The **"Distance Covered"** = Earned Value (actual work accomplished)
> - The **"Time Elapsed"** = Planned Value
> - The **"Fuel Burnt"** = Actual Cost
> - The **"Estimated Time of Arrival (ETA)"** = Estimate at Completion (EAC)
> - When you deviate, the GPS **recalculates** (Corrective Action) and suggests a new route.
> - If fuel is burning faster than planned, it warns you (Cost Variance).
> - If you are behind schedule, it tells you to drive faster or take a shorter path (Schedule Recovery).

The **purpose** of progress control is therefore:
1. **Visibility** – Provide transparent, data-driven status to stakeholders.
2. **Early Warning** – Detect slippage before it becomes catastrophic.
3. **Decision Support** – Enable informed trade-offs between scope, time, and cost.
4. **Accountability** – Establish a defensible audit trail of performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU syllabus explicitly mandates knowledge of **Earned Value Analysis, Schedule Tracking, Cost Monitoring, and Change Control** as sub-topics under Project Progress Control.

## 1.3 Core Metrics Glossary (Mandatory Terminology)

| Term | Expansion | Meaning |
|------|-----------|---------|
| **PV** | Planned Value | Budgeted cost of work scheduled |
| **EV** | Earned Value | Budgeted cost of work performed |
| **AC** | Actual Cost | Real cost incurred for work performed |
| **BAC** | Budget at Completion | Total authorized project budget |
| **EAC** | Estimate at Completion | Forecasted total project cost |
| **ETC** | Estimate to Complete | Remaining cost to finish project |
| **VAC** | Variance at Completion | BAC − EAC (cost overrun/savings) |
| **SPI** | Schedule Performance Index | EV / PV (efficiency of time use) |
| **CPI** | Cost Performance Index | EV / AC (efficiency of money use) |

All the above metrics are rendered in **bold** as they constitute the **standard project management vocabulary** required for KTU board examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Project Progress Control

Project progress control rests on three interlocking pillars. Understanding *why* each exists is more important than memorizing formulas.

### 2.1.1 Pillar I — Performance Measurement (The "What Happened?" Phase)
- Decompose the project into **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** elements.
- Assign a **budgeted cost** to each WBS element.
- At periodic status dates, capture:
  - How much work was **scheduled** to be done → *Planned Value (PV)*
  - How much work was **actually completed** → *Earned Value (EV)*
  - How much money was **actually spent** → *Actual Cost (AC)*

### 2.1.2 Pillar II — Variance Analysis (The "How Are We Doing?" Phase)
- Compare the three measurements pairwise to detect deviations.
- **Schedule Variance (SV)** answers: *Are we ahead or behind time?*
- **Cost Variance (CV)** answers: *Are we over or under budget?*
- Performance Indices (SPI, CPI) normalize the variances for projects of different sizes, allowing benchmarking.

### 2.1.3 Pillar III — Forecasting & Corrective Action (The "What Now?" Phase)
- Use current performance indices to **extrapolate** future outcomes:
  - **EAC** = Forecasted total cost
  - **ETC** = Cost needed to complete
  - **VAC** = Projected cost deviation at completion
- **Corrective actions** include:
  - Crashing (adding resources)
  - Fast-tracking (parallelizing tasks)
  - Scope re-baselining
  - Defect rework prioritization

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Formulas

> [!NOTE]
> The genius of **Earned Value Management (EVM)** is that it converts three different units (time, scope, cost) into a **single common monetary unit**. This allows apples-to-apples comparison: you can compare "hours spent" against "dollars budgeted" against "features completed" — all converted to **rupees (₹)** of work value.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Every formula below is fair game for KTU Part A (3-mark) derivations and Part B (14-mark) numerical problems.

| # | Metric | Formula | Interpretation | Unit |
|---|--------|---------|----------------|------|
| 1 | **Planned Value (PV)** | $\text{PV} = \text{BAC} \times \frac{\% \text{ Work Scheduled}}{100}$ | Budgeted cost of scheduled work | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 2 | **Earned Value (EV)** | $\text{EV} = \text{BAC} \times \frac{\% \text{ Work Completed}}{100}$ | Budgeted cost of completed work | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 3 | **Actual Cost (AC)** | Directly measured | Real money spent so far | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 4 | **Schedule Variance (SV)** | $\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV}$ | Negative → Behind Schedule | ₹ |
| 5 | **Cost Variance (CV)** | $\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC}$ | Negative → Over Budget | ₹ |
| 6 | **Schedule Performance Index (SPI)** | $\text{SPI} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}}$ | $\lt 1$ → Behind; $\gt 1$ → Ahead | Ratio |
| 7 | **Cost Performance Index (CPI)** | $\text{CPI} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}}$ | $\lt 1$ → Overrun; $\gt 1$ → Under | Ratio |
| 8 | **Estimate at Completion (EAC)** | $\text{EAC} = \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}}$ | Forecasted total cost | ₹ |
| 9 | **Estimate to Complete (ETC)** | $\text{ETC} = \text{EAC} - \text{AC}$ | Remaining cost required | ₹ |
| 10 | **Variance at Completion (VAC)** | $\text{VAC} = \text{BAC} - \text{EAC}$ | Projected final cost deviation | ₹ |
| 11 | **To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI)** | $\text{TCPI} = \frac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{BAC} - \text{AC}}$ | CPI needed for remaining work | Ratio |
| 12 | **Percent Spent (PS)** | $\text{PS} = \frac{\text{AC}}{\text{BAC}} \times 100$ | Budget consumed | % |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **Indian IT industry** (Infosys, TCS, Wipro, Cognizant), EVM is the **de-facto standard** for:
- **Fixed-price contracts** (where cost overruns directly erode margin)
- **CMMI Level 5** organizations (where quantitative project management is mandatory)
- **Government projects** (e.g., Smart Cities Mission, e-Governance, ISRO mission software) where audit and accountability are paramount
- **Agile + EVM hybrids** known as "Agile EVM" or "Scrum EVM", increasingly used for SaaS product companies

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Why is EVM superior to traditional cost-tracking?"* The answer is that traditional tracking monitors **spending** and **schedule** in isolation, but EVM **integrates scope, schedule, and cost** into a unified view, enabling early prediction of failures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Conceptual Derivation of EVM

Let us derive the rationale for the three base metrics (PV, EV, AC) from first principles.

### Step 1 — Project Decomposition
A project is decomposed into the Work Breakdown Structure. The total approved budget is the **Budget at Completion (BAC)**.

$$\text{BAC} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Budget}_i$$

where $n$ is the number of WBS elements.

### Step 2 — Planned Value (PV) at Status Date
If the project is $t$ days into a $T$-day schedule, and work is uniformly distributed, then the planned progress is $t/T$. More generally, from the Gantt chart, we read the cumulative budget of all tasks **scheduled to be complete** by the status date.

$$\text{PV} = \sum_{j \in S_t} \text{Budget}_j$$

where $S_t$ is the set of tasks scheduled for completion by date $t$.

### Step 3 — Earned Value (EV)
For each task, we measure the **percent complete** (e.g., 0%, 50%, 100%). The budget allocated to that task, multiplied by the percent complete, gives its earned value.

$$\text{EV} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{Budget}_j \times \frac{\% \text{ Complete}_j}{100}$$

### Step 4 — Actual Cost (AC)
This is the **sum of all money actually spent** on the project up to the status date — including salaries, infrastructure, software licenses, and overheads.

$$\text{AC} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \text{Expenditure}_k$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following problem is a **classic 7-mark EVM calculation** that has appeared multiple times in KTU university examinations.

### Problem Statement
> A software project has a **Budget at Completion (BAC) = ₹10,00,000** and is scheduled over **12 months**. At the end of **Month 6**, the project manager reports:
> - 40% of the work has been **physically completed**
> - **Actual cost incurred** so far = ₹5,50,000
> - As per the original schedule, 50% of the work was **planned** to be complete by Month 6
>
> **Calculate** PV, EV, AC, SV, CV, SPI, CPI, EAC, ETC, and VAC. Interpret the project status.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Given Data:**
- $\text{BAC} = ₹10,00,000$
- Planned % complete at Month 6 = **50%**
- Actual % complete at Month 6 = **40%**
- $\text{AC} = ₹5,50,000$

---

**Step 1 — Compute Planned Value (PV)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV} &= \text{BAC} \times \frac{\% \text{ Work Scheduled}}{100} \\
&= 10,00,000 \times \frac{50}{100} \\
&= ₹5,00,000
\end{aligned}$$

*Interpretation:* We had planned to have done work worth ₹5,00,000 by now.

---

**Step 2 — Compute Earned Value (EV)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{EV} &= \text{BAC} \times \frac{\% \text{ Work Completed}}{100} \\
&= 10,00,000 \times \frac{40}{100} \\
&= ₹4,00,000
\end{aligned}$$

*Interpretation:* We have actually accomplished work worth only ₹4,00,000 (as per the budgeted rate).

---

**Step 3 — Actual Cost (AC)** — Directly given as ₹5,50,000.

---

**Step 4 — Schedule Variance (SV)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SV} &= \text{EV} - \text{PV} \\
&= 4,00,000 - 5,00,000 \\
&= -₹1,00,000
\end{aligned}$$

*Interpretation:* Negative SV → **Project is behind schedule.**

---

**Step 5 — Cost Variance (CV)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CV} &= \text{EV} - \text{AC} \\
&= 4,00,000 - 5,50,000 \\
&= -₹1,50,000
\end{aligned}$$

*Interpretation:* Negative CV → **Project is over budget.**

---

**Step 6 — Schedule Performance Index (SPI)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SPI} &= \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}} \\
&= \frac{4,00,000}{5,00,000} \\
&= 0.80
\end{aligned}$$

*Interpretation:* For every 1 hour of scheduled work, only **0.80 hours** of actual progress is achieved. The project is running at **80% schedule efficiency**.

---

**Step 7 — Cost Performance Index (CPI)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CPI} &= \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} \\
&= \frac{4,00,000}{5,50,000} \\
&= 0.7273 \;(\text{approx.} \; 0.73)
\end{aligned}$$

*Interpretation:* For every ₹1 spent, we are getting only **₹0.73 worth of work**. The project is financially inefficient.

---

**Step 8 — Estimate at Completion (EAC)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{EAC} &= \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}} \\
&= \frac{10,00,000}{0.7273} \\
&= ₹13,75,000 \;(\text{approx.})
\end{aligned}$$

*Interpretation:* If current performance continues, the project will end up costing **₹13,75,000** — a **37.5% overrun** over the original budget.

---

**Step 9 — Estimate to Complete (ETC)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ETC} &= \text{EAC} - \text{AC} \\
&= 13,75,000 - 5,50,000 \\
&= ₹8,25,000
\end{aligned}$$

*Interpretation:* An additional ₹8,25,000 is required to finish the project at the current performance level.

---

**Step 10 — Variance at Completion (VAC)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{VAC} &= \text{BAC} - \text{EAC} \\
&= 10,00,000 - 13,75,000 \\
&= -₹3,75,000
\end{aligned}$$

*Interpretation:* The project is projected to **overshoot the budget by ₹3,75,000** at completion.

---

## 3.3 Final Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following consolidated table is the **expected answer format** for full marks in a KTU EVM numerical problem.

| Metric | Symbol | Computed Value | Status Indicator | Interpretation |
|--------|--------|----------------|------------------|----------------|
| Planned Value | PV | ₹5,00,000 | — | Plan baseline |
| Earned Value | EV | ₹4,00,000 | — | Actual accomplishment |
| Actual Cost | AC | ₹5,50,000 | — | Money spent |
| Schedule Variance | SV | **−₹1,00,000** | 🔴 Negative | **Behind schedule** |
| Cost Variance | CV | **−₹1,50,000** | 🔴 Negative | **Over budget** |
| Schedule Performance Index | SPI | **0.80** | 🔴 < 1 | Schedule inefficiency |
| Cost Performance Index | CPI | **0.73** | 🔴 < 1 | Cost inefficiency |
| Estimate at Completion | EAC | **₹13,75,000** | 🔴 > BAC | Forecasted overrun |
| Estimate to Complete | ETC | ₹8,25,000 | — | Funds required |
| Variance at Completion | VAC | **−₹3,75,000** | 🔴 Negative | Will exceed budget |

**Verdict:** The project is in **critical condition** — it is both **behind schedule** and **over budget**. Immediate corrective action (crashing, scope reduction, or re-baselining) is mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Project Progress Control Cycle

The following Mermaid block renders the **closed-loop control cycle** that governs how project progress is monitored and corrected.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: Establish Baseline Plan] --> B[StepB: Define WBS and BAC]
    B --> C[StepC: Schedule Tasks and Assign Budgets]
    C --> D[StepD: Execute Project Work]
    D --> E[StepE: Measure Performance Data]
    E --> F[StepF: Compute PV EV AC]
    F --> G[StepG: Calculate Variances SV CV]
    G --> H{Decision: SPI and CPI Acceptable}
    H -- Yes --> I[StepI: Continue Execution and Monitor]
    H -- No --> J[StepJ: Diagnose Root Cause]
    J --> K[StepK: Select Corrective Action]
    K --> L[StepL: Crashing or Fast Tracking or Re Baseline]
    L --> D
    I --> M[StepM: Forecast EAC ETC VAC]
    M --> N[StepN: Report Status to Stakeholders]
    N --> O[StepO: Capture Lessons Learned]
    O --> P[StepP: Project Closure or Next Phase]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The cycle shows that **measurement** triggers **analysis**, which triggers **decision-making**, which triggers **corrective action**, which loops back into **execution**. This is essentially **Deming's PDCA cycle** adapted to project management.

## 4.2 Earned Value Management — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer [Input Data Block]
        direction TB
        In1[Budget at Completion BAC]
        In2[Planned Percent Complete]
        In3[Actual Percent Complete]
        In4[Actual Money Spent]
    end

    subgraph ComputationLayer [Computation Engine Block]
        direction TB
        C1[PV Calculator]
        C2[EV Calculator]
        C3[AC Register]
        C4[Variance Engine SV and CV]
        C5[Performance Index Engine SPI and CPI]
        C6[Forecasting Engine EAC ETC VAC]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer [Output Status Block]
        direction TB
        Out1[Schedule Status Ahead or Behind]
        Out2[Cost Status Under or Over]
        Out3[Final Forecasted Cost]
        Out4[Required Corrective Action List]
    end

    In1 --> C1
    In1 --> C2
    In2 --> C1
    In3 --> C2
    In4 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C2 --> C4
    C3 --> C4
    C1 --> C5
    C2 --> C5
    C3 --> C5
    C2 --> C6
    In1 --> C6
    C3 --> C6
    C4 --> Out1
    C4 --> Out2
    C5 --> Out1
    C5 --> Out2
    C6 --> Out3
    C1 --> Out4
    C4 --> Out4
    C5 --> Out4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The architecture is a **pipeline** with three logical stages:
> 1. **Input Layer** — Static project data (BAC) and dynamic field measurements (% complete, money spent)
> 2. **Computation Layer** — Six parallel engines that process the inputs through the EVM formulas
> 3. **Output Layer** — Four categorized status outputs that feed into executive dashboards
>
> In a real **project management office (PMO)**, this corresponds to tools like **Microsoft Project, Primavera P6, Jira with EVM plugins, or OpenProject**.

## 4.3 Corrective Action Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Variance Detected SV negative or SPI less than 1] --> Q1{Is scope reducible}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Reduce Scope and Re Baseline]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is schedule negotiable}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Extend Deadline and Re Baseline]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Can additional resources help}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Crash Critical Path and Add Resources]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Are dependencies parallelizable}
    Q4 -- Yes --> R4[Fast Track Activities into Parallel Execution]
    Q4 -- No --> R5[Accept Variance and Document Risk]
    R1 --> Done[Update Baseline and Inform Stakeholders]
    R2 --> Done
    R3 --> Done
    R4 --> Done
    R5 --> Done
```

> [!NOTE]
> This decision tree reflects the **industry-standard sequence of corrective actions**: from least disruptive (scope reduction) to most invasive (accepting variance), guided by stakeholder constraints.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Define **Earned Value Management (EVM)**. List any **four** EVM metrics with their formulas.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Earned Value Management (EVM)** is a quantitative project management technique that integrates scope, schedule, and cost measurements to assess project performance and progress objectively.

**Four EVM Metrics:**

| # | Metric | Formula |
|---|--------|---------|
| 1 | Schedule Variance | $\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV}$ |
| 2 | Cost Variance | $\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC}$ |
| 3 | Schedule Performance Index | $\text{SPI} = \text{EV} / \text{PV}$ |
| 4 | Cost Performance Index | $\text{CPI} = \text{EV} / \text{AC}$ |

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark each for any 4 metrics = full 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> Differentiate between **Planned Value (PV)** and **Earned Value (EV)**. Why is EV considered a better indicator of actual progress than percent complete alone?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Planned Value (PV) | Earned Value (EV) |
|--------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Definition | Budgeted cost of work **scheduled** to be done | Budgeted cost of work **actually performed** |
| Source | Baseline schedule/Gantt chart | Physical progress measurement |
| Nature | Forward-looking (planned) | Backward-looking (earned) |
| Use | Sets the target at status date | Measures accomplishment against plan |

**Why EV is superior to % complete alone:**  
Percent complete is **subjective and binary** (e.g., "module is 60% done"). EV **monetizes** this progress by multiplying it with budget, making it **comparable across tasks of different sizes** and enabling mathematical variance analysis. *(1 mark for comparison table + 2 marks for the justification)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently confuse **SV (Schedule Variance)** with **CV (Cost Variance)** — remember **SV uses PV**, **CV uses AC**.
> - A common mistake is forgetting to compute **EAC, ETC, and VAC** in numerical problems — KTU explicitly awards marks for **forecasting**, not just variance calculation.
> - **Don't write the formula only** — show substitution of values and unit (₹) for full marks.
> - Always provide an **interpretation sentence** for SPI and CPI. A bare number without interpretation loses 1–2 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question]**

> A software project has a **BAC of ₹20,00,000** scheduled to be completed in **10 months**. At the end of **Month 4**, the project manager provides the following data:
> - 30% of the work has been **physically completed**
> - **Actual cost incurred** = ₹7,00,000
> - As per the original plan, **40%** of work was scheduled to be complete by Month 4
>
> **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the values of PV, EV, SV, CV, SPI, and CPI. Interpret the schedule and cost status of the project.
>
> **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Compute EAC, ETC, VAC, and TCPI. Suggest **three corrective actions** the project manager should initiate.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $\text{BAC} = ₹20,00,000$
- Planned % complete = 40% → $\text{PV} = 20,00,000 \times 0.40 = ₹8,00,000$
- Actual % complete = 30% → $\text{EV} = 20,00,000 \times 0.30 = ₹6,00,000$
- $\text{AC} = ₹7,00,000$

$$\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV} = 6,00,000 - 8,00,000 = -₹2,00,000$$

$$\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC} = 6,00,000 - 7,00,000 = -₹1,00,000$$

$$\text{SPI} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}} = \frac{6,00,000}{8,00,000} = 0.75$$

$$\text{CPI} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} = \frac{6,00,000}{7,00,000} = 0.857$$

**Interpretation (2 marks):**
- $\text{SV} = -₹2,00,000$ and $\text{SPI} = 0.75 \lt 1$ → **The project is behind schedule.** For every 1 hour of planned work, only 0.75 hours of actual progress is achieved.
- $\text{CV} = -₹1,00,000$ and $\text{CPI} = 0.857 \lt 1$ → **The project is over budget.** For every ₹1 spent, only ₹0.857 worth of work is delivered.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Forecasting Calculations:**

$$\text{EAC} = \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}} = \frac{20,00,000}{0.857} = ₹23,33,333 \;(\text{approx.})$$

$$\text{ETC} = \text{EAC} - \text{AC} = 23,33,333 - 7,00,000 = ₹16,33,333$$

$$\text{VAC} = \text{BAC} - \text{EAC} = 20,00,000 - 23,33,333 = -₹3,33,333$$

$$\text{TCPI} = \frac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{BAC} - \text{AC}} = \frac{20,00,000 - 6,00,000}{20,00,000 - 7,00,000} = \frac{14,00,000}{13,00,000} = 1.077$$

**Three Corrective Actions (3 marks):**

1. **Crash the Critical Path** — Allocate additional developers/QA engineers to in-progress modules to accelerate completion. This increases AC but improves SPI.
2. **Fast-Track Parallel Activities** — Identify tasks currently sequential in the schedule and execute them concurrently after risk assessment.
3. **Scope Re-prioritization with Client** — Defer low-priority features to a Phase 2 release, reducing the remaining BAC and bringing it closer to the revised EAC.

**TCPI Interpretation:** Since TCPI > 1, achieving the original BAC is **mathematically difficult** with current performance. A re-baseline is recommended.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Question]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Project Progress Control Process** as a closed-loop control system. Describe the role of **Earned Value Management** in this loop with a suitable block diagram.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A project has a BAC of ₹8,00,000. After 5 months out of a 10-month schedule, the following are reported:
> - Planned % work complete = 55%
> - Actual % work complete = 60%
> - Actual cost = ₹4,20,000
>
> Calculate **all EVM metrics** and state whether the project is in a **healthy state**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

The Project Progress Control Process is a **closed-loop, iterative control system** that follows Deming's PDCA cycle:

1. **Plan (Establish Baseline):** Define scope, schedule (Gantt chart), and cost baseline (BAC). Decompose via WBS.
2. **Execute (Do):** Perform the development, testing, and integration activities.
3. **Measure (Check):** At periodic status dates, capture PV, EV, and AC for each WBS element.
4. **Analyze (Check):** Compute SV, CV, SPI, CPI. Identify variances and root causes.
5. **Correct (Act):** Apply corrective actions (crashing, fast-tracking, scope reduction) to bring the project back on track.
6. **Re-baseline (Act):** Update the project plan if variances are accepted by stakeholders.
7. **Report:** Communicate status, forecasts (EAC, ETC, VAC), and risks to the steering committee.

**Role of EVM in the loop:** EVM provides the **measurement** and **analysis** stages with quantitative rigor. Without EVM, the loop becomes subjective and prone to optimistic bias. EVM introduces **objective, monetary-based performance indicators** that trigger the **correction** stage only when thresholds are breached (e.g., SPI < 0.9 or CPI < 0.9). *(Block diagram as shown in Section 4.2 above)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $\text{BAC} = ₹8,00,000$
- Planned % = 55% → $\text{PV} = 8,00,000 \times 0.55 = ₹4,40,000$
- Actual % = 60% → $\text{EV} = 8,00,000 \times 0.60 = ₹4,80,000$
- $\text{AC} = ₹4,20,000$

**Variances and Indices:**

$$\text{SV} = 4,80,000 - 4,40,000 = +₹40,000 \;(\text{Positive})$$

$$\text{CV} = 4,80,000 - 4,20,000 = +₹60,000 \;(\text{Positive})$$

$$\text{SPI} = \frac{4,80,000}{4,40,000} = 1.0909$$

$$\text{CPI} = \frac{4,80,000}{4,20,000} = 1.1429$$

**Forecasts:**

$$\text{EAC} = \frac{8,00,000}{1.1429} = ₹7,00,000$$

$$\text{ETC} = 7,00,000 - 4,20,000 = ₹2,80,000$$

$$\text{VAC} = 8,00,000 - 7,00,000 = +₹1,00,000 \;(\text{Savings})$$

**Project Health Verdict (2 marks):**  
The project is in a **HEALTHY state** because:
- $\text{SV} > 0$ and $\text{SPI} > 1$ → **Ahead of schedule**
- $\text{CV} > 0$ and $\text{CPI} > 1$ → **Under budget**
- $\text{VAC} > 0$ → Projected to **save ₹1,00,000** at completion

**Recommendation:** Continue current pace. Capture lessons learned for future projects. Consider early delivery bonus if contractually available.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Board Examination 2024 Scheme**

- [x] **Project Progress Control** = the systematic process of comparing actual performance against the baseline to drive corrective action.
- [x] The **three EVM pillars** are *Performance Measurement, Variance Analysis, and Forecasting + Corrective Action*.
- [x] **PV** uses *scheduled* progress; **EV** uses *actual* progress; **AC** uses *real money spent*.
- [x] **SV = EV − PV**; **CV = EV − AC** — memorize this ordering carefully.
- [x] **SPI < 1** → behind schedule; **CPI < 1** → over budget; both negative simultaneously indicates a **critical** project.
- [x] **EAC = BAC / CPI** is the most commonly used forecasting formula — practice its derivation.
- [x] **TCPI > 1** means the remaining work must be performed with greater efficiency than the original plan to meet the BAC.
- [x] Corrective actions in order of preference: **Scope reduction → Schedule extension → Crashing → Fast-tracking → Accept variance**.
- [x] EVM is **scope-schedule-cost integrated** — the *only* technique that does this in a single monetary view.
- [x] KTU examiners award marks for: (i) correct formula, (ii) substitution with values, (iii) numeric answer with ₹ unit, (iv) **interpretation sentence**.
- [x] The **PDCA cycle** is the philosophical foundation of progress control — every tool (EVM, Scrum, Six-Sigma) operationalizes it.
- [x] Industry tools: **MS Project, Primavera P6, OpenProject, Jira with EVM plugins** — KTU may ask for tool-based MCQs.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line takeaway:** *"EVM turns your project's vague feelings about progress into a number you can defend in a steering committee meeting."*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: From SDLC to Agile
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT752 - SOFTWARE QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** From SDLC to Agile

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is SDLC?

**Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)** is a structured, systematic process framework that defines the complete sequence of phases involved in building, deploying, and maintaining an information system, from initial feasibility study to final retirement. It is a **plan-driven, sequential engineering approach** prescribed by traditional software engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Sommerville & Pressman standard):**
> *SDLC is a process that produces software with the highest quality and lowest cost in the shortest time possible, while providing a well-defined framework consisting of phases that enable effective management, control, and documentation of the software development process.*

The classical SDLC adheres to the **Waterfall Model** invented by **Winston W. Royce in 1970**, which enforces strict phase-wise execution:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 6 Canonical SDLC Phases (KTU Exam Favorite):**
> 1. **Requirement Analysis & Feasibility Study**
> 2. **System Design (HLD + LLD)**
> 3. **Implementation / Coding**
> 4. **Testing / Verification**
> 5. **Deployment / Installation**
> 6. **Maintenance**

## 1.2 The Real-World Analogy: Building a House

Imagine you are constructing a **skyscraper in Kerala's coastal city of Kochi**.

- **SDLC (Waterfall) Approach:** The architect first draws **the entire blueprint** of all 40 floors. The civil engineer locks the structural design. Foundation is laid, then walls, then plumbing, then electrical — all **in a strict, predefined order**. If the client suddenly says *"Add a helipad on top"*, you must re-do structural analysis, costing crores of rupees and months of delay. This is SDLC — **plan everything upfront, then execute linearly**.

- **Agile Approach:** You build a **single small functional room first** (a prototype). The client lives in it, gives feedback ("add a window here", "make the kitchen bigger"), and you iterate. Every 2 weeks you deliver a slightly better version. There is no master plan — only a **compass direction (vision) and incremental progress**. This is Agile.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:**
> SDLC = Baking a wedding cake using a rigid recipe (you cannot un-bake if you mess up).
> Agile = Tasting the curry as you cook, adjusting salt and spices in small batches.

## 1.3 The Trigger: Why the Industry Pivoted from SDLC to Agile

The transition was not fashion — it was a **forced survival mechanism** triggered by the **Standish Group CHAOS Report (1994)** which reported that only **16.2%** of traditional SDLC projects succeeded on time, on budget, and with full features. The remaining 83.8% either failed outright, were delivered late, or missed critical requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3 Symptoms of SDLC Failure That Necessitated Agile:**
> 1. **Requirement Volatility** — Modern business needs change every quarter; SDLC freezes requirements upfront.
> 2. **Late Defect Detection** — In Waterfall, testing happens at the end; a defect found in Phase 6 costs **100x more** than one found in Phase 1.
> 3. **Zero Customer Involvement** — Customer sees the product only at delivery; no feedback loop.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of Traditional SDLC (Waterfall Model)

The Waterfall Model operates on the principle of **phase containment**. Each phase must be **completed, verified, and approved** before the next phase can begin. This is enforced through **formal stage-gate reviews** (e.g., SRS sign-off, Design Review, Test Plan Approval).

### 2.1.1 Phase-Wise Breakdown

| Phase | Key Deliverable (KTU Terminology) | Primary Stakeholder |
|---|---|---|
| **Feasibility Study** | Feasibility Report (Technical, Economic, Operational, Legal) | Management / Sponsor |
| **Requirement Analysis** | **SRS (Software Requirement Specification)** document as per IEEE Std 830 | Business Analyst |
| **System Design** | **SDD (Software Design Description)** with DFDs, ER diagrams, UML | System Architect |
| **Coding** | Source code, version-controlled in SCM (e.g., Git, SVN) | Developers |
| **Testing** | Test Plan, Test Cases, Defect Reports, Test Summary | QA Team |
| **Deployment** | Installation Manual, Release Notes | DevOps / Customer |
| **Maintenance** | Patch logs, Change Requests | Support Team |

### 2.1.2 The Boehm Cost-of-Change Curve (KTU High-Yield)

This is the **single most important metric** in SQA — it quantifies why Waterfall is risky. **Barry Boehm's 1981 empirical study** proved that the relative cost of fixing a defect **grows exponentially** as the project progresses through SDLC phases.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Defect Cost Amplification Factor** | $\text{CAF} = \dfrac{\text{Cost to fix at phase } n}{\text{Cost to fix at Requirements phase}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Project Success Rate (CHAOS)** | $\text{Success \%} = \dfrac{\text{Successful Projects}}{\text{Total Projects}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| **Velocity (Agile)** | $\text{Velocity} = \sum \text{Story Points completed in 1 Sprint}$ | Story Points / Sprint |
| **Sprint Duration** | Fixed: 1 to 4 weeks (typical = **2 weeks**) | Time |
| **Standish Failure Index** | $\text{Failure \%} = 100 - \text{Success \%} - \text{Challenged \%}$ | Percentage |
| **Phase Gate Compliance** | $\text{PGC} = \dfrac{\text{Phases completed with sign-off}}{\text{Total phases}}$ | 0 to 1 |
| **Defect Detection Efficiency** | $\text{DDE} = \dfrac{\text{Defects found pre-release}}{\text{Total defects pre + post release}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| **Rework Effort Ratio** | $\text{RER} = \dfrac{\text{Effort spent on re-fixing}}{\text{Total development effort}} \times 100$ | Percentage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boehm's Empirical Data (Must Memorize for KTU):**
> - Requirements phase fix cost: **1x** (baseline)
> - Design phase fix cost: **5x to 10x**
> - Coding phase fix cost: **10x to 20x**
> - Integration Testing: **50x**
> - System Testing: **100x**
> - Post-Deployment / Maintenance: **150x to 200x**
>
> **Implication:** A defect that costs ₹1,000 to fix during requirements may cost ₹1,50,000 to fix post-release. This is the **economic justification** for Agile's early and continuous testing.

## 2.2 The Agile Manifesto (2001) — The Birth of Agile

In **February 2001**, 17 software practitioners (including Kent Beck, Martin Fowler, Jeff Sutherland, Robert C. Martin) gathered at **Snowbird, Utah**, and authored the **Agile Manifesto** as a direct rebellion against the rigidity of document-heavy SDLC.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 4 Core Values of the Agile Manifesto (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> 1. **Individuals and Interactions** over processes and tools
> 2. **Working Software** over comprehensive documentation
> 3. **Customer Collaboration** over contract negotiation
> 4. **Responding to Change** over following a plan
>
> *The right-side items still have value, but the left-side items are valued more.*

### 2.2.1 The 12 Supporting Principles (Condensed for KTU)

1. Highest priority is to satisfy the customer through **early and continuous delivery** of valuable software.
2. Welcome changing requirements, **even late in development** — Agile processes harness change for the customer's competitive advantage.
3. Deliver working software **frequently** (weeks rather than months).
4. Business people and developers must work **together daily**.
5. Build projects around **motivated individuals**; give them the environment and support they need.
6. Face-to-face conversation is the **most effective method** of conveying information.
7. **Working software** is the primary measure of progress.
8. Agile processes promote **sustainable development** — maintain a constant pace indefinitely.
9. Continuous attention to **technical excellence and good design** enhances agility.
10. **Simplicity** — the art of maximizing the amount of work not done — is essential.
11. The best architectures, requirements, and designs emerge from **self-organizing teams**.
12. The team **reflects** on how to become more effective, then tunes and adjusts its behavior.

## 2.3 Comparative Analysis: SDLC vs Agile

| Dimension | Traditional SDLC (Waterfall) | Agile |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Sequential, plan-driven | Iterative, value-driven |
| **Requirements** | Frozen at start (Big Design Up Front) | Evolving, refined each sprint |
| **Delivery** | Single delivery at end | Incremental delivery every sprint |
| **Customer Involvement** | Only at start and end | Continuous, every sprint review |
| **Documentation** | Heavy (SRS, SDD, STD, etc.) | Just-enough, lightweight |
| **Change Handling** | Formally controlled via CCB | Embraced as a competitive edge |
| **Testing** | Phase-end (huge test phase) | Continuous (Test-Driven Development) |
| **Risk Profile** | High late-stage risk | Low, distributed risk per iteration |
| **Team Structure** | Specialized silos (BA, Dev, QA) | Cross-functional, T-shaped skills |
| **Feedback Loop** | Months/years | Days/weeks |
| **Success Metric** | On-time, on-budget delivery | Working software + customer satisfaction |
| **Best Suited For** | Well-defined, regulated domains (defense, medical devices) | Dynamic, evolving requirements (startups, web apps) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check:**
> Agile is **not** a replacement for SDLC — it is an **evolutionary improvement** that still follows a life cycle, but with shorter, overlapping cycles called **Sprints/Iterations**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Cost Analysis

## 3.1 The Transition Journey: From Waterfall to Agile

The migration is not a "flip the switch" event. Industry practice (per IBM, Microsoft, and Infosys transformation case studies) follows a **maturity ladder**.

### Step 1: Diagnose the Pain Points of Current SDLC

**Textual Logic:** Before changing anything, conduct a retrospective audit. Identify the top 3 failure symptoms — late delivery, scope creep, defect leakage, customer dissatisfaction.

$$\text{Pain Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$$

where $w_i$ is the weight of pain category $i$ and $s_i$ is the severity score (1–10).

### Step 2: Pilot with a Non-Critical Project

Do not transform the entire portfolio. Select a small team and a non-mission-critical project to run on Scrum/XP. Document learnings.

### Step 3: Define Agile Values and Pilot Team Structure

Adopt the 4 Manifesto values, 12 principles, and form a **cross-functional team** (3–9 members) with a Scrum Master and Product Owner.

### Step 4: Train, Coach, and Establish Cadence

Introduce **Sprint ceremonies** — Sprint Planning, Daily Standup, Sprint Review, Retrospective — and enforce timeboxing.

### Step 5: Scale and Institutionalize

Once the pilot succeeds, propagate to other teams, integrate with DevOps, and align HR/policies with Agile values.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Boehm's Cost-of-Change

> **Problem (KTU Style):**
> In a Waterfall project, a requirements analyst identifies a missing login validation rule. The cost to fix it now is **₹1,000**. If the same defect leaks to the design phase, coding phase, system testing, and post-deployment, calculate the total compounded cost using Boehm's amplification factors of 1x, 8x, 15x, 100x, and 200x respectively.

### Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1: Identify the baseline cost.**
$$C_{\text{baseline}} = ₹1{,}000 \text{ (at Requirements phase)}$$

**Step 2: Calculate cost at each subsequent phase.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Design}} &= C_{\text{baseline}} \times 8 = 1000 \times 8 = ₹8{,}000 \\
C_{\text{Coding}} &= C_{\text{baseline}} \times 15 = 1000 \times 15 = ₹15{,}000 \\
C_{\text{System Test}} &= C_{\text{baseline}} \times 100 = 1000 \times 100 = ₹1{,}00{,}000 \\
C_{\text{Post-Deployment}} &= C_{\text{baseline}} \times 200 = 1000 \times 200 = ₹2{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the cumulative cost (if the defect is fixed only at the latest point).**

$$
C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{baseline}} + C_{\text{Design}} + C_{\text{Coding}} + C_{\text{System Test}} + C_{\text{Post-Deployment}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total}} &= 1{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 \\
&= ₹3{,}24{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the Cost Amplification Factor at post-deployment.**

$$
\text{CAF}_{\text{post-deployment}} = \frac{C_{\text{Post-Deployment}}}{C_{\text{baseline}}} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1{,}000} = 200
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A defect that is free to fix at requirements costs **₹3.24 Lakhs** if propagated untouched. This single numerical proof is why Agile enforces **continuous testing, TDD, and early QA involvement** — to catch defects at the cheapest possible phase.

## 3.3 The Standish CHAOS Index (Decade-wise Evolution)

| Year | Success % | Challenged % | Failed % |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1994** | 16.2% | 52.7% | 31.1% |
| **2002** | 34.0% | 51.0% | 15.0% |
| **2010** | 37.0% | 44.0% | 19.0% |
| **2018** | 36.0% | 52.0% | 12.0% |
| **2020+ (Agile era)** | ~38–42% | ~50% | ~10–12% |

**Textual Logic:** The data shows that Agile adoption (post-2001) **doubled the success rate** and **halved the failure rate**, validating the SDLC-to-Agile transition.

## 3.4 Quality Implications of the Transition

1. **Shift-Left Testing:** Testers join from Day 1, not after coding completes.
2. **Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment (CI/CD):** Every commit triggers automated tests.
3. **Definition of Done (DoD):** Every user story must meet agreed quality criteria before being accepted.
4. **Test Automation:** Replaces manual regression, enabling frequent releases.
5. **Pair Programming & Code Reviews:** Built-in peer quality checks replace formal inspections.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Traditional SDLC (Waterfall) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Feasibility Study] --> node2[Requirement Analysis - SRS]
    node2 --> node3[System Design - SDD]
    node3 --> node4[Implementation - Coding]
    node4 --> node5[Integration and System Testing]
    node5 --> node6[Deployment]
    node6 --> node7[Maintenance]
    node7 --> node8[Phase End Review Gate]
    node8 --> node9[Project Closure or Retirement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice the **one-way flow** with no backward arrows. This represents the **biggest weakness** of Waterfall — once you cross a gate, returning upstream is prohibitively expensive (a phenomenon called **"phase imprisonment"**).

## 4.2 The Agile Sprint Iteration Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Product Backlog Grooming] --> nodeB[Sprint Planning]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Sprint Backlog Locked]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Daily Standup 15 min]
    nodeD --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[Development plus Unit Testing]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Continuous Integration]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Sprint Review Demo]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Sprint Retrospective]
    nodeH --> nodeA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Unlike Waterfall, the Agile cycle is a **closed loop** with continuous feedback. The Retrospective feeds improvements back into the next Sprint — this is the **Inspect and Adapt** engine of Agile.

## 4.3 The Transition Roadmap: From SDLC to Agile

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeT1[Stage 1 Traditional SDLC] --> nodeT2[Stage 2 Identify Pain Points]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3[Stage 3 Pilot Agile on Small Project]
    nodeT3 --> nodeT4[Stage 4 Train Teams in Scrum XP Kanban]
    nodeT4 --> nodeT5[Stage 5 Establish Cross Functional Teams]
    nodeT5 --> nodeT6[Stage 6 Integrate DevOps CI CD]
    nodeT6 --> nodeT7[Stage 7 Scale using SAFe LeSS Nexus]
    nodeT7 --> nodeT8[Stage 8 Enterprise Agile Maturity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This is the **8-stage maturity ladder** recommended by the Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe). Most KTU exam questions on transformation mention 3–5 stages; the full 8-stage model earns full marks.

## 4.4 Comparative Block Architecture: SDLC vs Agile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Traditional SDLC Architecture]
      s1a[Requirements] --> s1b[Design] --> s1c[Code] --> s1d[Test] --> s1e[Deploy]
    end
    subgraph S2[Agile Architecture]
      s2a[Plan] --> s2b[Build] --> s2c[Test] --> s2d[Review] --> s2e[Deploy]
      s2e --> s2a
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The left block (SDLC) is **linear and terminal** — it ends at Deploy. The right block (Agile) is **cyclical** — Deploy feeds back into Plan. This is the **single most visual difference** KTU examiners look for in diagrams.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define SDLC. List its phases.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 4]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) is a structured, phased framework that defines the processes and phases involved in the development of a software system, from initial feasibility study to deployment and maintenance, ensuring systematic, controlled, and high-quality software production.

**Phases (1.5 marks):**
1. Feasibility Study
2. Requirement Analysis and SRS preparation
3. System Design (HLD and LLD)
4. Implementation and Coding
5. Testing (Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance)
6. Deployment and Maintenance

---

### Question 2: What is the Agile Manifesto? State its four core values.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 4]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Agile Manifesto is a formal declaration of 4 values and 12 principles authored in February 2001 by 17 software practitioners at Snowbird, Utah, USA, to address the limitations of document-heavy, plan-driven SDLC methodologies.

**Four Core Values (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Individuals and Interactions** over processes and tools
2. **Working Software** over comprehensive documentation
3. **Customer Collaboration** over contract negotiation
4. **Responding to Change** over following a plan

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> Students often write the *definition* of Agile and SDLC but **forget to compare** them in a tabular or matrix form. KTU evaluators specifically award **2 marks** for a clear side-by-side comparison. Always include a comparison table. Also, never write about Agile without mentioning the **4 Manifesto values** — losing those 2 marks is the most common pitfall.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Comparison and Transition

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the traditional SDLC Waterfall model in detail. Discuss its phases and identify the key limitations that led to the adoption of Agile methodologies.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction to Waterfall (2 marks):** The Waterfall Model, introduced by Winston Royce in 1970, is a sequential, plan-driven software development methodology where each phase must be completed before the next begins. It treats software development like a manufacturing assembly line.

**Phases Explained (3 marks):**
1. **Feasibility Study:** Evaluates technical, economic, operational, and legal feasibility. Output: Feasibility Report.
2. **Requirement Analysis:** Captures functional and non-functional requirements in an SRS document (IEEE 830 standard). Frozen at this stage.
3. **System Design:** Translates SRS into architecture — HLD (High-Level Design) defines modules, LLD (Low-Level Design) defines logic, database schema, and interfaces.
4. **Implementation:** Developers write code in chosen programming language, following coding standards and version control.
5. **Testing:** Independent QA team executes unit, integration, system, and acceptance tests. Defects are logged and tracked.
6. **Deployment:** Software is installed in the customer environment. Release notes and user manuals are delivered.
7. **Maintenance:** Corrective, adaptive, perfective, and preventive maintenance over the software's operational lifetime.

**Key Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **No iteration** — late-stage changes are extremely expensive (Boehm's cost-of-change curve).
2. **Customer sees the product only at the end** — high rejection risk.
3. **Requirements freezing** is unrealistic in dynamic markets.
4. **Testing delayed to the end** — defect leakage is high.
5. **Heavy documentation** slows down delivery and adds no customer value.

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Compare SDLC and Agile across at least 8 dimensions. Explain Boehm's Cost-of-Change curve and justify why Agile reduces the cost of defects.

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Dimension | SDLC (Waterfall) | Agile |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | Sequential, plan-driven | Iterative, incremental |
| Requirements | Fixed at start | Evolving, refined per sprint |
| Delivery | One final delivery | Frequent small deliveries |
| Documentation | Extensive, formal | Lightweight, just-in-time |
| Customer Role | Peripheral | Active co-creator |
| Testing | Phase-end bulk testing | Continuous (Shift-Left) |
| Risk | High late-stage risk | Distributed per iteration |
| Team Structure | Specialized silos | Cross-functional teams |

**Boehm's Cost-of-Change Curve (2 marks):** Barry Boehm's 1981 empirical study proved that the cost to fix a defect **grows exponentially** as the project moves through phases. The amplification factors are:
- Requirements: **1x** (baseline)
- Design: **8x**
- Coding: **15x**
- Integration Test: **50x**
- System Test: **100x**
- Post-Deployment: **200x**

**Why Agile Reduces Cost (2 marks):** Agile practices such as **Shift-Left Testing**, **Test-Driven Development (TDD)**, **Continuous Integration**, **Pair Programming**, and **Daily Standups** ensure that defects are detected and fixed at the **earliest possible iteration** — ideally during the coding phase itself, when the cost amplification factor is only 15x instead of 200x. Additionally, **Sprint Retrospectives** and **working demos every 2 weeks** ensure rapid feedback, preventing the exponential cost accumulation seen in Waterfall.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Describe the Agile Manifesto in detail, including its 4 values and 12 principles. Explain how these values solve the limitations of traditional SDLC.

**Model Answer:**

**Origin and Context (1 mark):** The Agile Manifesto was authored in February 2001 at Snowbird, Utah, by 17 software practitioners (Kent Beck, Martin Fowler, Jeff Sutherland, Robert C. Martin, Mike Beedle, and others) as a direct response to the failures of document-heavy, plan-driven SDLC methodologies highlighted in the Standish CHAOS Report.

**Four Core Values (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Individuals and Interactions** over processes and tools — emphasizes people over rigid procedures.
2. **Working Software** over comprehensive documentation — prioritizes tangible deliverables over paperwork.
3. **Customer Collaboration** over contract negotiation — replaces adversarial contracts with ongoing partnership.
4. **Responding to Change** over following a plan — treats change as an asset, not a threat.

**Twelve Principles (3 marks — key ones to write):**
- Principle 1: Early and continuous delivery of valuable software.
- Principle 2: Welcome changing requirements, even late in development.
- Principle 3: Deliver working software frequently (2–4 week cycles).
- Principle 7: Working software is the primary measure of progress.
- Principle 9: Continuous attention to technical excellence and good design.
- Principle 11: Self-organizing teams produce the best architectures.
- Principle 12: Regular reflection on how to become more effective.

**How Values Solve SDLC Limitations (1 mark):**
- Customer Collaboration eliminates the SDLC problem of late feedback.
- Responding to Change removes the rigidity of frozen requirements.
- Working Software prioritizes tangible outcomes over bureaucratic documentation.
- Individuals and Interactions empower teams, reducing the silo-based inefficiency of SDLC.

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** With a neat diagram, explain the transition roadmap from traditional SDLC to Agile. Discuss the role of the Scrum framework in this transition.

**Model Answer:**

**Transition Roadmap Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stage1[Stage 1 Traditional SDLC] --> stage2[Stage 2 Identify Pain Points]
    stage2 --> stage3[Stage 3 Agile Pilot on Small Project]
    stage3 --> stage4[Stage 4 Train Teams in Scrum and XP]
    stage4 --> stage5[Stage 5 Form Cross Functional Teams]
    stage5 --> stage6[Stage 6 Adopt DevOps and CI CD]
    stage6 --> stage7[Stage 7 Scale via SAFe or LeSS]
```

**Explanation of Stages (2 marks):**
1. **Stage 1 — Traditional SDLC Baseline:** Establish the current process and metrics.
2. **Stage 2 — Pain Point Diagnosis:** Identify the top 3 SDLC failures (late delivery, defect leakage, scope creep).
3. **Stage 3 — Pilot Project:** Select a non-critical project, run on Scrum, measure improvement.
4. **Stage 4 — Team Training:** Train developers, testers, and BAs in Agile ceremonies, roles, and artifacts.
5. **Stage 5 — Cross-Functional Teams:** Break down silos; form squads of 5–9 members with all skills.
6. **Stage 6 — DevOps Integration:** Automate build, test, and deployment pipelines (CI/CD).
7. **Stage 7 — Scaling:** Use frameworks like **SAFe**, **LeSS**, or **Nexus** to scale Agile across the enterprise.

**Role of Scrum (2 marks):** Scrum is the most widely adopted Agile framework and serves as the **practical implementation engine** of the transition. It provides:
- **3 Roles:** Product Owner (prioritizes backlog), Scrum Master (facilitates process), Development Team (builds the product).
- **4 Ceremonies:** Sprint Planning, Daily Standup, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective.
- **3 Artifacts:** Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, Increment.

Scrum's timeboxed **Sprints (typically 2 weeks)** enforce the Agile value of *frequent delivery of working software*, while its **Retrospective** ensures continuous process improvement — directly addressing SDLC's rigidity and late-delivery problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
>
> - SDLC is **plan-driven, sequential, document-heavy** — first formalized by **Winston Royce in 1970**.
> - The 6 canonical SDLC phases are **Feasibility → Requirements → Design → Coding → Testing → Maintenance**.
> - **Boehm's Cost-of-Change** amplifies defect cost from **1x (Requirements)** to **200x (Post-Deployment)**.
> - The **Standish CHAOS Report (1994)** showed only **16.2%** of Waterfall projects succeeded — triggering the Agile revolution.
> - The **Agile Manifesto** was authored in **February 2001 at Snowbird, Utah**, by **17 practitioners** including **Kent Beck** and **Martin Fowler**.
> - The 4 Manifesto values prioritize: **People, Working Software, Customer Collaboration, Change Response** (in that order).
> - Agile is **iterative, incremental, customer-centric, and test-driven** (Shift-Left Testing).
> - Common Agile frameworks: **Scrum** (most popular), **XP (Extreme Programming)**, **Kanban**, **SAFe** (for scaling).
> - Scrum has **3 Roles, 4 Ceremonies, 3 Artifacts** — memorize this matrix.
> - The transition from SDLC to Agile is **gradual, not abrupt** — typically follows a **5 to 8 stage maturity ladder**.
> - **CI/CD (Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment)** is the technical backbone that enables Agile's frequent delivery.
> - KTU examiners reward **comparison tables, diagrams, and numerical problem-solving** — always include all three.
> - **Common pitfall:** Confusing "Agile means no documentation" — the Manifesto says documentation has value, just *less* value than working software.
